title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
2.1 Core Explanation
Collaborative filtering is a type of recommender system. Instead of looking at the content of items (like genre or description), it looks at the behaviour and preferences of users. The core idea is:

“If two people have rated many things similarly, they probably have similar taste – and we can use one person’s ratings to predict the other’s.”

There are two main flavours:

User-based collaborative filtering (UBCF): Find users who are similar to the target user. Use their ratings to predict what the target user would rate unseen items. Think of it as “people like you also liked…”
Item-based collaborative filtering (IBCF): Find items that tend to be rated similarly by many users. If two movies consistently receive similar ratings, seeing a rating for one lets you predict the other.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
2.2 Walkthrough in R (MovieLense Dataset)

2.2.1 Load and Explore
library(recommenderlab)
set.seed(42)
data(MovieLense)
# 943 users, 1664 movies
# rating = 0 means no rating was given


2.2.2 Filter the Data
Only keep users who rated ≥ 20 movies, and movies rated by ≥ 50 users. This removes sparse entries that would make predictions unreliable.
ratings = MovieLense[rowCounts(MovieLense) >= 20,
                     colCounts(MovieLense) >= 50]
dim(ratings)  # [1] 929 601


2.2.3 Normalise Ratings
Different users have different rating tendencies — some always rate things highly, others always rate things low. Normalisation centres each user’s ratings around zero so that these personal biases don’t distort comparisons.
norm_ratings = recommenderlab::normalize(ratings)


2.2.4 Split into Train / Test
The evaluationScheme() function splits data so the model can be trained and then evaluated. Key parameters:

given = 10: Each test user provides 10 known ratings for the model to use.
goodRating = 3: Ratings ≥ 3 count as a “good” recommendation.
k = 3: Run the evaluation 3 times (cross-validation).

evalScheme = evaluationScheme(data = ratings,
                              method = "split",
                              train = 0.9,
                              given = 10,
                              goodRating = 3,
                              k = 3)


2.2.5 Train and Evaluate Models
User-based (UBCF):
recommender = Recommender(data = getData(evalScheme, "train"),
                          method = "UBCF")
predictions = predict(object = recommender,
                      newdata = getData(evalScheme, "known"),
                      n = 12, type = "ratings")

accuracy1 = calcPredictionAccuracy(x = predictions,
                                   data = getData(evalScheme, "unknown"),
                                   byUser = FALSE)
# RMSE: 1.24  |  MAE: 0.98
Item-based (IBCF):
recommender = Recommender(data = getData(evalScheme, "train"),
                          method = "IBCF")
# RMSE: 1.24  |  MAE: 0.91  ← slightly better


2.2.6 Key Accuracy Metrics







Metric
Meaning




MAE (Mean Absolute Error)
Average prediction error in rating units. MAE ≈ 0.98 means predictions are off by ~1 star on average.


RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
Similar to MAE but penalises large errors more heavily.


MSE
Mean Squared Error – RMSE²

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
3.1 Core Explanation
Imagine a spreadsheet with 784 columns (one per pixel in an image). Most of those columns carry redundant information — neighbouring pixels tend to have similar values. Dimensionality reduction compresses this data into fewer columns while trying to preserve as much of the meaningful variation as possible.

Key insight: “Dimensions” = columns = features. Fewer dimensions → faster models, less memory, less noise.


3.1.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
PCA is the most widely used dimensionality reduction technique. Here’s how it works, step by step:

Imagine your data as a cloud of points in multi-dimensional space.
Find the line (direction) through that cloud that captures the most spread — this is the first principal component (PC1).
Find the next direction that is perpendicular (orthogonal) to PC1 and captures the second-most spread → PC2.
Repeat. Each component captures less and less variance.
You can then keep only the top N components that together explain a sufficient percentage of variance (e.g., 80%).

PCA is related to linear regression in its use of least-squares fitting — both minimise the squared distance from points to a line.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
3.2 MNIST Dataset
The MNIST database is a classic benchmark dataset: 70,000 greyscale images (28×28 pixels = 784 pixel columns) of handwritten digits 0–9. It is commonly used to test image classification and dimensionality reduction methods.
digits <- read.csv("data/train.csv")
dim(digits)  # 42000 rows × 785 columns (1 label + 784 pixels)

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
3.3 Applying PCA to MNIST

3.3.1 Step 1: Filter to One Digit Class
digits_5 <- digits[digits$label == 5, ]
digits_5$label <- NULL

# Remove pixels with zero variance (always black — no information)
digits_5b <- digits_5[, apply(digits_5, 2, var, na.rm = TRUE) != 0]


3.3.2 Step 2: Run PCA
pca <- prcomp(digits_5b, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
s <- summary(pca)
cumprop <- s$importance[3, ]  # Cumulative proportion of variance explained
plot(cumprop, type = "l", main = "Cumulative sum", xlab = "PCA component")
The cumulative variance plot rises steeply at first (the first few components explain a lot) and then flattens (adding more components gives diminishing returns).


3.3.3 Step 3: Choose How Many Components
# How many components are needed to explain 80% of variance?
num_cols <- min(which(cumprop > 0.8))
num_cols   # 82 — only 82 components needed instead of 600+!

82 components explain 80% of the variance in ~600 original pixel columns. That is an enormous reduction in data size.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
3.4 Key Formula
\[\text{Cumulative Variance Explained} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j} \lambda_j}\]
Where \(\lambda_i\) is the eigenvalue (variance) of the \(i\)-th principal component.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
4.1 Core Explanation
Outlier analysis (also called anomaly detection) finds data points that look very different from the rest. In a dataset of handwritten 5s, an outlier might be a particularly unusual or messy version of the digit — or even a mislabelled image.
Uses in practice: - Detecting fraudulent financial transactions - Identifying failing machinery - Flagging data entry errors
Outliers can sometimes be removed to improve model performance, or they can be the signal you care about (fraud detection).

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
4.2 Method 1: PCA Reconstruction Error
The idea: apply PCA to compress the data, then reconstruct it back to the original dimensions. If a data point reconstructs accurately → it looks like typical data. If reconstruction error is high → it is unusual.
preds <- predict(pca, digits_5b)

# Calculate MAE for each data point
error <- vector()
for (n in 1:1000) {
  actual_row <- digits_5b[n, ]
  pred_row   <- preds[n, ]
  error <- c(error, sum(abs(actual_row - pred_row)) / length(actual_row))
}

dfError <- data.frame(id = seq(1:length(error)), mae = error)

Low MAE → looks like a typical “5” (clean, standard handwriting).
High MAE → atypical “5” (very stylised, rotated, or possibly mislabelled as a 6).

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
4.3 Method 2: One-Class SVM
Support Vector Machines (SVM) are usually used for classification between two classes. A one-class SVM learns only from normal data and then flags any point that doesn’t fit the learned boundary as an outlier.
library(e1071)
svm.model <- svm(digits_5b, y = NULL,
                 type = 'one-classification',
                 nu = 0.10,       # ~10% of points expected to be outliers
                 scale = TRUE,
                 kernel = "radial")

svm.predtrain <- predict(svm.model, digits_5b)
table(svm.predtrain)
# FALSE: 383 outliers detected
# TRUE:  3412 normal points
The nu parameter controls how many outliers you expect (0.10 = allow up to 10%).

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
5.1 Core Explanation
Graph analytics (network analysis) studies structures made up of:

Nodes (also called vertices): The entities — e.g., people, web pages, towns.
Edges (also called links or relationships): The connections between entities — e.g., friendships, hyperlinks, roads.

Both nodes and edges can carry additional data (attributes), such as a user’s age (node attribute) or the distance between two towns (edge attribute/weight).

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
5.2 Graph Properties








Property
Description
Example




Connected vs Disconnected
Is there a path between every pair of nodes?
A social network is usually connected


Weighted vs Unweighted
Do edges have a numerical value?
Road distances between cities (weighted)


Directed vs Undirected
Do relationships have a direction (A→B ≠ B→A)?
Twitter follows (directed) vs Facebook friends (undirected)


Cyclic vs Acyclic
Can a path start and end at the same node?
A route map with loops is cyclic

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
section: 5.3 Creating a Simple Graph in R
text:
5.3 Creating a Simple Graph in R
library(igraph)
g1 <- make_graph(edges = c(1,2, 2,3, 3,4, 4,1), directed = FALSE)
plot(g1, vertex.size = 40, edge.width = 2, edge.color = "blue")

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
5.4 Types of Graph Algorithms

Pathfinding: Find the shortest route through nodes. Classic example: the Travelling Salesman Problem.
Centrality: Identify the most important nodes in a network (e.g., most influential person in a social network).
Community Detection: Find clusters/groups within the network — similar to clustering but on relational data.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
5.5 Real-World Applications

Marketing attribution: Tracing the sequence of adverts a user sees before a purchase.
Customer purchase modelling: Predicting what a customer will buy next based on purchase sequences.
Fraud network detection: Finding suspicious clusters of transactions.
Disease spread modelling: Mapping how an infection moves through a population.
Internet search engines: PageRank (see below).

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
5.6 PageRank
PageRank was the algorithm that powered Google’s original search engine. It estimates the importance of a web page by:

Counting how many other pages link to it.
Weighing those links by the importance of the linking page (a link from bbc.com is worth far more than a link from an obscure site).
Iterating thousands of times until the scores stabilise.

This means a page with fewer but higher-quality inbound links can rank higher than a page with many low-quality links.
# PageRank can be computed in igraph
page_rank(g1)

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
6.1 Why Do This?
Running a classifier on 784 raw pixel columns is slow and can suffer from the curse of dimensionality (too many features, not enough signal per feature). By applying PCA first, you:

Reduce the number of input features dramatically.
Remove noise (low-variance components).
Speed up training significantly.
Often improve accuracy.

title: STAT9013 – Week 8 Study Guide
text:
6.2 Full Workflow

6.2.1 1. Load and Filter Data
digits <- read.csv("data/train.csv")

# Keep only digits 5 and 6
data <- digits[digits$label %in% c(5, 6), ]
data$label <- as.factor(data$label)
# 3795 fives, 4137 sixes → 7932 total


6.2.2 2. Train/Test Split

Critical rule: Always split before applying PCA. If you apply PCA to the full dataset and then split, the test set influences the PCA — this is called data leakage and will give you falsely optimistic results.

set.seed(42)
input  <- setdiff(names(data), "label")
nobs   <- nrow(data)
train  <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test   <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

train_data <- data[train, ]
test_data  <- data[test,  ]


6.2.3 3. Apply PCA to Training Data Only
# Remove zero-variance pixels
train_data <- train_data[, apply(train_data, 2, var, na.rm = TRUE) != 0]
input <- setdiff(names(train_data), "label")

pca     <- prcomp(train_data[, input], center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
s       <- summary(pca)
cumprop <- s$importance[3, ]

# Find number of components for 80% variance
num_cols <- min(which(cumprop > 0.8))
# num_cols = 96  →  96 components explain 80% of variance


6.2.4 4. Project Both Sets onto PCA Space
# Training set: take first 96 principal components
train_pca <- as.data.frame(pca$x[, 1:num_cols])
train_pca$label <- train_data$label

# Test set: project using the SAME PCA transformation from train
test_pca <- as.data.frame(predict(pca, test_data[, input]))
test_pca$label <- test_data$label
test_pca <- test_pca[, names(train_pca)]  # keep same columns


6.2.5 5. Train a Logistic Regression Model
logistic_model <- glm(formula = label ~ .,
                      family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      data = train_pca)


6.2.6 6. Evaluate
pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_pca, type = "response")
pr <- ifelse(pr < 0.5, 5, 6)
pr <- factor(pr)

tab <- table(pr, test_pca$label)
accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
print(tab)
# Accuracy ≈ 97.4%
Confusion matrix result:




Actual 5
Actual 6




Predicted 5
379
10


Predicted 6
11
394



Only 21 misclassifications out of 794 test samples — excellent performance with greatly reduced data.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 1 Quick Recap — What You Should Know from Week 1
text:
1 Quick Recap — What You Should Know from Week 1
Before diving in, make sure these are solid from last week:







Topic
One-line Summary




Data → Information → Knowledge
Data is raw facts; processed data becomes information; applied information becomes knowledge


AI / ML / Deep Learning
AI is the broadest idea; ML and Deep Learning are subsets of it


CRISP-DM (6 stages)
Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Preparation → Modelling → Evaluation → Deployment


Supervised vs Unsupervised
Supervised = prediction with labelled data; Unsupervised = finding patterns without labels


Stochastic vs Deterministic
Traditional software: same inputs → same output. ML: same inputs may → different outputs



Questions about Week 1? Email mark.hodnett@mtu.ie or message via Canvas.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
2 Key Topics Covered This Week

CRISP-DM revisited — why 85–90% of a project happens before modelling
The PayPal churn case study — real-world business understanding done right
Summary statistics: mean, median, quartiles, IQR
The drawPlots helper function in R
Univariate plots: histograms, density plots, box plots
Interpreting the plots (not just producing them)
Skewness — what it is, why it matters for ML, and how to fix it with log transforms
The mammals dataset — a concrete R² improvement example
Working with built-in R datasets (datasets package)
Data types in R — especially factors
Full data exploration workflow on the Iris dataset (8 steps)
Introduction to the Titanic dataset and binary classification
Feature engineering overview — why it matters more than algorithm choice

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 3 Why This Week Matters — A Preview of What’s Coming
text:
3 Why This Week Matters — A Preview of What’s Coming






Note🔮 Forward Links — Where Week 2 Skills Are Used Later



Week 2 is the foundation of almost every later week. Your exploratory data analysis skills directly feed into:







Later Week
What Week 2 Skill You’ll Use




Week 3 (Decision Trees)
The setosa if statement rule (Petal.Length < 2) becomes the root split of a decision tree. The 177 missing Ages need imputation before any model can run.


Week 5 (Titanic deep dive)
The cabin encoding (77% missing) gets extracted as a deck letter. The Name column gets parsed for titles (Mr/Mrs/Miss/Master) to help impute Age better.


Week 6 (Logistic Regression)
Data leakage is introduced — and the key example is “don’t calculate your mean-age-imputation on the full dataset, only on the training set.”


Week 7 (Clustering)
The Iris setosa separation seen in Week 2 box plots is confirmed by k-means clustering — the model independently discovers the same pattern.



This is why data exploration matters. The insights you find in Week 2 become the features, rules, and imputations of the later weeks. Skip data exploration and you skip the foundation of everything else.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 4 CRISP-DM — Why Data Comes Before Modelling
text:
4 CRISP-DM — Why Data Comes Before Modelling

4.1 The Framework
CRISP-DM stands for Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining. It is a flexible, agile process for machine learning projects — not a rigid checklist. The arrows in the diagram go back and forth between stages because machine learning is a research process.








Stage
What you do
Key point




1. Business Understanding
What does the business actually need?
Wrong question = wrong model. Focus on revenue, not just accuracy.


2. Data Understanding
What data exists? Is it in the right format? Is it even collected?
You may discover the data you need doesn’t exist yet


3. Data Preparation
Clean, transform, engineer features
This is where most of your time goes


4. Modelling
Apply ML algorithms
Only ~10–15% of total project time!


5. Evaluation
Does the model meet the business goal?
A 99% accurate model can still be useless — see fraud example below


6. Deployment
How do stakeholders access results?
May include a human-in-the-loop review process









Important⭐ Exam Tip — The Single Most Important CRISP-DM Quote



Professor’s note: “You might only spend 10–15% of your time on a project actually in the modelling stage. The rest of it is business understanding, data preparation, and things like that.”
Two of the six stages are entirely about data — before any model is built. Know why this matters, not just what the stages are. The examiner wants to see that you understand the emphasis on preparation over modelling.




4.2 Machine Learning as a Research Problem
Machine learning is fundamentally different from standard software development. The professor made this distinction clearly:







Traditional Software
Machine Learning




Fixed requirements, known goal
Open-ended — you may not know if the problem is solvable


Same inputs → same outputs (deterministic)
Same inputs may not always → same outputs (stochastic)


Goal is fixed before you start
You discover whether the goal is achievable as you work


Jira tickets, two-week sprints
Iterative, experimental, research-like cycles



Professor’s note: “Machine learning is essentially a research problem. A lot of times, you’re given a dataset and you’re not told if you can actually solve the problem. The phrase we use a lot in the company that I work in — it’s a stochastic process. It’s not guaranteed to give you the same answers all the time.”






Note💡 Analogy — Cooking competition vs. assembly line



Think of a cooking competition. Most of your time is spent selecting ingredients, tasting, adjusting, and going back to try again. That’s CRISP-DM. An assembly line (traditional software) has fixed steps and a guaranteed output. Machine learning is the cooking competition.




4.3 The Fraud Detection Evaluation Problem






Important⭐ Exam Tip — Why 99% Accuracy Can Be Useless



The professor gave this example repeatedly. A model that predicts every transaction is fine would have 99.999% accuracy on a fraud dataset — because almost all transactions genuinely are fine. But it would catch zero fraudulent transactions.
The solution: Use the ML model as a filter, not a final decision-maker. Flag the top 1–2% of most suspicious transactions for a human to review. This is called human-in-the-loop machine learning.
Forward link: You will revisit this when you learn about confusion matrices, precision, recall, and the imbalanced dataset problem in Week 4+.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
5 The PayPal Churn Case Study






Important⭐ Exam Tip — This Case Study Was Repeated Across Two Weeks



When the professor repeats an example across multiple sessions, it is a strong signal it is exam-relevant. Be prepared to describe the steps and the lesson.


The problem: PayPal was losing approximately 1 million merchants per year for 17 years and could not fix it.
The key insight: The solution was not a fancy algorithm — it was carefully defining what they were actually trying to predict.

5.1 The Filtering Steps They Took








Step
What they removed
Why




Exclude cancellations
Accounts that already cancelled
By the time someone cancels, it’s too late. Focus on before cancellation


Exclude “one-and-dones”
Merchants who transacted once with tiny revenue
Not worth effort; negligible revenue impact


Separate activation from retention
New merchants with onboarding problems
Different problem, different team required


Remove false positives
Seasonal businesses, subscription-only, TOS violations
They’re not churning — they’re seasonal or were removed for cause


Focus on revenue churn
Top 10% of merchants = 90% of revenue
Losing a €100k/month merchant matters far more than losing a €500/month one



Professor’s note: “The task is not really to prevent churn. The task is to make money. And to make money, you look at the customers that are spending the most, and you prioritize them.”


5.2 What They Actually Did
After filtering, they had ~50,000 high-value merchants who churned. They then:

Examined each case individually — looking at all touchpoints (logging, risk, compliance, customer service)
Identified 20 “killer scenarios” — 20 distinct reasons why merchants leave
Applied a predictive model to bucket merchants into the relevant scenario
Sent results to customer service, who called the merchants to fix the problems

Professor’s note: “Their fancy predictive model was basically a series of SQL queries, and then basically sending the results to customer service, who would, in a lot of cases, call the customers and fix the problems.”






Note💡 The Core Lesson



Good business understanding — knowing exactly what you are predicting, for whom, and when — is more valuable than any algorithm. “Always ask: what am I actually trying to solve?” before reaching for a machine learning model.








Warning⚠️ Common Mistake — Predicting the Wrong Thing (Data Leakage Preview)



The professor said: “Cancelling is too late. By the time someone goes along and cancels their Netflix, it’s probably too late for Netflix to do anything about it.”
Data leakage warning: Never use a variable that only exists after the event you are predicting (e.g., “date account was closed” to predict churn). This is called data leakage — the model will look great in training but fail completely in production.
Forward link to Week 6: Data leakage is formally covered in Week 6, where you’ll see another form — accidentally computing transformations (like mean imputation) on the full dataset before the train/test split. Remember: any transformation you apply to predict future data must be based only on past (training) data.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 6 Summary Statistics — Mean, Median, Quartiles, IQR
text:
6 Summary Statistics — Mean, Median, Quartiles, IQR

6.1 The Five-Number Summary
For any column in a dataset, R’s summary() function gives you the five key statistics:


Code
summary(iris[, -5])   # summary of all numeric columns (excluding Species)










Statistic
What it means
R function




Min
Smallest value in the column
min(x)


1st Quartile (Q1)
25% of values are below this
quantile(x, 0.25)


Median (Q2)
Middle value — 50% below, 50% above
median(x)


Mean
Sum of all values ÷ count
mean(x)


3rd Quartile (Q3)
75% of values are below this
quantile(x, 0.75)


Max
Largest value
max(x)





6.2 Mean vs Median — When Each Is Better









Measure
Definition
Sensitive to outliers?
Best used for




Mean
Sum ÷ count
Yes — one extreme value can shift it a lot
Symmetric data with no extreme outliers


Median
Middle value of sorted data
No — extreme values barely affect it
Skewed data (income, house prices, COVID deaths)









Note💡 Analogy — Bill Gates walks into a room



11 people earn roughly €50,000 each. Mean and median are both ~€50,000 — they agree.
Now Bill Gates and Jeff Bezos walk in. Their wealth is billions.

The mean salary in the room might jump to €30 million — nobody in the room actually earns that.
The median shifts only slightly — it still represents a “typical” person in the room.

Professor’s note: “This is why the media report the median age of COVID deaths rather than the average — because the average really doesn’t mean anything. If the median is 60, that basically means 50 people were younger than 60, and 50 were older than 60. It’s more descriptive of what’s going on.”








Warning⚠️ Common Mistake — Defaulting to the Mean



Students often assume the mean is always the best measure of “average.” This is wrong when data is skewed or has outliers.
Rule of thumb: - Symmetric data (heights, test scores) → mean ≈ median → either is fine - Skewed data or outliers (income, house prices, body weights) → use the median




6.3 Quartiles and Interquartile Range (IQR)
Quartiles divide sorted data into four equal parts.








Name
Position
What it means




Q1 (25th percentile)
25% of data is below this
Lower boundary of the middle half


Q2 (50th percentile)
This is the median
The exact middle


Q3 (75th percentile)
75% of data is below this
Upper boundary of the middle half


IQR = Q3 − Q1
—
Spread of the middle 50% — robust to outliers



How to calculate:

Order the data from smallest to largest
Cut into 4 equal-size groups
Each group = one “quartile”
IQR = the range covered by the middle 2 quartiles (Q1 to Q3)

The IQR is used by box plots to define the “normal” range and identify outliers.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
7 Visualising Distributions — Univariate Plots
Univariate means “one variable at a time.” These three plot types all show the distribution (spread and shape) of a single column, but in different formats.









Plot type
Format
Best for
Audience




Histogram
Bars — count of values per bin
First look at shape
Technical and non-technical


Density plot
Smooth curve (like a smoothed histogram)
Comparing shapes; seeing skewness
Technical


Box plot
Compact: shows median, IQR, outliers
Side-by-side group comparison
Technical (avoid for business users)



Professor’s note: “The disadvantage of the box plot is that it’s typically not well understood by non-statistical people. If I’m presenting to business users, I tend to avoid box plots. It’s very useful for me and other people who are used to statistics, but not something a lot of people outside of statistics would be familiar with.”






Important⭐ The Most Important Meta-Lesson of Week 2



Professor’s note (from Week 3 recap): “If you’re just producing the plots, and then jumping straight into machine learning, you’re wasting your time. The plots don’t tell you anything. You should be looking at the plots, interpreting the plots, and basically asking yourself questions from those plots to see if it can guide you into your feature engineering.”
Producing plots is easy. Interpreting plots is the skill. Every plot you make should answer: “What does this tell me about the data? What questions does it raise? What decisions does it lead to?”



7.1 The qplot Function
qplot is part of the ggplot2 package — described by the professor as “probably the best graphical code library that exists for any programming language.”



You pass
Default result
Override with




1 column
Histogram
geom = "density", geom = "boxplot"


2 columns
Scatter plot
geom = "point", geom = "line"



Key parameters:


Code
qplot(
  data,                        # the column / vector to plot
  geom = "histogram",          # "histogram", "density", "boxplot", "point"
  bins = 30,                   # histogram only: how many bars?
  main = "My Plot Title",      # title
  xlab = "X-axis label",       # x-axis
  ylab = "Y-axis label"        # y-axis
)




7.2 Reading a Box Plot
     |-------[========|========]-------|    •  •
     ^        ^        ^        ^      ^   outliers
  whisker    Q1     median     Q3   whisker
             (25%)            (75%)
             |<------ IQR ------->|
             (box covers middle 50%)

Whiskers extend to 1.5 × IQR from Q1 and Q3.
Anything beyond the whisker = outlier (shown as a dot).
Blue dot (in drawPlots) = the MEAN.



Part
Represents




Left edge of box
Q1 — 25th percentile


Line inside box
Median (Q2)


Right edge of box
Q3 — 75th percentile


Whiskers
Up to 1.5 × IQR from Q1 and Q3


Dots beyond whiskers
Outliers


Blue dot (professor’s helper)
The mean





7.3 The drawPlots Helper Function
The professor wrote this helper function — it draws all three plots side by side. Blue line = mean. Green line = median. When they are in the same place, the data is symmetric. When they diverge, the data is skewed.


Code
drawPlots <- function(data, title) {
  p01 <- qplot(data, geom = "histogram", bins = 100) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")

  p02 <- qplot(data, geom = "density") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")

  p03 <- qplot(data, geom = "boxplot") +
    coord_flip() +
    geom_point(aes(x = mean(data), y = 0), size = 3, color = "blue")

  gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, nrow = 1, top = title)
}

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 8 Skewness — What It Is and Why It Matters
text:
8 Skewness — What It Is and Why It Matters

8.1 What Is Skewness?
Skewness describes how asymmetric a distribution is. A normal (bell-shaped) distribution has no skewness — the left side mirrors the right side.










Distribution
Shape
Tail direction
Mean vs Median
Real-life example




Normal
Symmetric bell curve
No tail
Mean ≈ Median
Heights of adult males in one country


Right (positive) skew
Most values low; few very high
Tail goes right →
Mean > Median
Income, house prices, animal body weights


Left (negative) skew
Most values high; few very low
Tail goes left ←
Mean < Median
Test scores after an easy exam









Important⭐ The Key Rule — Mean Chases the Tail



Right skew: Mean > Median (a few very high values pull the mean up) Left skew: Mean < Median (a few very low values pull the mean down) Normal: Mean ≈ Median
This rule lets you identify skew direction from a single statistic — without even plotting. Know it for the exam.




Code
# Normal — mean ≈ median
data <- rnorm(10000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
drawPlots(data, "Normal distribution (median ~= mean)")

# Left (negative) skew — median > mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 5, 1)
drawPlots(data, "Left skewed (median-green > mean-blue)")

# Right (positive) skew — median < mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 1, 5)
drawPlots(data, "Right skewed (median-green < mean-blue)")




8.2 Real-World Examples of Skewed Data








Variable
Skew type
Why




Personal income
Right
Most people earn modestly; a few earn billions


Average spend per customer
Right
Most customers spend little; a few spend a lot


Distance travelled to work
Right
Most commutes are short; some are very long


Animal body weights
Right
Most mammals are small; elephants/whales are extreme outliers


Test scores on an easy exam
Left
Most students score high; a few score very low


Age at retirement
Left
Most retire around 65; very few retire very young





8.3 Why Skewness Matters for Machine Learning
Professor’s note (from Week 3 recap): “Most machine learning algorithms that handle numeric values may — it might be an explicit or a hidden assumption — that the data is from a normal distribution. Because that’s typically what datasets form for any realistic measurements. If you measure something, typically most of the time it’s a normal distribution, so they have a problem with skewed datasets.”
When data is strongly skewed:

The model’s performance can degrade significantly
The relationship between variables is harder to capture linearly
Outliers exert disproportionate influence on the model

The fix: Apply a mathematical transformation to make the data more normal. The most common are:








Transformation
When to use
Notes




log()
Right-skewed data with all positive values
Simple and very common — used in class


sqrt()
Mild right skew
Less aggressive than log


Box-Cox
General-purpose — finds the best transformation automatically
More accurate than log; a real-world standard



Professor’s note: “There are other [transformations]. I think Box-Cox is probably the most common and more accurate than log. We just use log as a very simple example for our mammals dataset.”






Note💡 Analogy — Compressing a stretched photo



Imagine a photo that is stretched very wide on the right side (a few extreme values). The log transform is like compressing it back to a more normal shape. You’re not removing any data — you’re just rescaling it so the extreme values don’t dominate.








Warning⚠️ When NOT to Just Transform



Professor’s note: “Does this mean you should naturally go with all skew data, perform a log function on it? Possibly not. Maybe what you should do is try both — with and without — and see if it has an effect on the data. In this case, we only have one variable [brain weight], so of course it’s highly predictive. But it doesn’t work in all cases.”
Also consider: are your outliers real or errors? If they’re data entry errors, remove them. If they’re real extreme cases (like elephants), keep them and transform.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 9 The Mammals Dataset — A Concrete Log Transform Example
text:
9 The Mammals Dataset — A Concrete Log Transform Example
This is one of the most important examples in Week 2. It shows numerically how a simple data transformation can dramatically improve a model.

9.1 Step 1 — Plot the Raw Data


Code
p01 <- qplot(mammals$body_wt, geom = "density",
             main = "Mammals body weight", xlab = "body weight")
p02 <- qplot(mammals$brain_wt, geom = "density",
             main = "Mammals brain weight", xlab = "brain weight")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, nrow = 1)


Both plots are extremely right-skewed — a few large mammals (elephants, hippos) pull everything to the right.
Professor’s note: “If we try and plot [body weight vs brain weight], it’s almost impossible to draw a line that encompasses all this data, because the two elephants are so big that linear regression is going to be pulled… it’s going to underestimate the body weight.”


9.2 Step 2 — Build Model 1 on Raw Data


Code
model1 <- lm(body_wt ~ brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq1  <- cor(model1$fitted.values, mammals$body_wt)^2
r_sq1   # → 0.87


R² = 0.87 — not bad, but the skewed data is limiting the model.


9.3 Step 3 — Apply the Log Transform


Code
mammals$log_body_wt  <- log(mammals$body_wt)
mammals$log_brain_wt <- log(mammals$brain_wt)

# Compare raw vs transformed distribution:
p01 <- qplot(mammals$body_wt,     geom = "density",
             main = "Mammals body weight",       xlab = "body weight")
p02 <- qplot(mammals$log_body_wt, geom = "density",
             main = "Mammals body weight (log)", xlab = "body weight (log)")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, nrow = 1)


The log-transformed distribution is now approximately normal. The elephants are still in the dataset — they are just no longer extreme outliers relative to the others.


9.4 Step 4 — Why Not Just Delete the Elephants?
Professor’s note: “I don’t want to just throw out my larger records that I have data for. An outlier should be a record that was recorded incorrectly, or information not likely to occur in future. On the other hand, if my task is to predict body weight from brain weight, there certainly are larger mammals like elephants, hippos, giraffes — so I shouldn’t throw out the larger records. Also, if we delete the elephants, the cows and horses would become the new outliers. And if I ever encountered a mammoth, my model wouldn’t work for that either.”
This is an important principle for handling outliers:







Outlier Type
What to do




Data-entry error (e.g., age = 200)
Remove — it doesn’t represent reality


Real but extreme value (e.g., an elephant in a mammals dataset)
Keep and transform — it is a legitimate future case


A unique sub-population (e.g., the two elephants)
Investigate — may indicate a bimodal distribution





9.5 Step 5 — Plot the Relationship (Raw vs Log)


Code
p01 <- qplot(body_wt,     brain_wt,     data = mammals,
             main = "Body weight v brain weight")
p02 <- qplot(log_body_wt, log_brain_wt, data = mammals,
             main = "Log of Body weight v log of brain weight")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, nrow = 1)


The raw plot is a messy cloud — nearly impossible to draw a line through. The log-transformed plot shows a clear, near-linear relationship.


9.6 Step 6 — Build Model 2 on Log Data


Code
model2 <- lm(log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq2  <- cor(model2$fitted.values, mammals$log_body_wt)^2
r_sq2   # → 0.92








Important⭐ The Numbers to Memorise












Model 1 (raw)
Model 2 (log-transformed)




R²
0.87
0.92


Error rate
1 − 0.87 = 0.13
1 − 0.92 = 0.08


Error reduction
—
(0.13 − 0.08) / 0.13 = 37.79% fewer errors



The algorithm did not change. The improvement came purely from transforming the data.
Professor’s note: “Don’t just focus on the absolute value of the increase in accuracy. Focus on the relative difference in error rate. I decreased my error rate from 0.13 down to 0.08 — that’s a 30–40% improvement on the existing model.”

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 10 R Data Types and the datasets Package
text:
10 R Data Types and the datasets Package

10.1 R’s Basic Data Types
Understanding and setting the correct data types is a critical and often underestimated task in data preparation.








Type
Description
Example




numeric
Numbers with decimal points
5.1, 3.14, -0.5


integer
Whole numbers
1L, 42L


character
Text strings
“male”, “setosa”


logical
True or False
TRUE, FALSE


factor
Categorical variable — a fixed set of possible values
Species = {setosa, versicolor, virginica}









Important⭐ Factors — A Special R Data Type Worth Knowing



Professor’s note: “Factor is a special variable type, really only seen in R. It basically says there are three levels — the species must be one of these three. It’s almost like an enum — constrained to be one of a value in a set.”
Why it matters for ML: Some machine learning algorithms in R need categorical variables stored as factor, not as strings or integers. For example, if Pclass (1, 2, 3) is stored as integer, an algorithm may treat it as a continuous number — as if class 3 is “three times worse” than class 1. Converting it to factor tells the algorithm: these are categories, not numbers.
Professor’s note: “Crucial to get the column types correct!”
Forward link: In Week 3’s decision tree, the Survived column must be a factor — otherwise rpart will try to do regression instead of classification.




Code
# Converting types — the function usually starts with "as."
as.numeric("3")      # → 3       (works fine)
as.numeric("three")  # → NA      (fails — R can't convert text to number)

# Converting a column to factor:
titanic_train$Survived <- as.factor(titanic_train$Survived)
titanic_train$Pclass   <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)








Warning⚠️ Watch Out for Encoded Missing Values



Professor’s note: “A lot of systems might use minus 1 as the default value for empty. If you’re calculating revenue and the underlying system didn’t want to use an empty value, it might use minus 1 to say ‘no data’. If you apply that data blindly into a machine learning algorithm, the algorithm is not going to know the difference between 999 [meaning ‘missing’] and any other value.”
Common “fake” missing value encodings to look for:

-1 (especially in older systems)
999 or 9999
0 (when 0 is not a valid value)
Empty strings ""
NA (R’s official missing value marker)





10.2 The datasets Package
R comes with a built-in collection of datasets for practice. Some important ones:








Dataset
Description
Good for practising




iris
150 flower measurements across 3 species
Classification, correlation, EDA


titanic_train
Titanic passenger survival data
Binary classification, missing data


mammals
Brain and body weights of 62 mammals
Regression, log transforms


airquality
New York air quality measurements
Missing data, time series


USArrests
Violent crime rates by US state
Clustering, PCA


AirPassengers
Monthly airline passengers 1949–1960
Time series





Code
library(help = "datasets")   # see all available datasets
data(iris)                   # load a dataset by name

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 11 Data Exploration — The 8-Step Workflow on the Iris Dataset
text:
11 Data Exploration — The 8-Step Workflow on the Iris Dataset

11.1 Why Data Exploration Is Critical
Professor’s note: “Data exploration is critical to the success of your project. It is more important than anything you do in the machine learning model build stage, including picking an algorithm. The work done at this stage will mean a more robust and accurate machine learning model.”


11.2 About the Iris Dataset
The Iris dataset was created by British biologist and statistician Ronald Fisher in 1936. It contains 150 measurements from three species of iris flower:

Iris setosa
Iris versicolor
Iris virginica

Four features were measured from each of the 50 samples per species: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width.
Professor’s note: “The Iris dataset is the ‘Hello World’ of the machine learning process. It’s a very old dataset, but that’s why it’s always given as the example — because the patterns within it are well-understood and can be verified. In 1936, there wasn’t even the idea of colour photography, so the only way they had was measurements.”


11.3 The 8 Steps at a Glance








Step
Function
Purpose




1
dim(), names(), str()
Understand structure


2
summary()
Get summary stats


3
qplot(..., geom="histogram")
See distributions


4
qplot(..., geom="density")
Smooth view of distributions


5
qplot(..., geom="boxplot") with grouping
Compare groups


6
qplot(x, y, colour=group)
See relationships between variables


7
cor(), corrplot.mixed()
Measure linear relationships


8
pairs.panels()
Everything at once





11.4 Step 1 — Load and Inspect Structure


Code
data(iris)

dim(iris)    # 150 rows, 5 columns
names(iris)  # "Sepal.Length" "Sepal.Width" "Petal.Length" "Petal.Width" "Species"
str(iris)    # column types — numeric for measurements, factor for Species








Important⭐ The str() Function — Your First EDA Step



Always call str() on a new dataset first. It tells you:

How many rows and columns
The data type of every column
A preview of the first few values

This one line often reveals problems immediately — wrong types, unexpected values, or suspicious patterns.




11.5 Step 2 — Summary Statistics


Code
summary(iris[, -5])   # all columns except Species (column 5)


Iris summary statistics:



Stat
Sepal.Length
Sepal.Width
Petal.Length
Petal.Width




Min
4.300
2.000
1.000
0.100


Q1
5.100
2.800
1.600
0.300


Median
5.800
3.000
4.350
1.300


Mean
5.843
3.057
3.758
1.199


Q3
6.400
3.300
5.100
1.800


Max
7.900
4.400
6.900
2.500



Key observation: For Petal.Length, the median (4.35) is much higher than Q1 (1.60). This large gap hints at a bimodal distribution — confirmed in the next step.


11.6 Step 3 — Histograms


Code
p01 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Length, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Sepal.Length", xlab = "Sepal.Length", ylab = "")
p02 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Width,  geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Sepal.Width",  xlab = "Sepal.Width",  ylab = "")
p03 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Length, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Petal.Length", xlab = "Petal.Length", ylab = "")
p04 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Width,  geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Petal.Width",  xlab = "Petal.Width",  ylab = "")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)


What to look for — Iris histograms:








Variable
What you see
What it means




Sepal.Length
Roughly bell-shaped
Normal-ish spread across species


Sepal.Width
Roughly normal
Similar across species


Petal.Length
Bimodal (two humps!)
Two distinct groups exist in the data


Petal.Width
Bimodal (two humps!)
Same reason — setosa is distinct









Important⭐ Bimodal = Two Groups — A Red Flag in EDA



Professor’s note: “This is a red flag. I’m seeing two different distributions within my dataset. There’s something going on here. Already my exploratory data analysis is giving me tingly feelings that something different is within this dataset.”
Professor’s note (Week 3 recap): “A bimodal distribution in a single column is a really… it’s a red flag. It’s either saying data was not recorded correctly, or you have two different types of data, or two different data distributions within a single dataset.”
What bimodal means: If your data has two peaks (two humps), it strongly suggests there are two distinct sub-populations mixed together. For the iris dataset, that sub-population is the setosa species, which has much smaller petals than the other two species.
Analogy: If you measured the height of everyone in Ireland — men, women, and children all mixed together — you’d see a bimodal or trimodal distribution. That’s not one group; it’s three overlapping groups.
Why it matters for ML: It tells you the data is naturally separable. That’s extremely useful for a classification problem.




11.7 Step 4 — Density Plots


Code
p01 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Length, geom = "density",
             main = "Density plot on Sepal.Length", xlab = "Sepal.Length", ylab = "")
p02 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Width,  geom = "density",
             main = "Density plot on Sepal.Width",  xlab = "Sepal.Width",  ylab = "")
p03 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Length, geom = "density",
             main = "Density plot on Petal.Length", xlab = "Petal.Length", ylab = "")
p04 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Width,  geom = "density",
             main = "Density plot on Petal.Width",  xlab = "Petal.Width",  ylab = "")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)


Density plots confirm the same findings as histograms. The smooth curves make the bimodal shape of Petal.Length and Petal.Width even clearer — you can see the dip between the two peaks more easily.


11.8 Step 5 — Box Plots (by Species)
Splitting by species is powerful — it reveals how the groups differ.


Code
p01 <- qplot(Species, Sepal.Length, data = iris, geom = "boxplot",
             main = "Boxplot on Sepal.Length")
p02 <- qplot(Species, Sepal.Width,  data = iris, geom = "boxplot",
             main = "Boxplot on Sepal.Width")
p03 <- qplot(Species, Petal.Length, data = iris, geom = "boxplot",
             main = "Boxplot on Petal.Length")
p04 <- qplot(Species, Petal.Width,  data = iris, geom = "boxplot",
             main = "Boxplot on Petal.Width")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)


Key findings from box plots by species:








Variable
Observation
Implication




Petal.Length
Setosa box has zero overlap with versicolor and virginica
Petal length alone can perfectly separate setosa


Petal.Width
Same pattern — setosa is clearly distinct
Very useful feature for classification


Sepal.Length
Some overlap between species, but still broadly separated
Useful but not perfect


Sepal.Width
Surprisingly, setosa has wider sepals — but all species overlap
Less useful for separating species









Important⭐ What “No Overlap” Means for Classification



Professor’s note: “If you can draw a horizontal line that does not cross either box plot, then you can basically make a rule that perfectly separates those two classes.”
When box plots for two groups do not overlap at all, a simple rule can separate them nearly perfectly:


Code
# Test the rule: if Petal.Length < 2, it's setosa
table(iris$Species)                         # 50 of each

iris_temp <- iris[iris$Petal.Length < 2, ]
table(iris_temp$Species)                    # ALL 50 are setosa!


This is a 100% accurate rule — discovered purely through exploratory data analysis. No algorithm needed.
Forward link to Week 3: This exact Petal.Length < 2 rule becomes the first split in your decision tree. A decision tree is, in essence, a sequence of these “is there no overlap?” rules chained together automatically.
Forward link to Week 7: When you run k-means clustering on Iris, the algorithm independently discovers this same setosa group — confirming that the EDA insight was real.




11.9 Step 6 — Scatter Plots (Multivariate Exploration)
Scatter plots show the relationship between two variables simultaneously. Colouring by species shows how well the groups separate:


Code
p01 <- qplot(Sepal.Width,  Sepal.Length, data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Sepal.Width v Sepal.Length")
p02 <- qplot(Petal.Length, Petal.Width,  data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Petal.Length v Petal.Width")
p03 <- qplot(Petal.Length, Sepal.Length, data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Petal.Length v Sepal.Length")
p04 <- qplot(Sepal.Width,  Petal.Width,  data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Sepal.Width v Petal.Width")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)


Key insight: The Petal.Length vs Petal.Width plot shows the clearest separation between all three species. This tells you these two features will be the most powerful predictors for a classification model.






Note💡 Visual Pattern Recognition in Scatter Plots



Professor’s note (Week 7): “When you can do a vertical or horizontal line in a scatter plot, that means you can clearly differentiate them on a single variable using an if statement. If, when you do a scatter plot, you can draw a sloped line that differentiates the records completely, that indicates clearly that you can use linear regression to differentiate the two categories.”
Summary of scatter-plot separability patterns:








What you can draw
What ML model will work
Example




Horizontal or vertical line
Simple if statement / decision tree
Setosa separation on Petal.Length


Sloped straight line
Linear regression / logistic regression
Versicolor vs Virginica (partially)


Curved boundary
Tree-based methods, SVM with kernel
More complex real-world datasets


No clear boundary
More features or more complex models
—







11.10 Step 7 — Correlation Analysis
Correlation measures the linear relationship between two numeric variables. It ranges from −1 to +1.



Correlation value
Meaning




Close to +1
Strong positive: as X goes up, Y goes up


Close to 0
No linear relationship


Close to −1
Strong negative: as X goes up, Y goes down





Code
corr_mat <- cor(iris[, -5])    # compute correlation matrix (exclude Species)
corr_mat                        # show the numbers
corrplot.mixed(corr_mat)        # visualise with circles and numbers


Correlation values in the Iris dataset:



Pair
Correlation
Strength




Petal.Length ↔︎ Petal.Width
0.96
Very strong positive


Sepal.Length ↔︎ Petal.Length
0.87
Strong positive


Sepal.Length ↔︎ Petal.Width
0.82
Strong positive


Sepal.Width ↔︎ Petal.Length
−0.43
Moderate negative


Sepal.Length ↔︎ Sepal.Width
−0.12
Very weak / negligible









Warning⚠️ Correlation ≠ Causation



A correlation of 0.96 between Petal.Length and Petal.Width does not mean one causes the other. Both are driven by the underlying species and overall flower size. Always interpret correlations in the context of your domain knowledge.
Correlation tells you what varies together. It says nothing about why.




11.11 Step 8 — Pairs Plot (Everything at Once)
The pairs.panels() function from the psych package combines all of the above into a single view:

Diagonal: histogram + density curve for each variable
Below diagonal: scatter plots for every pair of variables
Above diagonal: correlation coefficients (the numbers)



Code
pairs.panels(iris[, -5],
             method   = "pearson",     # Pearson correlation
             hist.col = "#00AFBB",     # histogram fill colour
             density  = TRUE,          # show density curve on histogram
             ellipses = FALSE)         # no confidence ellipses


This is one of the most efficient exploratory tools available — a full picture of all pairwise relationships in a single plot. Use it early in any data exploration.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 12 The Titanic Dataset — Binary Classification Example
text:
12 The Titanic Dataset — Binary Classification Example
The question: Can machine learning predict which passengers survived the Titanic shipwreck?
This is a binary classification problem: the output is 0 (did not survive) or 1 (survived).
Professor’s note: “The Titanic dataset is more complicated than the Iris dataset. There are more columns, but we also have decisions to make about how we’re going to manage the various columns.”






Note💡 Binary Classification Encoding Convention



Professor’s note: “The default [convention] is usually that the more interesting or the most unusual class is the one that gets the value of 1, and the normal case gets the value of 0. So:

Fraud detection: 0 = non-fraud (common), 1 = fraud (unusual case we care about)
Medical: 0 = healthy (common), 1 = sick (unusual case we care about)
Titanic: 0 = did not survive (majority), 1 = survived (outcome of interest)”




12.1 Loading the Dataset


Code
data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)   # 891 observations, 12 variables




12.2 Variable Dictionary with ML Decisions









Variable
Definition
Values / Notes
ML Decision




PassengerId
Unique row ID
1–891 (every row unique)
Drop — unique IDs have zero predictive value


Survived
Did they survive?
0 = No, 1 = Yes
Keep — this is the target variable; convert to factor


Pclass
Ticket class
1 = 1st, 2 = 2nd, 3 = 3rd
Keep — very important; convert to factor


Name
Full passenger name
891 unique values
Drop raw — but extract title (Mr/Mrs/Dr/Miss/Master)


Sex
Gender
male / female
Keep — women were much more likely to survive


Age
Age in years
89 unique values; 177 missing (NA)
Impute (fill in) — use mean from training data


SibSp
# siblings/spouses aboard
0–8
Investigate — family size may matter


Parch
# parents/children aboard
0–6
Investigate — family size may matter


Ticket
Ticket number
681 unique (not 891)
Drop raw — but some share tickets (family groups)


Fare
Price paid
248 unique values
Keep — proxy for wealth/class


Cabin
Cabin number
687 blanks (77% missing!)
Extract deck letter (A, B, C…) as a new feature


Embarked
Port of boarding
C=Cherbourg, Q=Queenstown, S=Southampton; 2 blank
Keep — easy to fill 2 missing values; convert to factor





12.3 Checking Unique Values and Missing Data


Code
# How many unique values does each column have?
# If unique count = total rows, that column is a unique ID → drop it
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# How many blank / NA values does each column have?
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)


Professor’s note: “If you have a column that has a unique value for every single row inside it — drop it. It has to be dropped, because there is nothing informative in that column that a machine learning algorithm can use to differentiate the data.”


12.4 Titanic Survival Statistics — Cross-Tabulation
The professor demonstrated live in class. You can reproduce this:


Code
# Survival rate by gender
table(titanic_train$Survived, titanic_train$Sex)
# Result: males ~20% survival rate; females ~75% survival rate

# Survival rate by ticket class
table(titanic_train$Survived, titanic_train$Pclass)
# Result: 1st class >50% survived; 3rd class <20% survived








Important⭐ Titanic Cross-Tabulation Results — Know These Numbers






Group
Survived
Did Not
Survival Rate




Male
109
468
~19%


Female
233
81
~74%


1st class
>50%
<50%
>50%


2nd class
~47%
~53%
~47%


3rd class
119
~372
~24%



Professor’s note: “You can imagine if you are third class and an adult male, your chances of survival were very, very, very low.”
These numbers are the result of simple cross-tabulation — no machine learning algorithm was needed to find this insight. It is a perfect example of why data exploration comes before modelling.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
section: 13 Feature Engineering — Transforming Raw Data for ML
text:
13 Feature Engineering — Transforming Raw Data for ML
Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into features (columns) that are more useful for machine learning. It happens after data exploration and before model training.






Important⭐ Exam Tip — Feature Engineering > Algorithm Choice



Professor’s note: “Feature engineering is more important than your choice of algorithm. If you’re given a realistic dataset, feature engineering and picking an underperforming algorithm will outperform just blindly applying random forests or gradient-boosted machines to the raw data.”
This is a direct, quotable professor statement. Know it. Be able to justify it.



13.1 Common Feature Engineering Tasks







Task
Example from Titanic




Remove irrelevant columns
Drop PassengerId, raw Name


Fix column types
Convert Pclass from integer → factor


Handle missing values
Impute Age with mean; decide what to do with Cabin


Extract information from text
Pull title (“Mr”, “Mrs”, “Dr”, “Master”) out of Name


Create new binary features
Create is_child (TRUE if Age < 18) from Age


Log-transform skewed columns
Apply log() to right-skewed numeric variables


Scale numeric variables
Normalise so all variables are on a similar scale









Note💡 Why Scaling Matters



If you have Age (values 0–80) and Fare (values 0–500) in the same model without scaling, some algorithms will treat Fare as more important simply because its numbers are bigger. Scaling puts all variables on the same scale — for example, 0 to 1, or mean 0 with standard deviation 1.
Professor’s note (Week 7): “Because these things [iris measurements] are measuring the same part of the flower and they are all in the same unit of measurement, it doesn’t really matter whether they’re inches or centimetres — [for Iris] it doesn’t matter. But in a real dataset where you have Age (years) and Fare (dollars), scaling absolutely matters.”




13.2 How Your Week 2 Titanic Observations Become Week 5 Features






Note🔮 Forward Link — Your EDA Findings Turn Into Features



This is one of the most important concepts of the course. Every observation you made about the Titanic dataset in Week 2 becomes a concrete feature engineering task in later weeks:







Week 2 Observation
Week 5/6 Feature Engineering




“Age has 177 missing values (NAs)”
Impute missing ages using the mean from the training set (not the full dataset!)


“Cabin has 687 blanks (77% missing)”
Extract the first letter as a “deck” feature (A, B, C…); the rest treat as missing


“Name has 891 unique values”
Extract the title (Mr / Mrs / Miss / Master / Dr) — this reveals gender + age hints


“Mr Miss Master Dr are titles”
Discovery: “Master” is used for young boys — helps predict age for boys with missing Age


“Age becomes significant mainly for children”
Create is_child (Age < 18) as a binary feature — it captures the real signal


“SibSp and Parch are family sizes”
Create family_size = SibSp + Parch + 1 or is_alone (family_size == 1)



Better age imputation (Week 5): Rather than just using one mean for all missing ages, compute a mean age by ticket class AND title:

1st class Miss: average age ~29.7
1st class Mr: average age ~42.4
1st class Mrs: average age ~40.4
1st class Master: average age ~5.3 ← children!

Then plug in the appropriate mean for each missing record. This gives a much better estimate than a blanket “average age = 29.7” imputation.








Warning⚠️ Always Verify Data Encoding



Professor’s note: “I’m told survival is either 0 or 1. I certainly should be checking that. I’m told ticket class is 1, 2, or 3 — I should be checking that. Is the spread of age reasonable? Do we have any person with a negative age, or age over 120?”
Never trust the data dictionary alone. Always verify using table(), unique(), and summary().

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
15 Exercise/Exam Scenarios Table
This table maps common exercise and exam scenarios to the relevant Week 2 concept:








Scenario you might be given
Concept to apply
Answer pattern




“Mean > Median in a dataset”
Skewness direction
Right (positive) skew


“Mean < Median in a dataset”
Skewness direction
Left (negative) skew


“Histogram shows two humps”
Bimodal distribution
Two sub-populations; consider splitting the data


“Column has unique value for every row”
Unique ID column
Drop it — no predictive value


“Column has >70% missing values”
Heavy missing data
Either drop column, extract partial info (e.g., deck letter from Cabin), or treat missingness itself as a feature


“Column has <5% missing values”
Light missing data
Impute — use median (for skewed) or mean (for normal)


“Model has 99%+ accuracy but catches no fraud”
Imbalanced dataset
Use human-in-the-loop; report precision and recall, not just accuracy


“Predicting churn — should I use cancellation date?”
Data leakage
No — it’s known only after the event you’re predicting


“R² improves from 0.80 to 0.90 after transformation”
Relative error reduction
Error dropped from 0.20 to 0.10 — that’s a 50% error reduction


“Scatter plot shows a clear horizontal gap”
Perfectly separable groups
Simple if-statement or decision tree will work


“Algorithm treats class 3 as ‘worse’ than class 1”
Wrong data type
Convert integer → factor


“Income data is very skewed”
Right skew
Report median instead of mean; consider log transform

text:
16 Pre-Topic Checklist for Week 3
Before moving on to Decision Trees, make sure you can:

Load all required packages in R without errors

Write and run the drawPlots function from memory

Apply log() to a skewed column and verify the result looks more normal
Use cor() and corrplot.mixed() to produce and interpret a correlation matrix
Use pairs.panels() for a comprehensive multivariate view

Describe all 12 Titanic variables and state what you would do with each one
Run table() for cross-tabulation and interpret the output
Explain what a factor is, and convert a column to factor with as.factor()
Explain the if (Petal.Length < 2) → setosa rule and why it matters
Interpret a plot (don’t just produce one) — always ask “what decision does this lead to?”

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
17 Practice Questions






Important⭐ Practice Questions with Full Answers




Q1: If the mean of a dataset is much larger than the median, what type of skew does the distribution have? How can you tell from a plot?
Answer: Right (positive) skew. A few very large values pull the mean upward but leave the median (middle value) relatively unchanged. On a histogram or density plot, the data clusters on the left with a long tail extending to the right. On a drawPlots output, the blue line (mean) will be to the right of the green line (median).

Q2: You are building a churn prediction model. A colleague suggests using “date account was closed” as a predictor. What is the problem?
Answer: This is data leakage. The account closing date only exists after the churn event. At the time you would want to make a prediction (i.e., before the customer leaves), this value is not yet known. Including it gives artificially high model performance in testing, but the model would fail completely in production because the feature would be unavailable.

Q3: The Iris dataset shows a bimodal distribution in Petal.Length. What causes this, and why does it matter for ML?
Answer: There are two distinct groups (sub-populations) in the data. The setosa species has much shorter petals (around 1–2cm) than versicolor and virginica (3–7cm). On a histogram or density plot, this appears as two separate humps. For ML, this is valuable: it suggests the data is naturally separable, and petal length will be a very powerful classification feature. A simple rule (if Petal.Length < 2, predict setosa) is already 100% accurate. This rule becomes the root split of a decision tree in Week 3.

Q4: A mammal body weight model gives R² = 0.87 on raw data and R² = 0.92 after log-transforming both variables. How much did the error rate improve?
Answer: 37.79% reduction in error rate. Raw error = 1 − 0.87 = 0.13. Transformed error = 1 − 0.92 = 0.08. Improvement = (0.13 − 0.08) / 0.13 = 0.05 / 0.13 ≈ 38% (stated as 37.79% in the slides). The algorithm did not change — only the data preparation did. This demonstrates why feature engineering matters more than algorithm choice.

Q5: Why is PassengerId dropped from the Titanic ML model?
Answer: It has 891 unique values for 891 rows — every row has a different value. A column that is unique for every row contains no pattern that a machine learning algorithm can learn from. It cannot help predict survival. In general, any column that functions as a primary key or unique identifier should be removed before model training.

Q6: A fraud detection model predicts every transaction as “not fraudulent” and achieves 99.999% accuracy. Is this a good model? What should you do instead?
Answer: No — this is useless. Because fraud is extremely rare, predicting “not fraudulent” for everything will be technically accurate but catches zero actual fraud cases. This is the imbalanced dataset problem. A better approach is to use the model as a filter: flag the top 1–2% of most suspicious transactions for human review. This is called human-in-the-loop machine learning. The model is not the final arbiter — it is a prioritisation tool.

Q7: What is the difference between a character and a factor in R? Why does it matter?
Answer: A character is a free-text string — any value is valid. A factor is a categorical variable with a fixed set of allowed values (called “levels”). For example, Species in the iris dataset is a factor with levels {setosa, versicolor, virginica} — R knows no other value is valid. It matters for ML because some algorithms require categorical variables to be stored as factors, not strings. If a column like Pclass (1, 2, 3) is stored as integer, an algorithm may treat it as a continuous number; as a factor, it is treated as a category.

Q8: The Age column in the Titanic dataset has 177 missing values (NA). What are your three options, and which is usually best?
Answer: Three options:

Drop the column entirely. Not good here — Age is an important predictor (children were prioritised for lifeboats).
Drop the rows with missing Age. Not good — you lose 177 records (20% of data), and your production model can’t handle new records with missing Age.
Impute — fill in the missing values. This is usually best. You can use a blanket mean (simple), or better, a grouped mean (e.g., mean age by ticket class and title — giving much better estimates).

Important: The mean must be calculated on the training set only, then applied to both train and test sets. Using the full dataset’s mean to impute is data leakage.

Q9: You discover a scatter plot where you can draw a sloped line that perfectly separates two classes. What ML model would you try first, and why?
Answer: Linear regression (for a numeric target) or logistic regression (for a binary classification target). A sloped line is a linear decision boundary — that’s exactly what these models produce. If you could draw a horizontal/vertical line (axis-aligned), a simple if statement or shallow decision tree would suffice. If the boundary is curved, you need more flexible models (trees, SVMs, neural networks).

Q10: What does the professor mean when he says “if you’re just producing plots and jumping straight into ML, you’re wasting your time”?
Answer: Exploratory plots are only valuable if you interpret them — draw conclusions, form hypotheses, and let them guide your feature engineering. Examples from class: the setosa bimodal distribution → create an is_setosa rule; the Age distribution → identify missing values; the Cabin blanks → extract deck letters. A plot without interpretation teaches you nothing. A plot with interpretation can change your entire modelling approach.

title: Week 2: Basic Statistics, Visualisations in R, and Data Exploration
text:
19 Concept Relationship Map
The key ideas from Week 2 connect like this:
CRISP-DM Process
│
├── Business Understanding
│     └── PayPal Case: define the right problem first
│
├── Data Understanding + Data Preparation (most of your time)
│     │
│     ├── Summary Statistics
│     │     ├── Mean vs Median → sensitive vs robust to outliers
│     │     └── Quartiles / IQR → used in box plots
│     │
│     ├── Univariate Plots (INTERPRET, don't just produce)
│     │     ├── Histogram → shape, frequency, bimodal detection
│     │     ├── Density plot → smooth shape comparison
│     │     └── Box plot → median, IQR, outliers (statisticians only!)
│     │
│     ├── Skewness
│     │     ├── Right skew: Mean > Median → tail right
│     │     ├── Left skew: Mean < Median → tail left
│     │     └── Fix: log transform (simple) or Box-Cox (accurate)
│     │
│     ├── Multivariate Analysis
│     │     ├── Scatter plots → relationships between two variables
│     │     │     (horizontal line = if-statement; sloped = linear; curved = tree)
│     │     ├── Correlation matrix (cor()) → strength of linear relationships
│     │     └── pairs.panels() → everything in one view
│     │
│     └── Feature Engineering (informed by EDA)
│           ├── Drop: unique ID columns, irrelevant text
│           ├── Convert: integer→factor for categorical variables
│           ├── Impute: fill in missing values (Age in Titanic, by title + class)
│           ├── Transform: log() for skewed variables
│           ├── Extract: title from Name, deck letter from Cabin
│           └── Create: binary features like is_child, is_alone
│
└── Modelling (~10-15% of project time)
      │
      └── Forward to later weeks:
            ├── Week 3: Decision trees use the if-statement rules you found
            ├── Week 5/6: Titanic features you engineered become model inputs
            ├── Week 6: Data leakage — transformations on training data only
            └── Week 7: Clustering confirms the groups you saw in EDA

20 Annotation Space

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
Important⭐ Assessment Reminders




The professor left an exam-style thinking question at the end of this class (see the Thought Question section). Be ready to answer it in Week 4.
Next week covers machine learning metrics beyond accuracy (precision, recall, F1, ROC) and logistic regression — Week 3 is the foundation.
Homework: “Review the lab code in detail — understand every single line.” The professor emphasised this explicitly.
Slide 33 (the “minimum amount of code to run a classification task”) is the template that will reappear for every algorithm this semester — memorise its structure.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 3.1 The question we are trying to answer
text:
3.1 The question we are trying to answer

Can we use machine learning to predict which passengers survived the Titanic shipwreck, based only on information known about them before the ship sank?

This is a binary classification problem — for every passenger, the model outputs one of two classes: 1 (survived) or 0 (did not survive).

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:

Variable
Meaning
Type
Missing?
Professor’s first-pass verdict




PassengerId

Survived

int → factor
0

int → factor
0

Name
Passenger name

0
Drop — unique per row


Sex

0

Keep, but needs imputation

# siblings / spouses aboard

# parents / children aboard

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
3.3 Loading the data
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

if (!require("titanic"))
  devtools::install_github("paulhendricks/titanic")
library("titanic")

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)     # structure of the data frame






Important⭐ Exam tip — always profile a dataset before modelling



Run these two checks on every dataset, before you plot or model anything:
# 1. Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# 2. Count blanks (NA or empty string) per column
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)
Look for:

Columns where unique count == nrow → IDs, drop them
Columns with a high blank count → impute or drop
Columns where the unique count is one more than expected (e.g. Embarked shows 4 when it should be 3) → hidden blank entries

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.1 Why plot counts AND proportions?
This is one of the most emphasised ideas of the class. It appears again in Week 4 and is likely exam material.









What it tells you
Without it, you risk…




Count plot
How many records fall into each group — the effect size
Being fooled by strong signals in tiny groups


Proportion plot
How different the group’s survival rate is from the overall 38% baseline — the signal strength
Missing a real pattern because the group is small




Professor’s note: “If I had a ticket class four where the signal was very strong — 90/10 — but it only applied to 10 or 12 records, the signal is strong but the effect is small because it only applies to a small number of records.”







Warning⚠️ Common mistake



Reporting a proportion without reporting the count. A 90% survival rate in a group of 8 passengers is statistically meaningless.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.2 Family size vs. survival
A derived variable family = SibSp + Parch was created:
titanic_train$family   <- titanic_train$SibSp + titanic_train$Parch
titanic_train$Survived <- factor(titanic_train$Survived)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(x = family, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(stat = 'count', position = 'dodge') +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = min(titanic_train$family):max(titanic_train$family)) +
  labs(x = 'Family Size', title = 'Family size vs Survived')
Finding: Solo travellers had the worst survival rate. Families of 1–3 extra members had the best. Very large families (6+) had too few records to draw conclusions from.
Interpretation: Family travel correlates with children being present, and children were prioritised for lifeboats.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.3 Ticket class vs. survival



Class
Approximate survival rate




1st (Pclass = 1)
~63%


2nd (Pclass = 2)
~47%


3rd (Pclass = 3)
~24%



Baseline: 38% of all passengers survived.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.4 Gender vs. survival



Sex
Survival rate




Female
~74%


Male
~19%




Professor’s note: “Gender is even more strongly predictive than ticket class. I fully expect that whatever machine learning model I use, gender will be its most important feature.”

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.5 Fixing Embarked
Two passengers have blank Embarked. Because only two records are involved and Southampton is by far the dominant port, the blanks are assigned S:
table(titanic_train$Embarked)
#   C   Q   S
# 2 168  77 644       # the "2" is the blank category

titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'






Warning⚠️ Why not create a new “Unknown” category?



With only 2 records, the algorithm might memorise them — if both survived, the model would learn “blank Embarked ⇒ survive.” This is a hallucinated pattern — 2 records cannot support a real signal. The safe choice is to merge them into the majority class (the mode).
If there were 20+ missing records, treating them as their own category ("Unknown") would be more defensible.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.6 Fare vs. survival
A box plot of Fare by Survived shows that survivors paid somewhat higher fares — but this is almost entirely a proxy for Pclass. Higher class = higher fare.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 4.7 Age vs. survival — why the raw variable hides the signal
text:
4.7 Age vs. survival — why the raw variable hides the signal
A plain box plot of Age vs. Survived shows almost no difference in the median age of survivors vs. non-survivors. The signal is not “older = less likely to survive” — it is “under ~13 = more likely to survive.”
Fix: create a derived binary variable is_child:
temp <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]
temp$is_child <- ifelse(temp$Age < 17, 1, 0)






Important⭐ Key concept — derived variables



If a numeric variable has a threshold effect (the relationship changes sharply at some point), the raw value may hide the signal. Transform it:

Age → is_child (binary)
Income → quartiles or log-transformed income
Date → day-of-week, month-of-year

This helps the algorithm “see” a pattern that would otherwise be diluted across a continuous range.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.8 Investigating relationships between predictors
The Cherbourg passengers had an unusually high survival rate. Professor’s theory: maybe more first-class passengers boarded there. To test this, plot Embarked against Pclass (target variable ignored):
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Embarked, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  ggtitle("Ticket class by Embarkation")
Result (confirmed): About 50% of Cherbourg passengers were first class. Almost 100% of Queenstown passengers were third class.

Professor’s note: “Whether you survived the Titanic or not should have nothing to do with where you got on the boat. Embarked is a proxy for Pclass. I would strongly consider removing Embarked from my model entirely.”







Important⭐ Exam tip — proxy variables



A variable that appears predictive may actually be a proxy for another variable that is already in the model. Cross-check pairs of predictors, not just predictor-vs-target relationships. This is an example of collinearity.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
4.9 Pairs plot — correlation between predictors
columns <- c("Pclass", "Age", "Fare", "family")
pairs.panels(titanic_train[, columns], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)
Strongest correlation: Pclass vs. Fare = −0.55 (higher class = lower number = higher fare).

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
6.1 Definitions







Term
Meaning




Supervised learning
Training a model on labelled historical data so it can predict labels for new data.


Classification
Predicting a category (e.g. survived / not survived).


Binary classification
Two possible outcomes.
