Multi-class classification
3+ outcomes (e.g. iris species).


Regression
Predicting a continuous number (next week’s topic).

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
6.2 Popular classification algorithms



Algorithm
Strength




Decision Tree
Interpretable, good for exploration


Logistic Regression
Probabilistic, simple baseline (Week 4)


Naive Bayes
Fast, works well with text


Support Vector Machines (SVM)
Strong in high dimensions


Random Forest
Ensemble of trees, handles interactions


Gradient Boosted Trees (GBM)
Often best on tabular data


Deep Learning
Best for images, text, audio

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
6.3 The classification workflow

Collect a historical dataset where the outcome is already known.
Apply a machine learning algorithm.
Produce a model.
In the future, feed new data (predictors only) to the model and get a prediction.







Important⭐ Data leakage — critical exam concept



All predictor variables must be available at the moment the prediction is made. A field that only becomes known after the outcome has happened cannot be a predictor — that is data leakage.
Example from the lecture: using “credit card cancelled?” to predict “is this transaction fraudulent?” The cancellation typically happens because of the fraud, so including it leaks the answer.
Data leakage produces models that look amazing on paper and fail disastrously in production.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
7.1 Why set.seed?
set.seed(1987) fixes the random number generator. Without it, sample() returns different rows every time the script runs, so the train/test split changes and the accuracy changes.
With it, anyone running the script on any machine gets identical results — this is the principle of reproducible data science.

Professor’s note: “If scientific papers report statistically significant results but other people cannot reproduce their analysis, then there is a question mark over the paper itself.”

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 7.2 Train / test split — the absolute rules
text:
7.2 Train / test split — the absolute rules






Important⭐ The non-negotiable rules of a train/test split




No row appears in both sets.
Every row appears in exactly one set.
The model is fitted only on the train set.
Evaluation metrics are computed only on the test set.
Any transformation (imputation, scaling) is calculated from the train set and then applied to both sets.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
7.3 Optional: stratified sampling
When classes are imbalanced, a random split may put too few of the minority class into the test set. Stratified sampling fixes this by splitting within each class.
In iris (50/50/50) and Titanic (~38% survived) it is not critical. In something like image classification with 1,000 classes where some classes have only 10 images, it is essential.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
7.4 Overfitting vs. generalisation







Term
What it means




Generalisation
The model performs well on data it has never seen.


Overfitting / overtraining
The model has memorised the training data — including its noise — instead of learning the true pattern.


Symptom
Very high accuracy on train, much lower on test.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
7.5 Model drift and data drift








Term
Meaning
Mitigation




Data drift
The distribution of the input data changes over time (e.g. new customers look different from old ones).
Retrain periodically on fresh data.


Model drift
The relationship between inputs and the target changes over time.
Retrain periodically on fresh data.




Professor’s example: “Facial recognition systems trained on under-representative datasets performed poorly in production for women and minorities. That is data drift — the data distribution in production was significantly different from the training distribution.”

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
8.1 What they are
A decision tree is a flowchart of yes/no questions. Each internal node asks a question about one variable, and the record moves to the left branch if the answer is true and to the right branch if false. The leaf nodes give the final predicted class.






Note💡 Analogy — 20 Questions



A decision tree is like the game 20 Questions. Each question (“Is it an animal?”, “Does it fly?”) narrows down the possibilities. A good player asks the most informative question first — exactly what a decision tree algorithm does.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
8.2 How the tree is built
Start with all training data at the ROOT.
Repeat:
    1. Find the best column and best split condition.
    2. Split the data into two branches.
    3. For each branch: if all samples have the same class, make it a LEAF.
       Otherwise, repeat step 1 on that branch.
Stop when:
    - All leaves are pure, OR
    - No useful column remains to split on, OR
    - A heuristic stopping rule fires (e.g. too few rows, too little gain).

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 8.3 Choosing the best split — entropy, Gini, information gain
text:
8.3 Choosing the best split — entropy, Gini, information gain
At each node, the algorithm asks: “Which column-and-condition produces the purest child nodes?” It scores every candidate split using an impurity measure.
Entropy — measures disorder (0 = pure, 1 = perfectly mixed in a binary problem):
\[\text{Entropy}(t) \;=\; -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i \mid t) \log_2 p(i \mid t)\]
Gini index — same idea, slightly different formula:
\[\text{Gini}(t) \;=\; 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} \big[ p(i \mid t) \big]^2\]
Where c = number of classes and p(i|t) = probability that a record at node t belongs to class i.
Information gain = impurity of the parent − weighted average impurity of the children. The split with the highest information gain is chosen.






Note💡 Intuition — entropy at a glance






Node composition
Entropy
Purity




100% class A, 0% class B
0
Perfect


80% class A, 20% class B
0.72
Fairly pure


50% class A, 50% class B
1.0
Maximum impurity

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
8.4 Handling numeric variables
Decision trees only understand yes/no questions, so a numeric variable like Age is split on a threshold the algorithm chooses automatically, e.g. Age >= 13.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
8.5 Pruning
A deep tree will eventually memorise the training set — classic overfitting. Pruning cuts off some lower branches to force the tree to generalise.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
8.6 Advantages and disadvantages







Advantages
Disadvantages




Highly interpretable — can be drawn and explained
Usually beaten by random forests / GBMs in accuracy


Can be written as IF-THEN rules for any language
Prone to overfitting — needs pruning


Handles both categorical and numeric data
Handles missing data poorly


No need to scale or normalise variables
Greedy — poor at capturing variable interactions


Great as an exploratory tool
Sensitive to small changes in the training data









Important⭐ The “greedy algorithm” limitation



A decision tree picks the single best column at each node. Once it splits on Sex = male, the algorithm can never again consider combinations like “female AND 3rd class AND paid high fare” as one decision — each branch is handled in isolation.
This is why Random Forest (many trees, some trained on column subsets) and GBM (sequential trees correcting each other) outperform single decision trees on most real problems.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 8.7 Why use decision trees in real life?
text:
8.7 Why use decision trees in real life?

Professor’s note: “I always consider decision trees and logistic regression at the start of a project — not as the final model, but as a form of data exploration. If the tree shows me something surprising or a red flag, I go back and fix my data.”

Decision trees are chosen over more accurate models when explainability is legally or ethically required:

Credit scoring / loan approval (you must be able to tell a rejected applicant why)
Medical decision support
Anywhere a regulator or court could demand a justification

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
9.1 Setup
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(GGally)
library(titanic)

data(titanic_train)
set.seed(42)                     # note: lab uses 42, not 1987

nobs  <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)    # 801 rows
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train) # 90 rows

target <- "Survived"

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
section: 9.2 What happens if you leave Ticket in?
text:
9.2 What happens if you leave Ticket in?
The professor deliberately leaves Ticket in to show how decision trees fail spectacularly when given high-cardinality columns.
colsToIgnore <- c("Name")     # intentionally does NOT drop Ticket, Cabin, PassengerId
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_bad <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                   data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                   method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_bad)    # tree is a mess of ticket-number splits

# Predicting fails with:
#   Error: factor Ticket has new levels 110564, 111369, 111427, ...
pr <- predict(rpart_bad, newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)], type = "class")






Warning⚠️ Common mistake — two failure modes at once




Memorisation: the tree splits on individual ticket numbers — pure overfitting.
Unseen-category crash: when the test set contains a ticket number the model has never seen, predict() throws an error: “factor Ticket has new levels …”

This is why high-cardinality ID-like columns (Ticket, Cabin, Name, PassengerId) must be dropped before fitting.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
9.3 The correct setup
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Ticket", "Cabin")
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_model <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                     data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                     method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_model, main = "Decision Tree titanic_train $ Survived")

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
9.4 Reading the tree
The top splits match exactly what the exploratory analysis predicted:

Sex = male? — the most informative first question. Most males did not survive.
Down the male branch: Age ≥ 13? Younger boys had better odds. Further split on SibSp ≥ 3.
Down the female branch: Pclass ≥ 3? Third-class women had a mixed outcome; 1st- and 2nd-class women almost all survived.
Further splits on Fare inside the 3rd-class-female branch.


Professor’s note: “I don’t see any red flags. There’s nothing in this graph that is a surprise given the data exploration I already did. That is exactly what I want.”

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
9.5 Predicting and evaluating
pr <- predict(rpart_model,
              newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)],
              type = "class")

tab <- table(pr, titanic_train[test, ]$Survived,
             dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
print(tab)
#            Actual
# Predicted   0   1
#         0  50  19
#         1   4  17

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
sprintf("Accuracy = %.1f%%", accuracy)
# "Accuracy = 74.4%"

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
10.1 The accuracy formula
\[\text{Accuracy} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{17 + 50}{17 + 50 + 4 + 19} \;=\; \frac{67}{90} \;\approx\; 74\%\]

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
10.2 Computing TN/FP/FN/TP in R
testData <- titanic_train[test, ]
testData$pr <- pr

TN <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 0)
FP <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 1)
FN <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 0)
TP <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 1)

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
10.3 Class-wise performance
Look at the confusion matrix by row and column:




Did NOT survive (Actual = 0)
DID survive (Actual = 1)




Total in test set
54
36


Correctly classified
50 (93%)
17 (47%)



The model is much better at spotting non-survivors than survivors. Overall accuracy of 74% hides this imbalance.






Important⭐ Exam-style thought question — carry this into Week 4




Professor’s question (paraphrased): “Suppose someone builds a model to predict whether a patient has cancer, and reports 90% overall accuracy. Is this a good model? Would you put it in production?”

Hint — think about the base rate. If only 1 in 100 people in the screening population actually has cancer, a model that always says “no cancer” will be 99% accurate and miss every single sick person. Accuracy alone is dangerous when classes are imbalanced.
Week 4 introduces precision, recall, and F1 — the metrics that expose this problem.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
11.1 Mean imputation — done correctly






Important⭐ Exam-critical detail



The mean must be calculated on the training set only, then applied to both train and test sets.
Calculating the mean on the full dataset (train + test combined) is a form of data leakage — information from the test set has bled into the training process.


# Correct: mean is computed from TRAIN only (na.rm = TRUE ignores NAs)
meanAge <- mean(titanic_train[train, ]$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
meanAge
# 29.725 (approximately)

# Apply to the WHOLE dataset — so both train and test rows have Age filled in
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge
After imputation, the Titanic decision tree’s accuracy improves from 74.4% to 75.5% — a small but real gain, and far more important for algorithms like logistic regression that refuse to run with NAs.

title: Week 3: Classification & Decision Trees
text:
11.2 Mode imputation for categorical columns
For the 2 blank Embarked values, the same principle applies — use the mode (most common category) of the training set:
titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
Code
Code

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text: 📝 Note for review: This document is a summary of the Week 7 lecture. You can add your own annotations, extra examples, or questions in the spaces marked with <!-- ADD NOTES HERE -->.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
3.1 Why Communication Matters
One of the most important skills for a data scientist is the ability to communicate results clearly to people who are not data scientists. The best model in the world has no value if nobody understands it or acts on it.
Key guidelines:

Focus on business value, not the machine learning technique. Nobody in business wants to hear about logistic regression – they want to know how many customers you will keep or how much money will be saved.
Know your audience. A process engineer understands box plots and statistics. A marketing analyst may not.
Many data science projects do not need machine learning. Creating good dashboards and visualisations is often more valuable.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 3.2 Three Ways to Describe the Same Model
text:
3.2 Three Ways to Describe the Same Model
Here is an example of three ways to explain the same churn prediction model:








Summary
Audience
Quality




“Logistic regression with regularisation, recall 80%, precision 66%”
Data scientist peers
Too technical for business


“Identifies 80% of customers who will not return in 30 days; wrong 33% of the time”
Marketing team
Much better – explains the outcome


“Campaign will break even at 10% coupon redemption; at 20% redemption, incremental spend = €11,648, 1,500 extra customers retained”
Business decision makers
Best – connects model to money



Key idea: The third summary uses a mathematical model in Excel (not a machine learning model) to combine the data scientist’s metrics (recall, precision) with the marketing team’s assumptions (coupon cost, redemption rate). This allows both sides to be held accountable for their assumptions.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.1 What Is Clustering?
Clustering (also called segmentation) is an unsupervised machine learning task. This means there is no target variable – we are not trying to predict anything. Instead, we are trying to find groups of data that are similar to each other.
Practical example: A supermarket might cluster its customers into groups like “young families”, “students”, and “pensioners”, each of which has different shopping habits. This allows the business to target each group differently.

⚠️ Important: Clusters will almost never be the same size. Expect one large cluster (the “average” customer) and several smaller, more interesting ones.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 4.2 Why We Need to Standardise Data
text:
4.2 Why We Need to Standardise Data
Before clustering, we must standardise all numeric columns so they are on the same scale (0 to 1). Without this, columns with larger numbers (like square metres) will dominate over columns with smaller numbers (like number of bedrooms), even if the smaller column is more meaningful.

4.2.1 Standardisation Formula
\[x_{standardised} = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}\]
This converts any numeric value to a number between 0 and 1.
In R:
standardise <- function(x) {
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}

iris$Sepal.Length <- standardise(iris$Sepal.Length)
iris$Sepal.Width  <- standardise(iris$Sepal.Width)
iris$Petal.Length <- standardise(iris$Petal.Length)
iris$Petal.Width  <- standardise(iris$Petal.Width)
After standardisation, the minimum of every column will be 0 and the maximum will be 1.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.3 K-Means Clustering

4.3.1 How It Works

You choose k (the number of clusters) before running the algorithm.
The algorithm randomly places k centre points (called centroids).
Each data point is assigned to the nearest centroid.
Each centroid is moved to the average position of all points assigned to it.
Steps 3 and 4 repeat until the assignments stop changing.

The algorithm is not guaranteed to find the perfect solution, so it is usually run multiple times (e.g. nstart = 10) and the best result is kept.
K-means tries to minimise the within-cluster variation – it wants the points inside each cluster to be as close together as possible.


4.3.2 R Code: K-Means on the Iris Dataset
# Run k-means with 3 clusters, 10 random starts
k_clusters <- kmeans(iris[, 1:4], centers = 3, nstart = 10)

# View results
k_clusters
The output shows: - The cluster means (centre of each cluster for each variable) - Which cluster each data point belongs to - The within-cluster sum of squares (lower = tighter clusters)


4.3.3 How to Choose k: The Elbow Method
If you do not know the number of clusters in advance, use the elbow method:

Run k-means for k = 1, 2, 3, …, 10.
Plot the total within-cluster sum of squares for each k.
Find the point where the line “bends” like an elbow – that is your best k.

tot.withinss <- c()

for (k in 1:10) {
  k_clusters <- kmeans(iris[, 1:4], k, nstart = 10)
  tot.withinss <- c(tot.withinss, k_clusters$tot.withinss)
}

plot(main = "Selecting k: Elbow Method", tot.withinss, type = "l")

The curve drops steeply at first, then flattens out. The “elbow” (where the flattening begins) indicates the best number of clusters. For the iris dataset, the elbow is at k = 3.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.4 Hierarchical Clustering

4.4.1 How It Works
Hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach:

Every data point starts as its own cluster (150 points = 150 clusters).
The two closest clusters are merged into one.
Step 2 repeats until all points are in a single cluster.

The result is shown as a dendrogram (a tree diagram). You can then “cut” the tree at any height to get the number of clusters you want.
Advantage over k-means: You do not need to choose k in advance.


4.4.2 Reading a Dendrogram

Draw a horizontal line across the tree at any height.
Count how many vertical lines it crosses – that is the number of clusters.
The vertical distance between merges tells you how different the clusters are. Larger gaps = more distinct clusters.



4.4.3 R Code: Hierarchical Clustering on the Iris Dataset
# Calculate distances between points, then cluster
h_clusters <- hclust(dist(iris[, 1:4]))

# Cut the tree to get 3 clusters
clusterId <- cutree(h_clusters, 3)

# Add the cluster ID back to the data
iris$clusterId <- as.factor(clusterId)

# Plot the dendrogram
plot(h_clusters)

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.5 Interpreting Clusters
After clustering, a common question is: what do the clusters mean?
The answer is to use the same exploratory data analysis techniques from earlier weeks – but instead of colouring plots by a target variable, colour them by cluster ID.
library(ggplot2)

# Scatter plots coloured by cluster
p1 <- qplot(Sepal.Width, Sepal.Length, data = iris,
            colour = clusterId, main = "Sepal.Width vs Sepal.Length")

p2 <- qplot(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, data = iris,
            colour = clusterId, main = "Petal.Length vs Petal.Width")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1, p2)
Look for patterns in the plots: - Do certain clusters have higher values on certain variables? - Can you give a meaningful name to each cluster? (e.g. “Large flowers”, “Small flowers”)

4.5.1 Comparing Clusters to a Known Variable
Even though clustering does not use the Species column, we can check how well the clusters match it afterwards:
table(iris$clusterId, iris$Species)
A good match between clusters and the known species confirms the clustering is working well.

🔑 Key reminder: Clustering does not aim to create equal-sized groups. In real retail data, you might find 80% of customers in one large “average” cluster, and small but very interesting clusters of students, pensioners, or high-spending customers.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.1 What Are They?
Recommendation engines are systems that suggest products to customers based on what other customers bought. Examples:

“People who bought this also bought…”
“These items are commonly purchased together”

The most common algorithm for this is the Apriori algorithm, which finds patterns of items bought together in transactions (called market basket analysis).

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.2 Three Key Metrics

5.2.1 1. Support
How common is this item or item set?
\[\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing } X}{\text{Total number of transactions}}\]
Support is used to filter out rare items. If an item only appears in 0.01% of transactions, any rule involving it will not be useful in practice.


5.2.2 2. Confidence
If a customer buys X, how likely are they to also buy Y?
\[\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \text{ and } Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}\]
This measures the strength of the rule “if X then Y”.


5.2.3 3. Lift
Is the relationship between X and Y stronger than what we would expect by chance?
\[\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \text{ and } Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)}\]



Lift value
Meaning




> 1
Buying X makes buying Y more likely than normal


= 1
X and Y are bought independently of each other


< 1
Buying X makes buying Y less likely than normal




🔑 Lift is the most important metric. Select rules with the highest lift. A lift of 3.0 means a customer is 3× more likely to buy Y when they have X in their basket.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 5.3 R Code: Apriori on the Groceries Dataset
text:
5.3 R Code: Apriori on the Groceries Dataset
library(arules)
library(arulesViz)

# Load the built-in grocery dataset
data("Groceries")
summary(Groceries)

# Look at the first 3 transactions
inspect(head(Groceries, 3))

# See the most frequently bought items
freq <- itemFrequency(Groceries, type = "absolute")
freq <- sort(freq, decreasing = TRUE)
head(freq)

# Plot item frequencies for top 20 items
itemFrequencyPlot(Groceries, topN = 20)

5.3.1 Running the Apriori Algorithm
# Items must appear in at least 1% of transactions
# Rules must have at least 30% confidence
support    <- 0.01
confidence <- 0.30
parameters <- list(support = support, confidence = confidence, target = "rules")

rules <- apriori(Groceries, parameter = parameters)

# View rules as a data frame
dfRules <- data.frame(item_set = labels(rules), quality = rules@quality)
head(dfRules)


5.3.2 Plotting the Rules
# Scatter plot of all rules
plot(rules)

# Network graph of only the strongest rules (lift >= 3)
rulesLift <- subset(rules, lift >= 3.0)
plot(rulesLift, method = "graph")

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.4 Verifying Calculations Manually
You can verify what the algorithm is doing by calculating the metrics by hand:
# Convert sparse matrix to full data frame
dfTrans <- data.frame(as(Groceries, "matrix"))

# Calculate support, confidence, and lift for beef => root vegetables
support    <- sum(dfTrans$beef & dfTrans$root.vegetables) / nrow(dfTrans)
confidence <- sum(dfTrans$beef & dfTrans$root.vegetables) / sum(dfTrans$beef)
lift       <- support / ((sum(dfTrans$beef) / nrow(dfTrans)) *
                         (sum(dfTrans$root.vegetables) / nrow(dfTrans)))

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.5 Applying Recommendation Rules in Practice
Things to consider when using recommendations in a real business:

Transactional vs. member-based: You can group items by transaction ID (what is in one basket right now) or by member ID (what a person has bought over their whole history). Member-based rules can capture purchases that happen months apart (e.g. a cyclist buys a bike, then later a helmet, then cycling shoes).
Use segmentation + recommendations together: Run the recommendation engine separately on each customer segment. This gives much more targeted and relevant suggestions.
Substitute vs. complement products:

A substitute is a similar product (e.g. a different brand of TV). Goal: upsell (buy a better version).
A complement is a product bought alongside the main product (e.g. a TV cable). Goal: cross-sell (buy something extra).

Avoid bombarding customers. If someone just bought a TV, showing them more TV ads for 6 months is annoying and ineffective.
Ethics matter. A famous example: Target (US supermarket) identified pregnant women from their shopping habits and sent them baby product offers. One teenager’s father received the offers before he knew about the pregnancy. Always consider how customers will feel about personalised targeting.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.1 What Is a Hyperparameter?
In statistics, a parameter usually means a variable in your data (e.g. age, income).
In machine learning, a hyperparameter is a setting of the algorithm itself – something you choose before training, not learned from the data.
Examples:







Algorithm
Hyperparameter examples




Decision Tree
Splitting criterion (Gini, entropy)


Support Vector Machine
Kernel function (linear, RBF)


Neural Network
Number of layers, nodes per layer, learning rate


Random Forest
Number of trees (ntree), features per tree (mtry)



The process of finding the best set of hyperparameters is called hyperparameter optimisation.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.2 Methods for Hyperparameter Optimisation

6.2.1 Grid Search
Try every possible combination of hyperparameters. If you have 4 hyperparameters with 2, 5, 3, and 2 possible values each:
\[2 \times 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 60 \text{ experiments}\]
Grid search is exhaustive but very slow for large numbers of hyperparameters.


6.2.2 Random Search
Select combinations at random. Useful when you cannot afford to run all combinations. Less thorough than grid search, but much faster.


6.2.3 Bayesian Search
Starts like random search, then focuses on the most promising area based on the results seen so far. Think of it like the hot/cold game – it “gets warmer” and homes in on the best region.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 6.3 R Code: Manual Hyperparameter Search (Random Forest)
text:
6.3 R Code: Manual Hyperparameter Search (Random Forest)
library(randomForest)

# Define hyperparameter values to try
trees <- c(50, 200, 500)
mtrys <- c(4, 6)

best_accuracy <- 0

for (v1 in trees) {
  for (v2 in mtrys) {
    rf_model <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ .,
                             data  = train_data,
                             ntree = v1,
                             mtry  = v2)

    pr  <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data, type = "class")
    pr  <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))
    tab <- table(pr, actuals)
    accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)

    if (accuracy > best_accuracy) {
      best_tree     <- v1
      best_mtry     <- v2
      best_accuracy <- accuracy
    }
  }
}

print(paste0("Best accuracy = ", round(best_accuracy, 2),
             ", ntree = ", best_tree,
             ", mtry = ", best_mtry))

This runs 6 experiments (3 tree values × 2 mtry values). In practice, use the caret or tidymodels packages to do this automatically and more rigorously.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.4 Important Warnings

6.4.1 You Need THREE Data Splits for Hyperparameter Optimisation
When optimising hyperparameters, do not keep evaluating on the same test set – you are effectively peeking at the answers. Use:

Training set – train the model
Validation set – compare hyperparameter combinations
Test set – used only once at the very end to report final accuracy

A typical split might be 70% / 15% / 15%.


6.4.2 Hyperparameter Optimisation Is NOT the First Step

⚠️ Feature engineering and data quality matter much more than hyperparameter tuning. Most algorithms give reasonable results with default settings. Only optimise hyperparameters once your data preparation is solid.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.5 AutoML
Some tools (e.g. h2o.ai, AWS SageMaker) automate the whole process: selecting the algorithm AND tuning the hyperparameters. This is called AutoML.

Useful for extracting the last small improvement in performance.
Can run for days on large datasets using multiple cloud machines.
The improvement is usually small (often less than 1% accuracy difference between models).
Should be the last step in your workflow, not the first.

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
In this lab we learn a new machine learning method called Logistic Regression.
Think of it like this:

Imagine you are trying to guess if it will rain today. You look at clues — dark clouds? windy? cold? You combine all the clues and come up with a score between 0 and 1. If the score is above 0.5, you say “yes, it will rain.” If it is below 0.5, you say “no, it will not rain.”
Logistic regression does exactly this — it looks at clues (columns in the data) and gives a probability (a number from 0 to 1) of something happening. Here, the “something” is: did this Titanic passenger survive?

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
8.1 Get raw probabilities
pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
pr
type = "response" tells R to give us probabilities (numbers between 0 and 1), not raw scores.

🧒 Each number is like saying: “I am 73% sure this person survived” or “I am 12% sure this person survived.”

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
section: 8.2 Convert probabilities to yes/no using a threshold
text:
8.2 Convert probabilities to yes/no using a threshold
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)
ifelse() says: if the probability is above 0.5, say 1 (survived). Otherwise, say 0 (did not survive).

🧒 It is like saying: “If I am more than 50% sure it will rain, I will bring an umbrella. Otherwise I won’t.”

The 0.5 here is called the threshold — we will change it later and see what happens.

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
9.1 Confusion matrix and accuracy
actuals <- factor(test_data$Survived, levels = c(1, 0))
pr      <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))

tab1 <- table(pr, actuals)
tab1

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab1) / sum(tab1))
accuracy
We wrap the values in factor(..., levels = c(1, 0)) to tell R that 1 (survived) is the positive class — the thing we most care about predicting correctly.






Note



Confusion matrix reminder:









Actual: Survived (1)
Actual: Died (0)




Predicted: Survived (1)
✅ True Positive (TP)
❌ False Positive (FP)


Predicted: Died (0)
❌ False Negative (FN)
✅ True Negative (TN)




Accuracy = how often we were right overall
But accuracy alone can be misleading! (More on this below)

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
10.1 Precision-Recall curve
pr_c <- pr_curve(test_data, Survived, predicted)
autoplot(pr_c)






Note



Precision vs Recall — what is the difference?
These two measures tell you about different types of mistakes.

🧒 Imagine a doctor who tests patients for a disease:

Precision = “Of all the patients I said had the disease, how many actually had it?” (How accurate are my positive predictions?)
Recall = “Of all the patients who actually had the disease, how many did I catch?” (Did I miss anyone sick?)


In formulas:
\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]
\[
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\]
There is always a trade-off between the two — which brings us to the next section.

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
11.1 What is the threshold?
pr <- ifelse(pr >= threshold, 1, 0)
The threshold is the cut-off point. If the model says the probability is above the threshold, we predict “survived”. Below it, we predict “died”.

🧒 Imagine you are sorting packages at a post office. If a package feels heavy enough, you mark it “fragile — handle with care.” The weight you choose as “heavy enough” is the threshold. Change that weight and different packages get marked.

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
11.2 Threshold = 0.5 (default)
threshold <- 0.5
Balanced — you need to be at least 50% sure before predicting survival.

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
11.3 Threshold = 0.1 (very low)
threshold <- 0.1

🧒 You only need to be 10% sure someone survived to predict they did. This means you will predict “survived” for almost everyone.

✅ High recall — you will catch nearly all real survivors
❌ Low precision — you will also wrongly predict survival for many people who did not survive

title: Lab 4a: Logistic Regression in R
text:
11.4 Threshold = 0.9 (very high)
threshold <- 0.9

🧒 You need to be 90% sure before predicting survival. This means you will only say “survived” for passengers you are very confident about.

✅ High precision — when you say “survived”, you are almost always right
❌ Low recall — you will miss many actual survivors








Important



The big trade-off:
Lowering the threshold → catches more survivors, but makes more false alarms
Raising the threshold → fewer false alarms, but misses more survivors
Which is better depends on the problem. In medical diagnosis, it is usually worse to miss a sick patient (low recall) than to over-diagnose (lower precision). In spam filters, it may be worse to block real emails (lower precision) than to let some spam through.

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
This lab introduces Decision Trees, a popular machine learning method for classification — that is, predicting which category something belongs to.
We work through two real datasets:

Part 1 — Iris: Predict the species of a flower based on its measurements.
Part 2 — Titanic: Predict whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster.

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
3.1 Loading packages and data
library(rpart)       # builds decision trees
library(rpart.plot)  # plots decision trees nicely
library(GGally)      # for ggpairs() — a pairs plot
library(titanic)     # contains the Titanic dataset
library(datasets)    # contains built-in R datasets like Iris

data(iris)
head(iris)






Note



What is the Iris dataset?
It contains measurements (in cm) of 150 flowers from 3 species: setosa, versicolor, and virginica.
The 4 measurements are: Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width.
Our goal: can we predict the species just from these numbers?

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
3.2 Exploring the data
ggpairs(iris, columns = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
        mapping = aes(colour = Species, alpha = 0.5))
str(iris)
summary(iris)
ggpairs() creates a pairs plot — every column plotted against every other column, coloured by species. This helps us see whether the groups separate visually before we build a model.

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
section: 3.3 Splitting into training and test sets
text:
3.3 Splitting into training and test sets
set.seed(1987)            # makes random results repeatable
nobs  <- nrow(iris)       # total number of rows (150)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)          # 90% → training rows
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)     # remaining 10% → test rows






Important



Why split the data?







Set
Purpose




Training set (90%)
The model learns patterns from this data


Test set (10%)
We check how well the model works on new, unseen data



If we tested on the same data we trained on, the model would look artificially perfect — like memorising exam answers instead of understanding the subject.

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
3.4 Defining target and input columns
target <- "Species"                        # what we want to predict
input  <- setdiff(names(iris), target)     # everything else → predictors
setdiff() returns everything in names(iris) that is not in target. So input will be the four measurement columns.

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
3.5 Building the decision tree
rpart <- rpart(Species ~ .,
               data   = iris[train, c(input, target)],
               method = "class")

print(rpart)
rpart.plot(rpart, main = "Decision Tree iris $ Species")
Reading the formula Species ~ .:
“Predict Species using (.) all other columns in the data.”
method = "class" tells rpart this is a classification problem (predicting a category), not a regression problem (predicting a number).

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
3.6 Making predictions and checking accuracy
pr <- predict(rpart,
              newdata = iris[test, c(input, target)],
              type    = "class")

# Confusion matrix
tab <- table(pr, iris[test,]$Species, dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
tab

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
accuracy






Note



What is a confusion matrix?
A table that compares what the model predicted vs what was actually true.




Actual: A
Actual: B




Predicted: A
✅ Correct
❌ Wrong


Predicted: B
❌ Wrong
✅ Correct



The diagonal (top-left to bottom-right) = correct predictions.


Accuracy formula:
\[
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of correct predictions}}{\text{Total predictions}} \times 100
\]
In R: 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
4.1 Loading the data
data(titanic_train)
head(titanic_train)
The dataset contains passenger information: name, age, sex, ticket class, fare paid, and whether they survived (1) or not (0).

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
4.2 Splitting data (same process as Part 1)
set.seed(42)
nobs  <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
section: 4.3 Why we must exclude some columns
text:
4.3 Why we must exclude some columns
# WRONG — includes all columns naively
target      <- "Survived"
colsToIgnore <- c("Name")
input        <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))






Warning



The problem with unique columns
Columns like PassengerId, Name, Ticket, and Cabin are unique per person — no two passengers share the same name or ID. If you include these, the tree will “memorise” each individual passenger instead of learning general patterns. It will look perfect on training data but fail completely on new data. This is called overfitting.
Fix — exclude all unique identifier columns:
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Ticket", "Cabin")
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
section: 4.4 Building and evaluating the Titanic tree
text:
4.4 Building and evaluating the Titanic tree
rpart <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
               data   = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
               method = "class")

rpart.plot(rpart, main = "Decision Tree titanic_train $ Survived")
print(rpart)

test_data <- titanic_train[test, c(input, target)]
pr        <- predict(rpart, newdata = test_data, type = "class")

tab <- table(pr, test_data$Survived, dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
tab

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
accuracy

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
section: 4.5 Understanding the confusion matrix in detail
text:
4.5 Understanding the confusion matrix in detail
TN <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 0)  # correctly predicted NOT survived
FP <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 1)  # predicted survived, but actually did NOT
TP <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 1)  # correctly predicted survived
FN <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 0)  # predicted NOT survived, but actually DID






Note



The four outcomes explained:








Term
Meaning
Example




True Negative (TN)
Predicted “did not survive” — correct ✅
Passenger died; model said died


True Positive (TP)
Predicted “survived” — correct ✅
Passenger survived; model said survived


False Positive (FP)
Predicted “survived” — wrong ❌
Passenger died; model said survived


False Negative (FN)
Predicted “did not survive” — wrong ❌
Passenger survived; model said died

title: Lab 3: Decision Trees in R
text:
4.6 Handling missing values — Imputation
The Age column has many missing values (NA). Missing values cause problems for decision trees. We handle this by imputation: replacing missing values with a sensible estimate.
# Calculate the mean age from TRAINING data only
meanAge <- mean(titanic_train[train,]$Age, na.rm = TRUE)

# Replace all NAs in the full dataset with that mean
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age),]$Age <- meanAge






Important



Why use only the training set to calculate the mean?
If you calculated the mean using the test set too, information from the test set would “leak” into your training process. This would make your model seem better than it really is. Always compute statistics (mean, standard deviation, etc.) from the training data only.


After imputation, rebuild and re-evaluate the tree:
rpart <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
               data   = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
               method = "class")

rpart.plot(rpart, main = "Decision Tree titanic_train $ Survived (with imputed Age)")

pr  <- predict(rpart, newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)], type = "class")
tab <- table(pr, test_data$Survived, dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
tab

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
accuracy

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
This is different from your other two guides:

First guide = reference book (what everything is)
Speaking coach = scripts to memorise (what to say)
This guide = a teacher explaining WHY and HOW things work (deep understanding)

Why do you need deep understanding? Because scripts can fail. If the professor asks a question in an unusual way, memorised scripts won’t fit. But if you truly understand the idea, you can explain it in any words, even your own.
Read this guide like a story. Every topic explains: 1. The problem we are trying to solve 2. The idea that solves it 3. How it works 4. Why it works 5. A simple example you can remember
Take your time. Read slowly. Stop and think after each section.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
The fundamental question
All of data mining tries to answer one question:

“What can I learn from this data?”

There are two main answers:

I want to PREDICT something. (Will this customer leave? Is this transaction fraud? Will this patient survive?) → This is Supervised Learning.
I want to UNDERSTAND or GROUP something. (What types of customers do I have? Which products are bought together?) → This is Unsupervised Learning.

That’s it. Everything else — decision trees, random forest, k-means, recommendation engines — is a tool that fits into one of these two categories.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: The family tree of what you learned
text:
The family tree of what you learned
DATA MINING
│
├── SUPERVISED LEARNING (we know the answer, we want to predict)
│   │
│   ├── Classification (predict a category: yes/no, fraud/not fraud)
│   │   ├── Decision Trees
│   │   ├── Logistic Regression
│   │   ├── Naive Bayes
│   │   ├── Support Vector Machines (SVM)
│   │   ├── Random Forest (ensemble of trees)
│   │   ├── Gradient Boosted Machines (GBM)
│   │   └── Neural Networks
│   │
│   └── Regression (predict a number: price, temperature)
│       ├── Linear Regression
│       └── Many of the above can also do regression
│
└── UNSUPERVISED LEARNING (no answer, find patterns)
    │
    ├── Clustering (group similar items)
    │   ├── k-means
    │   └── Hierarchical clustering
    │
    └── Association rules (items bought together)
        └── Apriori algorithm (for recommendation engines)
Take five minutes to memorise this tree. If you can draw it on paper from memory, you understand the big picture.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: The workflow that connects everything — CRISP-DM
text:
The workflow that connects everything — CRISP-DM
Every data mining project follows roughly the same 6 steps. Think of it like cooking a meal:

Business Understanding = what does the customer want to eat?
Data Understanding = what ingredients do I have in the kitchen?
Data Preparation = wash, cut, season the ingredients
Modeling = cook the meal (this is where ML algorithms come in)
Evaluation = taste the meal, is it good?
Deployment = serve the meal to the customer

The key insight: most of the work is before and after “cooking.” Modeling itself is fast. The hard parts are understanding what you need and preparing the data correctly.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Foundation 1: What “training” actually means
When we say a model is “trained,” what is really happening?
Imagine teaching a child to recognise cats. You show them 1000 pictures, saying “cat,” “cat,” “not cat,” “cat,” and so on. Eventually, the child learns the pattern of what makes a cat a cat — whiskers, pointy ears, tail. Now they can look at a new picture and say “cat” even though they have never seen that specific cat before.
That is exactly what a machine learning model does:

Training data = the 1000 labelled pictures
Training = the process of finding the pattern
Model = the learned pattern
Test data = new pictures to check if the pattern actually works
Prediction = the model saying “cat” or “not cat” for a new picture

Why do we split data into training and testing? Because if you only look at training accuracy, the model could just memorise all 1000 pictures without learning the pattern. The test set is new data the model has never seen — it’s the honest test.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Foundation 2: The trade-off between simple and complex models
text:
Foundation 2: The trade-off between simple and complex models
This idea is called the bias-variance trade-off and it is everywhere in machine learning.
Here’s the core tension:

A simple model might be too stupid to capture the real pattern. It fails on both training AND test data. This is called underfitting (high bias).
A complex model might be so smart that it memorises random noise in the training data. It’s perfect on training, but fails on test data. This is called overfitting (high variance).
The right model captures the real pattern but ignores random noise.

Think of it like studying for an exam:



Student
What they do
Training grade
Test grade




Lazy student
Doesn’t study
40%
40%


Memorising student
Memorises old exams word by word
100%
50%


Smart student
Understands the concepts
85%
82%



The memorising student has overfit. They will fail the real exam. The lazy student has underfit. They never had a chance. The smart student has generalised — which is what we want from a machine learning model.
How do you know if you have overfitting? Big gap between training score and test score. How do you know if you have underfitting? Both scores are bad.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Foundation 3: Why “accuracy” is not enough
This one is critical. Many ML questions trap you on this.
Imagine a disease test. Only 1% of people actually have the disease. You build a model, and you proudly announce: “My model is 99% accurate!”
But your model just says “healthy” for everyone. Because 99% of people are healthy, you get 99% accuracy. But you catch zero sick people. Useless.
This is the imbalanced data problem. When one class is rare, accuracy becomes meaningless.
The solution: use better metrics.








Metric
Question it answers
When it matters




Accuracy
Of everyone, how many did I classify correctly?
Only when classes are balanced


Precision
When I said “positive,” how often was I right?
When false positives are costly (spam filter)


Recall
When someone WAS positive, did I catch them?
When false negatives are costly (disease diagnosis)


F1
A balance of precision and recall
When you want both



The way to remember precision vs recall (your professor’s own tip):

Precision → looks at Predicted column
Recall → looks at Real (actual) column

This is the same letter trick. It just works.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Decision Trees — the simplest idea

The problem it solves
You have data and you want to make a decision by asking questions. Like a doctor: “Does the patient have a fever?” → “Are they coughing?” → “Have they travelled recently?” Each question narrows down the diagnosis.


The idea
A decision tree is a flowchart of yes/no questions. At each step, the algorithm picks the question that best separates the data into clean groups.


How it picks each question
It uses a measure called entropy (or Gini) to measure impurity. Pure means all items in a group belong to the same class. Impure means mixed.
Think of a bag of marbles. If all marbles are red → pure (entropy = 0). If marbles are 50% red and 50% blue → maximum impurity (entropy = 1).
At each split, the tree picks the feature that reduces impurity the most. The reduction is called information gain.


Why it’s great

Easy to understand. You can literally draw it and show it to a non-technical person.
Handles both numbers and categories without much preparation.
Can be used as a data exploration tool — it tells you which features matter.



Why it’s not great

It overfits easily. A deep tree memorises the training data.
Not the most accurate algorithm. Better ones exist.
Unstable. Small changes in the data can produce a very different tree.



When you use it

When you need to explain your model to non-technical people.
As a first simple model to compare against more complex ones.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Random Forest — the genius idea of voting
text:
Random Forest — the genius idea of voting

The problem it solves
One decision tree is weak. It overfits and is unstable. Can we combine many trees to get something more accurate and stable?


The idea — the “wisdom of crowds”
Your professor used this analogy: imagine a jar of marbles. If you ask one person how many marbles are in it, they might be off by 30%. But if you ask 500 people independently, and take the average, you will be within 1–2% of the real number.
Random Forest works exactly like this. It builds many decision trees (usually 500) and each one casts a vote. The final answer is the most popular vote.


How it creates diversity between trees
Here is the key insight: if all 500 trees look at the same data, they will all look the same. Same answer 500 times. Useless.
So Random Forest deliberately makes each tree different:

Bagging (row randomness): Each tree gets a random sample of rows, with replacement. Same row can be picked multiple times. About 63% of rows end up unique per tree.
Feature randomness: At each split, only a random subset of features is considered. This prevents one dominant feature (like Sex in Titanic) from always being at the top.
Shallow trees: Trees are often kept small, so no single tree tries to overfit.

Each tree therefore gets a slightly different view of the data. When you combine their votes, the noise cancels out and the true pattern remains.


Why it works

Accuracy: usually beats a single tree by a lot.
Stability: robust to small changes in data.
Less overfitting: averaging smooths out the individual trees’ mistakes.
Feature importance: you can see which features the trees use most.



Why you might not use it

Harder to interpret than a single tree.
Slower to train (but trees can run in parallel, unlike GBM).



The key contrast with a single decision tree

Decision tree = one expert. Random forest = a committee of 500 slightly different experts voting together.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Gradient Boosted Machines (GBM) — the idea of learning from mistakes
text:
Gradient Boosted Machines (GBM) — the idea of learning from mistakes

The problem it solves
Random Forest builds trees independently. But what if we could build each new tree to specifically fix the errors of the previous trees? Wouldn’t that be smarter?


The idea
Build trees one at a time, in sequence. Each new tree focuses on the rows that the previous trees got wrong.
Your professor’s analogy: imagine guessing marbles again. The first person guesses 500 marbles. They’re wrong. The second person is told “the previous guess was 200 too high” and adjusts accordingly. The third person adjusts for what’s still off. Each guess corrects the previous mistake.


How it differs from Random Forest








Feature
Random Forest
GBM




Trees built…
independently, in parallel
sequentially, one at a time


Each tree sees…
a random sample
the errors of previous trees


Speed
can use many CPU cores
cannot parallelise


Accuracy
very good
usually even better


Interpretability
decent (feature importance)
harder to interpret


Data needed
handles categorical data
needs numeric data (one-hot encoding)





Why it works
Because each tree focuses on the hard cases, GBM often achieves the highest accuracy of any tabular algorithm. It’s a favourite in Kaggle competitions.


Why it can be painful

You can’t parallelise, so training is slow.
It’s very powerful — which means it can overfit if not tuned carefully.
Harder to explain to a non-technical person.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Logistic Regression — the interpretable classifier

The problem it solves
We want to predict a yes/no outcome (survived/not survived), but we also want to know which features matter and how much.


The idea
Take the linear regression formula: \[y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + ...\]
And wrap it in a sigmoid function that squashes the output to a number between 0 and 1 — which we interpret as a probability.
If probability ≥ 0.5 → predict class 1. If probability < 0.5 → predict class 0.


Why the coefficients are gold
Because the formula is additive, each coefficient tells you how much that feature pushes the prediction up or down.
Titanic example: - Coefficient for Sex=male is very negative → being male strongly reduces the probability of survival. - Coefficient for Pclass is negative → higher class number (3rd class) reduces survival probability.
This is why logistic regression is still used a lot in business — you can explain the model. “Being male reduced survival chances by X amount.”


Why it can fail

Assumes a linear relationship. If the real pattern is curvy, it will underfit.
Cannot handle categorical variables directly — needs one-hot encoding (though R does this automatically).
Sensitive to outliers.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Support Vector Machines (SVM) — the idea of margins
text:
Support Vector Machines (SVM) — the idea of margins

The problem it solves
When two classes overlap or are not linearly separable, how do we find the best boundary?


The idea — two brilliant tricks
Trick 1: the maximum margin. Don’t just find any line that separates the classes. Find the line that is as far as possible from both groups. The “safety zone” around the line is called the margin, and we want it to be as wide as possible.
The data points that sit on the margin are called support vectors. These are the critical points — the algorithm ignores all the other points. This makes SVM robust to outliers.
Trick 2: the kernel trick. What if the data cannot be separated by a straight line at all? SVM secretly maps the data into higher dimensions where separation becomes possible.
Imagine two groups of points arranged in concentric circles. In 2D, no line can separate them. But lift them into 3D (by adding a third dimension) and suddenly a flat plane can slice between them. That’s what the kernel trick does — mathematically, without actually computing the higher dimensions.


Why it’s great

High accuracy with little tuning
Robust to outliers
Works well with medium-sized datasets



Why not always

Does not scale well to very large datasets
Harder to interpret than logistic regression
Have to choose a kernel (linear, polynomial, radial)

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Naive Bayes — the probabilistic classifier

The problem it solves
Can we use simple probability to classify things?


The idea — Bayes’ theorem in plain English
\[P(\text{class} \mid \text{features}) = \frac{P(\text{features} \mid \text{class}) \times P(\text{class})}{P(\text{features})}\]
In words: “Given these features, what’s the probability of each class?” We estimate this by flipping the question: “Within each class, how often do these features appear?”


Why it’s called “naive”
It assumes all features are independent given the class. This is almost never true in reality — e.g., “fever” and “cough” are correlated when someone has flu. But the algorithm pretends they are independent anyway.


Why it works anyway
Even though the independence assumption is unrealistic, Naive Bayes often performs surprisingly well, especially for: - Text classification (spam detection) - Small datasets where more complex models overfit - When you need something very fast to train

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Neural Networks — the complex idea (briefly)

The idea
Inspired loosely by the brain. Stack many layers of simple computational units (“neurons”). Each layer transforms the data a little. Deep layers → complex patterns.


Important context
Your professor said: neural networks are NOT typically used for tabular data. For tabular data (like Titanic), Random Forest, GBM, and SVM usually win.
Neural networks dominate in: - Images - Text (large language models) - Audio - Very large-scale problems


For the Q&A
You probably don’t need more than the 30-second script. If asked, say: “Neural networks are a family of algorithms inspired by the brain. They use layers of nodes that apply mathematical transformations to create non-linear decision boundaries. They work best on unstructured data like images and text, not typically tabular data.”

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: One-hot encoding — why we need it
text:
One-hot encoding — why we need it

The problem
Many algorithms (logistic regression, GBM, neural networks) require numeric input. But what if you have text data like “male/female” or embarkation point “C/Q/S”?


The solution
Turn each category into its own binary column.
Before: | id | gender | |—-|——–| | 1 | male | | 2 | female |
After one-hot encoding: | id | gender_male | gender_female | |—-|————-|—————| | 1 | 1 | 0 | | 2 | 0 | 1 |
Each row has exactly ONE “1” — hence “one-hot.” Some algorithms (decision trees) don’t need this, but most do.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Bagging — why sampling with replacement matters
text:
Bagging — why sampling with replacement matters
Bagging = Bootstrap AGGregatING.
Two parts: 1. Bootstrap = sampling WITH replacement 2. Aggregating = combining the results
Sampling with replacement means: after you pick a row, you put it back. So the same row can be picked more than once.
If you draw 100 rows from 100 rows with replacement, about 63% will be unique and the rest are duplicates. This gives each tree a slightly different view — which is exactly what Random Forest needs.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Cross-validation — the fair evaluation trick

The problem
If you split your data 90/10, your test accuracy could be lucky or unlucky depending on which 10% ended up in the test set.


The solution — k-fold cross-validation
Split the training data into k equal parts (usually 5).

Round 1: train on folds 1–4, test on fold 5
Round 2: train on folds 1–3 and 5, test on fold 4
Round 3: train on folds 1–2 and 4–5, test on fold 3
Round 4: train on folds 1, 3–5, test on fold 2
Round 5: train on folds 2–5, test on fold 1

Average the 5 test scores. This gives a reliable estimate of the model’s performance because every row was used for testing once.


Why it matters
Especially on small datasets (like Titanic, 891 rows), a single split can vary wildly — accuracy from 73% to 81% just by changing the random seed. Cross-validation smooths this out.
Important: you still keep a separate test set for the final unbiased evaluation. Cross-validation is done on the training data only.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Handling imbalanced data — when the classes are unequal
text:
Handling imbalanced data — when the classes are unequal

The problem
When only 1% of transactions are fraud, models default to predicting “not fraud” for everyone and get 99% accuracy. Useless.


The four solutions

Use better metrics — F1 score, precision, recall instead of accuracy.
Adjust the probability threshold — instead of 0.5, use 0.3. This predicts positive more often, catches more real positives.
Resample the data:

Upsample the minority class (duplicate minority rows)
Downsample the majority class (remove some majority rows)

SMOTE — Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique. Instead of duplicating minority rows, create new synthetic rows by interpolating between real minority examples. This avoids the overfitting risk of simple duplication.
Weight the loss function — tell the algorithm that a false negative costs more than a false positive. The algorithm then tries harder to avoid false negatives.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Handling missing data
Three options:

Drop rows with missing values (fine if only a few rows).
Drop the column (if most values are missing, like the Cabin field in Titanic).
Impute — fill in with the mean, median, or a smarter estimate. In Titanic, a clever trick was imputing Age by gender AND title, so children (Master, Miss) got younger imputed ages than adults (Mr, Mrs).

Critical rule: always calculate the imputation value on the training data only. Otherwise you leak test information into training (= data leakage).

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Data leakage — the accuracy killer

What it is
When your training data contains information that wouldn’t exist at prediction time in the real world.


The two main types
Target leakage — a predictor was recorded AFTER the target event, or is a direct consequence of it.
Classic example from class: the “Carpathia” field in Titanic. The Carpathia was the rescue ship that picked up survivors. But at the moment the Titanic sank, nobody knew which ship would pick them up! If you add this field, the model achieves impossibly high accuracy — but the feature could not exist at prediction time.
Another example: predicting fraud using a “card cancelled” flag. Cards are usually cancelled AFTER the fraud is detected. The flag is a consequence of fraud, not a cause. Model looks perfect in testing, fails in production.
Preparation leakage — you use the whole dataset to compute something (like a mean) before splitting into train/test. The test set’s information has “leaked” into training.


How to detect it

If one feature single-handedly gives near-perfect accuracy → be suspicious.
Ask: “Would this feature actually exist at the moment I need to predict?”
Ask: “Was this feature recorded before or after the target event?”



How to fix it

Remove the leaking feature
Always split the data FIRST, then compute imputation values on the training set only

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
text:
Clustering — grouping similar things

The core idea
You have customers, transactions, or any data with rows. You have no target variable. But you suspect there are groups of similar items hiding in the data. Clustering finds these groups.


Two very different approaches

K-means clustering
How it works:

You tell the algorithm how many clusters you want. Call this number k.
The algorithm places k random “centre points” in the data.
Each data point is assigned to the nearest centre.
Each centre moves to the average location of its assigned points.
Repeat steps 3 and 4 until nothing changes.

Think of it like this: imagine 3 ice cream trucks landing randomly in a park full of people. Each person walks to the nearest truck. Then each truck moves to the centre of its customers. Then people re-check which truck is now closest. After a few rounds, it stabilises.
Key points: - You must choose k in advance. This is the main weakness. - It is not guaranteed to find the optimal solution — local minima happen. So you usually run it multiple times (the nstart parameter in R) and keep the best run. - It tries to minimise within-cluster variance — meaning items in the same cluster should be close to each other.
How to choose k — the elbow method: Run k-means for k=1, 2, 3, … 10 and plot the total within-cluster variance for each. The plot looks like a bent arm. The “elbow” — where the line bends sharply — is the best k. More clusters after that point doesn’t help much.


Hierarchical clustering
How it works — the opposite approach: 1. Start with every data point in its own cluster (e.g. 150 clusters for 150 points). 2. Find the two closest clusters and merge them. 3. Repeat until everything is in one big cluster.
The result is a tree diagram called a dendrogram — a tree of merges from bottom (individual points) to top (one big cluster).
The advantage over k-means: You don’t need to choose k in advance! You build the whole tree, then decide where to cut it.
How to cut the tree: - Draw a horizontal line through the dendrogram. - The number of vertical lines it crosses = the number of clusters. - The vertical distance between merges tells you how different the clusters are. Big vertical gaps = well-separated clusters.



K-means vs Hierarchical — when to use each








Feature
K-means
Hierarchical




Direction
Top-down (given k, split the data)
Bottom-up (merge until done)


Need to specify k?
Yes, in advance
No — decide after seeing the tree


Speed on large data
Fast
Slow (computes all distances)


Visual output
Scatter plot with cluster colors
Dendrogram





Important warnings about clustering
Your professor stressed two things:

Don’t expect equal-sized clusters. In real data (like credit card transactions), 80–90% of records may go into one big “normal” cluster, and the interesting small clusters are the ones worth analysing.
Clustering is just the first step. You still need to explore the clusters afterwards — what makes cluster 1 different from cluster 2? Use the same exploratory data analysis tools (summary statistics, plots) to understand what the algorithm grouped together.

title: Understanding Guide: Weeks 1–7
section: Recommendation engines — “people who bought this also bought…”
text:
Recommendation engines — “people who bought this also bought…”

The core problem
You have thousands of shopping baskets (or customer purchase histories). You want to find rules like “when people buy bread, they often buy butter” so you can make recommendations.


The Apriori algorithm
It finds frequent itemsets — groups of items that often appear together — and turns them into rules. This is also called market basket analysis.


The three key metrics
For a rule like “Beef → Root Vegetables” (meaning: if they bought beef, they also bought root vegetables):

1. Support
\[\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{transactions containing X}}{\text{total transactions}}\]
In words: how often does this itemset appear overall? If beef appears in 5% of baskets, support is 0.05.
Why it matters: very rare itemsets produce rules that aren’t useful. You set a minimum support threshold to filter them out.


2. Confidence
\[\text{Confidence}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X, Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}\]
In words: of the people who bought X, how many also bought Y?
If 33% of people who bought beef also bought root vegetables, confidence is 0.33.


3. Lift — THE most important metric
\[\text{Lift}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X, Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)}\]
In words: are X and Y bought together more often than random chance would suggest?

Lift > 1 means X and Y are associated — buying X really does make buying Y more likely.
Lift = 1 means no association.
Lift < 1 means X and Y are negatively associated — if they bought X, they’re LESS likely to buy Y.

Why lift is the key metric: confidence alone is misleading. If 80% of everyone buys whole milk anyway, then “bread → milk” will have high confidence, but it’s trivial — people just buy milk regardless. Lift corrects for this by asking “is the association stronger than the base rate of Y?”



A concrete example from class
Rule: Beef → Root Vegetables - Support: 0.017 (about 1.7% of baskets contain both) - Confidence: 0.331 (33% of beef buyers also buy root vegetables) - Lift: 3.04 (they buy root vegetables together with beef about 3× more than random chance)
Lift of 3+ is a strong association. This is a rule worth using.


What makes a good recommendation rule?

High lift (the main signal)
Reasonable support (not too rare)
Consider substitutes vs complements — you don’t want to recommend “milk” to someone buying “almond milk” (substitute, no one needs both)

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text: 📝 Note to self: These notes are a summary of the live lecture transcript. Review alongside the slides posted on campus and the lab exercises. Paula mentioned she will upload exercises after the break — revisit and annotate those sections once available.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.1 Multiple Linear Regression
Multiple linear regression is an extension of simple linear regression. Instead of one explanatory (independent) variable \(X\), you now have multiple explanatory variables \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k\).
The model looks like this:
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon
\]
The word “linear” here does not mean the relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\) is a straight line — it means that the response variable \(Y\) is a linear function of the coefficients (\(\beta\) values). This is an important distinction.

2.1.1 Interpreting the Coefficients
Each coefficient \(\beta_j\) represents the expected change in the response \(Y\) for a one-unit increase in \(X_j\), while all other explanatory variables are held constant.

⚠️ Exam tip (Paula’s emphasis): When interpreting a coefficient in a multiple regression model, you must state that the other variables are held constant. This is key to getting full marks.

Example from class (savings dataset):

For a one-unit increase in population under 15, the savings rate decreases by 0.216, holding all other variables constant.
For a one-unit increase in percentage rate of change of disposable income (DDPI), the savings rate increases by 0.443, holding all other variables constant.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.2 Matrix Notation
The entire regression model can be written compactly using matrix notation:
\[
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}
\]
Where:








Symbol
Meaning
Dimensions




\(\mathbf{Y}\)
Vector of response values
\(n \times 1\)


\(\mathbf{X}\)
Design matrix (includes a column of 1s for the intercept)
\(n \times (k+1)\)


\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)
Vector of coefficients (intercept + slopes)
\((k+1) \times 1\)


\(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\)
Vector of error terms
\(n \times 1\)



The first column of \(\mathbf{X}\) is a column of 1s — this is a placeholder for the intercept \(\beta_0\).

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.3 Least Squares Estimation
The least squares method finds the \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) that minimises the error sum of squares (SSE) — i.e., it minimises the total squared distance between the observed values and the fitted (predicted) values.
\[
SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})^T(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})
\]
The solution (in matrix form) is:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}
\]
The fitted values are then:
\[
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}
\]
Where \(\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\) is known as the Hat Matrix (because it “puts the hat” on \(Y\)).
The residuals are:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}
\]
The estimate of the error variance (\(\sigma^2\)) is:
\[
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{SSE}{n - k - 1}
\]
Where \(n\) = number of observations and \(k\) = number of explanatory variables.

📌 Paula’s note: You don’t need to compute this by hand — just understand the theory behind it.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.4 Assumptions of Multiple Linear Regression
The assumptions are the same as in simple linear regression:

Linearity — \(Y\) is a linear function of the coefficients.
Independence — Error terms are independent of each other.
Normality — Errors are normally distributed: \(\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\).
Constant variance (homoscedasticity) — The variance of the errors is constant across all values of \(X\).
Explanatory variables are measured without error — No distributional assumptions are placed on the \(X\) variables themselves.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.5 R² and Adjusted R²
R² (R-squared / Coefficient of Determination): The proportion of variation in \(Y\) that is explained by the model. Always expressed as a percentage.
\[
R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST}
\]

\(R^2 = 0.2075\) means 20.75% of the variation in Y is explained by X.
Whatever is not explained is due to other unknown variables or random error.

Problem with R²: It always increases (or stays the same) when you add more variables to the model, even if those variables are useless. This can make a model look better than it is.
Adjusted R² penalises the model for adding extra variables that don’t genuinely improve fit:
\[
\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE/(n-k-1)}{SST/(n-1)}
\]

⚠️ The Adjusted R² will always be lower than R² in a multiple regression model. This is expected and not a problem.


📌 Also note: R² for the model with both explanatory variables (28.75%) is less than the sum of the two individual model R² values (20.75% + 9.29% = 30.04%). This is because the two variables share some overlapping explanatory power.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.6 The Global F-Test
Before looking at individual coefficients, the first step when interpreting a regression output is to check the overall model significance using the global F-test.
Hypotheses: \[H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_k = 0\] \[H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0\]

If the p-value is significant → at least one explanatory variable is associated with the response.
This test is called the global F-test (also: comparing the full model to the null model).


⚠️ Exam tip: Always check the overall model p-value first, before interpreting individual coefficients.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.7 Comparing Nested Models
Sometimes you want to compare a full model (all variables) with a reduced model (fewer variables).
This is done using an ANOVA F-test:
\[
F = \frac{(SSE_{\text{reduced}} - SSE_{\text{full}}) / (p - q)}{SSE_{\text{full}} / (n - p)}
\]
Where: - \(p\) = number of parameters in the full model - \(q\) = number of parameters in the reduced model - \(n\) = number of observations

⚠️ Important: The summary output in R tests only one variable at a time. If you want to drop more than one variable, you must use ANOVA to compare models directly.

Example from class:
Testing whether population over 75 and DPI could both be dropped: F-statistic = 1.723, p-value = 0.19 → fail to reject H₀ → the larger model does not fit significantly better, so those two variables can potentially be dropped.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.8 Collinearity (Multicollinearity)
Collinearity occurs when two or more explanatory variables are highly correlated with each other.
In the savings dataset example, population under 15 and population over 75 both measure age-related demographics and were negatively correlated with each other. Including both made one or both appear insignificant — even though each was significant on its own.
Effects of collinearity: - Imprecise (unstable) estimates of \(\hat{\beta}\) with large standard errors - F-tests and t-tests may fail to reveal significance - The sign or magnitude of a coefficient can flip when variables are added or removed
Detection — Variance Inflation Factor (VIF):
\[
VIF_j = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j}
\]
where \(R^2_j\) is the R² from regressing \(X_j\) on all other explanatory variables.

📌 Rule of thumb: If VIF > 5, there is evidence of serious collinearity.

Solutions: - Remove one of the correlated variables - Use Principal Component Analysis (PCA) followed by regression — this creates a new, uncorrelated set of variables - Use shrinkage methods (e.g., Ridge regression, LASSO)

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.9 Model Diagnostics
After fitting a regression model, you must check the assumptions using diagnostic plots. Paula emphasised that these will appear in the labs.

2.9.1 1. Residuals vs Fitted Values

Plots the residuals (\(y_i - \hat{y}_i\)) on the y-axis against the fitted values (\(\hat{y}_i\)) on the x-axis.
What you want: A random, constant scatter of points with no pattern.
What’s bad: A curved or funnel-shaped pattern — this suggests the relationship is non-linear or the variance is non-constant (heteroscedasticity).


💡 If a curved pattern appears, you may need to add a quadratic term (e.g., \(X^2\)) to the model.



2.9.2 2. QQ Plot (Quantile-Quantile Plot)

Compares the quantiles of your residuals to the quantiles of a theoretical normal distribution.
What you want: Residuals that fall close to the diagonal line.
What’s bad: Strong deviations from the diagonal suggest non-normality.


📌 QQ plots are preferred over histograms (especially for small samples), because histograms are sensitive to bin width choice. You can use both together.



2.9.3 3. Leverage
Leverage measures how much an observation’s value on the explanatory variable(s) differs from the mean of those variables.
\[
h_{ii} = \text{diagonal entry of Hat Matrix } \mathbf{H}
\]

An observation with \(X\) equal to the mean has zero leverage.
