High leverage → the observation is far from the mean of \(X\) → it has the potential to influence the regression slope greatly.


📌 Rule of thumb: Flag observations where leverage \(> 2(k+1)/n\).



2.9.4 4. Studentised Residuals
Standardised version of the residuals used to identify outliers.

📌 Rule of thumb: An observation may be an outlier if \(|r_i| > 2\).


Note: Standardised and studentised residuals are sometimes defined differently — double-check how R computes them.



2.9.5 5. Cook’s Distance
Cook’s Distance measures the overall influence of a single observation on all the fitted values. It is proportional to the sum of squared differences between predictions made with and without that observation.

If the predictions are the same with or without the observation → no influence.
If the predictions differ → influential observation.


📌 Rule of thumb: Flag observations with Cook’s Distance \(> 1\).



2.9.6 Combined interpretation



Standardised Residuals
Leverage
Influence




Low
Low
Small


High
High
Highly influential

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.10 Cross-Validation
A model may fit the sample data well but fail to generalise to the broader population. Cross-validation tests this.
K-fold cross-validation process:

Randomly partition the dataset into \(K\) equal-sized subsamples.
Fit the model on \(K - 1\) of the subsamples.
Validate (test) the model on the one subsample left out.
Repeat \(K\) times so every subsample is used as a validation set exactly once.
Average the performance metrics across all \(K\) runs to get a single estimate.


📌 Bootstrapping is another method for assessing generalisation — Paula mentioned it briefly.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.1 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
Descriptive statistics simply describe the data you already have. For example, you might calculate the mean exam score or draw a chart. You are not trying to say anything beyond what is in front of you.
Inferential statistics go further. They use methods such as hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and regression analysis to make conclusions about a large group (a population) based on a smaller group (a sample). These methods are grounded in probability theory — we use probability to say how confident we are in our conclusions.






Note💡 Analogy



Think of a soup chef who wants to know if the soup is salty enough. They do not drink the entire pot — they take one spoonful (the sample) and taste it (the statistic). Their judgment about the whole pot is an inference about the population.



Professor’s note: “Statistical inferences are based on a process of reasoning based on probability theory.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.2 Population vs. Sample








Term
Definition
Symbol




Population
All individuals under study — the full group you want to learn about
\(N\)


Sample
A smaller subset taken from the population
\(n\)


Parameter
A number that describes the population (usually unknown)
\(\mu\), \(\sigma\)


Statistic
A number calculated from the sample, used to estimate a parameter
\(\bar{x}\), \(s\)



In practice, the population is almost always too large to measure fully. We use sample statistics to estimate unknown population parameters.
For the estimate to be reliable, the sample must be collected in an unbiased way.

Professor’s note: “In general, the parameters of a population are unknown. We use statistics to estimate parameters.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.3 Probability Sampling
For a sample to be a fair representation of the population, we use probability sampling. This means:

Every member of the population has a known, non-zero chance of being selected.
Units are chosen at random.
All statistical inference methods assume that the sample was collected this way.

If the sample is not random, the estimates may be biased — they may systematically over- or under-estimate the true value.






Warning⚠️ Common Mistake



Students sometimes confuse “random” with “haphazard”. In statistics, random means that every unit has an equal and known probability of selection — it is a mathematical concept, not just picking things carelessly.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.4 Point Estimates and Sampling Error
From a random sample, we calculate a point estimate — a single number that is our best guess for an unknown population parameter.
For example:

The sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is a point estimate of the population mean \(\mu\).

However, because the sample is not the whole population, there will always be a gap between the estimate and the true value. This gap is called sampling error.
\[
\text{Sampling Error} = \hat{\theta} - \theta
\]
where \(\hat{\theta}\) is the estimate and \(\theta\) is the true parameter.

2.4.1 Ways to Reduce Sampling Error







Method
How It Helps




Increase sample size
Larger samples are more representative


Use random/stratified sampling
Reduces systematic bias


Calculate the Standard Error of the Mean
Quantifies how much error to expect


Use Confidence Intervals
Gives a range that accounts for uncertainty

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.5 Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)
The Standard Error of the Mean tells us how much the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is expected to vary from sample to sample. It is calculated as:
\[
\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
Where:

\(\sigma\) = population standard deviation
\(n\) = sample size







Important⭐ Key Formula



\[\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
As \(n\) increases, SEM gets smaller — the estimate becomes more precise. This formula appears in confidence intervals, sampling distributions, and power analysis.








Note💡 Analogy



Imagine measuring the height of people in a country. If you measure 5 people, your average could easily be wrong. If you measure 500 people, you are much more likely to be close to the true average. The SEM captures this idea mathematically.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.6 Sampling Distributions
If you took many different random samples from the same population and calculated the mean for each, those means would form their own distribution — the sampling distribution of the mean.

The sample mean is a random variable — it changes each time you take a new sample.
If the underlying population follows a Normal distribution with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\):

\[
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)
\]
This means the sampling distribution has:

The same mean as the population (\(\mu\))
A smaller standard deviation equal to the SEM (\(\sigma / \sqrt{n}\))

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.7 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)






Important⭐ One of the Most Important Results in Statistics



Central Limit Theorem (CLT): For large sample sizes (\(n \geq 30\)), the distribution of the sample mean is approximately Normal, regardless of the shape of the underlying population.
\[
\bar{X} \approx N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \quad \text{when } n \geq 30
\]
This is what allows us to use Normal-distribution methods (confidence intervals, z-tests) even when the original data is not normally distributed.



2.7.1 R Code: Demonstrating the CLT
The exercise sheet asks you to simulate rolling a die to see the CLT in action. As the sample size grows, the distribution of the mean becomes more and more bell-shaped.


Show R code
n_samples <- 10000   # Number of simulated samples
n <- c(1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 1000)  # Sample sizes to try

X <- matrix(0, n_samples, length(n))

par(mfrow = c(3, 2))  # Arrange plots in 3 rows, 2 columns

for (i in n) {
  j <- which(n == i)
  for (k in 1:n_samples) {
    X[k, j] <- mean(as.integer(runif(i, 1, 7)))
  }
  hist(X[, j],
       main = paste("Distribution of the mean of", i, "rolls"),
       freq = FALSE,
       xlim = c(0.5, 6.5))
}



What to observe: With n = 1, the histogram looks flat (uniform). As n increases toward 1000, the histogram becomes a narrow, bell-shaped curve centred at 3.5 (the true mean of a fair die). This is the CLT at work.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.8 Confidence Intervals
A confidence interval (CI) is a range of values that we are highly confident contains the true population parameter.

2.8.1 The Logic Behind a 95% CI
If we took many samples and built a 95% CI for each one, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true population mean \(\mu\).
For a single sample, we say: we are 95% confident that \(\mu\) lies within two standard errors of \(\bar{x}\):
\[
\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]






Important⭐ Why Confidence Intervals Matter




A larger sample produces a narrower CI (greater certainty).
CIs show the range of plausible effect sizes, not just whether a result is “significant”.
They help you distinguish between statistical significance and practical significance.


Professor’s note: “If you provide a confidence interval for the parameter you are estimating, this will estimate the size of an effect. The use of confidence intervals facilitates the distinction between statistical significance and clinical significance.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.9 Hypothesis Testing
Hypothesis testing is a formal way of using data (evidence) to answer research questions. It ensures that — in the long run — we reach the correct conclusion a certain percentage of the time.

2.9.1 Steps in Hypothesis Testing

State the hypotheses — null (\(H_0\)) and alternative (\(H_1\)).
Set the significance level (\(\alpha\), usually 0.05).
Collect data and compute a test statistic.
Calculate the p-value.
Make a decision — reject or fail to reject \(H_0\).
State your conclusion in terms of the population parameter, and always quote the p-value.


Professor’s note: “When you are doing any reports or anything, you should always quote your p-value in your conclusions. And remember, when you’re drawing your conclusions, if you’re doing hypothesis testing, your conclusions are about the population parameter that you’re studying.”




2.9.2 The Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\))
The null hypothesis is the starting point — a statement of “no effect” or “no difference”. We assume it is true until the data gives us enough evidence to doubt it.

Always stated with an equality sign: \(H_0: \mu = 0\)
The statement is made about the population parameter, not the sample statistic.


Professor’s note: “We assume this statement is true until the data indicates otherwise.”




2.9.3 The Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_1\))
The alternative hypothesis is your research theory — what you believe is actually true.

It contains all values not covered by the null hypothesis.
Together, \(H_0\) and \(H_1\) must cover all possible situations.









Type
Hypotheses
Called




Two-sided
\(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu \neq 0\)
Two-tailed test


One-sided (greater)
\(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu > 0\)
One-tailed test


One-sided (less)
\(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu < 0\)
One-tailed test




Professor’s note: “The alternative hypothesis is your research theory. The researcher actually believes this statement to be true.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.10 The p-value
The p-value is the result of the significance test. It tells you how likely the null hypothesis is to be true, given the data you observed.
More precisely: it is the probability of observing data at least as extreme as yours, if the null hypothesis were true.






Important⭐ The p-value — Most Important Concept in Hypothesis Testing




A small p-value → the null hypothesis is unlikely to be true → we have strong evidence against \(H_0\).
Always report the exact p-value in your conclusions.


🎵 Professor’s classroom rhyme (do not write in exams!): “If p is low, null must go.” That is: if \(p \leq \alpha\) (e.g., \(p \leq 0.05\)), reject \(H_0\).

The only two valid conclusions you can write are:

“We reject the null hypothesis.” (when \(p \leq \alpha\))
“We fail to reject the null hypothesis.” (when \(p > \alpha\))

Anything else is incorrect.

Professor’s note: “When I teach hypothesis testing, there’s only two statements that you’re allowed to use. Everything else is incorrect.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.11 Level of Significance (\(\alpha\))
The significance level \(\alpha\) is set before the test is run. It is the amount of error you are willing to accept in your conclusion.







\(\alpha\)
Meaning




0.05 (5%)
Most common — you accept a 5% chance of a Type I error


0.01 (1%)
More stringent — used when false positives are very costly


0.001 (0.1%)
Very stringent — used in fields like genetics



Decision rule:

If \(p \leq \alpha\): Reject \(H_0\) → result is statistically significant
If \(p > \alpha\): Fail to reject \(H_0\) → not sufficient evidence


Professor’s note: “This is the degree of uncertainty we are prepared to accept in a conclusion. It depends on your study.”







Warning⚠️ Common Mistakes




A significant result does not mean the result is certainly true — only that we have evidence.
A non-significant result does not mean the null hypothesis is true — only that we do not have enough evidence to reject it.
Never say: “We accept \(H_0\)” or “We accept \(H_1\)”. These are wrong.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
section: 2.12 Type I and Type II Errors
text:
2.12 Type I and Type II Errors
Because we base conclusions on sample data, we can sometimes make mistakes.









\(H_0\) is actually TRUE
\(H_0\) is actually FALSE




We Reject \(H_0\)
❌ Type I Error (probability = \(\alpha\))
✅ Correct — this is Power


We Fail to Reject \(H_0\)
✅ Correct
❌ Type II Error (probability = \(\beta\))




Type I Error = “False positive” — rejecting a true null hypothesis. You can control this by setting \(\alpha\).
Type II Error = “False negative” — failing to detect a real effect. Its probability is \(\beta\). You cannot directly control it without knowing the true population value.







Important⭐ Key Point on Errors



We can control Type I errors by setting \(\alpha\). We cannot directly control Type II errors — because to calculate the exact probability of \(\beta\), we would need to know the true population parameter, which is unknown.

Professor’s note: “We controlled for Type 1 errors by setting the level of significance, your alpha, or your 5%, or your 1%.”









Note💡 Real-World Analogy



A Type I Error is like a false fire alarm — the alarm goes off (you reject \(H_0\)) but there is no fire (no real effect). A Type II Error is like a missed fire — there is a real fire (a real effect), but the alarm does not go off (you fail to detect it).

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.13 Statistical Power
Power is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — of rejecting \(H_0\) when it truly is false.
\[
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta
\]

High power is desirable — you want your study to have a good chance of finding an effect if one really exists.
The trade-off: Decreasing \(\alpha\) (being more careful about Type I errors) automatically increases \(\beta\) (makes Type II errors more likely), which reduces power.


2.13.1 Increasing Power
The best solution to this trade-off is to increase the sample size before the study begins. With enough data, you can keep both \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) at acceptable levels simultaneously.


Show R code
# Install packages if needed
# install.packages("pwr")
# install.packages("asbio")

library(pwr)
library(asbio)

# Q6: One-sample test — power of detecting a 0.5 hr difference
# sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q6d: Minimum sample size for 90% power, detecting 15-minute (0.25 hr) difference
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, power = 0.9, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.25,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q7a: Two-sample t-test — power, n=20 per group, pooled SD=6.2, effect=5 complaints
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7b: Recalculate with alpha = 0.01
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.01,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7c: Minimum sample size for 90% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.9, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q8c: HbA1c intervention — one-sample, one-sided, 80% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.8, d = (1/2.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "less")



Professor’s note: “We can select a sample size before the study commences so that alpha and beta are both at satisfactory levels.”

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.14 Effect Size
The effect size tells you how big or strong an effect is — separate from whether it is statistically significant.
A result can be statistically significant but practically tiny. The effect size tells you if the result actually matters in the real world.

Larger effect size = stronger relationship = more likely to be practically important
Associated with every effect size is a standard error (a measure of its uncertainty)







Important⭐ Why Effect Size is Important




Professor’s note: “It’s good in practice to report your effect size. You’re doing it anyway — you don’t know you’re doing it when you’re reporting a confidence interval.”

Reporting a confidence interval is a way of reporting effect size, even if you do not realise it.



2.14.1 Cohen’s d (for t-tests)
Used when comparing two means:
\[
d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}}
\]
The difference between two means, divided by the pooled standard deviation. This standardises the difference so it can be compared across studies.


2.14.2 \(R^2\) (for Linear Regression)
In linear regression, effect size is measured by \(R^2\) — the square of Pearson’s correlation coefficient \(r\):
\[
R^2 = r^2
\]
\(R^2\) is the coefficient of determination. It tells you what proportion of the variation in \(Y\) is explained by \(X\).






Important⭐ How to Interpret \(R^2\)




Professor’s note: “If you had a coefficient of determination that was 40%, the interpretation would be: 40% of the variation in Y is explained by the variation in X. Which leaves 60% — that’s unexplained variation, maybe due to something else you haven’t considered.”

Always express \(R^2\) as a percentage when interpreting it, and identify what the unexplained variation might be due to.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.15 Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance
This is one of the most important distinctions in applied statistics.









Statistical Significance
Clinical / Practical Significance




What it means
Evidence that an effect exists in the data
The effect is large enough to matter in practice


How it is judged
p-value vs. \(\alpha\)
Effect size


Risk when sample is large
Small, trivial effects may be “significant”
May still have very small effect size


Risk when sample is small
A real effect may not be detected
Under-powered study may miss important differences



For a result to be truly meaningful, it must be:

First — statistically significant (\(p \leq \alpha\))
Then — have a meaningful effect size (large enough to matter practically)







Important⭐ The Blood Pressure Example




Professor’s note: “If you’re studying blood pressure — small changes in blood pressure might be practically significant. It depends on the subject that you’re studying.”

In medicine, a statistically significant reduction of even 2 mmHg in blood pressure across a large population could prevent thousands of strokes. So “small” effect sizes can still be practically important — it always depends on context.








Warning⚠️ Common Mistake: Over-relying on the p-value




Professor’s note: “Instead of attaching too much importance on statistical significance, we should pay attention to the actual data as well as the p-value.”


A non-significant result does NOT mean there is no effect. It means you did not have enough evidence to detect one (perhaps your sample size was too small).
A significant result with a tiny effect may have little practical value.
Always interpret both the p-value and the confidence interval together.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.1 What Is Missing Data?
In real-world datasets, it is very common to find that some values are not recorded. These are called missing values. In R, missing values are stored as NA — short for “Not Available.”
Missing data can happen for many reasons:

A survey participant skipped a question
A sensor failed to record a reading
A patient dropped out of a clinical trial

Why does this matter? Because if you ignore missing data without thinking about it, your results may be wrong. Some methods in R quietly remove missing values — you need to understand when that is happening and whether it is safe to do so.






Note🧠 Analogy



Think of a missing value like a blank in an exam paper. If you calculate the average score for the class and some students left questions blank, you need to decide: do you treat the blank as zero, skip that question entirely, or estimate what the student might have written? Each choice leads to a different average — and some choices are more honest than others.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.2 R Functions for Missing Data

2.2.1 Basic Functions
These functions are useful when you have only a few missing values and you know there is no systematic pattern to which values are missing.


Code
is.na(x)             # Returns TRUE/FALSE for each value — is it missing?
which(is.na(x))      # Tells you the *position* (index number) of each NA
na.rm = TRUE         # Used inside functions like mean() to skip NAs
complete.cases(x)    # Returns TRUE for rows where all values are present
na.omit(x)           # Removes all rows that contain at least one NA








Important⭐ Professor’s Note on These Functions



“These functions are useful when you only have a few missing data values. If your dataset is small enough, and you know that you only have a few missing values, or that you know that there’s no missing data patterns within your dataset, then these functions can be useful.”
— Paula Harrison




2.2.2 Removing Rows with Conditions


Code
# Remove rows where a specific column has NA
df <- df[!is.na(df$specific_column), ]

# Remove rows where EITHER col1 OR col2 has NA
df <- df[!(is.na(df$col1) | is.na(df$col2)), ]








Warning⚠️ Common Mistake



Beginners often just run na.omit(df) without checking what they are losing. If a column has 55% of its values missing, removing all rows with any NA in that column could dramatically shrink your dataset. Always check how many complete cases remain after removal.
Professor’s note: “By eliminating Column 3 [which had 55% missing], we went from only 5 subjects with complete data to 12. So just by removing one column, we changed the completeness of the dataset — but we also changed the sample size. You would have to investigate the extent to which data is missing and make sure the data you have left is enough to be a truly representative sample.”

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.3 The Sleep Dataset
The examples this week use the sleep dataset — a classic study of sleep patterns across 39 mammal species (62 observations, 10 variables). This dataset is built into the VIM package and is a good example because it has a realistic spread of missing values.



Variable
Type
Description




BodyWgt
Continuous
Body weight (kg)


BrainWgt
Continuous
Brain weight (kg)


NonD
Continuous
Non-dream sleep (hours/day)


Dream
Continuous
Dream sleep (hours/day)


Sleep
Continuous
Total sleep = NonD + Dream (hours/day)


Span
Continuous
Maximum life span (years)


Gest
Continuous
Gestation time (days)


Pred
Categorical
Predation index (danger from predators)


Exp
Categorical
Sleep exposure index


Danger
Categorical
Overall danger (1 = least, 5 = most)



Reference: Allison & Chichetti (1976), Science 194(4266), 732–734.






Note💡 Why NonD + Dream = Sleep?



The Sleep variable is just the sum of NonD and Dream. This means if either of those two is missing, then Sleep will also be missing. This is an example of structured missing data — missingness in one variable causes missingness in another. The combination plot from aggr() reveals this pattern clearly.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.4 Summarising Data with summarytools
The summarytools package provides a quick, readable summary of your entire dataset. It shows you the mean, standard deviation, min, max, distinct values, and number of valid (non-missing) observations for each column.


Code
library(summarytools)
dfSummary(sleep)


This is a good first step in any analysis. If a column has fewer valid values than the total number of rows, that tells you there are missing values in that column.
Professor’s note: “It’s a useful package if you want to just quickly summarise your dataset — useful for seeing if it’s skewed or normal, how many distinct values there are, the mean, standard deviation, median, max, IQR, coefficient of variance.”

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.5 Visualising Missing Data (VIM Package)
The VIM package provides specialised plots for exploring missing data. The key colour convention across all VIM plots is:

🔴 Red = Missing
🔵 Blue = Complete


2.5.1 aggr() — Count and Show Patterns of Missingness


Code
library(VIM)
aggr(sleep, numbers = TRUE, prop = FALSE)


This produces two plots side by side:
Left plot (bar chart): How many values are missing in each variable. In the sleep data, NonD had ~14 missing values — the most of any variable.
Right plot (combination matrix): Which variables tend to be missing at the same time. For example, the combination matrix showed that rows 31 and 62 both had NonD, Dream, and Sleep missing simultaneously. This makes sense because Sleep = NonD + Dream.






Important⭐ Always Double-Check Your Plots



Professor’s note: “Don’t assume that the results are correct. Just if you can, try to test to see if the results are correct. So I’ve taken this combination showing NonD, Dream and Sleep missing together — we know we have two rows where those three variables are missing at the same time, and I went back to the dataset and had a look to make sure that was correct.”
Always verify surprising patterns by inspecting the actual rows in your data (e.g., sleep[c(31, 62), ]).





2.5.2 barMiss() — Missingness by a Categorical Variable
Use this when you want to explore whether missingness in one variable is related to the values of a categorical variable.


Code
barMiss(sleep[, c("Exp", "Sleep")])


How to read this plot: The x-axis shows categories of Exp (the exposure index). Each bar shows the total count of Sleep observations in that category. Red sections show how many Sleep values are missing within that category.
Findings for sleep data: - Sleep had 4 missing values in total - 1 missing when Exp = 1 - 3 missing when Exp = 5 - Exp itself had no missing values
The last column (“missing”) shows how many Exp observations were missing — in this case, zero.



2.5.3 histMiss() — Missingness by a Continuous Variable
This is the same idea as barMiss(), but for continuous variables, where a histogram is more appropriate than a bar chart.


Code
histMiss(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])


Findings for sleep data: - Sleep had 4 missing values - 2 occurred when Span was between 10 and 20 years - 2 occurred when Span was between 20 and 30 years - Span itself had 4 missing values (shown in the separate “missing” bar on the right)



2.5.4 marginplot() — Comparing Distributions With and Without Missingness
This is the most informative VIM plot. It shows a scatterplot of two variables, with extra boxplots added in the margins.


Code
marginplot(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])


How to read this plot:







Element
What it shows




🔴 Red dots (bottom margin)
Number of missing observations in Span


🔴 Red dots (left margin)
Number of missing observations in Sleep


🔴 Red boxplot (left margin)
Distribution of Sleep where Span is missing


🔵 Blue boxplot (left margin)
Distribution of Sleep where Span is observed


🔴 Red boxplot (bottom margin)
Distribution of Span where Sleep is missing


🔵 Blue boxplot (bottom margin)
Distribution of Span where Sleep is observed


Centre scatterplot
All observations with complete data for both variables



Key question to ask: Are the red and blue boxplots similar or different?

If similar → the missingness of Span is not related to the values of Sleep. This is consistent with Missing At Random (MAR).
If different → the missingness pattern may be informative, which could bias your analysis if you simply delete the missing rows.

Professor’s note: “Blue is not missing, red is missing — and in the centre is just your standard scatterplot.”

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.6 Dealing with Missing Data

2.6.1 Option 1: Complete Case Analysis (Deleting Missing Rows)
The simplest approach is to remove any row that contains a missing value and perform your analysis on only the complete rows.
In R’s lm() function, this is the default behaviour — it automatically uses only complete cases.


Code
# These two models give identical results:
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = sleep)
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = na.omit(sleep))


Other related functions:


Code
na.fail()     # Throws an error if any NAs are present — forces you to address the issue
na.exclude()  # Like na.omit() but keeps empty slots for NA predictions








Warning⚠️ When is Complete Case Analysis Acceptable?



Only when:

The amount of missing data is small
There is no systematic pattern to which cases are missing (i.e., the data is Missing Completely At Random, MCAR)

If certain types of cases are more likely to be missing (e.g., sicker patients dropping out of a study), removing them introduces bias — your remaining sample no longer represents the full population.
Professor’s note: “When you ask ‘is it okay just to remove the data?’ — it depends on your analysis, and if there is no missing data patterns. But you can see with this dataset how the complete cases improve by just removing one column. You would have to investigate the extent to which data is missing.”





2.6.2 Option 2: Imputation with irmi() — Iterative Robust Model-Based Imputation
Imputation means filling in missing values with estimated values — rather than discarding incomplete rows.
The irmi() function in the VIM package does this through a series of regression models.


Code
library(VIM)
imputed_sleep <- irmi(sleep)


How irmi() works (step by step):

Pick one variable (e.g., NonD) to be the response (outcome) variable.
Use all other variables as predictors in a regression model.
Where NonD is missing, use the predicted value from the regression as the imputed value.
Move on to the next variable with missing values, and repeat.
Keep cycling through all variables until the imputed values stabilise (i.e., stop changing much between iterations).

“Irmi — that is, Iterative Robust Model-Based Imputation. It basically runs iterative regression analysis in which each iteration uses one variable as the response variable and the remaining variables as the explanatory variables.” — Paula Harrison
After imputation, compare your models:


Code
# Before imputation — uses complete cases only (42 of 62 rows)
model_before <- lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = sleep)

# After imputation — uses all 62 rows
model_after  <- lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = imputed_sleep)

summary(model_before)
summary(model_after)


Expected findings: The intercept will be slightly larger for the imputed model, and the slope slightly less negative — because the imputed model now uses all 62 rows, not just the 42 complete cases.



2.6.3 Option 3: Multiple Imputation with MICE
The MICE package (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) is more sophisticated. Instead of creating one imputed dataset, it creates multiple datasets (e.g., 5), fits a model to each one separately, and then pools (averages) the results.


Code
library(mice)

# Step 1: Create 5 imputed datasets using predictive mean matching
imputed <- mice(sleep, m = 5, method = "pmm")

# Step 2: Fit a linear model to each of the 5 datasets
models  <- with(imputed, lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt))

# Step 3: Pool (average) the results across all 5 models
pooled  <- pool(models)
summary(pooled)


What is predictive mean matching (PMM)?
PMM works by: 1. Predicting a value for the missing observation using a regression model. 2. Finding a small set of real observed values that are closest to that prediction. 3. Randomly choosing one of those real values as the imputed value.
This means imputed values are always real values from your dataset — they will never be unrealistically extreme.






Important⭐ Why Are MICE Standard Errors Always Larger?



Professor’s note: “The standard error is larger for the pooled model — it’s always going to be larger — because it’s including the extra uncertainty that we have created in our dataset due to the imputation values.”
When we impute missing data, we are making an educated guess. The pooled standard errors are larger than those from a single imputed model because they account for the uncertainty introduced by not knowing the true values. This is more statistically honest than pretending our imputed values are perfectly correct.








Note💡 MICE vs irmi() — When to Use Which?












irmi()
MICE




Number of imputed datasets
1
Multiple (default = 5)


Accounts for imputation uncertainty?
No
Yes


Pooled standard errors
Not available
Available


Complexity
Simpler
More complex


Best used when
Quick analysis, small data
Publication-quality results, large missing proportion

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.1 What Is Statistical Power?
When you run a hypothesis test, there are four possible outcomes — two correct decisions and two types of error:









H₀ is True
H₀ is False




Reject H₀
❌ Type I error (probability = α)
✅ Correct! (probability = power = 1 − β)


Fail to reject H₀
✅ Correct! (probability = 1 − α)
❌ Type II error (probability = β)



Statistical power is the probability that your test will correctly detect a real effect when one truly exists:
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})\]
In plain English: if there is a real difference, how likely is your test to find it?






Note🧠 Analogy



Think of power like the sensitivity of a metal detector on a beach. A low-power detector might only find a large coin buried 2 cm deep. A high-power detector will find a small earring buried 30 cm deep. If you want to detect small or subtle effects, you need a high-power test — and that usually means a larger sample.


Professor’s note: “The statistical power of the hypothesis test is defined to be the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false. So 1 minus beta. So basically, to get the correct result.”

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.2 The Four Interconnected Quantities
Power analysis is built on the relationship between four quantities. If you know any three, you can calculate the fourth:



Quantity
Symbol
Typical default




Sample size
n
Chosen based on budget/feasibility


Effect size
δ
The minimum meaningful difference


Significance level (Type I error rate)
α
0.05


Power (1 − Type II error rate)
1 − β
≥ 0.80









Important⭐ You Cannot Know the True Effect Size



Professor’s note: “In reality, it’s not possible to know the true effect size. Because we don’t generally know the true population mean. So we don’t know what the true difference is that we’re looking for. So what we are going to choose instead is to have a minimum effect size — what is the minimum difference in means that we’re willing to accept as a practical difference?”
In practice, you define the minimum effect size of practical importance — the smallest difference that would actually matter for your application. This comes from prior research, expert knowledge, or clinical guidelines.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.3 Clinical vs Statistical Significance
Statistical significance means your test produced a p-value below α — the result is unlikely to be due to random chance.
Clinical (or practical) significance means the size of the effect is large enough to matter in the real world.






Note💡 The Fethney (2010) Example



This paper (available on Canvas) gives a real example of defining a minimum effect size.
Premature infants were randomly assigned to one of two treatment groups across four weight-gain interventions. Group A received the intervention; Group B received standard treatment. Weights were compared using the Mann-Whitney U-test.
The researchers decided in advance that a weight gain difference of 500 grams or more would be clinically significant. A negative lower confidence interval limit would indicate weight loss.
Professor’s note: “They have decided, based on previous research and knowledge, that the minimum difference that they want is 500 grams. So 500 grams is the minimum that we need in order for us to reject the null hypothesis.”
Key lesson: Before collecting data, you should define what size of difference you actually care about detecting.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.4 Key Formulas

3.4.1 Z-Test Statistic (Population Standard Deviation Known)
\[Z_{stat} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\]

\(\bar{x}\) = sample mean
\(\mu_0\) = the null hypothesis value of the population mean
\(\sigma\) = known population standard deviation
\(n\) = sample size



3.4.2 Critical Sample Mean (Threshold for Rejecting H₀)
\[\bar{X}_{crit} = \mu_0 + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \times Z_{crit}\]
If the sample mean exceeds \(\bar{X}_{crit}\), we reject H₀.


3.4.3 Calculating β (Probability of Type II Error)
Once you have \(\bar{X}_{crit}\), calculate the probability that the sample mean falls below it under the alternative hypothesis (i.e., assuming the true mean is \(\mu_A\)):
\[\beta = P\left(\bar{X} < \bar{X}_{crit} \mid \mu = \mu_A\right) = P\left(Z < \frac{\bar{X}_{crit} - \mu_A}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right)\]
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\]


3.4.4 Sample Size Formula — Known σ (from Confidence Interval Margin of Error)
\[ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]






Warning⚠️ Always Round Sample Size Up



If your formula gives n = 77.8, your sample size is 78, not 77. You cannot sample a fraction of a person (or resistor, or container). Always round up to the next whole number.
Professor’s note: “You can’t sample 77.8 units, so what is the sample size that you need? 78 — you would always go one up, yeah. Just round up.”




3.4.5 Sample Size Formula — Unknown σ (t-Distribution)
When the population standard deviation is unknown, use the sample standard deviation \(s\) and the t-distribution:
\[n = \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \times s}{ME}\right)^2\]
Note: this requires iteration because \(t_{n-1}\) depends on \(n\) — start with an initial estimate, then recalculate.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.5 Worked Example — Chemical Company

3.5.1 Problem Setup
A chemical company claims their product contains on average μ₀ = 4.0 ml of caustic material per litre. If the true mean exceeds 4.0, the product is dangerous.
A government inspector tests n = 30 containers and finds: - Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = 4.3\) ml - Known population standard deviation: \(\sigma = 1.3\) ml


3.5.2 Step 1: State the Hypotheses
The product is only dangerous if it contains more than 4.0 ml — so this is a one-tailed test (right-tailed):
\[H_0: \mu = 4.0 \qquad H_A: \mu > 4.0\]


3.5.3 Step 2: Identify the Error Types in Context

Type I error (α): Concluding the mean is greater than 4.0 ml when it actually is not. The inspector falsely accuses the company.
Type II error (β): Concluding the mean is not greater than 4.0 ml when it actually is. The inspector misses a genuine danger.

Professor’s note: “You can never say that the null hypothesis is true — it’s just that we haven’t disproved the null hypothesis. And we can’t say that the alternative hypothesis is true either. We can just say that it’s more likely.”


3.5.4 Step 3: Conduct the Hypothesis Test (α = 0.05)
\[Z_{stat} = \frac{4.3 - 4.0}{1.3 / \sqrt{30}} = \frac{0.3}{0.2374} = 1.264\]
For a one-tailed test at α = 0.05, the critical value is \(Z_{crit} = 1.645\).
Since \(Z_{stat} = 1.264 < 1.645\), we fail to reject H₀.
The p-value is 0.103 > 0.05, confirming this decision.
Conclusion: The data does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that the mean level of caustic material exceeds 4 ml per litre.
The 95% lower bound is 3.910 — we are 95% confident that the true mean is greater than 3.910 ml.






Important⭐ Professor’s Decision Rule (Critical Value Method)



“If the test statistic is greater than the critical value, we reject. If it’s less than, we fail to reject the critical value. So if the Z score is less than 1.645, which it is in this case, then our decision is that we fail to reject the null hypothesis.”
Summary: - \(Z_{stat} > Z_{crit}\) → Reject H₀ - \(Z_{stat} < Z_{crit}\) → Fail to reject H₀




3.5.5 Step 4: Calculate the Power of the Test
The test did not find a significant result — but was the sample large enough to detect a real difference?
Suppose a difference of 0.35 ml/litre is practically important — i.e., we want to detect if the true mean is \(\mu_A = 4.35\).
Find the critical sample mean (rejection threshold):
\[\bar{X}_{crit} = 4.0 + \frac{1.3}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.645 = 4.0 + 0.39 = 4.39\]
We only reject H₀ if the sample mean exceeds 4.39 ml.
Calculate β (probability of Type II error under μ_A = 4.35):
\[\beta = P(\bar{X} < 4.39 \mid \mu = 4.35) = P\left(Z < \frac{4.39 - 4.35}{1.3/\sqrt{30}}\right) = P(Z < 0.17) = 0.5675\]
Power:
\[\text{Power} = 1 - 0.5675 = \mathbf{0.4325} \approx 43.25\%\]
Interpretation: There is only a 43% chance of correctly detecting a 0.35 ml difference with n = 30. This is quite low — the test is underpowered. We would ideally want power ≥ 0.80.






Important⭐ What Low Power Means



Professor’s note: “There’s a greater chance that we’re going to commit a Type 2 error. The probability is higher for committing a Type 2 error over a Type 1 error. And that could be because the sample size is small. Maybe it’s to do with the difference that we are looking to see — the 0.35 could be to do with that.”
A test result of “fail to reject” is not the same as proof that there is no effect. It might just mean your sample was too small to detect the effect you were looking for.

title: Week 3: Missing Data & Power Analysis
section: 3.6 Sample Size from Margin of Error
text:
3.6 Sample Size from Margin of Error
Power analysis connects directly to confidence interval precision. The margin of error (ME) in a confidence interval is:
\[ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
Rearranging to solve for the required sample size:
\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]

3.6.1 Worked Example: Resistor Voltage
An analyst wants to estimate the mean voltage of resistors. The population standard deviation is σ = 22.5 volts. What sample size is needed to achieve a margin of error of 5 volts at a 95% confidence level (two-sided)?
\[n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 22.5}{5}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{44.1}{5}\right)^2 = (8.82)^2 = 77.8 \approx \mathbf{78}\]
Code
Code

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
This week covered two main areas:

Multiple Comparisons — how to fairly compare more than two group means after a significant ANOVA result
Two-Way ANOVA — extending one-way ANOVA to handle two independent variables simultaneously, including their interaction effect
Factorial Design — the correct experimental design when testing for interactions
Effect Size — measuring how much of the variance is explained by each factor (partial η²)
Model Validation in R — checking ANOVA assumptions with diagnostic plots
Lab Practice — running one-way ANOVA by hand in Excel and in R, including post-hoc tests

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.1 The Multiple Comparisons Problem
When ANOVA tells us that at least one group mean is different, it does not tell us which groups differ. A natural first instinct is to run separate t-tests for every pair of groups — but this creates a serious problem.
Every individual test carries a risk (α, usually 5%) of a Type I error (falsely concluding there is a difference). When you run many tests on the same data, those error risks stack up. This inflated overall error rate is called the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER).

Example: Testing 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam) means 3 pairwise comparisons.
With 6 cities (from the New Car Interest Rate dataset), there are 15 pairwise comparisons — the chance of at least one false positive becomes very large.


2.1.1 Post-Hoc Tests
Post-hoc (after-the-fact) tests are designed to run all pairwise comparisons while controlling the FWER at the chosen α level. They are more conservative than plain t-tests.








Method
How it works
When to use




Tukey’s HSD (Honest Significant Difference)
Controls FWER by adjusting critical values
Most common; good general choice


Bonferroni
Divides α by the number of comparisons
Simple; conservative with many comparisons


Separate t-tests
No adjustment
Avoid — inflated Type I error





2.1.2 Worksheet 4 Results (Soil Yield Data)



Comparison
Separate t-tests
Tukey’s HSD
Bonferroni




Loam – Clay
0.081
0.179
0.234


Loam – Sand
0.006
0.020
0.023


Clay – Sand
0.351
0.555
0.914




Key takeaway: Only the Loam vs. Sand comparison is statistically significant.
Notice how the adjusted p-values (Tukey, Bonferroni) are larger than the raw t-test p-values — this is the conservative adjustment at work.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.2 Two-Way ANOVA

2.2.1 What is it?
One-way ANOVA tested one independent variable (e.g., soil type) against one continuous dependent variable (yield).
Two-way ANOVA tests two independent (categorical) variables simultaneously against one continuous dependent variable. This is powerful because it also reveals whether the two factors interact.


2.2.2 Main Effects vs. Interaction Effect

Main effect of Factor A — the overall effect of Factor A on the response, ignoring Factor B.
Main effect of Factor B — the overall effect of Factor B on the response, ignoring Factor A.
Interaction effect (A × B) — does the effect of Factor A depend on which level of Factor B we’re at? This is the most important question in two-way ANOVA.


Always check the interaction effect first. If it is significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — you must look at them within each level of the other factor (called simple effects).



2.2.3 The Three Null Hypotheses
For a two-way ANOVA with factors A and B:

H₀ (Factor A): All population means for Factor A are equal (no main effect of A)
H₀ (Factor B): All population means for Factor B are equal (no main effect of B)
H₀ (Interaction): The effect of Factor A is the same at all levels of Factor B (no interaction)


Thinking further: A three-way ANOVA has 7 hypotheses — 3 main effects + 3 two-way interactions + 1 three-way interaction.



2.2.4 The Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model
\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(Y_{ijk}\)
Response variable (observation \(k\) at level \(i\) of A and level \(j\) of B)


\(\mu\)
Overall population mean


\(\alpha_i\)
Treatment effect of Factor A at level \(i\)


\(\beta_j\)
Treatment effect of Factor B at level \(j\)


\((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\)
Interaction effect


\(\varepsilon_{ijk}\)
Residual / error term


\(k\)
Replicate index

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.3 Factorial Design
To test for an interaction effect, you must use a factorial (fully crossed) design:

“All possible combinations of all treatment levels must be included for every replicate.”

If Factor A has a levels and Factor B has b levels:
\[\text{Number of treatment combinations} = a \times b\]
With \(r\) replicates:
\[\text{Total observations} = r \times a \times b\]

Example (Viscosity study):
pH has 2 levels (5.6, 5.9) × Catalyst concentration has 2 levels (2.5, 2.7) = 4 combinations.
With 3 replicates → 12 total viscosity measurements.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.4 Interaction Plots
An interaction plot graphs the mean response for each combination of factor levels. It is the clearest way to visually assess whether an interaction exists.







Plot appearance
Conclusion




Lines are approximately parallel
No interaction between factors


Lines cross or diverge sharply
Interaction is likely present




Tip: Don’t overcomplicate it. Pick one line, trace how the response changes as the other factor changes — then repeat for the other line. If the direction or magnitude of change differs between lines, that’s an interaction.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.5 Partitioning Variance in Two-Way ANOVA
Total variability is split into components:
\[SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}\]
The ANOVA table for two-way:










Source
SS
df
MS = SS/df
F = MS / MSₑ




Factor A
\(SS_A\)
\(a - 1\)
\(MS_A\)
\(F_A\)


Factor B
\(SS_B\)
\(b - 1\)
\(MS_B\)
\(F_B\)


Interaction A×B
\(SS_{AB}\)
\((a-1)(b-1)\)
\(MS_{AB}\)
\(F_{AB}\)


Error
\(SS_E\)
\(ab(r-1)\)
\(MS_E\)
—

\(SS_T\)
\(abr - 1\)
—

A p-value (or critical F value) is then used to make the decision for each row.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.6 Significance Stars in R Output
R uses shorthand to indicate significance levels:



Symbol
p-value range
Interpretation




(blank)

Not significant


.

*

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.7 Simple Effects (Post-Hoc for Two-Way ANOVA)
If the interaction is significant, examine simple effects: the effect of one factor while holding the other factor constant at each level.

Viscosity Example: The interaction was significant (p = 0.03).
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.5: not significant
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.7: significant (difference = 15, p = 0.01)

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.8 Effect Size — Partial Eta Squared (η²ₚ)
To measure how much of the variance is explained by each factor:
\[\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{error}}\]
This is analogous to R² in regression. It tells you the proportion of variance in the response explained by a given factor, after removing variance accounted for by the other variables.

Interpretation example: η²ₚ = 0.33 for pH means 33% of the variation in viscosity is associated with pH.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.9 ANOVA Assumptions (Checklist)
Before trusting ANOVA results, verify:

Random sampling — ensured at data collection stage
Equal variances (homogeneity) — test with Levene’s test or Bartlett’s test
Independence of error terms — observations should not influence each other
Normality of residuals — check with QQ-plot and/or Shapiro-Wilk test


If equal variance assumption is violated → use the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test (uses ranks/medians instead of means).

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
section: 4.1 Example 1: Soil Yield — One-Way ANOVA + Post-Hoc (Worksheet 4 / Lab)
text:
4.1 Example 1: Soil Yield — One-Way ANOVA + Post-Hoc (Worksheet 4 / Lab)
Setup: Crop yields measured for 10 fields on each of 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam).
Group means: Sand = 9.9, Clay = 11.5, Loam = 14.3
ANOVA p-value: 0.025 → significant (at least one mean differs)
Post-hoc conclusion: Only Loam vs. Sand is significantly different (Tukey p ≈ 0.020, Bonferroni p ≈ 0.023). Clay is not significantly different from either.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
section: 4.2 Example 2: Viscosity Study — Two-Way ANOVA with Interaction
text:
4.2 Example 2: Viscosity Study — Two-Way ANOVA with Interaction
Factors: - Factor A = pH (2 levels: 5.6, 5.9) - Factor B = Catalyst Concentration (2 levels: 2.5, 2.7) - 3 replicates → 12 total measurements
R Output Interpretation:



Effect
p-value
Conclusion




pH (main)
> 0.05
Not significant alone


Catalyst (main)
> 0.05
Not significant alone


pH × Catalyst (interaction)
0.03
Significant ✓



Because the interaction is significant:
→ The main effects are not meaningful in isolation
→ Must examine simple effects (e.g., effect of pH when catalyst is held at 2.5 vs. 2.7)
→ When catalyst = 2.7, changing pH from 5.6 to 5.9 produces a statistically significant difference (p = 0.01)

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
section: 4.3 Example 3: New Car Interest Rate (Worksheet 4, Part 2)
text:
4.3 Example 3: New Car Interest Rate (Worksheet 4, Part 2)
Setup: 1 factor (city) with 6 levels → 15 pairwise comparisons
Task: Run one-way ANOVA, then compare Separate t-tests vs. Tukey vs. Bonferroni across all 15 pairs.

Purpose: To show how the multiple comparisons problem scales with more groups — with 15 tests, the chance of a false positive grows substantially if uncorrected p-values are used.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text: 📝 Note to self: These notes are a living document. Add your own annotations, extra examples, and corrections as you go through the course.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.1 2.1 What Is a Research Hypothesis?
Before collecting a single data point, you need to know what question you are trying to answer. This is your research hypothesis.

A hypothesis is just a question that you want to answer, framed clearly enough that you can actually test it with data.

The lecturer’s golden rule: “Statistics means what it says. When you interpret your plots and findings, just say what you see.”

3.1.1 A Good Hypothesis Is:








Property
Meaning
Example




Simple
One predictor, one outcome
Does smoking affect lung capacity?


Specific
No ambiguity about subjects or variables
Does cigarette smoking (yes/no) reduce FEV1 (litres) in adults aged 18–65?


Stated in advance
Written before you look at the data
Prevents fishing for results





3.1.2 Simple vs. Complex Hypotheses
A simple hypothesis has: - 1 predictor variable (the thing you think causes an effect), AND - 1 outcome variable (the thing you are measuring)
A complex hypothesis has more than one of either. The trick: break complex hypotheses into multiple simple ones.
Example — Combining predictors: Instead of separating vaping, cigarettes, cigars, and pipes, you might combine them into one variable: smoker vs. non-smoker. That is a combined predictor.


3.1.3 Specific Hypothesis Rules

Leave no ambiguity about who is being studied or what is being measured
Define variables clearly: are they continuous (e.g., age in years) or categorical (e.g., yes/no)?
State how statistical significance will be tested
Being specific prevents “moving the goalposts” after you see the data

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.1 3.1 Planning the Study
Before you collect data, you must decide:

Who and what you are studying (the target population)
How many subjects to include (sample size)
How you will sample them (probability vs. non-probability sampling)


4.1.1 Key Design Concepts







Term
Plain English Explanation




Response variable
The outcome you are measuring (also called the dependent variable)


Factor / Level
A variable you are manipulating; each category is a “level” (e.g., Low / Medium / High dose)


Treatment
The specific condition applied to a subject


Replication
Repeating the experiment to ensure results are consistent


Randomisation
Assigning subjects to groups by chance, to avoid bias


Blocking
Grouping similar subjects together to reduce noise


Factorial design
Testing multiple factors at the same time


Main effects
The individual effect of each factor


Interaction effects
When the effect of one factor depends on another

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.1 4.1 Types of Variables
Variables can be measured at different scales, which affects what statistics you can use:









Type
Description
Example
Appropriate Stats




Nominal
Categories with no order
Blood type (A, B, O)
Frequencies, mode


Ordinal
Categories with order, but unequal gaps
Pain scale (mild/moderate/severe)
Median, percentiles


Interval
Ordered, equal gaps, no true zero
Temperature (°C)
Mean, SD


Ratio
Ordered, equal gaps, true zero
Weight, Height
Mean, SD, ratios


Continuous
Can take any value (usually ratio/interval)
Cholesterol (mmol/L)
All statistics


Categorical
Discrete groups
Gender, Smoking status
Frequencies, proportions




💡 Key rule: Collect data as continuous wherever possible — it contains more information, gives more statistical power, and allows a smaller sample size. You can always convert it to categorical later.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.1 5.1 Precision
Precision = getting the same result every time you measure something.
Think of precision like a dart player who always hits the same spot — even if it’s not the bullseye. The measurements are consistent and reproducible.

Precision is affected by random error (chance)
The more precise a measurement, the greater the statistical power of your study


6.1.1 Sources of Random Error (that reduce precision):

Observer variability — different people measure slightly differently
Instrument variability — equipment changes due to temperature, wear, etc.
Subject variability — the subject themselves changes between measurements (e.g., blood pressure fluctuates)

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.2 5.2 Accuracy
Accuracy = measuring the correct thing in the correct way.
Think of accuracy like a dart player who hits the bullseye — even if their shots are scattered around it. The measurements are valid and represent what they are supposed to represent.

Accuracy is affected by systematic error (consistent bias in one direction)
Accuracy is essential for the validity of your study conclusions

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 6.3 5.3 They Are NOT the Same Thing!
text:
6.3 5.3 They Are NOT the Same Thing!









Accurate
Inaccurate




Precise
✅ Best case: consistent AND correct
❌ Consistently wrong (biased)


Imprecise
❌ Correct on average but noisy
❌ Worst case: all over the place




6.3.1 Quick Quiz Answers (from Exercise Slide):

➡️ Accuracy — “the degree to which a variable represents what it’s supposed to represent”
➡️ Precision — “almost the same value when measured repeatedly”
➡️ Accuracy — increases validity of conclusions
➡️ Accuracy — threatened by systematic error
➡️ Precision — threatened by random error
➡️ Precision — assessed using repeated measures

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
7.1 6.1 What Does IDA Include?

Summarising data (numerical statistics)
Visualising data (graphs)
Univariate profiling — looking at each variable on its own
Bivariate profiling — looking at relationships between pairs of variables
Identifying missing data
Identifying outliers
Checking model assumptions
Variable transformation (if needed)

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
7.2 6.2 Summarising Data
When you get a continuous variable, the workflow is:
Raw data → Frequency table → Histogram → Describe key features
Key features to describe (your summary statistics):

Centre: mean, median, mode
Spread: standard deviation, variance, range, interquartile range (IQR)
Shape: skewness, number of peaks

For categorical variables, use frequency tables and bar charts.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
8.1 7.1 Why Visualise?

“Don’t underestimate the power of a graph. It is a really good cognitive tool.” — Lecturer

The goal of data visualisation is to communicate information clearly and effectively through graphical representation. Statistics alone can be deeply misleading — which brings us to:

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 8.2 7.2 Anscombe’s Quartet — The Most Important Example in EDA
text:
8.2 7.2 Anscombe’s Quartet — The Most Important Example in EDA
Anscombe’s Quartet is a set of four datasets (I, II, III, IV) designed to show why you must always plot your data before analysing it.
Here is the key point: all four datasets have almost identical summary statistics:



Statistic
Dataset I
Dataset II
Dataset III
Dataset IV




Mean of X
9.00
9.00
9.00
9.00


Mean of Y
7.50
7.50
7.50
7.50


SD of X
3.16
3.16
3.16
3.16


SD of Y
1.94
1.94
1.94
1.94


Correlation (r)
0.82
0.82
0.82
0.82



Yet if you plot them as scatterplots, they look completely different:

Dataset I: A proper linear relationship — the only one where a straight line fits
Dataset II: A curved (curvilinear) relationship — a line is wrong here
Dataset III: Mostly linear but with one massive outlier pulling the line
Dataset IV: Vertical cluster of points with one extreme outlier — very weird


8.2.1 The lesson:

You cannot determine the shape of a relationship from the correlation coefficient alone. You MUST look at a graph.

This is a likely exam point.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 8.3 7.3 How to Interpret a Correlation Coefficient (Exam Skill!)
text:
8.3 7.3 How to Interpret a Correlation Coefficient (Exam Skill!)

From the lecture: “When you are asked to interpret the correlation coefficient, there are only two things you can say about it.”

The correlation coefficient (r) ranges from -1 to +1.

8.3.1 The Two Things You Can Say:

Direction — Is it positive or negative?

Positive: As X increases, Y increases
Negative: As X increases, Y decreases

Strength — How close is it to 1 (or -1)?

|r| = 0.8–1.0 → Strong relationship
|r| = 0.5–0.8 → Moderate relationship
|r| = 0–0.5 → Weak relationship




8.3.2 ❌ What You CANNOT Say:

“The relationship is linear.”

You cannot tell if a relationship is linear just from r. Anscombe’s Quartet proves this — all four datasets have r = 0.82, but only one is actually linear. You need a scatterplot to judge linearity.


8.3.3 Example Interpretation Template:
“The correlation coefficient is r = 0.82, indicating a strong, positive relationship between X and Y. However, to determine whether the relationship is linear, a scatterplot should be examined.”

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
9.1 8.1 Why Does Missing Data Happen?

Errors during data collection (e.g., instrument failure)
Data entry errors
Respondents refusing to answer certain questions
Study dropout (participants leaving before the end)
Experimental failure (e.g., a plant dying before measurement)

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
9.2 8.2 Why Is It a Problem?

Statistical analysis cannot be performed on incomplete data (by default)
Reduces your effective sample size
Can bias your results if the missingness is not random

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 9.3 8.3 The Four Types of Missing Data
text:
9.3 8.3 The Four Types of Missing Data
This is a key exam topic. Think about why the data is missing:

9.3.1 Type 1 — Ignorable Missing Data
The data is missing by design — it was expected and planned for.
Example: A questionnaire about children’s health has a section only for parents. Adults without children simply don’t fill it in. This is expected. No action needed.



9.3.2 Type 2 — MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)
The probability of being missing is the same for everyone, completely by chance. The missing data has nothing to do with the data itself.
Example: A participant flips a coin to decide whether to answer a question.
🔑 Causes: unrelated to the data.
✅ Easiest to deal with — can delete safely with minimal bias.



9.3.3 Type 3 — MAR (Missing At Random)
The data is missing, but the reason for it being missing can be explained by other variables you have measured. Once you account for those variables, the missingness is random.
Example: Older people are less likely to report income. If you know someone’s age, you can predict whether income is likely to be missing — but within each age group, the missingness is random.
🔑 The missingness is related to observed data, not the missing value itself.
⚠️ More complex — deletion can bias results; imputation is better.



9.3.4 Type 4 — MNAR (Not Missing At Random)
The most dangerous type. The data is missing because of its own value — the very value that is missing is the reason it’s missing.
Example: People with very high incomes deliberately choose not to report their income. The chance of income being missing depends on the income itself.
🔑 The missingness depends on unobserved or missing data.
❌ Most difficult — standard methods can be biased. Requires specialist methods.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
9.4 8.4 Practical Examples (Exercise 4 Answers)








Scenario
Type
Explanation




(a) Census section for parents — households without children skip it
Ignorable
By design; no children = no data needed


(b) Half of survey respondents randomly selected for extra questionnaire
MCAR
Selection is truly random


(c) Plants receiving highest pesticide dose die — concentration not recorded
MNAR
Whether the measurement exists depends on its own value (extreme concentration killed the plant)


(d) Weighing scales max 5kg — heavier items show error
MNAR
Missingness depends directly on the value (weight > 5kg) — also called censoring


(e) Fitbit battery dies at random times
MCAR
Completely random — no relationship with activity levels


(f) Fitbit battery dies some evenings (when activity is lower)
MAR
Time of day (observed) predicts missingness; activity level predicts time of day


(g) Person is unwell and takes off fitbit
MNAR
Low activity causes the missingness — the very variable being measured

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 9.5 8.5 The 4 Steps for Dealing With Missing Data
text:
9.5 8.5 The 4 Steps for Dealing With Missing Data

9.5.1 Step 1: Determine the Type
Is it ignorable? If yes, stop — no action needed.


9.5.2 Step 2: Determine the Extent

How many values are missing overall?
Which variables have the most missing data?
Should a heavily-missing variable be dropped entirely?


Rule of thumb: If more than ~20–30% of a variable’s values are missing, consider dropping it.



9.5.3 Step 3: Diagnose the Randomness
Is it MCAR, MAR, or MNAR? (This determines what you can do about it.)


9.5.4 Step 4: Select an Imputation Method
Imputation = filling in the missing values using an educated estimate.








Method
Description
Notes




Listwise deletion
Remove any row with a missing value
Simple but loses data


Pairwise deletion
Use all available data per analysis
More efficient but inconsistent


Mean imputation
Replace missing values with the variable mean
Easy but distorts distribution


Regression imputation
Predict missing value using other variables
More sophisticated


Stochastic regression
Adds random error to regression predictions
Preserves variance


LOCF (Last Observation Carried Forward)
For time series: use previous value
Used in clinical trials


k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN)
Use similar observations to estimate
Machine learning approach

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
9.6 8.6 Missing Data in R
# Check if a value is missing
is.na(x)

# Calculate mean, ignoring NAs
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)

# Find complete rows (no NAs anywhere)
complete.cases(df)

# Remove all rows with any missing values
na.omit(df)

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
10.1 9.1 What Is an Outlier?
An outlier is an observation that is unusually different from the rest of the data. It stands out because: - It has an unusually high or low value on one variable, OR - It has a unique combination of values across several variables

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
10.2 9.2 Why Do Outliers Occur?








Source
Description
What to do




Procedural error
Data entry mistake, measurement error
Fix or remove


Extraordinary event
A genuine but unusual occurrence
Investigate, note, possibly remove


Extraordinary observation
A genuinely extreme but valid data point
Keep — it’s real data




⚠️ Key point: Always consider outliers in context. An outlier might be an error — or it might be the most interesting data point in your study.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 11.1 10.1 The Four Assumptions for Linear Models
text:
11.1 10.1 The Four Assumptions for Linear Models
LINE








Letter
Assumption
What It Means




L
Linearity
The relationship between X and Y is a straight line


I
Independence
Observations do not affect each other


N
Normality
The errors (residuals) follow a normal distribution


E
Equal variance (Homoscedasticity)
The spread of errors is constant across all values of X

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
11.2 10.2 What If Assumptions Are Violated?
Apply a data transformation to make the data better behaved.







Transformation
When to Use




Log transformation
Right-skewed data, multiplicative relationships


Square root
Count data, mild skewness


Squaring
Left-skewed data


Standardising
When variables have different scales

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
section: 12.1 Exercise 1 – Census Missing Data (Age and Income)
text:
12.1 Exercise 1 – Census Missing Data (Age and Income)
A census collected age and income data. Figures 1–3 show missing data patterns.

12.1.1 Part (a): Comment on the extent of missing data
How to interpret these plots: - The blue bars represent observed data - The red bars represent missing data - The grey bar on the right shows overall missingness
Income variable (Figure 1): - Missing income values (red) appear scattered across the income distribution - There appear to be around 5–8 missing values concentrated around the middle income range (€30,000–50,000) - The overall extent of missingness appears moderate (~5–10% of the sample)
Age variable (Figure 2): - Missing age values (red) appear concentrated among older individuals (ages 40–60) - This pattern suggests the missingness is not random — older people are more likely to have missing ages - The extent is smaller than for income but potentially more problematic


12.1.2 Part (b): Is there a missing data pattern?
Income: The red bars (missing) appear across multiple income levels without a clear pattern → could be MCAR or MAR
Age: The red bars cluster at higher age values → suggests MAR at minimum, possibly MNAR

📌 Interpretation tip: When red bars are evenly spread across the x-axis, think MCAR. When they cluster at one end, think MAR or MNAR. When the variable being plotted itself seems to predict its own missingness, think MNAR.

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
12.2 Exercise 2 – Cholesterol Dataset EDA

12.2.1 Setting Up in R
# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(VIM)       # For missing data visualisation
library(GGally)    # For scatterplot matrix

# Load the data - must be a data.frame (not tibble) for VIM
cholesterol <- read.csv("cholesterol.csv")  # read.csv gives a data.frame automatically

# If you loaded with read_csv() (tibble), convert like this:
# cholesterol <- as.data.frame(cholesterol)

# Check the structure
str(cholesterol)
head(cholesterol)


12.2.2 Part (a): Summary Table
# Base R summary
summary(cholesterol)

# Or a nicer table
library(skimr)
skim(cholesterol)
How to interpret summary output: - For continuous variables: check the min, max, mean, median — if mean ≠ median, the data may be skewed - For categorical variables: check the frequency counts - Look for NA counts — these are your missing values
Example interpretation: “The dataset contains [n] observations and 7 variables. The mean age is [X] years (SD = [Y]). Cholesterol ranges from [min] to [max] mmol/L. There are [n] missing values in total.”


12.2.3 Part (b): Variable Distributions
# Histograms for continuous variables
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))  # 2x2 grid of plots
hist(cholesterol$Age, main = "Age", xlab = "Age (years)", col = "steelblue")
hist(cholesterol$Cholesterol, main = "Cholesterol", xlab = "Cholesterol (mg/dL)", col = "steelblue")
hist(cholesterol$SystolicBP, main = "Systolic BP", xlab = "mmHg", col = "steelblue")
hist(cholesterol$BMI, main = "BMI", xlab = "kg/m²", col = "steelblue")

# Boxplots are great too - they show outliers AND summary stats in one plot
par(mfrow = c(1, 4))
boxplot(cholesterol$Age, main = "Age")
boxplot(cholesterol$Cholesterol, main = "Cholesterol")
boxplot(cholesterol$SystolicBP, main = "Systolic BP")
boxplot(cholesterol$BMI, main = "BMI")

# Bar charts for categorical variables
barplot(table(cholesterol$Gender), main = "Gender", col = c("salmon", "steelblue"))
barplot(table(cholesterol$Smoking), main = "Smoking Status", col = c("grey", "tomato"))
barplot(table(cholesterol$Education), main = "Education", col = "lightgreen")
How to interpret a histogram: - Shape: Is it symmetric (bell-shaped), right-skewed (tail to the right), or left-skewed (tail to the left)? - Centre: Where is the peak? - Spread: How wide is it? - Unusual features: Any gaps? More than one peak (bimodal)?
How to interpret a boxplot: - The box spans from Q1 (25th percentile) to Q3 (75th percentile) — this is the IQR - The line inside the box is the median - The whiskers extend to the most extreme non-outlier values - Dots beyond the whiskers = potential outliers


12.2.4 Part (c): Scatterplots
# Pairwise scatterplot matrix for continuous variables
continuous_vars <- cholesterol[, c("Age", "Cholesterol", "SystolicBP", "BMI")]
pairs(continuous_vars, pch = 19, col = "steelblue")

# Or a prettier version with correlations
library(GGally)
ggpairs(continuous_vars)


12.2.5 Part (d): Interpreting Relationships Between Continuous Variables
How to describe a scatterplot — use this template:
“There appears to be a [positive/negative/no] [strong/moderate/weak] relationship between [X] and [Y]. The relationship appears [linear/curved/unclear].”
Remember: - You can only say direction and strength from the correlation coefficient - You need the scatterplot to judge linearity
Possible findings (to fill in with actual results): - Age and Systolic BP often show a positive relationship (BP tends to rise with age) - BMI and Cholesterol may show a weak positive association - Check the actual correlation matrix and scatterplots for the specific values
# Calculate correlation matrix (only for complete cases)
cor(continuous_vars, use = "complete.obs")


12.2.6 Part (e): Missing Data Analysis
# Quick check of missing values
colSums(is.na(cholesterol))

# Proportion missing per variable
colMeans(is.na(cholesterol)) * 100

# Visualise with VIM package
aggr(cholesterol, col = c("skyblue", "red"),
     numbers = TRUE, sortVars = TRUE,
     labels = names(cholesterol), cex.axis = 0.7,
     gap = 3, ylab = c("Missing Data", "Pattern"))
How to interpret the VIM aggr plot: - The left plot shows the proportion missing for each variable (red = missing) - The right plot shows the missing data patterns — each row is a pattern, each column is a variable - A blue cell means observed, red means missing - Look for rows where multiple variables are missing together — that suggests a pattern
Template interpretation: “The variable [X] has the highest proportion of missing data at [%]. [Variable Y] and [Variable Z] tend to be missing together, suggesting a systematic pattern. The missingness in [Variable A] does not appear to be related to the observed values of other variables, which is consistent with MCAR.”


12.2.7 Part (f): Outlier Detection
# Boxplot method - the dots beyond whiskers are potential outliers
par(mfrow = c(1, 4))
boxplot(cholesterol$Age, main = "Age", col = "skyblue")
boxplot(cholesterol$Cholesterol, main = "Cholesterol", col = "skyblue")
boxplot(cholesterol$SystolicBP, main = "Systolic BP", col = "skyblue")
boxplot(cholesterol$BMI, main = "BMI", col = "skyblue")

# Identify the values
boxplot.stats(cholesterol$Cholesterol)$out  # Shows the actual outlier values
How to report outliers: “The boxplot for Cholesterol reveals [n] potential outliers with values above [X] mg/dL. These should be investigated further to determine whether they are data entry errors, extraordinary observations, or legitimate extreme values.”

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
13.1 Correlation Coefficient (Pearson’s r)
\[r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}\]
Where: - \(x_i\), \(y_i\) = individual observations - \(\bar{x}\), \(\bar{y}\) = means of X and Y - \(n\) = number of observations
Range: \(-1 \leq r \leq +1\)

title: Week 1 – Data Analysis Protocol
text:
13.2 Mean Imputation
\[\hat{x}_{missing} = \bar{x}_{observed} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}\]

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text: 📌 How to use these notes: Read the concept explanations first, then work through each exercise with the solution. Spaces marked [Your notes here] are for you to add your own annotations later.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.1 Population vs. Sample
Imagine you want to know the average height of every person in Ireland. You can’t measure everyone — that would take forever! So instead, you measure a smaller group (a sample) and use it to make an educated guess about the whole country (the population).

Population: Everyone you care about (e.g., all Irish adults).
Sample: The smaller group you actually measure.
Parameter: A number describing the whole population (e.g., true average height μ). Usually unknown.
Statistic: A number calculated from your sample (e.g., sample mean x̄). This is your estimate of the parameter.


🔑 The goal of statistics is to use what we know (the sample) to make informed guesses about what we don’t know (the population).

For a sample to be trustworthy, it must be random — every person in the population must have an equal chance of being picked. A biased sample (e.g., only measuring athletes) would give a misleading answer.
[Your notes here — can you think of an example where a biased sample could mislead you?]

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.2 Point Estimates and Sampling Error
When you take a sample and compute the mean, you get a point estimate — a single number that is your best guess for the true population mean.
But there’s always a gap between your estimate and the truth. This gap is called sampling error. It’s not a mistake — it’s just the natural variability that comes from looking at a subset instead of everything.
Ways to reduce sampling error:

Increase the sample size.
Use proper random sampling methods (e.g., stratified sampling).
Quantify the error using the Standard Error of the Mean (SEM).

\[\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
Where \(\sigma\) is the population standard deviation and \(n\) is the sample size. A larger n gives a smaller SEM, meaning your estimate is more precise.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.3 The Sampling Distribution
If you repeated your study 10,000 times (each time taking a new random sample), you’d get 10,000 slightly different sample means. The distribution of all these means is called the sampling distribution.
Think of it like this: flip a coin 10 times and count heads. You’d probably get something between 3 and 7. Do this 10,000 times — the distribution of those counts is the sampling distribution.
Key properties of the sampling distribution of the mean (\(\bar{X}\)):
