Its centre equals the true population mean: \(E[\bar{X}] = \mu\)
Its spread (standard deviation) is the SEM: \(SD(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)
If the population is Normal, the sampling distribution is also Normal

\[\text{If } X \sim N(\mu, \sigma), \text{ then } \bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\]

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.4 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
This is one of the most powerful ideas in all of statistics.

The CLT says: Even if your original data does NOT follow a normal distribution, the distribution of sample means WILL be approximately normal — as long as your sample size is large enough (n ≥ 30).

Analogy: Imagine rolling a single die — the outcomes (1 to 6) are equally likely (a flat, uniform distribution). But if you roll 100 dice and average the results, that average will always be close to 3.5, and the distribution of averages will look like a bell curve. That’s the CLT in action.
This is demonstrated in Question 5 below.
[Your notes here — why is the CLT so important for hypothesis testing?]

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.5 Confidence Intervals
Instead of saying “I think the average waiting time is exactly 5.5 hours” (a point estimate), a confidence interval says:

“I’m 95% confident the true average waiting time is between 5.1 and 5.9 hours.”

95% CI formula (when σ is known):
\[\bar{X} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{X} \pm 1.96 \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
Where 1.96 is the critical value for 95% confidence.



Confidence Level
Z critical value




90%
1.645


95%
1.96


99%
2.576




📌 A wider confidence interval means more uncertainty. A narrower CI means more precision (usually from a larger sample).

The confidence interval and the hypothesis test are closely linked — if the CI does not contain the null value (e.g., μ₀), you reject H₀.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 2.6 Hypothesis Testing — Making Decisions from Data
text:
2.6 Hypothesis Testing — Making Decisions from Data
Hypothesis testing is like a courtroom: you assume innocence (the null hypothesis) until the evidence proves otherwise.
The setup:

H₀ (Null Hypothesis): The “no effect / no difference” statement. Always stated using =. Example: μ = 6 hours
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis): What you suspect is actually true. Example: μ ≠ 6 hours (two-tailed) or μ < 6 (one-tailed)

The process:

State H₀ and H₁.
Choose a significance level α (usually 0.05).
Calculate a test statistic from your data.
Find the p-value.
If p ≤ α → reject H₀ (the result is statistically significant).
If p > α → fail to reject H₀ (not enough evidence).

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.7 The p-value
The p-value answers this question:

“If the null hypothesis were true, how likely is it that I’d get data at least this extreme by chance alone?”


Small p-value (e.g., p = 0.001): Very unlikely to see this result if H₀ is true → strong evidence against H₀ → reject H₀.
Large p-value (e.g., p = 0.4): The data is consistent with H₀ → not enough evidence to reject.


⚠️ A p-value does NOT tell you the probability that H₀ is true. It tells you how surprising your data is under the assumption that H₀ is true.

[Your notes here — common misconception: p = 0.05 does NOT mean there’s a 5% chance H₀ is true.]

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 2.8 Type I and Type II Errors
text:
2.8 Type I and Type II Errors
When we make a decision based on sample data, we can be wrong in two ways:








Decision / Reality
H₀ is True
H₀ is False




Reject H₀
❌ Type I Error (false positive)
✅ Correct (Power)


Fail to reject H₀
✅ Correct
❌ Type II Error (false negative)




Type I Error (α): You conclude there IS an effect when there isn’t one. You false-alarm. Controlled by your choice of significance level α.
Type II Error (β): You conclude there is NO effect when there actually is one. You miss a real signal.


🎯 Think of a medical test: A Type I error means diagnosing a healthy person as sick. A Type II error means telling a sick person they’re healthy. Both are bad — just in different ways.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.9 Power
Power = the probability that your test correctly detects a real effect.
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\]
Where β is the probability of a Type II error.

Power of 0.80 means: if there really is an effect, your test will detect it 80% of the time.
Higher power is always better.
Power increases with: larger sample size, larger effect size, higher α (but this also increases Type I error risk).


📌 α and β are a trade-off. If you make it harder to reject H₀ (lower α), you increase the chance of missing a real effect (higher β, lower power). Choosing sample size before the study is how you balance both.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 2.10 Effect Size and Statistical vs. Practical Significance
text:
2.10 Effect Size and Statistical vs. Practical Significance
Just because a result is statistically significant (p < 0.05) doesn’t mean it’s important in real life.
Example: With a huge sample (n = 100,000), you might find that a new drug reduces blood pressure by 0.2 mmHg and get p < 0.001. But 0.2 mmHg is clinically meaningless — no doctor would prescribe a drug for that!

Statistical significance = “We’re confident the effect isn’t zero.”
Clinical/Practical significance = “The effect is large enough to matter.”

Cohen’s d is a standardised measure of effect size for t-tests:
\[d = \frac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}}\]
Rule of thumb: d = 0.2 (small), d = 0.5 (medium), d = 0.8 (large).
For linear regression, R² measures the proportion of variation in the response explained by the predictor.

📌 Always report both the p-value AND a measure of effect size (or a confidence interval). The CI shows the smallest and largest plausible effects — giving context to practical importance.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
2.11 How to Interpret Histograms / Distributions
When looking at a histogram of data, ask yourself:

Shape: Is it symmetric (bell-shaped = normal), skewed left, skewed right, or uniform (flat)?
Centre: Where is the peak? This is approximately the mean.
Spread: How wide is the distribution? Wider = more variability.
Outliers: Are there bars far from the main group?

For sampling distributions specifically:

As sample size n increases → the distribution becomes narrower and more bell-shaped (this IS the CLT).
The centre stays at the same mean (μ = 3.5 for a die).
The spread decreases as \(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\) — doubling n reduces the SEM by \(\sqrt{2}\).

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.1 Q1 — What exactly does a p-value tell you?
text:
3.1 Q1 — What exactly does a p-value tell you?
The answer:
The p-value gives you the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one you calculated from your data, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
In plain English: If H₀ were really true (e.g., the drug has no effect), how often would we see data as unusual as what we observed, just by random chance? A small p-value (e.g., 0.01) means: “This result would be very surprising if H₀ were true — so maybe H₀ isn’t true.”

⚠️ What the p-value is NOT: It is NOT the probability that H₀ is true.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.2 Q2 — What is a Type II error?
text:
3.2 Q2 — What is a Type II error?
The answer:
A Type II error occurs when the null hypothesis is false, but we fail to reject it — a false negative. We miss a real effect.
Real-world example: A doctor concludes a drug doesn’t work (fails to reject H₀: “no effect”), but the drug actually DOES work. The trial missed the real effect, perhaps because the sample was too small.
The probability of committing a Type II error is denoted β.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.3 Q3 — As sample size increases, does the probability of a Type II error increase or decrease?
text:
3.3 Q3 — As sample size increases, does the probability of a Type II error increase or decrease?
The answer: It decreases.
Why? A larger sample gives you a more precise estimate (smaller SEM), which makes it easier to detect small deviations from H₀. In other words, your test becomes more powerful (Power = 1 - β). With more data, you’re less likely to miss a real effect.
Think of it like listening for a quiet sound:

Small n = lots of background noise → you might miss the signal (Type II error).
Large n = less noise → you can hear even faint signals clearly.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.4 Q4 — Define bias of an estimator
text:
3.4 Q4 — Define bias of an estimator
The answer:
The bias of an estimator is the difference between the estimator’s expected value and the true value of the parameter being estimated:
\[\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\]
An estimator is unbiased if its expected value equals the true parameter:
\[E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta \implies \text{Bias} = 0\]
Example: The sample mean \(\bar{X}\) is an unbiased estimator of the population mean μ, because on average, across all possible samples, \(\bar{X}\) will equal μ.
Analogy: Imagine you’re throwing darts. An unbiased dart thrower hits the bullseye on average (some throws go left, some right, but they’re centred). A biased dart thrower consistently misses to one side, no matter how many throws they take.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.5 Q5 — Central Limit Theorem in Action (R Simulation)
text:
3.5 Q5 — Central Limit Theorem in Action (R Simulation)
Concept:
We simulate rolling a die to show the CLT in action. A single die follows a uniform distribution — each value (1–6) is equally likely. But as we average more and more dice rolls, the distribution of those averages becomes bell-shaped.
Expected value of one die roll:
\[E[X] = \sum x_i P(X = x_i) = 1 \times \frac{1}{6} + 2 \times \frac{1}{6} + \cdots + 6 \times \frac{1}{6} = 3.5\]
R Code:
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

n_samples <- 10000   # Number of repeated samples

# Sample sizes to demonstrate CLT
n <- c(1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 1000)

# Create matrix: 10,000 rows, one column per sample size
X <- matrix(0, n_samples, length(n))

par(mfrow = c(3, 2))  # 3 rows, 2 columns of plots

for (i in n) {
  j <- which(n == i)
  
  for (k in 1:n_samples) {
    X[k, j] <- mean(as.integer(runif(i, 1, 7)))
  }
  
  hist(X[, j],
       main  = paste("Distribution of the mean of", i, "rolls"),
       xlab  = "Sample Mean",
       freq  = FALSE,
       xlim  = c(0.5, 6.5),
       col   = "steelblue",
       border = "white")
}
How to interpret the histograms:








Sample size (n)
Shape of histogram
What this shows




n = 1
Flat / uniform
One die roll — all values equally likely


n = 2
Slightly triangular
Averages of 2 rolls — extreme values rarer


n = 5
Starting to look bell-shaped
CLT beginning to kick in


n = 10
More clearly bell-shaped
Normal approximation improving


n = 100
Tall, narrow bell curve
Very close to normal, small spread


n = 1000
Extremely narrow spike at 3.5
Near-perfect precision around the true mean



Key takeaway: As n increases, the distribution of sample means:

Becomes more symmetric and bell-shaped (approaches Normal)
Centres on 3.5 (the true population mean — stays the same)
Gets narrower — the SEM = \(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\) shrinks as n grows

This is the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.6 Q6 — Order Processing Times (Power Analysis by Hand)
text:
3.6 Q6 — Order Processing Times (Power Analysis by Hand)
Problem: A company targets a mean order processing time of 6 hours. A sample of 50 customers showed a mean of 5.2 hours with SD = 1.35 hours.


3.6.1 Part a — State the hypotheses
Let μ = the true mean population waiting time.
\[H_0: \mu = 6 \text{ hours}\] \[H_A: \mu \neq 6 \text{ hours}\]
This is a two-tailed test (we’re testing whether the mean differs from 6, not just whether it’s above or below).



3.6.2 Part b — Power Analysis by Hand
Goal: Find the power to detect a difference of 0.5 hours from the null (so we’re interested in detecting μ = 6.5 hours).
Step 1: Find the critical x̄ values that would lead to rejection of H₀.
For a two-tailed test at α = 0.05, the critical Z value is Z = ±1.96.
The upper critical sample mean:
\[\bar{X}_{\text{crit}} = \mu_0 + Z_{\text{crit}} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 6 + 1.96 \times \frac{1.35}{\sqrt{50}} = 6 + 0.374 = 6.374 \text{ hrs}\]
So we reject H₀ if \(\bar{X} > 6.374\) or \(\bar{X} < 5.626\).
Step 2: Calculate β (probability of failing to detect μ = 6.5).
Under H_A, the true mean is μ_A = 6.5. We fail to reject H₀ if \(\bar{X} < 6.374\).
\[\beta = P(\bar{X} < 6.374 \mid \mu = 6.5)\]
\[= P\!\left(Z < \frac{6.374 - 6.5}{\frac{1.35}{\sqrt{50}}}\right) = P(Z < -0.66) = 1 - P(Z < 0.66) = 1 - 0.7454 = 0.2546\]
Step 3: Power = 1 - β
\[\text{Power} = 1 - 0.2546 = \mathbf{0.7454 \approx 74.5\%}\]
In plain English: With this sample of 50 people, the test has a 74.5% chance of detecting a shift of 0.5 hours from the target. That means there’s still a 25.5% chance of missing a real difference of this size.
Diagram interpretation: The diagram (Part c) shows two overlapping bell curves:

The left curve is centred on 6 (H₀’s world).
The right curve is centred on 6.5 (H_A’s world).
The α region is in the tails of the H₀ curve (where we’d reject H₀).
The β region is the part of the H_A curve that falls inside the non-rejection region — the area we’d miss.
Power is the remaining area of the H_A curve (to the right of x̄_crit = 6.374).




3.6.3 Part d — Minimum Sample Size for 90% Power (R)
library(asbio)

# To detect a difference of 15 minutes = 0.25 hours
# with 90% power and α = 0.05 (two-tailed)
power.z.test(
  sigma  = 1.35,
  power  = 0.9,
  alpha  = 0.05,
  effect = 0.25,          # 15 minutes = 0.25 hours
  test   = "two.tail",
  strict = FALSE
)
# Answer: n = 307
Result: You would need at least n = 307 customers in your sample to have a 90% chance of detecting a true difference of 15 minutes from the 6-hour target.
Why so many? 15 minutes (0.25 hrs) is a small effect relative to the variability (SD = 1.35 hrs). Detecting subtle signals in noisy data requires more observations.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
3.7 Q7 — Supplier Complaints (Two-Sample Power Analysis)
Problem: Compare mean daily complaints between Supplier A (mean = 41.55) and Supplier B (mean = 38.76) over 20 days each. Pooled SD = 6.2.
Key concept: Here we have two independent groups, so we use a two-sample t-test. The effect size is the standardised mean difference:
\[d = \frac{\text{difference in means}}{s_{\text{pooled}}} = \frac{5}{6.2} = 0.806\]
This is a large effect by Cohen’s conventions (d > 0.8).


3.7.1 Part a — Power at α = 0.05
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(
  n           = 20,          # observations per group
  d           = 5 / 6.2,    # Cohen's d
  sig.level   = 0.05,
  type        = "two.sample",
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
# Result: Power ≈ 0.80 (80%)
Interpretation: With 20 days of data per site and a significance level of 5%, the test has approximately 80% power to detect a difference of 5 complaints between the suppliers.



3.7.2 Part b — Power at α = 0.01
pwr.t.test(
  n           = 20,
  d           = 5 / 6.2,
  sig.level   = 0.01,       # stricter threshold
  type        = "two.sample",
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
# Result: Power ≈ 0.57 (57%)
Interpretation: By making the test stricter (α = 0.01 instead of 0.05), the power drops to about 57%. This illustrates the α–β tradeoff: demanding stronger evidence before rejecting H₀ means you’ll miss more real effects.



3.7.3 Part c — Minimum Sample Size for 90% Power at α = 0.05
pwr.t.test(
  power       = 0.9,
  d           = 5 / 6.2,
  sig.level   = 0.05,
  type        = "two.sample",
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
# Result: n ≈ 34 per group (68 total)
Interpretation: You’d need at least 34 days of observations at each site (68 total) to have a 90% chance of detecting a true 5-complaint difference between suppliers.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
text:
3.8 Q8 — HbA1c Clinical Trial (One-Sample, One-Tailed)
Problem: A researcher wants to test if an intervention reduces HbA1c levels in diabetic patients. Known population mean without intervention: μ₀ = 8.7%, SD = 2.2%. The intervention is worthwhile if it reduces levels by 1%.


3.8.1 Part a — Hypotheses
Let μ = mean HbA1c with the intervention.
\[H_0: \mu = 8.7\%\] \[H_A: \mu < 8.7\%\]
This is a one-tailed (lower) test — we only care if the intervention reduces HbA1c, not increases it.



3.8.2 Part b — Type I and Type II Errors in Context

Type I Error (α): Concluding the intervention does reduce HbA1c when it actually does not → patients receive an ineffective treatment.
Type II Error (β): Concluding the intervention does not reduce HbA1c when it actually does → a beneficial treatment is abandoned.




3.8.3 Part c — Minimum Sample Size for 80% Power
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(
  power       = 0.8,
  d           = 1 / 2.2,         # effect size = reduction / SD
  sig.level   = 0.05,
  type        = "one.sample",
  alternative = "less"            # one-tailed, looking for reduction
)
# Result: n ≈ 40 patients
Interpretation: You need at least 40 patients in the trial to have an 80% chance of detecting a clinically meaningful 1% reduction in HbA1c.



3.8.4 Part d — Power with n = 50 Patients
By hand:
For a one-tailed test at α = 0.05, Z_crit = 1.645.
The minimum sample mean that would lead to rejection of H₀:
\[\bar{X}_{\min} = \mu_0 - Z_{\text{crit}} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 8.7 - 1.645 \times \frac{2.2}{\sqrt{50}} = 8.7 - 0.512 = 8.188\]
Under H_A, the true mean is μ_A = 7.7 (a 1% reduction from 8.7).
\[P(\bar{X} < 8.19 \mid \mu = 7.7) = P\!\left(Z < \frac{8.19 - 7.7}{\frac{2.2}{\sqrt{50}}}\right) = P(Z < 1.57) = 0.9418\]
Power ≈ 94%
Interpretation: With 50 patients, the trial has a 94% chance of detecting a 1% reduction in HbA1c. This is very good power. The larger sample (50 vs. the minimum of 40) gives extra confidence.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.9 Q9 — Battery Life Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
text:
3.9 Q9 — Battery Life Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
Problem: SD = 2.5 hrs, 95% CI, margin of error ± 0.5 hrs. How many phones to test?
Formula for required sample size:
\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{\text{MoE}}\right)^2\]
Solution:
\[n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 2.5}{0.5}\right)^2 = (9.8)^2 = 96.04\]
Always round UP (never down, as rounding down would give a margin of error that’s too large):
\[n = \mathbf{97} \text{ smartphones}\]
In plain English: To be 95% confident that your estimated average battery life is within 30 minutes of the truth, you need to test at least 97 phones.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.10 Q10 — Sugar Content Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
text:
3.10 Q10 — Sugar Content Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
Problem: SD = 4g, 99% CI, margin of error ± 1g.
Solution:
For 99% CI, Z = 2.576 (often rounded to 2.58).
\[n = \left(\frac{2.58 \times 4}{1}\right)^2 = (10.32)^2 = 106.5\]
Round up: \(n = \mathbf{107}\) samples.
In plain English: The inspector must test 107 cans to estimate average sugar content within ±1g with 99% confidence. The stricter confidence level (99% vs 95%) requires a larger sample than the battery life example despite a similar setup.

title: STAT9004 – Week 2 Study Guide
section: 3.11 Q11 — Airline Flight Delays Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
text:
3.11 Q11 — Airline Flight Delays Sample Size (Confidence Interval)
Problem: SD = 22 mins, 99% CI, margin of error ± 5 mins.
Solution:
\[n = \left(\frac{2.58 \times 22}{5}\right)^2 = (11.352)^2 = 128.87\]
Round up: \(n = \mathbf{129}\) flights.
In plain English: Sample 129 flights to estimate mean delay time within ± 5 minutes with 99% confidence. The relatively large standard deviation (22 mins) — indicating highly variable delays — is why so many flights are needed.

title: Home
text:
Data Analytics — Semester Notes
  A living reference for lecture content, lab work, and exam prep. Browse by module in the sidebar, or follow the guide below to run this site locally or push updates to GitHub Pages.




  
    ⛏️
    Data Mining
    Weeks 1–8
  
  
    📈
    Regression Analysis
    Weeks 1–9
  
  
    🕒
    Time Series
    Weeks 1–2
  


What you'll find here:

  Weekly lecture notes
  Lab practice & R examples
  Revision summaries
  Exam preparation notes






  Running this site locally
  Ideal when you want to preview changes before publishing — works offline too.

  
    1
    
      Open your terminal and navigate to the project folder. If you cloned the repo, it'll be wherever you saved it.
      cd data-analytics-notes
    
  

  
    2
    
      Start the Quarto preview server. This builds the site and opens it in your browser automatically.
      quarto preview
      The site will live-reload whenever you save a .qmd file — no need to restart.
    
  

  
    3
    
      Open your browser and go to the address shown in the terminal — usually:
      http://localhost:4321
    
  

  
    ✓ Tip: Press Ctrl + C in the terminal when you're done to stop the preview server.
  






  Publishing to GitHub Pages
  Quarto has a built-in command for this — no manual file copying needed.

  
    1
    
      Make sure GitHub Pages is set up. In your repo on GitHub, go to Settings → Pages and set the source to the gh-pages branch. You only need to do this once.
    
  

  
    2
    
      Commit your changes to the main branch as normal. Quarto publishes separately from your source files.
      git add .
      git commit -m "Update week 3 notes"
      git push
    
  

  
    3
    
      Publish with Quarto. This builds the site and pushes the rendered output to your gh-pages branch in one go.
      quarto publish gh-pages
      The first time you run this, Quarto may ask you to confirm the publishing destination — just hit Enter.
    
  

  
    4
    
      Wait ~30–60 seconds, then visit your site. GitHub Pages takes a moment to deploy the new build.
      https://lgolin.github.io/data-analytics-notes/
    
  

  
    ⚠ Note: Don't edit the gh-pages branch directly — it's managed by Quarto. Always make edits in your source files and re-run quarto publish gh-pages.

title: Quiz Preparation
text: 🎯 What to expect: ~25 MCQ/short answer questions in ~45 minutes. Your professor said “all the answers are in the lecture notes” and the exam can be done in 20–25 minutes if you know the material. This file drills every concept that could appear.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
1.1 Concept Flash Cards
Read the question. Think of your answer. Then reveal with the toggle.

Q: What is a time series?






TipAnswer





A collection of observations recorded at regular, equally-spaced intervals over time. The equally-spaced condition is critical — if data is not equally spaced, it cannot be analysed as a time series.




Q: What are the four components of a time series?






TipAnswer






Trend (T) — Long-term increase or decrease in the data
Seasonal (S) — Repeating pattern that completes within one calendar year
Cyclical (C) — Long irregular waves lasting more than 2 years (e.g., economic cycles)
Random / Residual (R) — Unexplained noise left after removing other components





Q: What is the difference between a seasonal and a cyclical component?






TipAnswer














Seasonal
Cyclical




Period
Completes within 1 year
Lasts more than 2 years


Pattern
Regular and predictable
Irregular, harder to predict


Example
Christmas sales spike every December
Economic recession every ~10 years







Q: When do you use an additive vs. multiplicative decomposition model?






TipAnswer






Additive → when the seasonal swings are constant in size over time (homogeneous seasonality). Formula: \(y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative → when the seasonal swings grow larger as the trend increases (increasing variance). Formula: \(y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t\)

Visual test: Look at the plot. If the up-and-down swings get bigger over time → multiplicative.




Q: How can you convert a multiplicative model into an additive one?






TipAnswer





Take the logarithm of the data: \[\log(y_t) = \log(T_t) + \log(S_t) + \log(R_t)\] This stabilises the variance and turns multiplication into addition.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
1.2 MCQ Practice — Block 1
1. A time series of monthly hotel bookings shows larger seasonal spikes every summer as the years go by. Which model is most appropriate?


Additive decomposition



Multiplicative decomposition ✅



ARIMA(1,0,0)



Simple Exponential Smoothing



2. Which of the following is NOT a requirement for a dataset to be treated as a time series?


Observations must be collected over time



Observations must be equally spaced ✅ (actually this IS required — trick!)



There must be at least one seasonal component ✅



The variable must be numerical



💡 Trick question: a time series does NOT need to have seasonality. It only needs to be equally spaced. The kings-of-England dataset was NOT a valid time series because kings don’t die at regular intervals.


3. After removing the trend and seasonal components from a time series, the leftover is called:


The cyclical component



The random / residual component ✅



The baseline



The white noise process

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
2.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: What does “pure time series model” mean?






TipAnswer





A model that uses only the historical values of the variable itself to make forecasts — no external variables. Exponential smoothing methods are pure time series models. This makes them useful when you only have data for one variable.




Q: What does the smoothing parameter α (alpha) control in SES, and what happens at extreme values?






TipAnswer





α controls how much weight is given to recent vs. old observations:








α value
Memory
Effect




α → 1
Short memory
Only the last observation matters (forecast = last value)


α → 0
Long memory
All past values weighted equally (forecast = overall mean)


α = 0.83 (example)
Short memory
Most weight on recent values



R minimises the sum of squared errors to find the optimal α automatically.




Q: Name the three exponential smoothing methods and when to use each one.






TipAnswer













Method
ETS Code
Use when…




Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
ETS(A,N,N)
No trend, no seasonality


Holt’s (Double)
ETS(A,A,N)
Has trend, no seasonality


Holt-Winters (Triple)
ETS(A,A,A) or ETS(A,A,M)
Has trend AND seasonality



Additive Holt-Winters: seasonal effect is constant size → ETS(A,A,A)
Multiplicative Holt-Winters: seasonal effect grows → ETS(A,A,M)




Q: What does ETS stand for and what does each letter mean?






TipAnswer





Error · Trend · Seasonality
Each component can be: - N = None - A = Additive
- M = Multiplicative
- Ad = Additive damped (trend slows down over time)
Examples: ETS(A,N,N) = SES; ETS(A,A,A) = Holt-Winters Additive




Q: What is a “damped trend” and why might you prefer it?






TipAnswer





A damped trend is one that gradually flattens out as you project further into the future, rather than continuing at the same rate indefinitely. It is often more realistic because few trends continue forever at the same rate. In R: holt(data, damped = TRUE).




Q: What does the forecast look like for SES (no trend, no seasonality)?






TipAnswer





A flat horizontal line — all future forecast values are the same. This is because SES only estimates a level (weighted average of past), with no component to create upward/downward slope or seasonal waves. The confidence interval widens the further ahead you forecast.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
2.2 MCQ Practice — Block 2
4. You have monthly sales data with a clear upward trend but no seasonal pattern. Which method is most appropriate?


SES — Simple Exponential Smoothing



Holt’s method ✅



Holt-Winters Multiplicative



ARIMA(0,0,0)



5. In Holt-Winters, the parameter γ (gamma) controls:


The level



The trend



The seasonality ✅



The error variance



6. The ETS(A,N,N) model is equivalent to:


Holt’s method



Holt-Winters additive



Simple Exponential Smoothing ✅



ARIMA(1,1,0)



7. If α = 0.9 in a SES model, this means:


The model has a long memory and considers all past values equally



The model has a short memory and the last observation dominates the forecast ✅



The trend is strongly upward



The seasonal effect is 90% additive

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
3.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: What does STL stand for and what are its key advantages?






TipAnswer





Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (Loess = local regression)
Advantages over basic decompose(): - No missing values at the beginning and end of the series - Robust to outliers — extreme values don’t distort the components - User can control the flexibility of the seasonal and trend windows
Disadvantage: Only works directly with additive models (take logs for multiplicative).




Q: What is the s.window = "periodic" argument in the stl() function?






TipAnswer





It tells R to assume the seasonal component is fixed (repeats identically each year). You can also give a number (e.g., s.window = 10) to allow the seasonality to change slowly over time. Using "periodic" is the default safe choice.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
4.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: What do MAE, RMSE, and MAPE measure? Which penalises large errors more?






TipAnswer





Let \(e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t\) (actual minus forecast):








Metric
Formula
Property




MAE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum|e_t|\)
Average error size; treats all errors equally


RMSE
\(\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2}\)
Penalises large errors more (squaring amplifies big mistakes)


MAPE
\(\frac{100}{n}\sum|\frac{e_t}{y_t}|\)
Error as a %; good for comparing across different scales



RMSE penalises large errors most heavily.




Q: What is AIC and when is it used?






TipAnswer





Akaike Information Criterion — a measure of model quality that balances fit vs. complexity. It penalises models with more parameters (to prevent overfitting).
Rule: Lower AIC = better model. Used to compare models on the same dataset.
Also see: BIC (Bayesian IC) — similar but with a stronger complexity penalty.




Q: How do you split data for model evaluation in R?






TipAnswer





train <- window(my_ts, end = c(2020, 12))   # training set
test  <- window(my_ts, start = c(2021, 1))  # test set

model <- ets(train)
accuracy(forecast(model, h = length(test)), test)
Compare RMSE and MAE on the test set — this is the honest measure of how well the model generalises.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
4.2 MCQ Practice — Block 4
8. You compare two models. Model A: RMSE = 45, AIC = 1200. Model B: RMSE = 38, AIC = 1210. Which would you most likely choose for forecasting?


Model A — because AIC is lower



Model B — because RMSE is lower ✅



They are equivalent



Cannot decide without more information



💡 Your professor said: “These are just numbers. You can include extra information.” But between RMSE and AIC that are close, a large drop in RMSE is often preferred.


9. MAPE is particularly useful because:


It always gives the smallest error value



It expresses error as a percentage, making it comparable across datasets ✅



It penalises large errors more than small errors



It only works for additive models

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
5.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: What are the three conditions for a time series to be stationary?






TipAnswer






Constant mean — the average does not drift up or down over time
Constant variance — the spread of values stays the same throughout
Constant covariance — if there’s a correlation at lag k, it is the same throughout the series (not just at one point in time)

Memory trick: Same mean, same spread, same memory — always, everywhere in the series.




Q: Why does ARIMA require stationary data?






TipAnswer





The statistical equations in ARIMA assume the data’s behaviour is stable and consistent throughout. If the mean is drifting, the model cannot reliably estimate what “normal” is. If variance is changing, predictions become unreliable. You must transform the data to be stationary first.




Q: What is differencing and when do you use it?






TipAnswer





Differencing means computing the change from one period to the next: \[\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\]
Use it when your data has a non-constant mean (trend). The number of times you difference is the d in ARIMA(p,d,q).

d=1: removes a linear trend

d=2: removes a quadratic trend (rare)

For non-constant variance → use log transformation first.




Q: Explain ARIMA(p, d, q) — what does each letter mean?






TipAnswer













Letter
Name
Meaning




p
AutoRegressive order
How many past values influence today


d
Integrated order
How many times to difference the data


q
Moving Average order
How many past errors influence today



Example: ARIMA(1,1,1) = use last value + one differencing + last error.




Q: What is the difference between AR and MA components?






TipAnswer














AR (AutoRegressive)
MA (Moving Average)




Depends on
Past values of y
Past forecast errors


Analogy
“My commute today depends on my commute yesterday”
“I adjust today based on how wrong I was yesterday”


Parameter
φ (phi)
θ (theta)







Q: How do you use ACF and PACF to identify the model?






TipAnswer













ACF pattern
PACF pattern
Model




Cuts off after lag q
Decays slowly
MA(q)


Decays slowly
Cuts off after lag p
AR(p)


Both decay slowly
Both decay slowly
ARMA(p,q)


All within bounds
All within bounds
White noise (no model needed)



“Cuts off” = drops suddenly inside the blue dashed lines
“Decays slowly” = gradually decreases bar by bar




Q: What is white noise, and why is it important?






TipAnswer





White noise is a series with: - Mean = 0
- Constant variance
- No autocorrelation (each observation is independent)
Why it matters: Your model’s residuals should look like white noise. If they do, the model has captured all the structure. If they don’t, the model is missing something.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
5.2 MCQ Practice — Block 5
10. A time series has a clearly increasing trend. Before fitting an ARIMA model you should:


Apply Holt’s method first



Difference the series once to remove the trend ✅



Multiply all values by a constant



Remove the seasonal component only



11. In ARIMA(2, 1, 0), how many past values directly influence the current observation?


0



1



2 ✅



3



12. The PACF of a stationary series cuts off sharply after lag 3, while the ACF decays slowly. What model is suggested?


MA(3)



AR(3) ✅



ARIMA(0,1,3)



ARMA(3,3)



13. Which test is used to formally check if a time series is stationary?


Ljung-Box test



Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test ✅



Shapiro-Wilk test



F-test



💡 p-value < 0.05 in ADF → stationary (reject null of non-stationarity)


14. After fitting an ARIMA model, the Ljung-Box test returns p-value = 0.003. This means:


The residuals are white noise — the model is good



The residuals still contain structure — the model needs improvement ✅



The data was not stationary



The AIC is too high



💡 Ljung-Box: p > 0.05 = residuals are random (good); p < 0.05 = residuals have pattern (bad)

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
6.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: What four plots do you use to check model residuals?






TipAnswer












Plot
What you look for




Histogram
Symmetric bell shape (normal distribution)


Boxplot
Symmetric, median near zero, no extreme outliers


Q-Q plot
Points follow the diagonal line


Residuals vs. Time
Random scatter, no visible pattern or trend







Q: What does it mean if the Q-Q plot shows points curving off the line at both ends?






TipAnswer





It means the residuals have heavier tails than a normal distribution (more extreme values than expected). This is a sign that the model is not fully capturing all structure. You should note this in your report and potentially try a different model or transformation.




Q: What does standardising residuals mean and why do it?






TipAnswer





Standardising transforms residuals to have mean = 0 and standard deviation = 1: \[e_{std} = \frac{e_t - \bar{e}}{sd(e)}\]
Why: It lets you immediately see which values are unusual. Values beyond ±2 are potential outliers (outside 95% of a normal distribution). Values beyond ±3 are serious outliers.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
7.1 Concept Flash Cards
Q: How does SARIMA extend ARIMA?






TipAnswer





SARIMA adds a seasonal layer on top of ARIMA:
\[\text{SARIMA}(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_m\]

Lowercase (p,d,q): handle regular (non-seasonal) autocorrelation
Uppercase (P,D,Q): handle seasonal autocorrelation at period m
m: the season length (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly)

Example: SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)₁₂ for monthly data with annual seasonality.




Q: In auto.arima(), what does R do automatically?






TipAnswer





R automatically: 1. Tests for stationarity and chooses d 2. Searches over many combinations of p and q 3. Selects the model with the lowest AICc (corrected AIC) 4. Returns the best ARIMA or SARIMA model
It is a good starting point, but you should always check residuals and potentially compare manually specified alternatives.

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
7.2 MCQ Practice — Block 7
15. You have quarterly GDP data from 2000 to 2020 and want to fit a SARIMA model. What value of m would you use?


12



4 ✅



1



52



16. The auto.arima() function selects the best model based on:


RMSE on a test set



AICc (corrected Akaike Information Criterion) ✅



The Ljung-Box p-value



The number of observations

title: Quiz Preparation
text:
8.1 Things students often get wrong






Warning



Common mistake 1: Confusing seasonal and cyclical components.
→ If it repeats within a year and is predictable: seasonal.
→ If it takes years and is irregular: cyclical.








Warning



Common mistake 2: Using a multiplicative model on data with zero or near-zero values.
→ Multiplicative models fail when values approach zero (you can’t multiply by zero). Use additive or log-transform first.








Warning



Common mistake 3: Thinking a low AIC always means the best model.
→ AIC and RMSE together tell a fuller story. A slightly higher AIC but much lower RMSE might still be preferred, especially if residuals look better.








Warning



Common mistake 4: Forgetting to check stationarity before ARIMA.
→ Always plot the series and run adf.test() before fitting ARIMA. Never skip this step.








Warning



Common mistake 5: Interpreting the Ljung-Box test backwards.
→ p > 0.05 = residuals ARE random = good model ✅
→ p < 0.05 = residuals have structure = model needs work ❌
Code

title: Module Overview
section: 1.1 The core idea in one sentence
text:
1.1 The core idea in one sentence
Time series analysis is the art of listening to data over time, understanding its patterns, and using those patterns to predict the future.

title: Module Overview
section: 1.2 A real-world analogy to make it stick
text:
1.2 A real-world analogy to make it stick
Imagine you work at a coffee shop and every day you record how many coffees you sell. After a year, you notice:

Sales have been growing overall (more customers each month) → this is Trend
Sales spike every Monday morning and dip on Sundays → this is Seasonality
One week sales dropped because of a snowstorm → this is Random noise

Time series analysis is exactly this: you look at your coffee sales data, pull apart these different layers, understand what drives each one, and then use that understanding to say “next month we’ll probably sell around X coffees.”

title: Module Overview
section: 1.3 Why does it matter beyond this module?
text:
1.3 Why does it matter beyond this module?
This thinking transfers to any subject or job that involves data collected over time:



Field
Time Series application




Finance
Predicting stock prices, exchange rates


Healthcare
Tracking disease spread over weeks


Marketing
Forecasting monthly sales


Engineering
Monitoring machine performance


Economics
Modelling GDP, inflation, unemployment


Climate Science
Analysing temperature changes



The concepts you learn here — patterns, prediction, uncertainty — are universal.


✏️ Self-check: In your own words, what is a time series? Give one example from your own life. Time series is the way we can analyse data overtime, find its patterns to predict what is going to happen in the future.

title: Module Overview
text:
2.1 The recipe analogy
Think of a time series like a recipe. A cake is made of flour, sugar, butter, and eggs — but you only see the finished cake. Time series analysis is like reverse-engineering a recipe: you have the finished cake (your data), and you want to figure out how much of each ingredient went in.
The “ingredients” of any time series are:

2.1.1 Trend (T) — The long-term direction
What it is: The overall movement of your data upwards or downwards over a long period.
Example: Global average temperatures have been rising for 100 years. If you plot this, the long-term direction is upward — that upward slope is the trend.
Key insight: A trend doesn’t have to be a straight line. It can curve, slow down, or reverse.


2.1.2 Seasonality (S) — The repeating pattern
What it is: A regular, predictable pattern that repeats within a fixed period (usually within one year).
Example: Ice cream sales are always high in summer and low in winter — every single year, without fail. This is seasonal behaviour.
Key insight: Seasonality is predictable and regular. It completes itself within a calendar year.


2.1.3 Cyclical Component (C) — The long, irregular wave
What it is: Patterns that repeat over multiple years, but are not as regular as seasons. Usually linked to economic cycles.
Example: After the 2009 financial crisis, nearly every economic indicator dropped. This is a cyclical downturn — it lasts years, not months, and cannot be predicted with the same precision as seasons.
Key difference from Seasonality: Seasonality repeats within a year. Cyclical patterns take more than 2 years to complete and are not perfectly regular.


2.1.4 Random / Residual (R) — The unexplained noise
What it is: Everything left over after you remove trend, seasonality, and cyclical patterns. This is the unexplained, random variation.
Example: One particular Tuesday, your coffee shop sold 300 coffees instead of the usual 200. Maybe a conference was in town. You couldn’t have predicted it — that extra 100 is “random.”
Key insight: If your model is good, the residuals should look like pure random noise (no pattern left).

title: Module Overview
text:
2.2 The Decomposition Equation
Once you accept that data is made of these components, you can write a mathematical expression for any time series observation \(y_t\) at time \(t\):

2.2.1 Additive Model
Use this when the seasonal swings stay roughly the same size over time.
\[y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t\]
When to use: The ups and downs in your data look the same height throughout the series (constant variance).


2.2.2 Multiplicative Model
Use this when the seasonal swings get bigger as the trend grows.
\[y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t\]
When to use: The wiggling in your data gets wider over time (increasing variance).
Tip: You can convert a multiplicative model into an additive one by taking the logarithm of your data: \[\log(y_t) = \log(T_t) + \log(S_t) + \log(R_t)\]


✏️ Self-check: You’re looking at monthly electricity usage data. In summer and winter the spikes get bigger every year. Should you use an additive or multiplicative model? Why?  You should use the multiplicative model because the spikes get bigger every year. Different than the additive model that stays the same size.

title: Module Overview
text:
3.1 What decomposition does
Decomposition is the process of separating a time series into its components so you can study each one individually. Think of it like using a prism to split white light into individual colours.
In R, the main tools are:
# Load the forecast library (your main toolbox for this module)
library(forecast)

# Basic decomposition
plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "additive"))
plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "multiplicative"))

# Better decomposition: STL (Seasonal & Trend using Loess)
stl_result <- stl(my_timeseries, s.window = "periodic")
autoplot(stl_result)

title: Module Overview
text:
3.2 STL Decomposition — the preferred method
STL stands for Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (Loess is a type of local regression).
Why STL is better than basic decomposition:

It handles data at both ends of the series (no missing values at the start or end)
It is robust — extreme values (outliers) don’t distort the components
You can control how flexible the trend and seasonal components are

The trade-off: STL only works for additive models. For multiplicative data, take logs first, apply STL, then back-transform.


✏️ Self-check: What is the difference between decompose() and stl() in R? Which should you prefer and why? >  For decompose() you miss values at the start or the end, outliers distort the components. stl() only works for additive models.

title: Module Overview
section: 4.1 The core idea: recent data matters more
text:
4.1 The core idea: recent data matters more
Exponential smoothing is based on a simple but powerful idea: the most recent observations should have more influence on your forecast than older ones.
Imagine you’re estimating how long your commute will take tomorrow. Yesterday’s commute matters more than the commute from 6 months ago. But you don’t completely ignore the older data — you just weight it less.
That’s exactly what exponential smoothing does: it applies exponentially decreasing weights to past observations.

title: Module Overview
text:
4.2 The three exponential smoothing methods
Think of these as tools with increasing capability:

4.2.1 1. Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
Use when: Data has no trend and no seasonality — just a level that fluctuates randomly.
The key parameter — α (alpha):








Alpha value
Meaning
Practical effect




α close to 1
Short memory
Only the very last observation matters


α close to 0
Long memory
All past observations weighted equally


α = 0.83 (example)
Short memory
Forecast closely tracks last value



The formula: \[\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t\]
This says: “My next forecast = (alpha × what just happened) + (1-alpha × what I previously predicted)”
In R:
library(forecast)

# Method 1: specific SES function
ses_model <- ses(my_timeseries, h = 5)  # h = number of periods to forecast

# Method 2: using the ETS family
ses_model <- ets(my_timeseries, model = "ANN")
# ANN = Error(Additive), No trend, No seasonality
What the forecast looks like: A flat horizontal line — because with no trend or seasonality, the best guess for all future periods is the same value.



4.2.2 2. Holt’s Method (Double Exponential Smoothing)
Use when: Data has a trend but no seasonality.
Two parameters:

α (alpha): Controls the level (same as SES)
β (beta): Controls how quickly the trend estimate updates

In R:
holt_model <- holt(my_timeseries, h = 5)

# Damped trend (trend slows down over time — often more realistic)
holt_damped <- holt(my_timeseries, damped = TRUE, h = 5)
What the forecast looks like: A sloped line following the trend direction.



4.2.3 3. Holt-Winters Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing)
Use when: Data has both trend and seasonality — the most complete model.
Three parameters:

α (alpha): Level
β (beta): Trend
γ (gamma): Seasonality

Two versions:

Additive Holt-Winters: Seasonal effect is constant in size
Multiplicative Holt-Winters: Seasonal effect grows with the level

In R:
# Additive
hw_add <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "additive", h = 12)

# Multiplicative
hw_mult <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12)

# Let R choose automatically using ETS
auto_model <- ets(my_timeseries)
summary(auto_model)

title: Module Overview
section: 4.3 The ETS Framework — the big family
text:
4.3 The ETS Framework — the big family
All exponential smoothing methods belong to the ETS family:
ETS = Error · Trend · Seasonality
Each letter takes a value: - Error: A (additive) or M (multiplicative) - Trend: N (none), A (additive), Ad (additive damped), M (multiplicative) - Seasonality: N (none), A (additive), M (multiplicative)








Model name
ETS code
Use case




Simple Exp. Smoothing
ETS(A,N,N)
No trend, no seasonality


Holt’s Method
ETS(A,A,N)
Trend, no seasonality


Holt-Winters Additive
ETS(A,A,A)
Trend + additive seasonality


Holt-Winters Multiplicative
ETS(A,A,M)
Trend + multiplicative seasonality



# Let R automatically find the best ETS model
best_model <- ets(my_timeseries)
summary(best_model)
forecast(best_model, h = 12)


✏️ Self-check: You have monthly data showing sales with an upward trend and seasonal peaks at Christmas. Which ETS method would you choose? What would α, β, and γ control? I would choose Holt-Winters Multiplicative because you can see an increase in sales trand, and seasonal peaks every Christmas. The Beta would be the Trend, Gamma seasonality and I am not sure about alpha.

title: Module Overview
text:
5.1 Why this matters
Any model can give you a forecast. But is it a good forecast? You need numbers to measure how far off your predictions are from reality.
The standard approach:

Split your data into a training set (to build the model) and a test set (to check accuracy)
Forecast the test period using your model
Compare forecasts to actual values using error measures

title: Module Overview
text:
5.2 The main error measures
Let \(y_t\) = actual value and \(\hat{y}_t\) = forecasted value. The error is \(e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t\).








Measure
Formula
What it tells you




MAE (Mean Absolute Error)
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum |e_t|\)
Average size of errors, ignoring direction


RMSE (Root Mean Square Error)
\(\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2}\)
Penalises large errors more heavily


MAPE (Mean Absolute % Error)
\(\frac{100}{n}\sum \left|\frac{e_t}{y_t}\right|\)
Error as a percentage (good for comparison)


AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)
—
Penalises model complexity; lower = better


BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion)
—
Similar to AIC, stronger complexity penalty



The golden rule for model selection: When comparing two models, the one with the lower AIC/BIC and lower RMSE/MAE is preferred.
# Get accuracy metrics in R
accuracy(forecast_result, test_data)

# Compare AIC between two models
summary(model1)$aic
summary(model2)$aic


✏️ Self-check: You compare two models. Model A has AIC = 1200 and RMSE = 45. Model B has AIC = 1180 and RMSE = 52. Which would you choose and why? (Hint: there’s no single right answer — discuss the trade-off.)

title: Module Overview
text:
6.1 The shift in thinking
Everything up to now (exponential smoothing) was about finding patterns and extrapolating them. ARIMA is different — it’s based on statistical theory about how data relates to its own past values.
The key concept: autocorrelation — the idea that today’s value is correlated with yesterday’s value, last week’s value, etc.
Think of it like your mood. If you’re happy today, you’re probably somewhat happy tomorrow. Your mood is correlated with your own past mood. Data often behaves the same way.

title: Module Overview
text:
6.2 Stationarity — the crucial requirement
Before fitting an ARIMA model, your data must be stationary. This is perhaps the most important concept in the second half of the module.

6.2.1 What does stationary mean?
A time series is stationary if it has three properties that don’t change over time:

Constant mean — the average doesn’t drift up or down
Constant variance — the spread of the data stays roughly the same
Constant covariance — if observation at time 1 is correlated with observation at time 3 (lag 2), then this same correlation exists throughout the entire series

The visual test: If you cover different parts of your time series plot with your hand and each section looks basically the same — same level, same spread — it’s likely stationary.


6.2.2 Why does stationarity matter?
ARIMA models are built on statistical equations that only work when the data’s statistical properties are stable. If the mean is drifting, the model can’t figure out what “normal” is.


6.2.3 How to make data stationary
Problem: non-constant mean (trend) → Solution: Differencing
Differencing means computing the change from one period to the next: \[\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\]
If differencing once doesn’t work, you can do it twice (second-order differencing). The number of times you difference is the d in ARIMA(p, d, q).
Problem: non-constant variance → Solution: Log transformation
Taking the log of your data compresses large values and stabilises variance. This is the same reason multiplicative seasonal data responds to log transformation.
# Visual check: does the data look stationary?
plot(my_timeseries)

# Differencing in R
diff_series <- diff(my_timeseries)        # First difference
diff2_series <- diff(my_timeseries, differences = 2)  # Second difference

# Formal statistical test for stationarity (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)
library(tseries)
adf.test(my_timeseries)
# p-value < 0.05 → stationary
# p-value > 0.05 → NOT stationary, need differencing

title: Module Overview
text:
6.3 The ARIMA building blocks
ARIMA(p, d, q) is made of three parts:

6.3.1 AR(p) — AutoRegressive
The idea: Today’s value depends on its own past values.
\[y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \ldots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t\]
Analogy: “My commute today (y_t) is partly explained by my commute yesterday (y_{t-1}) and the day before (y_{t-2}).” The p tells you how many past values to include.


6.3.2 MA(q) — Moving Average
The idea: Today’s value depends on past forecast errors (not past values themselves).
\[y_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \ldots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}\]
Analogy: “When I was late yesterday, I leave earlier today to compensate — I’m reacting to past errors in my schedule.” The q tells you how many past errors to include.


6.3.3 I(d) — Integrated
The idea: The d stands for how many times you need to difference the data to make it stationary.

d = 0: already stationary
d = 1: one differencing needed (most common)
d = 2: two differences needed (rare)



6.3.4 Putting it together: ARIMA(p, d, q)
ARIMA(p, d, q)
       |  |  |
       |  |  └── q: how many past ERRORS influence today
       |  └───── d: how many times to DIFFERENCE the data
       └──────── p: how many past VALUES influence today
# Fit a specific ARIMA model
arima_model <- arima(my_timeseries, order = c(1, 1, 1))  # ARIMA(1,1,1)

# Let R automatically choose the best ARIMA model
library(forecast)
auto_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries)
summary(auto_model)
forecast(auto_model, h = 12)

title: Module Overview
section: 6.4 How to identify p and q: ACF and PACF
text:
6.4 How to identify p and q: ACF and PACF
The ACF (AutoCorrelation Function) and PACF (Partial AutoCorrelation Function) are visual tools that help you choose p and q.

6.4.1 ACF — AutoCorrelation Function
Shows the correlation between the series and its own past values at each lag.
Reading the ACF plot: - Each bar shows correlation at that lag - Dashed lines = significance boundaries (95% confidence) - Bars beyond the dashed lines = significant correlation at that lag


6.4.2 PACF — Partial AutoCorrelation Function
Shows the correlation with a specific lag after removing the influence of all shorter lags.


6.4.3 The identification rules (the patterns to memorise)








Pattern
Meaning
Model




ACF: slow decay; PACF: cuts off after lag p
Autoregressive
AR(p)


ACF: cuts off after lag q; PACF: slow decay
Moving average
MA(q)


Both decay slowly
Mixed
ARMA(p,q)



# Plot ACF and PACF
acf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30)   # AutoCorrelation Function
pacf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30)  # Partial AutoCorrelation Function


✏️ Self-check: What is the difference between ACF and PACF? If the PACF cuts off sharply after lag 2 and the ACF decays slowly, what model would you suggest?

title: Module Overview
text:
7.1 Extending ARIMA to handle seasonality
When your data has both regular autocorrelation AND seasonal patterns, you need SARIMA:
\[\text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_m\]
The lowercase letters (p, d, q) handle the regular (non-seasonal) part — exactly as in ARIMA.
The uppercase letters (P, D, Q) handle the seasonal part, repeating at period m (e.g., m = 12 for monthly data with annual seasonality).







Parameter
What it does




P
Seasonal AR — correlation with observations m periods ago


D
Seasonal differencing — remove seasonal trend


Q
Seasonal MA — correlation with seasonal errors


m
Season length (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly)



# Automatic SARIMA selection
library(forecast)
sarima_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries, seasonal = TRUE)
summary(sarima_model)

# Manual SARIMA specification
sarima_manual <- arima(my_timeseries, 
                        order = c(1,1,1),          # non-seasonal part
                        seasonal = list(order = c(1,1,1), period = 12))  # seasonal part

title: Module Overview
text:
8.1 Why check residuals?
After fitting any model, you must check the residuals (the leftovers). If the model has done its job, the residuals should contain no pattern — they should look like random white noise.
Think of it like this: after a good model has explained everything it can, the only thing left should be unexplainable randomness. If you can still see a pattern in the residuals, your model is missing something.

title: Module Overview
text:
8.2 The four residual checks
# Plot residuals in four ways
par(mfrow = c(2,2))

# 1. Histogram - should look like a bell curve (normal distribution)
hist(residuals(my_model))

# 2. Boxplot - should be symmetrical around zero
boxplot(residuals(my_model))

# 3. Q-Q Plot - points should follow the diagonal line
qqnorm(residuals(my_model))
qqline(residuals(my_model), col = "red")

# 4. Residuals over time - should look random, no pattern
plot(residuals(my_model))
abline(h = 0, col = "red")

8.2.1 What you’re looking for in each plot








Plot
Good sign
Bad sign




Histogram
Symmetric bell shape
Skewed, heavy tails


Boxplot
Symmetric, median near zero
Asymmetric, many outliers


Q-Q Plot
Points on the diagonal line
Points curve away from line


Time plot
Random scatter, no pattern
Visible trend or waves

title: Module Overview
text:
8.3 The Ljung-Box Test
A formal statistical test for whether residuals are truly random:
Box.test(residuals(my_model), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box")
# p-value > 0.05 → residuals are random (good!)
# p-value < 0.05 → residuals still have structure (bad — model needs improvement)


✏️ Self-check: You fit an ARIMA model and plot the residuals. The Q-Q plot shows points curving off the line at both ends. What does this tell you? Is your model acceptable?

title: Module Overview
section: 9.1 Your step-by-step process for any dataset
text:
9.1 Your step-by-step process for any dataset
This is the workflow you will use in your project and exams. Memorise this sequence:
Step 1: LOOK at the data
        → Plot the time series
        → What do you see? Trend? Seasonality? Growing variance?
        → Is it additive or multiplicative?

Step 2: DECOMPOSE the data
        → Use STL decomposition
        → Examine each component separately
        → Is the seasonal component stable?

Step 3: CHECK stationarity (for ARIMA)
        → Plot ACF of original data — does it decay slowly? (non-stationary)
        → Run ADF test
        → Apply transformations if needed (log, differencing)

Step 4: FIT models
        → Try multiple candidate models
        → For classical: SES / Holt / Holt-Winters
        → For stochastic: ARIMA / SARIMA
        → Use auto.arima() as a starting point

Step 5: COMPARE models
        → Compare AIC, BIC, RMSE, MAE
        → Lower = better

Step 6: DIAGNOSE residuals
        → Check histogram, Q-Q plot, time plot
        → Run Ljung-Box test
        → If residuals have pattern → go back to Step 4

Step 7: FORECAST
        → Generate forecast with confidence intervals
        → Plot and interpret the results

title: Module Overview
text:
9.2 Quick R workflow template
# ---- Step 1: Load and plot ----
library(forecast)
library(tseries)

ts_data <- ts(my_data, start = c(2000, 1), frequency = 12)
plot(ts_data)

# ---- Step 2: Decompose ----
stl_decomp <- stl(ts_data, s.window = "periodic")
autoplot(stl_decomp)

# ---- Step 3: Check stationarity ----
acf(ts_data)
adf.test(ts_data)

# Difference if needed
ts_diff <- diff(ts_data)
adf.test(ts_diff)

# ---- Step 4: Fit models ----
# Classical
hw_model <- hw(ts_data, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12)

# ARIMA
arima_auto <- auto.arima(ts_data, seasonal = TRUE)

# ---- Step 5: Compare ----
accuracy(hw_model)
summary(arima_auto)  # check AIC

# ---- Step 6: Diagnose residuals ----
checkresiduals(arima_auto)
Box.test(residuals(arima_auto), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box")

# ---- Step 7: Forecast ----
fc <- forecast(arima_auto, h = 12)
autoplot(fc)

title: Module Overview
section: 10.1 Links to regression analysis (your other module)
text:
10.1 Links to regression analysis (your other module)
Time series and regression share the same underlying goal: explain variation in an outcome variable. The key differences are:







Regression
Time Series




Data points are independent
Data points are correlated over time


Predict using other variables (X → Y)
Predict using own past values (Y_past → Y_future)


Residuals should be independent
Residuals should be random (no autocorrelation)



The bridge concept — regression with time: Your professor mentioned that you can use regression techniques on time series data. This is called a causal time series model — where you use both the series’ own history AND external variables (like weather, income) to explain the data.

title: Module Overview
text:
10.2 Links to statistics fundamentals







Concept you know
How it appears in time series




Normal distribution
Residuals should follow N(0, σ²)


Hypothesis testing
ADF test for stationarity; Ljung-Box test for residuals


Confidence intervals
Forecast intervals widen as you predict further


Minimising sum of squared errors
How α, β, γ parameters are estimated


Model selection (R², AIC)
AIC/BIC used to choose between models

title: Module Overview
text:
11.1 Model selection cheat sheet
Look at your data:
├── No trend, no seasonality → SES / ARIMA(p,0,q)
├── Trend only → Holt's / ARIMA(p,1,q) 
├── Trend + seasonality, constant swings → Holt-Winters Additive / SARIMA
└── Trend + seasonality, growing swings → Holt-Winters Multiplicative (or log first)

title: Module Overview
text:
11.2 Key terms glossary







Term
Plain English definition




Time series
Data recorded at regular intervals over time


Trend
Long-term upward or downward direction


Seasonality
Predictable repeating pattern within a year


Cyclical
Irregular long-term waves (>2 years)


Residual
What’s left after removing all patterns


Additive model
Components added together; seasonal size is constant


Multiplicative model
Components multiplied; seasonal size grows with level


Stationary
Data with constant mean, variance, and covariance over time


Differencing
Subtracting consecutive values to remove trend


ACF
How correlated a series is with its own past values


PACF
Direct correlation at each lag (removing shorter lag effects)


α (alpha)
Smoothing parameter for the level (0–1)


β (beta)
Smoothing parameter for the trend


γ (gamma)
Smoothing parameter for the seasonality


AIC/BIC
Model quality scores — lower means better


ARIMA(p,d,q)
Autoregressive + Differencing + Moving Average model


SARIMA
ARIMA extended for seasonal data

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text: 📝 Annotation space: Use the callout blocks and blank lines throughout this guide to add your own notes after reviewing the material.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.1 What is a Time Series?
A time series is a sequence of data points recorded at regular intervals over time — for example, daily attendance figures, quarterly income, or monthly airline passengers. The defining feature is that the order of observations matters: what happened yesterday influences what happens today.
The core goal of time series analysis is to:

Understand the historical behaviour of the data
Model the underlying patterns mathematically
Forecast future values with measurable confidence


📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 2.2 The Four Components of a Time Series
text:
2.2 The Four Components of a Time Series
Every time series can be broken down into four building blocks:








Component
Symbol
What it represents




Trend
T
The long-run direction (upward, downward, or flat)


Seasonality
S
Regular, repeating patterns within a fixed period (e.g., every week, every year)


Cyclical
C
Longer-term waves not tied to a fixed period (e.g., economic boom/bust cycles)


Residual / Remainder
R (or ε)
What is left after removing the other components — random noise



Example from the lecture: A drug sales dataset (used in the textbook) shows:

An upward trend over 30 years
A seasonal pattern: sales rise and fall in the same shape each year
Residuals that contain random fluctuation


📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.3 Decomposition Models: Additive vs. Multiplicative
How you combine the components determines your decomposition model.

2.3.1 Additive Model
Use this when the size of the seasonal swings stays roughly constant over time (homogeneous variance).
\[
y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t
\]
Therefore, the residual is:
\[
R_t = y_t - T_t - S_t
\]


2.3.2 Multiplicative Model
Use this when the seasonal swings grow or shrink proportionally with the trend (heterogeneous variance / heteroscedasticity). A log transformation can convert this to an additive form.
\[
y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t
\]
Therefore, the residual is:
\[
R_t = \frac{y_t}{T_t \times S_t}
\]
How to decide which model? Look at the time series plot. If the peaks and troughs get wider as the trend increases → Multiplicative. If they stay the same width → Additive.

📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.4 Moving Average (MA) — Estimating the Trend
A Moving Average smooths out the short-term noise to reveal the underlying trend. You take a window of consecutive values, average them, and slide the window one step forward.
For a periodicity of \(p\), the moving average at time \(t\) is:
\[
MA_t = \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=0}^{p-1} y_{t-i}
\]
From the Excel lab (Ex1 — Weekly Attendance, p = 5):
The data records daily attendance (Mon–Fri) for 3 weeks. A 5-point moving average is computed:
MA = (Mon + Tue + Wed + Thu + Fri) / 5
This strips out the day-of-week seasonal pattern, leaving a cleaner picture of the trend.

📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.5 Centred Moving Average (CMA)
When the period \(p\) is even (e.g., \(p = 4\) for quarterly data), the simple MA falls between time points. To realign it with actual observations, you compute the Centred Moving Average:
\[
CMA_t = \frac{MA_t + MA_{t+1}}{2}
\]
From the Excel lab (Ex2 — Quarterly Income, p = 4):
Because there are 4 quarters, the 4-point MA sits between Q2 and Q3. Averaging two consecutive MAs centres it on Q3 of each year.

📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 2.6 Seasonal Index — Measuring the Seasonal Effect
text:
2.6 Seasonal Index — Measuring the Seasonal Effect
The seasonal index quantifies how much each period (e.g., each day of the week, each quarter) systematically differs from the average.

2.6.1 Step-by-step (Additive):

Compute CMA (the trend estimate)
Calculate deviations: \(y_t - CMA_t\)
Average the deviations for each season across all years → raw seasonal index
Correct the indices so they sum to zero:

\[
\text{Corrected Index} = \text{Raw Index} - \bar{\text{Index}}
\]


2.6.2 Step-by-step (Multiplicative):

Compute CMA
Calculate ratios: \(y_t / CMA_t\)
Average ratios per season → raw index
Correct so they average to 1 (i.e., sum = number of seasons):

\[
\text{Corrected Index} = \frac{\text{Raw Index}}{\bar{\text{Index}}}
\]

📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.7 Big Picture: ARIMA and SARIMA
The lecturer gave a preview of where the course is heading. The classical decomposition methods (above) are intuitive but limited. The more powerful statistical approach is the SARIMA framework:
\[
\text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s
\]

AR(p): AutoRegressive — today’s value depends on the past \(p\) values
I(d): Integrated — we difference the series \(d\) times to achieve stationarity
MA(q): Moving Average — today’s value depends on past \(q\) residual errors
The seasonal \((P, D, Q)_s\) terms do the same for the seasonal pattern

Building SARIMA requires first achieving stationarity (constant mean and variance). This will be covered in detail in later weeks.

📝 Your notes here:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.1 Exercise 1 — Daily Attendance (p = 5, Additive)

Data: Mon–Fri attendance over 3 weeks
Task: Compute 5-point MA, seasonal index per day, and residuals
Key insight: Friday has a consistently negative index (~-8.5), while Monday/Tuesday are positive. This makes intuitive sense — attendance drops at the end of the week.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.2 Exercise 2 — Quarterly Income (p = 4)

Data: Quarterly income figures from 2007–2010 across four years
Tabs:

Ex2 (A) — Additive decomposition (Y = T + S + R)
Ex2 (m) — Multiplicative decomposition (Y = T × S × R)
Ex2 (A v M) — Side-by-side comparison of both approaches

Key insight: The income series shows heteroscedasticity (variance changes over time), suggesting the multiplicative model is more appropriate. This can also be addressed with a log(y_t) transformation.
