title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text: 📝 Note for reviewer: These notes are based on the Week 7 lecture transcript. Leave comments or annotations in the sections marked with <!-- ADD MORE HERE -->.

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.1 The Analysis Template
The main goal of this week was to learn a reusable template — a series of steps you can follow for almost any univariate time series dataset. The steps are:

Plot the data – Look at the time series visually.
Identify components – Does it have trend? Seasonality? Both?
Decompose the series – Separate it into its parts.
Analyse the residuals – Check if the “leftover” noise looks random and normal.
Fit models – Try different Exponential Smoothing models.
Compare models – Use error metrics and information criteria to choose the best.
Check final residuals – Make sure the chosen model’s errors are well-behaved.







TipWhy use a template?



A template helps you stay consistent and not miss important steps. Most time series follow the same kind of analysis, so this checklist works for the majority of datasets.

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.2 Step 1 – Plot and Identify Components
The first thing you do is plot your data. Ask yourself:

Is there a trend? (Does the data go up or down over time?)
Is there seasonality? (Does the pattern repeat at regular intervals, like every 12 months?)
Is the seasonality additive (same size each year) or multiplicative (getting bigger each year)?

Example – Air Passengers dataset (1949–1960):

Clear increasing trend over time.
Seasonality present – repeating every 12 months (monthly data → periodicity = 12).
The seasonal pattern gets bigger over time → this suggests multiplicative seasonality.

# Load the dataset
data("AirPassengers")

# Plot the time series
autoplot(AirPassengers)

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.3 Step 2 – Decompose the Time Series
Decomposition means separating the time series into its parts:

Trend component
Seasonal component
Residual (random) component

You can do this two ways:

Additive decomposition: assumes seasonal effect is constant → \(Y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative decomposition: assumes seasonal effect grows with the trend → \(Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t\)

# Additive decomposition
decomposed_add <- decompose(AirPassengers, type = "additive")
plot(decomposed_add)

# Multiplicative decomposition
decomposed_mult <- decompose(AirPassengers, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomposed_mult)






NoteHow to choose: Additive or Multiplicative?



Look at the residual (random) component:

You want residuals that look random, with no pattern, and with constant variance.
If the multiplicative residuals look more random → use multiplicative.
For Air Passengers: multiplicative was better because the seasonal variation grows over time.




📌 Missing values in decomposition: You will always see 6 missing values at the start and 6 at the end (for monthly data). This is normal — it happens because the moving average needs observations on both sides. Formula: missing values = periodicity / 2 = 12 / 2 = 6

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.4 Step 3 – Analyse the Residuals
After decomposition, you need to check the residuals (the random part). Good residuals should:

Have a mean close to zero
Have constant variance (the spread doesn’t change over time)
Look random (no visible pattern)
Be approximately normally distributed


2.4.1 Standardising the Residuals
To better understand and compare residuals, we standardise them. This means rescaling them so that:

Mean = 0
Standard deviation = 1

\[z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}\]
Where:

\(x\) = your residual value
\(\bar{x}\) = mean of all residuals
\(s\) = standard deviation of all residuals

After standardising, you can use the ±2 rule: 95% of values in a normal distribution fall between -2 and +2. This makes it easy to spot outliers.
# Extract residuals from multiplicative decomposition
residuals_mult <- decomposed_mult$random

# Standardise the residuals
mean_res <- mean(residuals_mult, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_res   <- sd(residuals_mult, na.rm = TRUE)
std_res  <- (residuals_mult - mean_res) / sd_res

# Plot: histogram, boxplot, QQ plot, scatter
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
hist(std_res, main = "Histogram of Standardised Residuals", xlab = "")
boxplot(std_res, main = "Boxplot")
qqnorm(std_res); qqline(std_res, col = "red")
plot(std_res, main = "Scatter Plot of Residuals")
abline(h = c(-2, 2), col = "blue", lty = 2)






TipReading the residual plots










Plot
What you look for




Histogram
Roughly bell-shaped (normal curve)


Boxplot
Symmetric around 0, not too many outliers outside ±2


QQ Plot
Points close to the red diagonal line (especially between ±2)


Scatter
Random scatter, constant width band (no funnel shape)

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.5 Step 4 – Fitting Classical Models

2.5.1 Overview of the Models
The three main classical models are:








Model
Also called
Components




SES
Single Exponential Smoothing
Level only (no trend, no seasonality)


Holt
Double Exponential Smoothing
Level + Trend


Holt-Winters
Triple Exponential Smoothing
Level + Trend + Seasonality



For datasets with both trend and seasonality (like Air Passengers), you use Holt-Winters.


2.5.2 Using ETS in R (Automatic Model Selection)
The ets() function in R automatically selects the best model:
library(forecast)

# Let R choose the best model automatically
best_model <- ets(AirPassengers)
summary(best_model)
The output tells you:

Error type: Additive (A) or Multiplicative (M)
Trend type: None (N), Additive (A), Damped (Ad)
Seasonal type: None (N), Additive (A), Multiplicative (M)

For Air Passengers, R suggested: M, Ad, M → Multiplicative error, Damped trend, Multiplicative seasonality.


2.5.3 Fitting Holt-Winters Manually
You can also try specific models yourself and compare them:
# Model 1: Additive (expected to be worst for Air Passengers)
hw_add <- hw(AirPassengers, h = 24, seasonal = "additive")

# Model 2: Multiplicative, linear trend
hw_mult_lin <- hw(AirPassengers, h = 24, seasonal = "multiplicative", damped = FALSE)

# Model 3: Multiplicative, damped trend
hw_mult_damp <- hw(AirPassengers, h = 24, seasonal = "multiplicative", damped = TRUE)

# Plot all three for visual comparison
autoplot(hw_add)
autoplot(hw_mult_lin)
autoplot(hw_mult_damp)

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.6 Step 5 – Comparing Models (Goodness of Fit)
Once you have multiple models, you need to decide which is the best one. You use these metrics:

2.6.1 Error Metrics








Metric
Full name
What it measures




MSE
Mean Squared Error
Average of squared prediction errors


RMSE
Root Mean Squared Error
Square root of MSE – same units as data


MAE
Mean Absolute Error / Deviation
Average of absolute errors (not penalised)









ImportantWhy RMSE vs MAE?



RMSE penalises large errors more than MAE, because it squares the distances first. This means a model with a few large mistakes will have a much higher RMSE than MAE. Prefer RMSE when big errors are especially bad.




2.6.2 Information Criteria








Metric
Full name
Use




AIC
Akaike Information Criterion
Balance between fit and complexity


AICc
Corrected AIC
Better for small samples (< 30 obs)


BIC
Bayesian Information Criterion
Similar to AIC, penalises complexity more



Lower AIC/BIC = better model. But AIC has a crucial interpretation:

AIC balances model fit and model complexity. If adding one more parameter (making the model more complex) reduces the errors only a little, the AIC will tell you it’s NOT worth the added complexity.

# Compare accuracy metrics for all three models
accuracy(hw_add)
accuracy(hw_mult_lin)
accuracy(hw_mult_damp)

# Compare AIC/BIC
AIC(hw_add$model)
AIC(hw_mult_lin$model)
AIC(hw_mult_damp$model)


2.6.3 Example – Air Passengers Result



Model
RMSE
AIC




Additive
highest
highest


Multiplicative, Linear
lower
lowest ✓


Multiplicative, Damped
lowest RMSE
slightly higher




According to AIC: Model 2 (multiplicative, linear) is best → simpler model, not worth adding the extra damping parameter.
According to RMSE: Model 3 (damped) is best → smaller prediction errors.
Decision: Prefer the model with the lowest AIC (Model 2), because the improvement in RMSE from adding a 4th parameter is not worth the added complexity.

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.7 Step 6 – Verify Final Model Residuals
Once you pick your best model, you must check its residuals one more time. The same rules apply:

Residuals should be normally distributed, mean ≈ 0, constant variance, random pattern.
If the residuals look good → you can trust your forecast and confidence intervals.
If the residuals are not normal → your confidence intervals may not be reliable.

# Get residuals from chosen model
final_residuals <- residuals(hw_mult_lin)

# Standardise and plot
std_final <- (final_residuals - mean(final_residuals)) / sd(final_residuals)

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
hist(std_final, main = "Final Model Residuals")
boxplot(std_final)
qqnorm(std_final); qqline(std_final, col = "red")
plot(std_final); abline(h = c(-2, 2), col = "blue", lty = 2)

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.8 Key Equations and Formulas

2.8.1 Standardisation Formula
\[z_t = \frac{r_t - \bar{r}}{s_r}\]
Where \(r_t\) is the residual at time \(t\), \(\bar{r}\) is the mean, and \(s_r\) is the standard deviation.


2.8.2 Additive Decomposition
\[Y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t\]


2.8.3 Multiplicative Decomposition
\[Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t\]


2.8.4 ETS Model Notation
\[\text{ETS}(E, T, S)\]

\(E\) = Error type: A (additive) or M (multiplicative)
\(T\) = Trend: N (none), A (additive/linear), Ad (additive damped)
\(S\) = Seasonality: N (none), A (additive), M (multiplicative)

title: Time Series – Week 7: Classical Models Analysis (Step-by-Step Template)
text:
2.9 Practical Examples from This Class

2.9.1 Wine Industry Dataset

Trend: present, possibly cyclical (hidden cycle)
Seasonality: present, periodicity = 12
Best model: Multiplicative seasonality + Damped trend (confirmed by AIC)
Key insight: AIC confirmed it was worth adding the damp parameter here (unlike Air Passengers)



2.9.2 Air Miles Dataset

Trend only: no seasonality (annual data, frequency = 1)
Models compared: Holt linear vs. Holt damped
Issue: Only 24 observations → small sample → residual plots will NOT look perfect. This is expected.
Best model: Linear trend model had lower AIC



2.9.3 Unemployment Dataset

Negative trend (decreasing) + Seasonality
Best model: Holt-Winters (all three components needed)
ETS result: Linear trend (no damping needed)



2.9.4 US Accidents Dataset (1973–1978)

Seasonality present, periodicity = 12
Trend: unclear – could be no trend or a slight curve
Best model: Additive seasonality + Damped trend

AIC barely higher than the “no trend” version, but RMSE was much better

Key lesson: Sometimes you must weigh AIC vs. RMSE and use your judgment







NoteEuro Stock Market (Question 5)



This dataset was not analysed with classical methods this week. It will be covered with ARIMA models starting next week.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text: 📝 How to use this guide: Read each section carefully before trying the code in RStudio. Every code block is explained line-by-line in plain English. Add your own annotations in the spaces marked “Your notes:”.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.1 What is scan()?
The scan() function is R’s way of reading raw numbers from a file — either on your computer or directly from the internet. Think of it as R saying “go grab those numbers and bring them back to me.”

2.1.1 The Kings Dataset
kings <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/misc/kings.dat", skip=3)
kings
What’s happening here, step by step:

scan("http://...") → Go to that web address and read the numbers in the file.
skip=3 → The file has 3 lines of text at the top (like a title/header) that aren’t data. We skip them so we only get the actual numbers.
kings <- → Store the result in a variable called kings. The <- is just R’s way of saying “save this as”.
The second line kings → Print what we just saved so we can see it.

The kings dataset contains the age at death of successive kings of England. Each number = one king.

⚠️ At this point, kings is just a plain list of numbers. R does not yet know these are time-ordered. It’s like having a list of exam scores — it’s just numbers, nothing more.


Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 2.2 Turning Raw Numbers into a Time Series: ts()
text:
2.2 Turning Raw Numbers into a Time Series: ts()
kingstimeseries <- ts(kings)
kingstimeseries
What’s happening here:

ts(kings) → Takes the plain list of numbers and wraps it in a special R object that says “these numbers are ordered in time.”
R now knows this is a sequence, not just a bag of numbers.

Why does this matter?
Once something is a ts object, R can:

Plot it on a proper time axis
Decompose it into trend/seasonal/noise
Apply forecasting models to it

Think of it like the difference between a pile of photos and a photo album. The photos are the same — but the album gives them order and context.

🔑 Key concept: ts() = transform raw data into a time-aware object.


Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.3 The Births Dataset (with extra options)
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")
birthstimeseries <- ts(births, frequency=12, start=c(1946,1))
birthstimeseries
What’s happening here:

births → Load the raw data (monthly birth counts in New York).
ts(births, frequency=12, start=c(1946,1)) → Convert to time series, but now with extra information:

frequency=12 → There are 12 observations per year (one per month). This tells R the data is monthly.
start=c(1946,1) → The data starts in January 1946 (year=1946, month=1).


Why frequency matters:
If you set frequency=12, R knows that observation 1 and observation 13 are both “January” — just a year apart. This is crucial for detecting seasonality (patterns that repeat yearly).



frequency value
Meaning




12
Monthly data


4
Quarterly data


7
Daily data (weekly cycle)


1
Annual data (no sub-year pattern)




Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.4 The Souvenir Dataset
souvenir <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/fancy.dat")
souvenirtimeseries <- ts(souvenir, frequency=12, start=c(1987,1))
souvenirtimeseries
What’s happening here:

Same pattern as births — monthly souvenir shop sales data, starting January 1987.
frequency=12 again, because the data is monthly.

Bonus exercise in the lab: The lab asks you to recreate the ts() output using matrix() instead. Here’s what that looks like from the solution file:
nrow = seq(1987, 1993)   # years as row labels
ncol = c("J","F","M","A","My","Jn","Jl","Au","S","O","N","D")  # month labels

souvenir.mat = matrix(souvenir, ncol=12, byrow=FALSE,
                      dimnames = list(nrow, ncol))
What this does: Arranges the souvenir numbers into a grid (matrix) with years as rows and months as columns. This is what ts() is doing internally — organising data by time. byrow=FALSE means R fills the matrix column by column (January first for all years, then February, etc.).

Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 3.1 Opening a Plot Window and Plotting
text:
3.1 Opening a Plot Window and Plotting
x11()
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot.ts(kingstimeseries)
plot.ts(birthstimeseries)
plot.ts(souvenirtimeseries)
Line by line:

x11() → Opens a new graphics window (so your plot doesn’t overwrite something else). Think of it as opening a fresh sheet of paper.
par(mfrow=c(1,3)) → Set up the plotting area to show 1 row and 3 columns of plots side by side. mfrow = “multi-frame, row-wise”.
plot.ts(...) → Plot a time series object. This is smarter than plain plot() — it automatically puts time on the x-axis.

What to look for in the plots:



Dataset
What you’d typically see




Kings
Irregular, no obvious pattern — random variation


Births
Clear repeating seasonal pattern + possible trend


Souvenir
Strong upward trend + increasing seasonal spikes




🔑 Exam tip: Looking at a plot and identifying trend vs seasonality vs noise is a core skill. Practice describing what you see in words.


Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.1 The Big Idea
Every time series can be thought of as a mix of three ingredients:








Component
What it means
Example




Trend (T)
The overall long-run direction
Sales gradually increasing over years


Seasonal (S)
Regular repeating pattern
Ice cream sales spike every summer


Random/Noise (R)
Everything else — unpredictable
A one-off spike due to a news event



Two ways to combine them:

Additive model: \(Y_t = T + S + R\)

Use when the seasonal swings stay roughly the same size over time.

Multiplicative model: \(Y_t = T \times S \times R\)

Use when the seasonal swings grow bigger as the trend increases.



💡 Look at the souvenir plot — the peaks get bigger over time. That suggests multiplicative. The births plot has roughly equal-sized seasonal swings — that suggests additive.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 4.2 Installing and Loading the TTR Package
text:
4.2 Installing and Loading the TTR Package
install.packages("TTR")
library("TTR")

install.packages("TTR") → Download and install the package (only needed once).
library("TTR") → Load the package into your current R session (needed every time you start R).

TTR stands for Technical Trading Rules — it’s a finance package, but it provides useful tools for time series smoothing.

Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.3 Running the Decomposition
births.ts = birthstimeseries
births.ts.comp <- decompose(births.ts)
plot(births.ts.comp)
Line by line:

births.ts = birthstimeseries → Just creating a shorter name to work with. Same data, easier to type.
decompose(births.ts) → R’s built-in function that splits the time series into its three components (Trend, Seasonal, Random) using moving averages.
plot(births.ts.comp) → Plots all four panels: original data, trend, seasonal, and random. This is very useful for visual inspection.

What decompose() gives you:
births.ts.comp$seasonal   # the seasonal component
births.ts.comp$trend      # the trend component
births.ts.comp$random     # the residual/noise component
You can access each piece using the $ operator (think of it like opening a specific drawer in a filing cabinet).

Your notes:

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.4 Multiplicative Decomposition (from the solution file)
births.ts.comp.m <- decompose(births.ts, type = "multiplicative")
plot(births.ts.comp.m)

Adding type = "multiplicative" switches the model from additive to multiplicative.
The solution file applies this to the souvenir data specifically, since its seasonal variation grows with the trend.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.5 Removing Components (Seasonal Adjustment)

4.5.1 Remove the Seasonal Component
births.ts.comp <- decompose(births.ts)
births.ts.sadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$seasonal
plot(births.ts.sadj)
What’s happening:

We subtract the seasonal part from the full series.
What’s left is the trend + noise — the “seasonally adjusted” series.
Why do this? Economists and analysts often do this to see the true underlying trend without seasonal noise (e.g., unemployment figures are often reported “seasonally adjusted”).



4.5.2 Remove the Trend Component
births.ts.tadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$trend
plot(births.ts.tadj)

Subtract the trend → you’re left with seasonal + noise.
Useful for studying the seasonal pattern in isolation.



4.5.3 Remove the Random (Noise) Component
births.ts.nadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$random
plot(births.ts.nadj)

Subtract the noise → you’re left with trend + seasonal.
This “smooths” the series — like a cleaned-up version.


🔑 The key equation to remember:
\[Y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t \quad \text{(Additive)}\]
Rearranging: \(S_t = Y_t - T_t\) and \(R_t = Y_t - T_t - S_t\)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
4.6 Differencing (Bonus — from the solution file)
plot(diff(births.ts))

diff() computes the difference between consecutive values — i.e., how much the series changed from one period to the next.
This is used later in the course to make a series stationary (constant mean and variance), which is required for ARIMA models.
For now, just know it exists and what it looks like.


Your notes:

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text: 📝 How to use this guide: Read each section carefully before running the code. Every code chunk has a plain-English explanation above it. The 🧠 icons mark key interpretation points your lecturer expects you to discuss in the exam.

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
2.1 The Core Formula
Every observation \(y_t\) in a time series is made up of three pieces:
\[y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)\]
Where:








Symbol
Name
Meaning




\(y_t\)
Observation
The actual value you recorded at time \(t\)


\(S_t\)
Seasonal component
The repeating pattern (e.g., higher sales every December)


\(T_t\)
Trend-cycle component
The long-run direction (going up? going down? flat?)


\(R_t\)
Random (Remainder) component
Everything left over — pure noise

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
2.2 Additive Model
\[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\]
Use when: The seasonal swings stay roughly the same size over time (called homogeneous seasonality).
🧠 How to spot it: Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are about the same height throughout the whole series → use additive.
Seasonal adjustment (additive):
\[\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\]

You literally subtract the seasonal part away to see just the trend + noise.

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
2.3 Multiplicative Model
\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]
Use when: The seasonal swings grow bigger as the trend goes up (called increasing seasonality). Think of airline passengers — summer peaks get bigger every year as more people fly.
🧠 How to spot it: Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are getting larger over time → use multiplicative.
Seasonal adjustment (multiplicative):
\[\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = \frac{y_t}{S_t} = T_t \times R_t\]

You divide out the seasonal part.

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 2.4 The Trick: Turning Multiplicative Into Additive
text:
2.4 The Trick: Turning Multiplicative Into Additive
If you take the logarithm of a multiplicative model, it becomes additive:
\[\log(y_t) = \log(S_t) + \log(T_t) + \log(R_t)\]
This is called a Box-Cox transformation. It’s very useful because additive models are simpler to work with!

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
3.1 The Data
The lab gives us this quarterly data (3 years, 4 quarters each):
