title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 3.2 Step 1: Enter the Data and Plot It
text:
3.2 Step 1: Enter the Data and Plot It

# ── Step 1: Create the time series ──────────────────────────────────────────
# We have quarterly data (frequency = 4), starting in Year 1, Quarter 1

x <- c(5.0, 6.0, 6.5, 6.3,   # Year 1
        7.5, 8.3, 8.4, 7.8,   # Year 2
        8.7, 9.4, 9.7, 9.6)   # Year 3

x_ts <- ts(x, frequency = 4, start = c(1, 1))

# ── Step 2: Plot it ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(x_ts,
     main = "Quarterly Time Series X",
     ylab = "Value", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

🧠 What to look for when you plot:

Is there an upward or downward trend? → Yes, values go from ~5 up to ~9.7
Is there a repeating seasonal pattern? → Q3 tends to be a peak, Q1 tends to be a trough
Are the seasonal swings (the up-down wiggles) staying the same size, or growing? → They look roughly constant in size → this suggests additive model

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 3.3 Step 2: Decompose Using R’s decompose() Function
text:
3.3 Step 2: Decompose Using R’s decompose() Function

# ── Additive decomposition (default) ────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_add <- decompose(x_ts, type = "additive")
plot(decomp_add)


# ── Multiplicative decomposition ─────────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_mult <- decompose(x_ts, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp_mult)

🧠 How to read the decomposition plots:
The decompose() function produces 4 panels stacked on top of each other:

observed — the original raw data (what you started with)
trend — the smooth long-run direction (note: there will be missing values at the start and end — this is normal! It happens because the moving average needs data on both sides of each point)
seasonal — the repeating pattern. For quarterly data you should see the same 4-quarter wave repeating every year.
random — the leftover noise after removing trend and seasonality.


⚠️ Missing values in trend: With quarterly data (frequency = 4), you’ll lose 4/2 = 2 observations at the beginning and 2 at the end of the trend line. This is expected.

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 3.4 Step 3: Which Model Is Better? — Look at the Residuals (Random Component)
text:
3.4 Step 3: Which Model Is Better? — Look at the Residuals (Random Component)

# Extract and plot just the random component for both models
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))  # Put 2 plots on top of each other

plot(decomp_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive Model",
     col = "darkblue", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative Model",
     col = "darkgreen", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))  # Reset layout

🧠 The golden rule for residuals: A good model leaves behind residuals that look like pure random noise — no patterns, no trends, just random up-and-down around zero. We also want the spread (variance) to be roughly constant over time.

If you can still see a wave pattern in the residuals → the model hasn’t captured seasonality properly
If the spread of residuals gets bigger over time → try the other model

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
3.5 Step 4: Seasonal Adjustment (Manually Remove Seasonality)

# ── Additive: subtract the seasonal component ────────────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_add <- x_ts - decomp_add$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_add,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Additive)",
     col = "darkorange", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")


# ── Multiplicative: divide by the seasonal component ─────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_mult <- x_ts / decomp_mult$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_mult,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Multiplicative)",
     col = "purple", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")

🧠 What does seasonal adjustment give us? It shows us \(T_t + R_t\) (or \(T_t \times R_t\) for multiplicative). The series is not smooth — it still has the random noise. But the regular seasonal peaks and troughs are gone. This lets you see the underlying trend more clearly, and also spot unusual events (like a sudden spike that isn’t seasonal).

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
5.1 Load the Data

# Read the shampoo data directly from the web
shampoo_raw <- read.csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbrownlee/Datasets/master/shampoo.csv",
  header = TRUE
)

# Inspect what we got
head(shampoo_raw)
str(shampoo_raw)


# The data is monthly – 3 years, 36 months
# We only need the Sales column (column 2)
shampoo_ts <- ts(shampoo_raw[, 2],
                 frequency = 12,   # 12 months per year
                 start = c(1, 1))  # Start at Year 1, Month 1

plot(shampoo_ts,
     main = "Monthly Shampoo Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
5.2 Additive or Multiplicative? – The Discussion
🧠 This is the key exam-style question. Here’s how to think through it:
Step 1 – Look at the trend: Is there a clear upward or downward movement?
→ Yes, shampoo sales increase strongly over the 3 years.
Step 2 – Look at the seasonal swings: Do the peaks/troughs stay the same size, or do they grow along with the trend?
→ As sales increase, the swings tend to grow proportionally → this suggests multiplicative.
Step 3 – Confirm with decomposition: After decomposing, look at the residuals. The model with more random-looking residuals (no patterns, constant variance) is the better one.

# Decompose both ways
decomp_sh_add  <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "additive")
decomp_sh_mult <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "multiplicative")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add,  main = "Shampoo – Additive Decomposition")
plot(decomp_sh_mult, main = "Shampoo – Multiplicative Decomposition")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


# Compare the residuals side by side
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive", col = "darkblue", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_sh_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative", col = "darkgreen", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 5.3 Which Components Should Be in the Model?
text:
5.3 Which Components Should Be in the Model?
🧠 Exam-style answer template:
For the shampoo data, the model should include:

✅ Trend — there is a clear and strong upward trend over the 3 years
✅ Seasonality — there are repeating monthly patterns (some months sell more than others)
✅ Random component — there is always some unexplained noise

Given the growing seasonal variation, the multiplicative model is the most appropriate:
\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 6.1 What Is a Moving Average? (ELI5 Version)
text:
6.1 What Is a Moving Average? (ELI5 Version)
Imagine you get a test score every week. One bad week (you were sick) makes the graph spike downward. A moving average smooths this out by saying: “instead of showing your score this week, show the average of this week and the weeks around it.” The spikes disappear and you can see the real trend.
Formally, for a moving average of order \(m\) (where \(m\) is odd):
\[MA_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} y_{t+j}\]
For a 5-point moving average (m = 5), each smoothed value is the average of 2 values before, the current value, and 2 values after.

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
6.2 Key Formula
\[F_t = MA_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} D_i}{p}\]
Where: - \(F_t\) = forecast/smoothed value at time \(t\) - \(p\) = the order (number of periods to average) - \(D_i\) = the observed data values

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 6.3 Births Dataset – How m Affects the Smoothing
text:
6.3 Births Dataset – How m Affects the Smoothing

# Install fpp3 if you haven't already (only run once!)
# install.packages("fpp3")
library(fpp3)

# ── Download the NY births data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")

# ── Convert to time series: monthly, starting January 1946 ──────────────────
births <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# ── Convert to a tsibble (modern time series format used by fpp3) ─────────────
births_tsibble <- as_tsibble(births)
names(births_tsibble) <- c("year_month", "births_per_month")

head(births_tsibble)


# ── Plot the raw data first ────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "Monthly Births in New York City (1946–1959)",
     ylab = "Births per Month",
     xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 1)

🧠 What to see here: - Clear trend (births gradually change over the years) - Clear seasonality (more births in summer months than winter) - Some random noise from month to month

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
section: 6.4 Calculating Moving Averages with Different Orders of m
text:
6.4 Calculating Moving Averages with Different Orders of m

# ── Using the slider package (part of fpp3) ──────────────────────────────────
# before = how many values before current point to include
# after  = how many values after current point to include
# complete = TRUE means: only calculate if you have a full window (no partial edges)

library(slider)

# 5-month moving average (2 before + current + 2 after)
MA_5  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 2, after = 2, complete = TRUE)

# 11-month moving average (5 before + current + 5 after)
MA_11 <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 5, after = 5, complete = TRUE)

# 3-month moving average (1 before + current + 1 after)
MA_3  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 1, after = 1, complete = TRUE)


# ── Plot all three together to see how m affects smoothing ──────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "NY Births – Moving Averages with Different Orders of m",
     ylab = "Births per Month", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "lightgray", lwd = 1)

lines(ts(MA_3,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "blue",   lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_5,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "red",    lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_11, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "MA(3)", "MA(5)", "MA(11)"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "blue", "red", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2))

🧠 The effect of m – key interpretation:







m is…
Effect




Small (e.g. 3)
Less smoothing. The line follows the data closely. You still see the seasonal wiggles.


Large (e.g. 11 or 12)
More smoothing. The seasonal pattern gets averaged out. You see only the trend.


Equal to periodicity (e.g. 12 for monthly)
Removes the seasonal component completely → shows pure trend




💡 Rule of thumb: To extract the trend from monthly data (frequency = 12), use m = 12 (or a centred 2×12 moving average for even orders).

title: Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages
text:
6.5 Alternative Implementations of Moving Average

# ── Using the forecast package ────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(forecast)

# ma() from forecast package — simple to use
births_ma_forecast <- ma(births, order = 5)

# ── Using the TTR package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(TTR)

# SMA() = Simple Moving Average
births_ma_ttr <- SMA(births, n = 5)

# ── Using the zoo package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(zoo)

# rollmean() with a window of 5, align = "center"
births_ma_zoo <- rollmean(births, k = 5, fill = NA, align = "center")


# ── Compare all implementations ──────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births, col = "lightgray", lwd = 1,
     main = "Comparing MA Implementations (m=5)",
     ylab = "Births")
lines(births_ma_forecast, col = "red",  lwd = 2, lty = 1)
lines(births_ma_ttr,      col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
lines(births_ma_zoo,      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 3)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "forecast::ma()", "TTR::SMA()", "zoo::rollmean()"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "red", "blue", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2),
       lty    = c(1, 1, 2, 3))

🧠 They should all give the same result! Slight differences may exist in how they handle the edges (missing values at the beginning and end), but the central values should match. This is a good way to double-check your calculations.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text: 📝 How to use this guide: Each exercise follows the same pattern — plot → decompose → fit models → compare → forecast. Master the pattern and you can handle any dataset. Leave space below each section for your own annotations!

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
1.1 What is a Time Series?
A time series is just a sequence of numbers recorded over time — like monthly wine sales, yearly unemployment rates, or daily stock prices. The goal is to understand the pattern and predict the future.
Every time series can be broken into up to four components:








Component
What it means
Example




Trend (T)
Long-term direction — going up or down?
Wine sales growing over decades


Seasonality (S)
Regular repeating pattern within a year
More wine sold in December every year


Cycle (C)
Longer-term waves (usually folded into Trend)
Economic booms and busts


Residual / Random (R)
What’s left after removing the above — noise
Unpredictable spikes

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 1.2 Additive vs Multiplicative: The Big Choice
text:
1.2 Additive vs Multiplicative: The Big Choice
This is one of the most important decisions you’ll make.
\[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t \quad \text{(Additive)}\]
\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative)}\]
How to decide?

Additive → seasonal peaks stay roughly the same height over time (flat seasonality)
Multiplicative → seasonal peaks grow bigger as the series grows (expanding seasonality)


🧠 Tip for the exam: Look at your plot. If the “wobbles” get bigger as time goes on → Multiplicative. If the “wobbles” stay the same size → Additive.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 1.3 The Classical Models: SES, Holt, Holt-Winters
text:
1.3 The Classical Models: SES, Holt, Holt-Winters
Think of these as three levels of complexity:








Model
R Function
When to use




Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
ses()
No trend, no seasonality


Holt (Double ES)
holt()
Has trend, no seasonality


Holt-Winters (Triple ES)
hw()
Has trend AND seasonality


ETS (automatic)
ets()
Let R decide the best combination

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
1.4 How to Compare Models (GOF Measures)
After fitting models, use these to decide which is best:

RMSE / MAPE → lower = better fit to the data (from accuracy())
AIC / BIC → lower = better balance of fit AND simplicity (from summary())


🏆 Francisco’s golden rule: Prefer AIC/BIC over RMSE alone — they penalise you for making the model too complex. A simpler model that fits almost as well is usually better.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.1 The Questions

Find the moving average. Use the appropriate period. Plot it.
Decompose the time series using different commands. Comment.
Fit 3 different classical models. Compare them.
Forecast using the best model.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.2 Step 1 — Plot the Raw Data


Code
# Always start by looking at your data!
plot(wineind)


What to look for when you plot:

Is there an overall upward or downward trend?
Do you see repeating peaks and valleys (seasonality)?
Do those peaks get bigger over time (multiplicative) or stay the same size (additive)?


🍷 Interpretation of wineind: The wine dataset shows monthly wine sales in Australia. You should see a clear seasonal pattern (sales spike regularly — think holiday periods) and a possible upward trend. The seasonal spikes appear to grow with the level of the series, suggesting a multiplicative model will be more appropriate.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.3 Step 2 — Moving Average (Smoothing the noise)
A moving average smooths out the short-term noise so you can see the trend more clearly. Think of it like drawing a smooth line through a messy scribble.
For monthly data, the period is 12 (12 months in a year).


Code
# Calculate and plot the 12-period moving average
sm <- ma(wineind, order = 12)

plot(wineind)
lines(sm, col = "red", lwd = 2)  # overlay the smooth line in red



💡 Why order = 12? Because wineind is monthly data — one full seasonal cycle = 12 months. For quarterly data, you’d use order = 4.

What to say about this plot: The red line shows the underlying trend after removing seasonal noise. It reveals whether the series is generally increasing, decreasing, or flat.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.4 Step 3 — Decompose the Series
Decomposition splits the series into its components so you can study each one separately.


Code
# Method 1: Classic decompose (multiplicative, since seasonality grows with level)
autoplot(decompose(wineind, type = "multiplicative"))

# Look at the random component — it should look like random noise
rr <- decompose(wineind, type = "multiplicative")$random
hist(rr)           # should look like a bell curve (normal distribution)
plot(rr, type = "p")  # should look like scattered random dots




Code
# Method 2: STL decomposition (more flexible, handles changing seasonality)
autoplot(stl(wineind, s.window = "periodic"))




Code
# Method 3: MSTL (Multiple STL — good for detecting multiple seasonal patterns)
wineind %>% mstl() %>% autoplot()



🧠 How to interpret the decomposition panels:

Top panel (Data): The original series
Seasonal panel: The repeating pattern — does it look consistent?
Trend panel: The overall direction — going up, down, or flat?
Remainder/Random panel: What’s left — ideally this looks like random noise with no pattern. If you see a clear pattern here, your model hasn’t captured everything!

For the exam: Always comment on whether the residuals look random. If they don’t, the model is missing something.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.5 Step 4 — Fit 3 Classical Models
Since wineind has both trend and seasonality, we use Holt-Winters (hw()).


Code
# Model 1: Holt-Winters WITHOUT damping (assumes trend continues forever)
h1 <- hw(wineind, damped = FALSE)
summary(h1)
autoplot(h1)




Code
# Model 2: Holt-Winters WITH damping (trend slows down over time — often more realistic)
h2 <- hw(wineind, damped = TRUE)
summary(h2)
autoplot(h2)




Code
# Model 3: STLF (STL decomposition + ETS on seasonally adjusted data)
autoplot(stlf(wineind, s.window = "periodic"))


What is “damped”? A damped trend means R assumes the trend will slow down and level off in the future, rather than continuing at the same rate forever. In real life, things rarely grow (or shrink) at a constant rate forever — so damped models are often more realistic.

🧪 Comparing models: Use summary() and look at AIC/BIC. The model with the lowest AIC wins. Also check RMSE from accuracy().

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
2.6 Step 5 — Automatic ETS (Let R Choose)


Code
# Let R find the best ETS model automatically
fit <- ets(wineind)   # "ZZZ" means auto-select all components
summary(fit)
plot(fit)


Reading the ETS output:
The model name looks like ETS(M, Ad, M):

1st letter = Error type: A = Additive, M = Multiplicative
2nd letter = Trend: N = None, A = Additive, Ad = Additive Damped, M = Multiplicative
3rd letter = Season: N = None, A = Additive, M = Multiplicative


📌 ETS(M, Ad, M) means: Multiplicative errors, Damped additive trend, Multiplicative seasonality. This is a common “best” model for data with growing seasonality and a trend that levels off.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 2.7 Step 6 — Forecast with the Best Model
text:
2.7 Step 6 — Forecast with the Best Model


Code
# Forecast 12 months ahead (= 1 year for monthly data)
forecast(fit, h = 12)
plot(forecast(fit, h = 12))



💡 Rule of thumb for h: Use h = frequency × n_years. For monthly data, h = 12 = 1 year ahead. For quarterly data, h = 4 = 1 year ahead.

Interpreting the forecast plot:

The dark blue line = point forecast (our best guess)
The shaded areas = confidence intervals (80% and 95% — the wider they are, the more uncertain we are)

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
3.1 The Questions

Visual and descriptive analysis. Transform to TS and plot.
Forecast next year using Simple Exponential Smoothing (ses()).
Find the best ETS model automatically, forecast 12 months, plot.
Summarise: seasonality? trend? what type of model?
Use ets() to estimate models with and without trend, compare.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 3.2 Step 1 — Load and Plot the Data
text:
3.2 Step 1 — Load and Plot the Data


Code
# Read in the unemployment CSV (monthly data, 2012-2017)
df <- read.csv("unemployment.csv")

# Convert to a time series object
# start = c(year, month), frequency = 12 for monthly
unempl <- ts(df[, 1], start = c(2012, 1), frequency = 12)

# Plot it
plot(unempl)



🔍 What to look for: You should see that unemployment was relatively high in 2012 and declined steadily toward 2017 — this reflects the US economic recovery after the 2008 financial crisis. There may also be very mild seasonal patterns.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
3.3 Step 2 — Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
SES is the simplest forecasting method. It just takes a weighted average of past values, giving more weight to recent observations.


Code
# Fit SES and forecast 12 months ahead
fcast_ses <- ses(unempl, h = 12)
summary(fcast_ses)
plot(fcast_ses)


What alpha (α) tells you:

α close to 1 → model relies mostly on the most recent observation (short memory)
α close to 0 → model uses a long history of past data (long memory)


⚠️ Limitation of SES: Since it has no trend component, its forecast is a flat horizontal line into the future. If your data has a trend (like unemployment declining), SES will underperform. This is why we check other models!

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 3.4 Step 3 — Let ETS Choose the Best Model
text:
3.4 Step 3 — Let ETS Choose the Best Model


Code
# Let R auto-select the best ETS model
fit_ets_default <- ets(unempl)   # equivalent to model = "ZZZ"
fit_ets_default                  # prints the chosen model type
summary(fit_ets_default)

# Forecast 12 months
fcast_ets_default <- forecast(fit_ets_default, h = 12)
plot(fcast_ets_default)

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
3.5 Step 4 — Interpret the Model
After running summary(fit_ets_default), answer these questions:








Question
Where to look
What it means




What type of model?
Model name e.g. ETS(A,A,N)
Error / Trend / Season


Is there a trend?
2nd letter — A, M or N?
N = no trend


Is there seasonality?
3rd letter — A, M or N?
N = no seasonality


Is the trend damped?
damped = TRUE in output or d in name
Trend slows down


How good is the fit?
AIC, RMSE from accuracy()
Lower = better




💬 Sample interpretation: “The model selected by R is ETS(A,N,N) — additive errors, no trend, no seasonality. This is equivalent to Simple Exponential Smoothing. Alpha = 0.85, indicating the model has a short memory and relies heavily on recent unemployment figures. The AIC is [value].”

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 3.6 Step 5 — Compare Models With and Without Trend
text:
3.6 Step 5 — Compare Models With and Without Trend


Code
# Model WITH damped trend
fit_ets_damped_trend <- ets(unempl, damped = TRUE)
fcast_ets_damped_trend <- forecast(fit_ets_damped_trend, h = 12)
plot(fcast_ets_damped_trend, main = "With Damped Trend")

# Model WITHOUT trend (force no trend component)
fit_ets_no_trend <- ets(unempl, model = "ZNZ")   # Z=auto, N=no trend, Z=auto
fcast_ets_no_trend <- forecast(fit_ets_no_trend, h = 12)
plot(fcast_ets_no_trend, main = "No Trend")




Code
# Compare all three with accuracy metrics
accuracy(fcast_ets_damped_trend)
accuracy(fcast_ets_default)
accuracy(fcast_ets_no_trend)

# Also compare AIC from summary
summary(fcast_ets_damped_trend)
summary(fcast_ets_no_trend)



📊 How to write up the comparison:
“Model A (with damped trend) has RMSE = X and AIC = Y. Model B (no trend) has RMSE = X2 and AIC = Y2. Since Model [A/B] has a lower AIC, it represents a better balance between fit quality and model complexity, and is therefore preferred.”

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
4.1 The Questions

Find the moving average. Use appropriate period. Plot.
Decompose using decompose, stl, mstl. Comment.
Fit 3 classical models. Compare.
Forecast using best model.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
4.2 Step 1 — Plot and Moving Average


Code
# airmiles is ANNUAL data (frequency = 1), so NO seasonal moving average
# We still plot to see the trend
plot(airmiles)

# Since it's annual, there's no seasonal period to average
# mstl handles this gracefully
airmiles %>% mstl() %>% autoplot()



✈️ Interpretation of airmiles: This dataset records revenue passenger-miles for US airlines. You should see a clear upward trend — air travel grew dramatically after WWII. There is no seasonality because the data is annual. This means we only need SES or Holt (no Holt-Winters needed).

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
4.3 Step 2 — Fit 3 Models
Since airmiles has a trend but no seasonality, use SES and Holt:


Code
# Model 1: SES (no trend — expect this to be worst)
fcast_s <- ses(airmiles)
plot(fcast_s)
accuracy(fcast_s)
summary(fcast_s)




Code
# Model 2: Holt (captures trend — should fit better)
fcast_h <- holt(airmiles)
plot(fcast_h)
accuracy(fcast_h)
summary(fcast_h)




Code
# Model 3: Linear Regression on trend (off the record — extra insight)
fit_reg <- tslm(airmiles ~ trend)
summary(fit_reg)
plot(forecast(fit_reg, h = 20))

# Check the residuals
rr <- fit_reg$residuals
plot(rr, type = 'p')   # should look random



🧠 Why does Holt beat SES here? Because airmiles has a clear upward trend. SES ignores trend (its forecast is flat), while Holt explicitly models trend with a second parameter (beta). Always match the model to the data components you observe!

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
5.1 The Questions

Plot the data. What do you observe?
Apply the best method based on your observation.
Which model — additive or multiplicative?
Use GOF measures (accuracy()) to support.
Which model fits better? Use appropriate period.
Forecast with all models, plot and compare.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
5.2 Step 1 — Plot and Identify Components


Code
plot(USAccDeaths)



🚗 Interpretation of USAccDeaths: Monthly accidental deaths in the US, 1973-1978. You should observe:

Seasonal pattern: Clear peaks in summer months (July) — more outdoor activity, more accidents. This repeats every year.
No strong long-term trend: The level appears roughly stable.
Seasonality appears roughly constant in size → suggests additive is reasonable, but check multiplicative too since the seasonal spikes track the overall level.

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
5.3 Step 2 — Decompose


Code
# Full decomposition
plot(decompose(USAccDeaths))

# STL decomposition
USAccDeaths %>% stl(s.window = "periodic") %>% autoplot()
USAccDeaths %>% mstl() %>% autoplot()

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 5.4 Step 3 — Fit Models: Additive vs Multiplicative
text:
5.4 Step 3 — Fit Models: Additive vs Multiplicative


Code
# Model 1: Holt-Winters MULTIPLICATIVE seasonal
deaths.fcast1 <- hw(USAccDeaths, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 48)
summary(deaths.fcast1)
plot(deaths.fcast1)




Code
# Model 2: Holt-Winters ADDITIVE seasonal
deaths.fcast2 <- hw(USAccDeaths, seasonal = "additive", h = 48)
summary(deaths.fcast2)
plot(deaths.fcast2)




Code
# Model 3: Holt-Winters ADDITIVE with DAMPED trend
deaths.fcast3 <- hw(USAccDeaths, seasonal = "additive", damped = TRUE, h = 48)
summary(deaths.fcast3)
plot(deaths.fcast3)

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
5.5 Step 4 — Compare All Models


Code
accuracy(deaths.fcast1)   # Multiplicative
accuracy(deaths.fcast2)   # Additive         (lower AIC?)
accuracy(deaths.fcast3)   # Additive + Damped (lowest AIC?)



📊 What the lecturer expects you to say:
“Comparing the three models, Model 3 (additive with damped trend) has the lowest AIC = 1141, followed by Model 2 (additive, AIC = 1145). Despite having an extra parameter (phi for damping), the reduction in RMSE justifies the added complexity. However, since the AIC difference is small, the simpler additive model is also defensible. Multiplicative seasonality did not improve fit significantly for this dataset, suggesting the seasonal amplitude is relatively constant.”


⚠️ Note on GOF vs AIC: RMSE measures absolute error. AIC penalises complexity. Use AIC as your primary criterion when choosing between models. The model with MORE parameters will almost always have lower RMSE — but that doesn’t mean it’s better!

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
6.1 The Questions

Plot EuStockMarkets. What do you observe?
Implement ETS methods.
Plot fitted lines on observations 200-300, compare to true TS.
Are classical models useful for this type of data?

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
6.2 Step 1 — Plot and Convert


Code
# EuStockMarkets has 4 stock indices — we use the first one (DAX)
EuStock <- ts(EuStockMarkets[, 1], start = c(1991, 130), frequency = 260)

# Plot it
plot(EuStock)
EuStock %>% mstl() %>% autoplot()



📈 Interpretation of EuStockMarkets: European stock market indices from 1991-1998. You should see:

A strong upward trend throughout the 1990s (bull market period)
No clear seasonality — stock prices don’t follow yearly seasonal patterns
High volatility and irregular movements
Frequency = 260 (trading days per year) → R’s ets() cannot handle frequency > 24, so seasonality is ignored

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
text:
6.3 Step 2 — Fit ETS Models


Code
# Note: ets() will warn that seasonality is ignored (frequency > 24)
ets(EuStock)

# Use stlf() if you want seasonal forecasts despite high frequency
stlf(EuStock)

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 6.4 Step 3 — Fit and Evaluate on Observations 200-300
text:
6.4 Step 3 — Fit and Evaluate on Observations 200-300
This is about backtesting — training on early data and testing on later data.


Code
# MODEL 1: SES
# Train on first 200 observations, forecast next 100
fit1 <- ses(EuStock[1:200], h = 100)
accuracy(fit1)
plot(fit1)
lines(EuStock[1:300])    # overlay the actual data

# How well did we predict obs 201-300?
accuracy(fit1, EuStock[201:300])




Code
# MODEL 2: Holt (with trend)
fit2 <- holt(EuStock[1:200], h = 100)
accuracy(fit2)
plot(fit2)
lines(EuStock[1:300])

accuracy(fit2, EuStock[201:300])

title: Time Series: Classical Models Lab
section: 6.5 Step 4 — The Big Question: Are Classical Models Useful Here?
text:
6.5 Step 4 — The Big Question: Are Classical Models Useful Here?

💬 This is the most important part of Exercise 5 — the professor wants your interpretation!
Expected answer:
“Classical models (SES, Holt, Holt-Winters) are limited for financial stock market data. Stock prices exhibit highly irregular, volatile movements driven by external news, economic events, and investor sentiment — factors that classical exponential smoothing models cannot capture.
When we train on observations 1-200 and predict 201-300, the forecast errors are large. The actual stock price moves significantly away from the model’s prediction (confidence intervals are very wide or the series falls outside them entirely).
This suggests that ARIMA or SARIMA models (which we study later) or even more advanced financial models would be more appropriate for stock market data. Classical models work well for data with stable seasonal patterns and smooth trends, but struggle with high-volatility financial series.”

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text: 📝 Note for the student: These notes are a summary of the Week 6 lecture. Leave space below each section for your own annotations and extra examples. Words in bold are important terms to remember.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.1 What is a Stochastic Process?
A stochastic process is simply a series of data values that change over time in a way that has some randomness (some part we cannot predict perfectly). Time series data — like monthly sales, stock prices, or rainfall — are all examples of stochastic processes.
The big idea is that the current value of the data depends (in some way) on past values of the same data. For example:

“Umbrella sales in 2026 depend on umbrella sales in 2025, which depend on 2024, and so on.”

This relationship with the past is what we want to find and model.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.2 Stationarity
Stationarity describes how a time series “behaves” over time. A time series is called stationary if its statistical properties do not change over time — it looks the same whether you look at the beginning, the middle, or the end.

2.2.1 Three conditions for stationarity
For a time series to be stationary, it must have:

Constant mean — The average of the data does not go up or down over time (no trend).
Constant variance — The “spread” of the data (how far values go from the average) stays the same over time.
Constant covariance — The relationship between two observations that are k periods apart is always the same, no matter where in the series you look.







Tip



Easy way to remember: Imagine a quiet, flat river. It does not get higher or lower over time, and the width stays the same. That is a stationary series. A river that is sometimes a flood and sometimes a trickle is not stationary.




2.2.2 Why stationarity is important
ARIMA models — the main models we use — require the data to be stationary. If the data is not stationary, we must transform it first.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.3 Transformations to Achieve Stationarity
When the data is NOT stationary, we use transformations to fix the problems:

2.3.1 Problem 1: Non-constant mean (trend)
If the data has a trend (going up or down), we remove it by taking differences.

First difference: Subtract the previous value from the current value.

\[\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}\]

If a linear trend, one difference is usually enough.
If the trend is curved (exponential), you may need two differences.



2.3.2 Problem 2: Non-constant variance
If the “spread” of the data increases over time (called multiplicative seasonality), we apply a logarithm transformation:
\[\log(Y_t)\]
Taking the log “compresses” the big values and makes the variance more constant. After this, the additive model can be used.






Note



Additive vs Multiplicative — quick reminder:








Type
When to use
Formula




Additive
Seasonality is constant
\(Y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\)


Multiplicative
Seasonality grows over time
\(Y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\)



Taking the log converts a multiplicative model into an additive one.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.4 White Noise
White noise is the simplest possible time series. It is completely random — there is no pattern, no trend, no seasonality.
Properties of white noise:

Mean = 0 (constant)
Variance is constant
No correlation between any two observations — they are independent

White noise is important because it is our target. When we build an ARIMA model:

“We want the leftover errors (residuals) of the model to look like white noise — because that means we have captured all the useful information.”

If the residuals still show patterns, the model is not complete.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.5 Random Walk
A random walk is a very common type of non-stationary time series. The equation is:
\[Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\]
where \(\varepsilon_t\) is white noise (a random error).
This means: “My value today equals my value yesterday, plus a random shock.”
Think of it like a person walking randomly — each step is in a random direction, but their position depends on where they were before.
Visual shape of a random walk: You will see “trends” and “jumps” — sudden rises and falls that look like a trend but are actually random.

2.5.1 Key property of a random walk
If you take the first difference of a random walk, you get white noise:
\[Y_t - Y_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t \quad \text{(white noise)}\]
This tells us: to “fix” a random walk, take one difference.






Warning



Random walk vs Autoregressive (AR) process: These two look very similar on a plot! Both show trends and jumps. The difference is that in an AR process, the relationship with the past has a weight (a parameter \(\phi\)) that is less than 1. In a random walk, that weight equals exactly 1.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.6 Introduction to ARIMA
ARIMA stands for:








Letter
Name
Meaning




AR
Autoregressive
How \(Y_t\) relates to its past values


I
Integrated
The differencing needed to make data stationary


MA
Moving Average
How \(Y_t\) relates to past errors (residuals)



The model is written as ARIMA(p, d, q) where:

p = number of past values used (AR order)
d = number of differences taken (to remove trend)
q = number of past errors used (MA order)


2.6.1 The AR part — Autoregressive
In an AR(p) model, the current value depends on the p most recent past values:
\[Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t\]

\(\phi\) (phi) = the weight given to each past value
\(\varepsilon_t\) = white noise error

Example: In AR(1), today’s sales depend only on yesterday’s sales.


2.6.2 The I part — Integrated (Differencing)
The “I” part records how many times we needed to difference the data to make it stationary.

If \(d = 1\): we took one difference (removed a linear trend)
If \(d = 2\): we took two differences (removed a curved trend)



2.6.3 The MA part — Moving Average
In an MA(q) model, the current value depends on past errors (residuals):
\[Y_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \cdots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}\]

\(\theta\) (theta) = weight for each past error
This is different from the “Moving Average” method used earlier for smoothing trends — do not confuse them!



2.6.4 SARIMA — Seasonal ARIMA
When the data also has seasonality, we extend the model to SARIMA(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)s:

Lowercase (p, d, q): Non-seasonal part
Uppercase (P, D, Q): Seasonal part
s: The seasonal period (e.g., 12 for monthly data)

Example: If we take one seasonal difference with period 12: \(D = 1\), \(s = 12\).

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
section: 2.7 ACF and PACF — Reading the Correlograms
text:
2.7 ACF and PACF — Reading the Correlograms
To identify which ARIMA model is best (what values of p and q to use), we use two important tools:

2.7.1 ACF — Autocorrelation Function
The ACF shows the correlation between \(Y_t\) and \(Y_{t-k}\) for different values of \(k\) (called lags).

Lag 1 = correlation with yesterday’s value
Lag 12 = correlation with the value 12 periods ago



2.7.2 PACF — Partial Autocorrelation Function
The PACF shows the “direct” correlation between \(Y_t\) and \(Y_{t-k}\), after removing the effect of everything in between.


2.7.3 The significance lines (dashed blue lines)
On a correlogram plot, you will see dashed blue lines at approximately:
\[\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}\]
where \(n\) is the number of observations.

Values inside the lines: not significant (can be due to chance)
Values outside the lines: significant — there is a real pattern



2.7.4 How to use ACF and PACF to identify the model







Pattern you see
What it means




ACF: slow decay, PACF: drops after lag 1
Autoregressive process AR(1)


ACF: drops after lag q, PACF: slow decay
Moving Average process MA(q)


Both decay slowly
Mixed ARMA(p, q) model


ACF and PACF: all values inside lines
White noise — no model needed


Seasonal spikes at lag 12, 24…
Seasonal pattern present









Tip



Think of it this way: The ACF and PACF are like “X-ray glasses” — they let you see the hidden structure (patterns and correlations) inside your data that you cannot see just by looking at the raw plot.

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text: 📝 These notes are a summary of the Week 5 lecture. Use the space under each section to add your own annotations, questions, or examples as you review.

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
3.1 Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) – Quick Recap
Use when: Your data has no trend and no seasonality — just a fluctuating level.
The key idea is that the prediction equals the level, which is a weighted average of past observations. The weight of each past observation decays exponentially as you go further back in time.

α close to 1 → short memory → only recent observations matter
α close to 0 → long memory → many past observations are used

The forecast is constant: every future prediction is the same value, only the confidence interval grows wider the further out you predict.

✏️ Your annotation space:

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
3.2 Holt’s Method (Double Exponential Smoothing)
Use when: Your data has a trend (upward or downward), but no seasonality.
Holt’s method adds a trend component on top of the level.

3.2.1 How it works
The forecast equation is similar to the equation of a straight line:
\[\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t\]
Where:

\(\ell_t\) = level at time \(t\) (how far back to look for relevant information)
\(b_t\) = trend / slope at time \(t\)
\(h\) = number of steps ahead

The level is updated using α (0 to 1) and the trend is updated using β (0 to 1), both found by minimising the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) — R does this automatically using quadratic programming.

💡 Think of it like fitting a line of best fit, but the line is constantly being updated as new data arrives.


✏️ Your annotation space:

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
3.3 Damped Trend (Holt with Damping)
Use when: The trend is not linear — it may slow down, flatten, or follow a cyclical pattern over time.
The damped trend adds a φ (phi) parameter that “bends” the trend forecast, preventing it from projecting indefinitely upward or downward. This is useful when the data contains a cyclical component (long-term oscillation, not to be confused with short-term seasonality).

A polynomial trend with more degrees captures more complex shapes.
The lecture referred to this as the damp component in R output.


✏️ Your annotation space:

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
3.4 Holt-Winters Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing)
Use when: Your data has trend AND seasonality.
Three parameters are estimated:

α → level
β → trend
γ (gamma) → seasonality


3.4.1 Additive vs. Multiplicative Seasonality








Type
When to use
Behaviour




Additive
Seasonal swings are constant in size
Pattern has the same amplitude over time


Multiplicative
Seasonal swings grow with the level
Pattern amplitude increases as data increases




💡 If your time series looks like a trumpet (widening fluctuations), use multiplicative. If the ups and downs stay the same height, use additive.

The lecture noted that multiplicative better captures a stable seasonal pattern, while additive can misrepresent a growing seasonal effect.

✏️ Your annotation space:

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
4.1 SES Forecast
\[\hat{y}_{t+1} = \ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}\]

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
4.2 Holt’s Method Forecast
\[\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t\]
\[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\]
\[b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)b_{t-1}\]

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
4.3 Confidence Interval (general form)
\[\hat{y} \pm z^* \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
Where \(z^* = 1.96\) for 95% confidence (standard normal distribution).

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
4.4 Error Metrics








Metric
Formula
Key Property




MAE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum \|y_t - \hat{y}_t\|\)
Average absolute error


MSE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2\)
Penalises large errors more


MAPE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum \left|\frac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t}\right| \times 100\)
Unit-free — good for comparing different scales




💡 Prefer MSE when large errors are costly. Use MAPE when comparing models across different datasets or units.

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
section: 5.1 Fitting models with the forecast library
text:
5.1 Fitting models with the forecast library
library(forecast)

# Single Exponential Smoothing
fit_ses <- ses(data, h = 5)

# Holt's method (trend, no seasonality)
fit_holt <- holt(data, h = 5)

# Holt with damped trend
fit_holt_damp <- holt(data, damped = TRUE, h = 5)

# Holt-Winters (additive seasonality)
fit_hw_add <- hw(data, seasonal = "additive", h = 12)

# Holt-Winters (multiplicative seasonality)
fit_hw_mult <- hw(data, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12)

# ETS automatic (R chooses best model minimising BIC)
fit_ets <- ets(data)
summary(fit_ets)

# ETS with specified components (additive error, additive trend, additive seasonal)
fit_ets_aaa <- ets(data, model = "AAA")
summary(fit_ets_aaa)

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
5.2 Comparing models using error metrics
# Extract accuracy metrics
accuracy(fit_ses)
accuracy(fit_holt)
accuracy(fit_holt_damp)

# Compare AIC / BIC (lower is better)
fit_ses$model$aic
fit_holt$model$aic

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
5.3 Plotting forecasts
plot(forecast(fit_holt, h = 10))

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
6.1 Oil Data Set

Used to compare SES vs. Holt (possible trend present)
Resulted in α ≈ 0.4 → short memory data set
SES prediction was constant (flat line) with growing confidence intervals

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
6.2 Livestock Data Set
Three models were fitted and compared using MSE and MAE:



Model
MSE
MAE




SES
14.7
8.72


Holt (linear)
13.98
7.80


Holt (damped)
13.90
7.74



➡️ Holt damped had the lowest MAE. Holt linear had the lowest MSE. Selection depends on which metric you prioritise.

title: Week 5 – Exponential Smoothing Methods (Holt & Holt-Winters)
text:
6.3 Hotel Stays Data Set

Data: number of hotel stays (millions)
Used to compare Holt-Winters additive vs. multiplicative
Multiplicative better captured the stable, consistent seasonal pattern
Additive produced a non-constant (incorrect) seasonal component

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text: 📝 Note to self: These notes combine the Week 4 lecture (Aula_4), the Lab 1 handout, the Lab 1 solution R script, and the Lecture 2 Classical Models slides. Gaps and follow-up slides are flagged throughout.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
section: 2.1 Is It Actually a Time Series? — The Kings Dataset
text:
2.1 Is It Actually a Time Series? — The Kings Dataset
Before doing any analysis, you must verify that your data actually qualifies as a time series. The first requirement is equally spaced observations — data points must be recorded at regular, consistent intervals.
The Kings dataset records the ages at death of successive Kings of England. At first glance it looks like sequential data, but it is not a time series. Each value is just the age a king happened to die — there is no regular time interval between observations. This is better framed as a regression problem (predicting the next king’s age at death), not a forecasting problem. Francisco made this distinction explicitly in class: we forget about the Kings dataset for this module.
The rule of thumb: ask yourself “Am I predicting future values of a process that unfolds over equally spaced time?” If yes, it is a time series. If no, it is something else.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
section: 2.2 Loading Data and the ts() Function
text:
2.2 Loading Data and the ts() Function
In R, raw data loaded with scan() is just a numeric vector — it has no time awareness. The ts() function wraps that vector with time series metadata.
# Kings — no frequency info needed (annual-ish, frequency = 1 by default)
kings <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/misc/kings.dat", skip = 3)
kingstimeseries <- ts(kings)

# Births — monthly data starting January 1946
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")
birthstimeseries <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# Souvenir sales — monthly data starting January 1987
souvenir <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/fancy.dat")
souvenirtimeseries <- ts(souvenir, frequency = 12, start = c(1987, 1))
The key arguments are:

frequency — number of observations per cycle (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly, 1 = annual)
start — vector giving the start time, e.g. c(1946, 1) means January 1946

Always plot immediately after creating the time series object to get your first visual read on the data.
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plot.ts(kingstimeseries)
plot.ts(birthstimeseries)
plot.ts(souvenirtimeseries)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.3 Identifying Components Visually
Every time series is assumed to be a function of up to four components:
\[y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)\]
Where:

\(y_t\) = observed value at time \(t\)
\(S_t\) = seasonal component — repeating patterns within a calendar year
\(T_t\) = trend-cycle component — long-run direction (includes multi-year cycles)
\(R_t\) = remainder/residual — everything unexplained (random noise)

When you plot your data, you are trying to answer three questions visually:
1. Is there a trend?
Look for a consistent upward or downward direction over the full series. The births dataset shows a clear increasing linear trend, interrupted by a notable drop around 1955–1956.
2. What type of seasonality is present?
Compare the amplitude (height) of seasonal swings across different years: - If the swings stay roughly the same height → homogeneous seasonality → use additive model - If the swings grow bigger as the series increases → increasing/multiplicative seasonality → use multiplicative model
The births dataset shows fairly constant swings → additive expected.
The souvenir dataset shows swings that grow year on year → multiplicative expected.
3. Are there any cycles?
Multi-year business or economic cycles are visible as broad wave patterns that stretch beyond a single year. In the tourism employment example from the lecture slides, the 2008–2009 economic crisis created a visible dip and recovery cycle. Cycles are handled by the damped parameter in Holt methods.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.4 Decomposition: Additive vs. Multiplicative

2.4.1 The Two Models
Additive (for constant seasonality): \[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\]
Multiplicative (for growing seasonality): \[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]
You can convert multiplicative to additive using a log transformation: \[\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t\]
This is the Box-Cox transformation idea mentioned in class and in the Lecture 2 slides.


2.4.2 Running Decomposition in R
# Additive decomposition (default)
births.ts.comp <- decompose(births.ts)
plot(births.ts.comp)

# Multiplicative decomposition
souvenir.ts.comp <- decompose(souvenirtimeseries, type = "multiplicative")
plot(souvenir.ts.comp)


2.4.3 What to Look At in the Decomposition Output
The plot shows four panels: data → trend → seasonal → residual (random)

Trend panel: Should show smooth directional movement.
Seasonal panel: Should show a consistent, repeating pattern — the same shape each year. This is by definition: the seasonal component is the average of each period (mean of all Januaries, mean of all Februaries, etc.) repeated throughout.
Residual panel: This is the most important panel for model selection. You want it to look like white noise — no obvious patterns, no trend, no obvious clusters.


Key insight from class: If the residual panel of your additive decomposition looks like a shrunken version of the original data (you can still see the pattern), it means the model has not captured the structure properly. Switch to multiplicative.



2.4.4 Missing Values in Decomposition
The classical decompose() function uses a centred moving average to estimate the trend. Because this requires averaging neighbouring values symmetrically, it cannot produce estimates for the first and last \(p/2\) observations (where \(p\) is the frequency). For monthly data (\(p = 12\)), you lose 6 observations at each end of the trend and residual components.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.5 Residual Analysis — Confirming Your Model
Once you have chosen a decomposition, extract the residuals and check four things:
residuals <- births.ts.comp$random
# Remove NAs introduced by moving average
residuals_clean <- na.omit(residuals)
1. Mean ≈ 0 and Mean ≈ Median (symmetry)
summary(residuals_clean)
If mean and median are close to zero and close to each other, the residuals are approximately symmetric.
2. Histogram — should look bell-shaped
hist(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals", xlab = "Residual value")
A good residual histogram is roughly symmetric, centred near zero.
3. Scatter plot / time plot — should look random
plot(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals over time")
abline(h = c(-2, 2), col = "blue")
About 95% of standardised values should fall within ±2 and 99% within ±4.
4. QQ plot — checks normality
qqnorm(residuals_clean)
qqline(residuals_clean, col = "red")
Points should sit close to the red diagonal line, especially in the central ±2 range. Small departures at the tails are acceptable.

Why does this matter? The residual distribution is what generates your confidence intervals. If your residuals follow a normal distribution centred on zero, you can construct statistically valid prediction intervals. If they don’t, your confidence intervals are unreliable.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.6 STL Decomposition
STL = Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (locally weighted regression)
Instead of calculating a simple moving average for the trend, STL fits a local regression line through small windows of data, sliding that window across the entire series. This avoids missing values at the ends.
library(forecast)
fit <- stl(elecequip, s.window = 7)
autoplot(fit) + xlab("year")

2.6.1 Key Parameters







Parameter
What it controls




s.window
Controls variation in the seasonal component. Use "periodic" to fix the season as constant.


t.window
Controls smoothness of the trend. Larger = smoother trend.


robust = TRUE
Makes estimation resistant to outliers.





2.6.2 Choosing the Right s.window

Too small (e.g. 5): The seasonal component is allowed to change too much → it absorbs signal that should be in the trend or residual.
"periodic" (recommended default): Forces the seasonal component to repeat identically every year — matches our theoretical definition of seasonality.

As shown in the lecture slides, using s.window = "periodic" usually gives a cleaner, more constant seasonal component and a more random-looking remainder.


2.6.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of STL
Advantages:

Robust to outliers (when robust = TRUE)
No missing values at series ends
Can handle seasonal component that changes gradually over time
User can control how smooth the trend and seasonal extraction are

Disadvantages:

No calendar effect (cannot model Easter, public holidays that shift dates each year)
Only handles additive models natively (apply log() transform first for multiplicative data)
Only handles one seasonal period (use mstl() for multiple seasonalities)



2.6.4 Useful STL-related R Functions
seasonal(fit)     # Extract seasonal component
trendcycle(fit)   # Extract trend-cycle component
remainder(fit)    # Extract residual component
seasadj(fit)      # Seasonally adjusted series (= data minus seasonal)
mstl(x)           # Multiple STL — handles more than one seasonal period


2.6.5 Seasonal Adjustment
After decomposition, you can strip out the seasonal component to expose the underlying trend more clearly:
Additive: \(y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative: \(y_t / S_t = T_t \times R_t\)
# Additive seasonal adjustment
births.ts.sadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$seasonal
plot(births.ts.sadj)

Important caveat (from slides): Seasonally adjusted series still contain trend AND random noise, so they can look jagged and create “false” ups and downs. The trend-cycle component alone is smoother and better for identifying inflection points.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.7 Forecasting with Decomposition (Naïve Methods)
Before using formal models, you can produce simple forecasts directly from decomposition:

The seasonal component is forecast by repeating the pattern from the last year (naïve seasonal forecast).
The trend-cycle is forecast using a simple method like naïve (random walk) on the seasonally adjusted series.
The two are combined to produce the full forecast.

# STL + naïve forecast
fit <- stl(elecequip, t.window = 13, s.window = "periodic")
fit %>% seasadj() %>% naive() %>% autoplot()

# Or use the stlf() shortcut
elecequip %>% stlf(method = "naive") %>% autoplot()
The confidence intervals come from the residual distribution — another reason clean, normal residuals are important.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.8 Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

2.8.1 When to Use It
Use SES when your data:

Has no trend (or trend is negligible)
Has no seasonality
Is a confirmed time series (equally spaced)



2.8.2 The Core Idea
SES computes a weighted average of all past observations, where weights decrease exponentially as you go further back in time. More recent observations get more weight.


2.8.3 Key Equations
Forecast equation: \[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t\]
Smoothing (level) equation: \[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}\]
By iterating, this expands to: \[\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \sum_{j=0}^{T-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j y_{T-j} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0\]


2.8.4 The Smoothing Parameter α
\[0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\]







α value
Meaning




Close to 1
Short memory — almost all weight on last observation (approaches random walk)


Close to 0
Long memory — weights spread far back in history (approaches overall mean)




Trick for interpretation: If R estimates α = 0.83 (as in the oil data example), the model has a very short memory — your prediction will be close to your last known value.



2.8.5 Optimization
α and the initial level \(\ell_0\) are chosen by minimising the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE):
\[\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} e_t^2\]
This is a non-linear optimization problem (unlike OLS regression), so R uses numerical methods.


2.8.6 R Code
library(forecast)

# Method 1: ses() directly
fc <- ses(oildata, h = 5)
autoplot(fc) + autolayer(fitted(fc), series = "Fitted")

# Method 2: ets() family function — equivalent for SES
ses_model <- ets(train.ts, model = "ANN", alpha = 0.2)
# ANN = Additive error, No trend, No seasonality


2.8.7 SES Prediction Behaviour
Because SES produces a flat forecast (same value for all h steps ahead), the point forecast never changes after the last observation. However, the confidence interval widens as h increases, because the uncertainty accumulates with each step forward.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide
text:
2.9 Overview of All Classical Models
From the lecture, the full family is:








Components Present
Model
R Function




No trend, no seasonality
Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
ses() or ets(..., model="ANN")


Trend only (linear)
Holt’s Linear Method
holt() or ets(..., model="AAN")


Trend + cycle/damping
Holt Damped
holt(..., damped=TRUE)


Trend + seasonality (constant)
Holt-Winters Additive
hw(..., seasonal="additive")


Trend + seasonality (growing)
Holt-Winters Multiplicative
hw(..., seasonal="multiplicative")


Best of all the above
ETS (automatic)
ets()



ETS stands for Error, Trend, Seasonality. The ets() function automatically selects the best model by minimising AICc.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
Time Series Decomposition — components, additive vs multiplicative models
Seasonal Adjustment — removing the seasonal signal to expose trend
STL Decomposition — Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess
Prediction from Decomposition — using decomposed components to forecast
Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) — weighted averaging with exponential decay
Trend Methods — Holt Linear and Holt Damped
Prediction Error Measurements — MAD/MAE, MSE, MAPE
Seasonal Methods — Holt-Winters additive, multiplicative, and damped
ETS in R — automated model selection with ets()

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.1 Time Series Components
A time series (TS) can be broken into three building blocks that together explain the observed data.

Seasonal variation — a repeating pattern that completes itself within one calendar year (e.g., higher ice-cream sales every summer). This is a periodic cycle.
Cyclical variation — longer-term waves of ups and downs that last more than two years and do not follow a fixed calendar (e.g., economic booms and recessions).
Trend — the long-run direction of the series (up, down, or flat). A time series has one overall trend, but it can be interrupted by multiple cyclical swings.

The ETS framework (Error, Trend, Seasonality) provides a unified way to describe and model all three simultaneously.

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.2 Time Series Decomposition
The general decomposition model says the observed value \(y_t\) is a function of three components:
\[y_t = f(S_t,\; T_t,\; R_t)\]



Symbol
Meaning




\(y_t\)
Observed value at time \(t\)


\(S_t\)
Seasonal component at time \(t\)


\(T_t\)
Trend–cycle component at time \(t\)


\(R_t\)
Random (remainder) component at time \(t\)



There are two forms of this model:
Additive decomposition (use when seasonal swings are roughly constant over time):
\[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\]
Multiplicative decomposition (use when seasonal swings grow as the level of the series rises):
\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]

Tip — converting multiplicative to additive: Taking logarithms turns a multiplicative model into an additive one. This is an example of a Box-Cox transformation:
\[\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t\]
In R you can pass lambda = "auto" to mstl() to do this automatically.


2.2.1 Key R functions for extracting components
library(forecast)
library(fpp2)

fit <- stl(elecequip, s.window = 7)

seasonal(fit)    # seasonal component  S_t
trendcycle(fit)  # trend–cycle component  T_t
remainder(fit)   # random component  R_t
seasadj(fit)     # seasonally adjusted series  (T_t + R_t for additive)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.3 Seasonal Adjustment
Seasonal adjustment means stripping the seasonal component out of the observed series so you can see the underlying trend more clearly.

Additive: \(y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative: \(y_t \,/\, S_t = T_t \times R_t\)

The seasonal component \(S_t\) is estimated from past data (e.g., via STL) and then used to adjust current values.

Important caveat: A seasonally adjusted series still contains the random component \(R_t\), so it will look jagged and can give the false impression of real ups and downs. To identify genuine turning points, focus on the smooth trend-cycle component instead.

# Example — elecequip dataset
fit <- stl(elecequip, s.window = 7)

autoplot(elecequip, series = "Data") +
  autolayer(seasadj(fit), series = "Adjusted Seasonality")

autoplot(elecequip, series = "Data") +
  autolayer(trendcycle(fit), series = "Trend-cycle")

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.4 STL Decomposition
STL stands for Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess. Loess is a local smoothing technique that is robust to extreme (outlier) values.
Advantages of STL:

Robust — not distorted by unusual observations
Can handle any seasonality with a single peak per cycle
Both the seasonal and trend-cycle smoothness can be tuned by the user

Disadvantages of STL:

No built-in calendar-effect correction
Strictly additive — apply log() or Box-Cox for multiplicative series


2.4.1 Key parameters







Parameter
Controls




s.window
How much the seasonal component is allowed to vary over time. Use "periodic" to force a fixed seasonal pattern.


t.window
How smooth (wavey) the trend-cycle component is. Larger = smoother.


robust = TRUE
Downweights outliers when fitting.



# Fixed seasonal pattern, robust fitting
fit <- stl(elecequip,
           t.window = 15,
           s.window = "periodic",
           robust   = TRUE)
autoplot(fit) + ggtitle("STL Decomposition Electric Equipment index")

# Automatic window selection (plus optional Box-Cox)
elecequip %>% mstl(lambda = "auto") %>% autoplot()

mstl() automatically selects s.window and also supports multi-seasonal data (e.g., both weekly and annual seasonality). It accepts a lambda argument for Box-Cox transformations.



2.4.2 Spain Tourism example (from class data)
x <- scan("IncomesTourism.txt", skip = 4)
x.ts <- ts(x, frequency = 12, start = c(1990, 1))

# Visual inspection
autoplot(x.ts)
autoplot(log(x.ts))   # check whether log stabilises variance

# STL on data from 2000
x.ts %>%
  window(start = 2000) %>%
  stl(s.window = 11, robust = TRUE) -> fit

autoplot(fit)
autoplot(seasadj(fit))

# Seasonal plot to inspect year-on-year patterns
ggseasonplot(window(x.ts, start = c(1990, 1)),
             year.labels = TRUE) +
  ggtitle("Total Income Tourism") +
  ylab("€ x 1,000")

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.5 Prediction Using Decomposition
The workflow for forecasting with decomposition:

Decompose the series using STL.
Seasonal component: repeat the last year’s seasonal pattern (naïve seasonal forecast).
Trend-cycle / seasonally-adjusted component: apply a non-seasonal method (e.g., naïve, SES, Holt).
Combine the forecasts.


Decomposition is primarily used to understand a series before choosing a dedicated forecasting model. The stlf() function in R automates steps 1–4.

# STL decomposition → naïve forecast on seasonally-adjusted data
fit <- stl(elecequip, t.window = 13, s.window = "periodic")

fit %>% seasadj() %>% naive() %>%
  autoplot() +
  ylab("New orders index") +
  ggtitle("Seasonal adjusted data — Naïve Forecast")

# Shortcut with stlf()
elecequip %>%
  stlf(method = "naive") %>%
  autoplot() +
  ylab("New orders index")

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.6 Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

2.6.1 When to use SES
SES belongs to the family of pure time-series methods — they only use the series itself (no external predictors). Use SES when:

There is no trend and no seasonality in the series.
You have few or no independent variables to include.



2.6.2 How it works
Rather than giving equal weight to all past observations (like a simple moving average), SES gives more weight to recent observations and exponentially decreasing weight to older ones.
The smoothing parameter \(\alpha\) (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) controls how fast the weights decay:







\(\alpha\) value
Effect




Close to 1
Almost all weight on the most recent observation — reacts quickly, less smooth


Close to 0
Weight spread evenly over all past observations — very smooth, slow to react





2.6.3 Equations
Forecast equation (the prediction for any future horizon \(h\) is the same value):
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t\]
Smoothing (level) equation:
\[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}\]
\(\ell_t\) is called the level of the series at time \(t\).
By iterating the smoothing equation backwards, the forecast becomes a weighted average of all past observations:
\[\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \sum_{j=0}^{T-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j y_{T-j} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0\]
The weights \(\alpha,\; \alpha(1-\alpha),\; \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \ldots\) decay exponentially — hence the name.


2.6.4 Optimisation
The parameters \(\alpha\) and the initial level \(\ell_0\) are chosen by minimising the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE):
\[\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} e_t^2\]
This requires numerical (non-linear) optimisation, which R handles automatically.
library(fpp2)

oildata <- window(oil, start = 1996)
fc <- ses(oildata, h = 5)
fc   # shows alpha, initial level, AIC, BIC, forecasts

autoplot(fc) +
  autolayer(fitted(fc), series = "Fitted") +
  ylab("Oil (1M Tonnes)") +
  xlab("Year")

# Equivalent via ets()  — ETS(A,N,N) = Additive error, No trend, No seasonality
SES_model <- ets(oildata, model = "ANN", alpha = 0.2)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.7 Trend Methods

2.7.1 Holt Linear Trend
An extension of SES for series with a linear trend but no seasonality. It adds a second smoothing equation for the slope (trend):
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t + h b_t\]
\[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\]
\[b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1}\]



Parameter
Role




\(\alpha\)
Smoothing for the level


\(\beta^*\)
Smoothing for the slope (trend)


\(\ell_t\)
Current level estimate


\(b_t\)
Current slope estimate



Both \(\alpha\) and \(\beta^*\) lie in \([0, 1]\). \(\ell_0\) and \(b_0\) are also optimised by minimising SSE.


2.7.2 Holt Damped Trend
Holt’s method keeps projecting the trend indefinitely into the future, which is often unrealistic. The damped version introduces a damping parameter \(\phi\) (\(0 < \phi < 1\)) that gradually flattens the forecast towards a constant:
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \cdots + \phi^h)b_t\]
\[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})\]
\[b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)\phi b_{t-1}\]

When \(\phi = 1\) the damped model is identical to linear Holt. In practice the damped Holt method is often more accurate because real trends rarely continue indefinitely.

# Linear Holt
window(ausair, start = 1990, end = 2004) %>%
  holt(h = 5, PI = FALSE) %>%
  autoplot()

# Damped Holt
window(ausair, start = 1990, end = 2004) %>%
  holt(damped = TRUE, h = 5, PI = FALSE) %>%
  autoplot()

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.8 Prediction Error Measurements
To compare models, we measure how far forecasts are from actual values.

2.8.1 Common metrics
Mean Absolute Deviation / Error (MAD / MAE) — average of absolute forecast errors, in the same units as the data:
\[\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum |Y_t - F_t|}{n}\]
Mean Squared Error (MSE) — penalises large errors more heavily (units are squared):
\[\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum (Y_t - F_t)^2}{n - 1}\]
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) — unit-free measure, useful for comparing across different series:
\[\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \left|\frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t}\right| \times 100\]
Other criteria used for model selection include AIC, AICc (corrected AIC), and BIC — lower values indicate a better-fitting model relative to its complexity.
# Compare SES, Holt, and Damped Holt on livestock data
livestock2 <- window(livestock, start = 1970, end = 2000)
fit1 <- ses(livestock2)
fit2 <- holt(livestock2)
fit3 <- holt(livestock2, damped = TRUE)

accuracy(fit1, livestock)
accuracy(fit2, livestock)
accuracy(fit3, livestock)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.9 Seasonal Methods — Holt-Winters
Holt-Winters extends Holt by adding a third equation for seasonality, making it suitable for series that have both trend and season.

2.9.1 Additive Holt-Winters
Use when seasonal fluctuations are roughly constant in size over time.
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t + hb_t + s_{t+h-m(k+1)}\]
\[\ell_t = \alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\]
\[b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1}\]
\[s_t = \gamma(y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}\]


2.9.2 Multiplicative Holt-Winters
Use when seasonal fluctuations grow proportionally with the level of the series.
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = (\ell_t + hb_t)\, s_{t+h-m(k+1)}\]
\[\ell_t = \alpha\frac{y_t}{s_{t-m}} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\]
\[s_t = \gamma\frac{y_t}{\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}} + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}\]


2.9.3 Damped Holt-Winters
Usually the best-performing method for seasonal series. Combines the damped trend (\(\phi\)) with multiplicative seasonality.


2.9.4 Parameters summary



Parameter
Range
Role




\(\alpha\)
\([0,1]\)
Level smoothing


\(\beta^*\)
\([0,1]\)
Trend smoothing


\(\gamma\)
\([0, 1-\alpha]\)
Seasonal smoothing


\(m\)
integer
Seasonal period (e.g., 12 for monthly)


\(\phi\)
\((0,1)\)
Damping factor (damped model only)



\(k = \lfloor(h-1)/m\rfloor\) ensures the most recent full year of seasonal estimates is used in every forecast.
aust <- window(austourists, start = 2005)

fit1 <- hw(aust, seasonal = "additive")
fit2 <- hw(aust, seasonal = "multiplicative")

# Damped multiplicative (often the best choice)
fc <- hw(subset(hyndsight, end = length(hyndsight) - 35),
         damped   = TRUE,
         seasonal = "multiplicative",
         h        = 35)

autoplot(hyndsight) +
  autolayer(fc, series = "HW multi damped", PI = FALSE)

title: STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
text:
2.10 ETS in R
The ets() function in the forecast package is the master tool for ETS models. It:

Automatically selects the best combination of error type, trend, and seasonality using AICc (by default).
Can fit any combination you specify manually.

The model is named ETS(error, trend, seasonality) where each position takes: A = Additive, M = Multiplicative, N = None, d = damped trend.







Example
Meaning




ETS(A,N,N)
SES (additive error, no trend, no season)


ETS(A,A,N)
Holt linear (additive error and trend, no season)


ETS(M,Ad,M)
Multiplicative error, damped additive trend, multiplicative season



library(fpp2)

aust <- window(austourists, start = 2005)
fit  <- ets(aust)        # automatic model selection
summary(fit)
autoplot(fit)

# Forecast
fit %>% forecast(h = 8) %>% autoplot()

# Manual model specification
ets(h02, model = "AAA", damped = FALSE)

# Train / Test split workflow
train <- window(h02, end   = c(2004, 12))
test  <- window(h02, start = 2005)
fit1  <- ets(train)
fit2  <- ets(test, model = fit1, use.initial.values = TRUE)

accuracy(fit2)
accuracy(forecast(fit1, 10), test)

# Residual diagnostics
cbind("Residuals"      = residuals(fit),
      "Forecast errors" = residuals(fit, type = "response")) %>%
  autoplot(facet = TRUE) +
  xlab("Year")
