Clay
11.5AB
3.92
10


Loam
14.3B
2.67
10



Note. Means sharing a superscript letter are not significantly different at the .05 level (Tukey’s HSD).

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.1 What is Experimental Design?
Experimental design is the process of planning a study before you collect any data. The goal is to make sure you collect the right data, in the right amount, using the right methods — so that your conclusions are valid and objective.
Think of it like preparing a recipe before you cook. If you don’t plan what ingredients you need and in what order to add them, the result may not be what you expected.
Before running any study, you need to decide:

A clear research question — one that can be turned into testable hypotheses (H₀ and Hₐ).
An analysis plan — which variables will you measure? Are they categorical or numeric? Which statistical test is appropriate?
A significance level (usually α = 0.05) — decided before looking at the data.
How to avoid p-hacking — do not keep changing your analysis until you get a significant result.







Important⭐ Professor’s Emphasis: p-Hacking



Professor’s note: “If you don’t get the result that you want, you don’t modify your analysis to get what you want, or you don’t modify or look at a different variable so that you get a statistically significant result. A null result is also important and should be encouraged to publish.”
p-hacking (also called data dredging) means looking at many variables or analyses until you find a p-value below 0.05. This is dishonest and scientifically invalid. A null result — finding no significant difference — is still a valid result.




2.1.1 The Three Principles of Efficient Experimental Design
Professor’s note: “There are three basic principles to increase efficiency, and they are randomization… blocking… and replication.”








Principle
What it means
Why it matters




Randomisation
Every experimental unit has an equal chance of being in any treatment group
Eliminates selection bias; satisfies the assumption that observations are independent


Blocking
Group similar units together first, then randomise within each group
Similar to stratified random sampling — reduces the effect of known sources of variation and adds precision


Replication
Repeat each experimental condition independently
Lets you estimate experimental error; more replication = more precise estimates and greater power to detect real differences









Note💡 Analogy — Blocking



Imagine you want to test a new fertiliser on crops. If one side of the field gets more sun than the other, the sunnier side might grow better regardless of the fertiliser. By blocking (dividing the field into sun/shade blocks first, then applying treatments within each block), you separate the effect of sunlight from the effect of the fertiliser.

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.2 Experimental Studies vs. Observational Studies
It is essential to distinguish between the two types of study when interpreting results.








Feature
Experimental Study
Observational Study




Researcher role
Intervenes / manipulates conditions
Observes existing conditions only


Randomisation
✅ Possible
❌ Not possible


Confounding
Can be controlled via design
Must be controlled statistically


Causal inference
Stronger — can claim causation
Weaker — can only claim association


Example
Clinical trial (drug vs placebo)
Survey on coffee and heart disease


Ethical issues
Sometimes not possible to manipulate
Often the only ethical option



Professor’s note: “In an experiment, the researcher intervenes or manipulates the environmental conditions. So they’ve set up the conditions under which the experiment is going to be run. Whereas, with an observational study, the researcher is just observing the existing situation. Some studies are primarily observational studies — down to ethical reasons.”






Note💡 Quick Rule for Identifying Study Type



Ask yourself: Did the researcher set the conditions under which the data were collected?

Yes → Experimental study
No → Observational study




2.2.1 Two Main Problems in Observational Studies

Non-random selection — If participants are not chosen randomly, the sample may not represent the general population.
Uncontrolled extraneous variables — Variables you did not account for that may affect the outcome.

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.3 Association vs. Causation






Important⭐ Core Concept — “Association Is Not Causation”



Professor’s note: “Everybody kind of knows that association is not causation, but I don’t think everybody actually understands what it means.”
Association means two variables move together in a systematic way — as one increases, the other tends to increase (or decrease). This does not mean one causes the other.



2.3.1 Types of Association








Type
Description
Example




Statistical association
Variables are correlated; no causation implied
X and Y move together statistically


Causal association
One variable directly causes a change in the other
Sugar consumption → tooth decay


Non-causal association
A third variable explains the relationship
Coffee and heart disease — both linked to smoking


Chance / Spurious association
The relationship is a coincidence
Ice cream sales and shark attacks — both linked to hot weather



Professor’s note (on sugar and tooth decay): “Sugar consumption would not 100% cause tooth decay. It’s direct, but it’s not on its own. So we have other factors to consider — oral bacteria, saliva, fluoride, diet, oral hygiene.”


2.3.2 Common Measures of Association








Measure
Type
When to use




Pearson’s r (or ρ)
Parametric
Linear association between two numeric variables


Spearman’s Rank ρ
Non-parametric
When data is ordered/ranked, or non-linear association; tests median


Chi-square (χ²)
Categorical
Association between two categorical variables


Odds Ratio / Relative Risk
Categorical
Used in logistic regression and epidemiology



Professor’s note: “Pearson’s correlation coefficient — r represents the sample, and rho represents the population. That’s a parametric association. Spearman’s rank is non-parametric — the test statistic you’re studying is the median.”

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.4 Extraneous, Confounding, and Spurious Variables
Understanding these three concepts is essential for interpreting any study correctly.

2.4.1 Extraneous Variables
Extraneous variables are any variables other than the independent variable that may affect the result. They are not the main variable of interest.
They can be:

Controlled — the researcher accounts for them in the study design.
Uncontrolled — they are not accounted for, and this can cause problems.



2.4.2 Confounding Variables
A confounding variable is a specific type of uncontrolled extraneous variable that:

Influences both the independent variable and the dependent variable
Makes it impossible to determine whether the observed effect is truly caused by the independent variable, or by the confounding variable

Professor’s note: “Just think of a confounding variable as being a third variable that you cannot see. This third variable will influence both the independent and the dependent variable, and the relationship between these two variables that you’re studying can be either fully or only partially explained by the third variable.”






Warning⚠️ Common Mistake — Confounding vs. Extraneous



All confounding variables are extraneous variables. But not all extraneous variables are confounding.
An extraneous variable only becomes confounding if it influences both the independent variable and the dependent variable at the same time.








Note💡 Example: Alcohol, Age, and HDL Cholesterol



A study looks at the effect of alcohol consumption on HDL (good) cholesterol levels in a group of males.

Independent variable: Alcohol consumption
Dependent variable: HDL cholesterol
Confounding variable: Age

Why is age confounding? Because age independently affects cholesterol levels — as people get older, cholesterol changes naturally. So any association observed between alcohol and cholesterol could be partly or entirely due to age, not alcohol itself.
In a linear model, age would be included and held constant to control for this:
\[\text{HDL} = \alpha + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Alcohol} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Age} + \varepsilon\]
Professor’s note: “We’re only interested in the effect of alcohol on HDL levels. Age is included there — it’s held constant, but we’re controlling for that confounding variable.”




2.4.3 Controlling for Confounding







Study Type
How confounding is controlled




Experimental
Randomisation — the gold standard. Participants are randomly assigned, which distributes confounders evenly across groups


Observational
Statistical methods — linear regression, logistic regression — hold confounders constant mathematically



Professor’s note: “Randomisation is the key. Randomized controlled trials are considered the gold standard in research for evaluating the effectiveness or the safety of a new treatment.”


2.4.4 Spurious Correlation
A spurious correlation is when two variables appear to be associated, but there is no logical or causal reason for the association — it is a coincidence.
Professor’s note: “A spurious correlation is when one variable is associated with another but there’s no plausible or logical reason for the association. The relationship is perceived as real but is coincidence. The example that comes up for spurious correlation is ice cream sales and drownings. They appear associated — but it’s actually spurious. They’re not related. What’s the real driver? Weather — summer causes both.”

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.5 Key Terms in Experimental Design






Important⭐ Exam Tip — Know These Terms in Context



Professor’s note: “The type of exam question that might appear is that you could be given a scenario of an experiment, and then from there you have to identify: What are the experimental units? What are the treatments? What’s the response variable? How many treatment groups are there? How many factors? How many levels? What is the design?”










Term
Definition
Example




Experimental unit
The subject or item on which the experiment is performed
Patients, animals, soil samples


Factor
A controlled explanatory variable whose levels are set by the experimenter
Type of drug; Vitamin C (yes/no)


Level
Each distinct setting or category of a factor
Drug A, Drug B, Drug C = 3 levels


Treatment
A specific combination of factor levels applied to one experimental unit
Drug A + Vitamin C = Treatment 1


Response variable
The outcome being measured (= dependent variable, Y)
HDL cholesterol level


Explanatory variable
The variable used to explain the outcome (= independent variable, X)
Alcohol consumption









Note💡 Tip — How to Identify Independent vs. Dependent Variable



Professor’s note: “If you don’t know which is the independent and which is the dependent — put it into a sentence. Does tooth decay depend on sugar consumption? Or is sugar consumption dependent on tooth decay? That generally almost clears it up.”



2.5.1 Worked Exam Example: Drug + Vitamin C Study
Six groups of patients are each treated with one of three different drugs, either in combination with Vitamin C or alone.




Drug A
Drug B
Drug C




Vitamin C
Treatment 1
Treatment 2
Treatment 3


No Vitamin C
Treatment 4
Treatment 5
Treatment 6










Question
Answer




Experimental units
Patients


Number of factors
2 (Type of Drug; Vitamin C)


Levels per factor
Drug: 3 levels (A, B, C); Vitamin C: 2 levels (yes/no)


Number of treatment groups
6 (= 2 × 3)


Design
Factorial design


Statistical test
Two-way ANOVA (two factors)



Professor’s note: “Think of this as a 2 by 3 table — like a contingency table. The headers are the factors. The cells are the treatment combinations.”

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.6 Randomised Designs and Blind Studies

2.6.1 Completely Randomised Design (CRD)
All experimental units are randomly assigned to treatment groups. Every unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment.

Eliminates selection bias
Satisfies the statistical assumption that observations are independent
Randomisation also averages out the effects of extraneous variables

Professor’s note: “In a completely randomised design, all possible allocations are equally likely — every member of the population has the same equally likely chance of being selected as every other member.”


2.6.2 Randomised Block Design (RBD)
When known differences between units exist (e.g., gender, age group), units are first divided into homogeneous blocks, then randomly assigned to treatment groups within each block.
Example: In the osteoporosis drug study, giving the new drug to all men and the placebo to all women is wrong — it does not control for gender differences. The correct approach is to split patients into male and female blocks first, then randomly assign drug/placebo within each block.







Block Design Type
Description




Complete block
Every block contains every treatment


Incomplete block
Not all treatments appear in every block (adjusted statistically)





2.6.3 Blind Studies — Reducing Bias Further







Design
Who is unaware of treatment assignment




Single blind
Participants only


Double blind
Participants and the researchers administering the treatment


Triple blind
Participants, researchers, and the data analysts



Professor’s note: “A single blind experiment is when the participants don’t know what treatment group they belong to. That’s generally the case — otherwise you’re just introducing bias.”






Note💡 Why Blind Studies Matter



If a patient knows they received the real drug (not a placebo), they may report feeling better simply because of their belief — this is called the placebo effect. Double and triple blinding removes this source of bias from both sides.

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
text:
2.7 ANOVA — Introduction and Preview
ANOVA stands for Analysis of Variance. It is an extension of the two-sample t-test to compare two or more means simultaneously.
Professor’s note: “ANOVA is just an extension of the t-test. With the t-test, you’re comparing one sample mean or the difference between two means. ANOVA is for more than two means.”









Type
Independent Variables
Dependent Variable
When to use




One-way ANOVA
1 factor
1 outcome
Comparing 3+ group means on one variable


Two-way ANOVA
2 factors
1 outcome
Testing main effects and interaction between two factors




2.7.1 ANOVA Hypotheses (one-way, 3 groups)
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad \text{(all means are equal)}\] \[H_A: \text{at least one mean is different from the others}\]






Warning⚠️ Important — ANOVA Tells You “If”, Not “Where”



Professor’s note: “ANOVA tells you if there’s a difference, or there’s not a difference. It doesn’t tell you where the differences lie.”
After ANOVA, you need a post-hoc analysis (e.g., Tukey’s test) to identify which specific pairs of group means are significantly different.
Example: ANOVA says groups differ → Post-hoc says Group 1 vs Group 2 is significant; Group 1 vs Group 3 is not significant.




2.7.2 How ANOVA Partitions Variation
ANOVA divides the total variation in the data into two parts:
\[SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Between groups}} + SS_{\text{Within groups}}\]








Source of variation
Also called
Meaning




Between groups
Explained variation
Variation due to the treatment / factor


Within groups
Unexplained variation / Error
Natural variation within each group

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
section: 2.8 Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power
text:
2.8 Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power
These three quantities are deeply connected. Changing one affects the others. This is the foundation of power analysis.

2.8.1 Effect Size (Cohen’s d)
Effect size measures the practical significance of a difference — how large the difference between two means is, relative to the natural variability in the data.
\[d = \frac{\mu_A - \mu_0}{\sigma}\]
Where:

\(\mu_A\) = mean under the alternative hypothesis (the “true” mean)
\(\mu_0\) = mean under the null hypothesis
\(\sigma\) = population standard deviation

A larger effect size = easier to detect the difference = need fewer observations.






Note💡 Analogy — Effect Size



Imagine trying to hear a whisper versus a shout in a noisy room. The “effect” is the sound level of what you’re trying to hear. If the room is very noisy (high variability), you need the sound to be much louder (large effect size) before you can reliably hear it. With a small effect and a lot of noise, you need to listen for a very long time (large sample size) to be sure.




2.8.2 Statistical Power (1 − β)
Power is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — i.e., correctly rejecting H₀ when it is actually false.
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})\]

Low power → high risk of a Type II error (missing a real effect)
Typical target power is 80% (0.80) or 90% (0.90)

Professor’s note: “The power of the test — given that we wanted to observe an effect of 5 — is an 85% chance of obtaining a significant result when the effect is real.”


2.8.3 The Relationship Between the Four Quantities
For a power analysis, you need three of the following four quantities to calculate the fourth:



Quantity
Symbol
Relationship to power




Sample size
n
↑ n → ↑ Power


Effect size
d
↑ d → ↑ Power


Significance level
α
↓ α → ↓ Power


Power
1 − β
The target; often set to 0.80 or 0.90



Also:

↓ Variability (σ) → ↑ Power
One-tailed test → Higher power than a two-tailed test (for the same n, d, α)







Important⭐ One-tailed vs. Two-tailed Power



Professor’s note: “For a given effect size, sample size, and alpha — a one-tailed test is more powerful than a two-tailed test.”
In the height exercise: one-tailed power = 85%, two-tailed power = 76% (same n, d, and α). The one-tailed test is more powerful because all of the rejection region is on one side.
When to use a one-tailed test: Only when you have a specific, pre-stated prediction about the direction of the difference AND you have no interest in the opposite outcome.

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
section: 4.1 Simulation Exercise — Effect of Sample Size on p-values
text:
4.1 Simulation Exercise — Effect of Sample Size on p-values
The worksheet uses simulated data to show how sample size affects hypothesis test conclusions.
set.seed(1)   # ensures results are reproducible

# Case 1: Means differ by 1 (effect size = 1/4 = 0.25), sd = 4
A_10  <- rnorm(10, 0, 4);  B_10  <- rnorm(10, 1, 4)
A_50  <- rnorm(50, 0, 4);  B_50  <- rnorm(50, 1, 4)
A_100 <- rnorm(100, 0, 4); B_100 <- rnorm(100, 1, 4)

t.test(A_10, B_10)    # usually not significant — too few observations
t.test(A_50, B_50)    # may be borderline
t.test(A_100, B_100)  # usually significant — enough power

# Case 2: Very small effect (0.1), same sd = 4
# Need n = 10,000+ to reliably detect this tiny difference
A_10000 <- rnorm(10000, 0, 4); B_10000 <- rnorm(10000, 0.1, 4)
t.test(A_10000, B_10000)

4.1.1 Four Simulation Cases Compared



Case
Mean A
Mean B
SD
Effect (d)
Sample needed




1
0
1
4
0.25 (small-medium)
~100s


2
0
0.1
4
0.025 (tiny)
~100,000s


3
0
0.1
1
0.1 (very small)
~10,000s


4
0
0.2
10
0.02 (tiny)
~1,000,000s



Key lesson: Smaller effect size + larger variability = need far more observations to detect the difference.

title: Week 4: Design of Experiments
section: 4.2 Power Analysis in R — pwr Package
text:
4.2 Power Analysis in R — pwr Package
install.packages("pwr")
library(pwr)

4.2.1 Key pwr Functions



Function
Use case




pwr.t.test
One sample, two sample, or paired t-test


pwr.anova.test
Balanced one-way ANOVA


pwr.chisq.test
Chi-square test


pwr.2p.test
Two proportions (equal n)


pwr.r.test
Correlation


pwr.f2.test
General linear model



For each function, provide three of the four: n, d, sig.level, power — and the function calculates the fourth (set the fourth to NULL).



4.2.2 Worked Example: Chemical Company
A government inspector tests whether a product contains more than 4.0 ml of caustic material (the legal limit). She has n = 30 samples, σ = 1.3, and the true mean may be as high as 4.35.
Step 1: Calculate effect size
\[d = \frac{4.35 - 4.0}{1.3} = 0.2692\]
Step 2: Calculate power
pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.2692, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
# Power = 0.419
Result: Power ≈ 42% — only a 42% chance of detecting the violation. The sample size is too small for this effect size.



4.2.3 Exercise 1: Height of Students
\[H_0: \mu = 160 \text{ cm}, \quad H_A: \mu > 160 \text{ cm}\]
Sample: n = 25, \(\bar{x}\) = 173.4 cm, s = 9
Type I Error: Reject H₀ and conclude mean height > 160 cm when it is not.
Type II Error: Fail to reject H₀ and conclude mean height ≤ 160 cm when it actually is greater.
Professor’s note: “That question — what are the Type I and Type II errors — is coming up all the time, so it’s good to practice it in the context of the question.”
Power (one-tailed, true μ = 165):
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
# Power ≈ 0.85 → 85%
Power (two-tailed, same conditions):
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample")
# Power ≈ 0.76 → 76%
Conclusion: The one-tailed test gives 85% power; the two-tailed gives only 76%. One-tailed tests are more powerful when you have a clear directional hypothesis.



4.2.4 Exercise 2: Light Bulb Lifespan
A manufacturer claims bulbs last 850 hours (σ = 50). A consumer group believes the true mean is 810 hours.
\[H_0: \mu = 850, \quad H_A: \mu < 850\]
Required sample size for 90% power:
pwr.t.test(d = (810 - 850) / 50, power = 0.9,
           sig.level = 0.05, type = "one.sample",
           alternative = "less")
# n = 14.something → round UP to n = 15
Simulation to verify (10,000 t-tests):
pvalues <- rep(0, 10000)
for (i in 1:10000) {
  sA <- rnorm(15, 810, 50)
  pvalues[i] <- t.test(sA, y = NULL, "less", 850)$p.value
}
sum(pvalues < 0.05)   # Should be approximately 9,000 (≈ 90%)
Professor’s note: “Just to summarise — what we try to do here in the practical is to show the effect of sample size on the significance of the test. How, if the effect size is small, but the variation is greater, how does that affect what kind of sample size you need?”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text: 📌 Beginner-friendly notes for Week 1. Every concept is explained simply. Space is left at the end of each section for your own annotations.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
0.1 Key Topics

Research Question & Hypothesis Development: Formulating clear, testable research questions
Study Planning & Design: Designing studies with proper sampling and measurement strategies
Data Measurement Scales: Understanding continuous vs categorical variables
Measurement Quality: Precision and accuracy in data collection
Initial Data Analysis (EDA): Exploratory techniques for understanding data
Data Visualization: Using graphs to uncover patterns and anomalies
Missing Data: Identifying, understanding, and handling missing values
Outliers: Detection and classification of unusual observations
Model Assumptions: Testing whether data meets statistical requirements
Data Transformation: Modifying data when assumptions are violated

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
0.2 Assessment Reminders






Important⭐ Know your assessments











Assessment
Weight
Due




Assessment 1 — Group project: critique a published research paper’s statistical methods + presentation (pre-recorded)
25%
Week 7–8 (check Canvas)


Lab Exam
25%
~Week 10


Final Exam (on campus)
50%
End of semester



The Professor said: “Look at the learning outcomes and try to identify which question is ticking which box” when reviewing the 2019 past paper on Canvas.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.1 Research Questions and Hypotheses
Before any data analysis can begin, you must start with a clear research question. This is the foundation of your entire study.






Note



Simple analogy: Before you cook a meal, you decide what you want to make. The research question is your “meal”. You cannot collect data before you know what question you are answering.



1.1.1 What Makes a Good Research Question?
A good research question has three essential characteristics:
1. Simple

Contains one predictor variable and one outcome variable
More complex hypotheses with multiple predictors or outcomes should be broken into multiple simple hypotheses
Example: instead of studying all types of smoking (vape, cigarette, cigar, pipe), simplify to one variable — “smoking status” (smoker vs. non-smoker). This is called a combined predictor variable.

2. Specific

Leaves no ambiguity about the subjects or variables being studied
Clearly defines how statistical significance will be tested
Uses precise operational definitions that summarize:

The nature and source of the subjects
How variables will be measured
Whether variables are continuous or categorical



Professor’s note: “If you’re studying deep sea fisheries, you need to define what exactly is a deep sea fish — it might mean something different to different people.”

3. Stated in Advance

The hypothesis must be determined before examining the data
Prevents the temptation to test multiple hypotheses after seeing results
Minimizes the opportunity to “fish” for significant findings







Warning



Why this matters in practice: If your results are not significant, your supervisor might ask you to test something different. Being specific about your objectives before the study protects you from this. The Professor said: “Being clear about your objectives before you do a study is very important.”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.2 Study Planning

1.2.1 Research Plan Components
Your research plan should document:

Study Design: How will data be collected? (observational vs experimental)
Target Population: Who or what will you study?
Sample Size: How many subjects will you need?
Predictor and Outcome Variables: What are you measuring and why?







Note



Observational vs Experimental data (asked in class):

Observational — you watch what happens without changing anything (e.g., observing what food people choose in a canteen)
Experimental — you manipulate something and measure the effect (e.g., giving one group a drug and another a placebo)





1.2.2 Conceptualizing the Target Population
Before collecting data:

Define the specific population you are interested in
Establish inclusion criteria: what characteristics define who belongs in your study?
Establish exclusion criteria: who should NOT be included?


Professor’s example: “All second-year students in MTU that are male, that smoke, and that have diabetes — that is your target population. You have to be as specific as possible.”



1.2.3 Sampling Strategy
Once you define your population, decide how to select subjects:
Probability Sampling (preferred for statistical inference)

Every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected
Allows you to generalize findings to the larger population

Non-Probability Sampling

Subjects are selected based on convenience or specific criteria
Makes it harder to generalize results

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.3 Statistical Design Concepts
When designing your study, understand these key concepts:







Concept
Definition




Response Variable
The outcome you are measuring (what you want to explain)


Factor
An independent variable in your study


Level
The different values or categories of a factor


Treatment
The specific condition applied to subjects


Replication
Using multiple subjects per treatment group


Randomization
Randomly assigning subjects to treatment groups


Blocking
Grouping similar subjects to reduce variation


Factorial Design
Studying multiple factors simultaneously


Main Effect
The effect of one factor on the response


Interaction Effect
How one factor’s effect depends on another factor




Professor’s example of interaction: “If you had gender and treatment as two factors, the main effects are gender and treatment individually. The interaction effect is: does gender change the effect of treatment?”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
2.1 Measurement Scales






Important⭐ EXAM TIP — Know the 4 measurement scales!



The Professor asked students about this in class. It appears in exercises and exams.


The type of measurement scale you use affects:

How precise your data is
What statistical tests you can perform
The statistical power of your analysis
Your ability to answer the research question


2.1.1 The 4 Types of Variable
DATA
├── Categorical (groups / labels)
│   ├── Nominal   → unordered groups     e.g. gender, blood type
│   └── Ordinal   → ordered groups       e.g. pain level: low / medium / high
└── Numerical (numbers)
    ├── Discrete  → counted values        e.g. number of children: 0, 1, 2 ...
    └── Continuous → measured values      e.g. height, weight, temperature









Variable type
Best graph
Best statistics
Information content




Nominal
Bar chart, frequency table
Mode, proportions
Low


Ordinal
Bar chart
Median, mode
Low–Medium


Discrete
Bar chart, table
Counts, frequencies
Medium


Continuous
Histogram, boxplot, scatterplot
Mean, standard deviation
High





2.1.2 Why Continuous Variables Are Preferred

Professor’s exact words: “Continuous variables are preferred. They contain more information, which improves efficiency. The result is a study with more power and a smaller sample size.”

Key principle: Always collect data as continuous if possible, even if you might later categorize it. For example, collect exact age (continuous) rather than age groups (categorical) — you can always group later, but you cannot “un-group” data.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
2.2 Measurement Quality: Precision and Accuracy
These two concepts are not the same and are equally important for valid research.

2.2.1 Precision
Definition: How consistently a measurement produces the same value when repeated.

Reflects reproducibility, reliability, and consistency
Affected by random error (chance variation)
Determines the statistical power of your study
Greater precision → greater power to detect effects at the same sample size

3 sources of random error (reduce precision):

Observer variability — differences in how observers take measurements (skills, technique)
Instrument variability — equipment affected by temperature, mechanical wear, calibration
Subject variability — natural variation within subjects (e.g., heart rate changes throughout the day)

How to reduce random error: Repeat the experiment multiple times and average the results.


2.2.2 Accuracy
Definition: The degree to which a measurement actually represents what it is supposed to measure.

Reflects the validity of the measurement
Determined by systematic error (consistent bias in one direction)
Accuracy and precision are not necessarily linked

How to reduce systematic error: Calibrate instruments regularly; use controls.


2.2.3 The Relationship Between Precision and Accuracy
Think of throwing darts at a target:







Situation
Meaning




🎯 All darts near centre, spread out
Accurate but not precise


🎯 All darts clustered together, far from centre
Precise but not accurate


🎯 All darts clustered at the centre
Both — the ideal!









NotePractice Quiz (from class exercise)



Match each statement to either Precision or Accuracy:

The degree to which a variable represents what it is supposed to represent → Accuracy
The degree to which a variable has almost the same value when measured repeatedly → Precision
Increases validity of conclusions → Accuracy
Threatened by systematic error → Accuracy
Threatened by random error → Precision
Assessed using repeated measures → Precision

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.1 What is EDA?
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the critical first step after data collection. It includes:

Summarising and visualising data (graphs, tables)
Univariate profiling — examine each variable one at a time
Bivariate profiling — examine pairs of variables together
Missing data — are there gaps in the data?
Outliers — are there unusual values?
Check model assumptions — does the data meet model requirements?
Variable transformations — does the data need to be transformed?

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.2 The Anscombe’s Quartet Lesson






Important⭐ EXAM TIP — Classic exam topic. Know the lesson.



The Professor said: “You can see the differences [between datasets] only when you visualize them as a scatterplot.” Numbers alone can be deeply misleading.


Anscombe’s quartet is a famous example with 4 datasets that have nearly identical summary statistics:



Statistic
All 4 datasets




Mean of X
9.00


Mean of Y
7.50


Standard deviation of X
3.16


Standard deviation of Y
1.94


Correlation coefficient (r)
0.82



You would think they are the same dataset. But they are not. When plotted as scatterplots:

Dataset I → Normal linear relationship ✓
Dataset II → Curved (non-linear) relationship
Dataset III → Linear with one extreme outlier
Dataset IV → Vertical cluster — one point drives the whole correlation







Warning



Critical lesson about the correlation coefficient:
The correlation coefficient only tells you two things:

The direction of the relationship (positive or negative)
The strength of the relationship (0 = none, 1 = perfect)

It cannot tell you if the relationship is linear. Only a graph can show that.

Professor’s exact words: “A lot of people just say ‘oh yeah, it’s linear’, but you can’t see if it’s linear because you can’t see it. You have to see it on a graph.”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.3 Components of Initial Data Analysis

3.3.1 1. Summarising and Visualising Data
Numerical summaries:

Mean, median, standard deviation
Percentiles and quartiles
Min and max values

Visual methods:

Histograms (distribution shape)
Box plots (median, quartiles, outliers — “two for the price of one” said the Professor)
Scatter plots (relationships between variables)



3.3.2 2. Univariate Profiling
Examining each variable individually:

What is the distribution shape?
Are there outliers?
Is the variable skewed?
What is the range of values?



3.3.3 3. Bivariate Profiling
Examining relationships between pairs of variables:

Scatter plots for continuous variables
Cross-tabulations for categorical variables
Correlations between continuous variables



3.3.4 4. Data Quality Checks

Missing data extent and patterns
Outliers and unusual values
Data entry errors
Inconsistencies in the data

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.1 Understanding Missing Data
Missing data occurs when values are not recorded for some variables in some observations. In R, missing values appear as NA.

4.1.1 Why Data Goes Missing

Experimental or procedural errors
Data entry mistakes (very common when going from paper to digital)
Survey respondents skipping questions
Equipment malfunction
Subject dropout in longitudinal studies



4.1.2 Why Missing Data Is a Problem

Reduces sample size — fewer observations means less statistical power
Can introduce bias — if missingness is related to the values themselves
Complicates analysis — many statistical tests cannot handle missing values
May require remediation before any analysis can proceed


Professor’s note: “Missing data is part of the data cleaning process. Before you even look at your means and graphs, you should look at missing data first.”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.2 4 Steps to Address Missing Data

4.2.1 Step 1: Determine the TYPE of missing data







Type
Description




Ignorable
Expected — part of the study design. No action needed.


Not ignorable
Caused by some known or unknown process. Action required.



Example of ignorable missing data: A questionnaire has a section only for parents. For households without children, this section will be blank — this is expected and ignorable.



4.2.2 Step 2: Determine the EXTENT of missing data

How much data is missing for each variable?
How many complete cases are there?
Is the amount small enough not to affect results?

Rule of thumb:

< 5% per variable → Simple deletion is acceptable
5–30% per variable → Consider imputation
> 30% per variable → Consider removing the variable entirely
≥ 50% per variable → Remove the variable from analysis




4.2.3 Step 3: Diagnose the RANDOMNESS of missing data






Important⭐ Know these 3 types with definitions AND examples






MCAR — Missing Completely At Random

The probability of missingness is the same for all observations
Causes of missing data are completely unrelated to the data
✅ Easiest to handle: use complete cases or simple imputation

MAR — Missing At Random

Missingness depends on another observed variable, not the missing value itself
Example: Older respondents are more likely to skip the earnings question, but this depends only on age (which you observe)
✅ Manageable: use imputation methods that borrow information from other variables

MNAR — Not Missing At Random (also called Informative Missing)

Missingness depends on the missing value itself
Example: People with very high income are less likely to report it
❌ Hardest to handle: bias is very difficult to correct; must report it










Type
What causes missingness?
Detectable from data?
Recommended approach




MCAR
Pure chance
Only by checking patterns
Complete cases or impute


MAR
Another observed variable
Yes — compare groups
Impute using other variables


MNAR
The missing value itself
No — needs external knowledge
Report it; use domain knowledge






4.2.4 Step 4: Select the IMPUTATION METHOD
Imputation = estimating (filling in) a missing value using other information you have.








Method
How it works
Limitation




Mean imputation
Replace with the variable’s average
Can distort the distribution


Regression imputation
Predict using a regression model
May underestimate variability


Stochastic regression
Regression + random noise
Better than deterministic


k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN)
Borrow value from similar observations
More complex


LOCF (Last Observation Carried Forward)
Use previous measurement (longitudinal data)
Conservative — underestimates change


Multiple imputation
Create multiple complete datasets
Most sophisticated; accounts for uncertainty


Listwise deletion
Remove all rows with any missing value
Reduces sample size


Pairwise deletion
Use all available data per calculation
Can cause inconsistency

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.3 Practical Implementation in R
# Check for missing values
is.na(x)                         # TRUE if x is missing
sum(is.na(x))                    # Count missing values in x

# Remove / work with missing data
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)            # Calculate mean, ignoring NAs
complete.cases(df)               # Identify rows with NO missing values
na.omit(df)                      # Remove rows with ANY missing values
df <- df[complete.cases(df), ]   # Keep only complete cases

# VIM package for visualisation and imputation
# library(VIM)
# histMiss(df)       # Histogram showing missing vs observed (red = missing)
# marginplot(df)     # Scatterplot with missing data patterns on margins
# irmi(df)           # Iterative robust model-based imputation

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.4 Missing Data Scenarios — Exercise Sheet 1






Tip



These exact scenarios appeared in Exercise Sheet 1. Know them for the exam.










Scenario
Classification
Reason




Census: section only for parents (no children = blank)
Ignorable / MAR
Missingness depends on whether children are in the household


Electoral poll: half chosen at random for extra questions
MCAR
Random selection — pure chance


Plants die from high pesticide — leaf concentration not recorded
MAR
Depends on treatment group (High), which is known


Weighing scale fails for items over 5 kg
MNAR
Missingness depends on the weight value itself


Fitbit battery dies at completely random times
MCAR
Pure chance


Fitbit battery dies more often in the evening
MAR
Depends on time of day (an observed variable)


Person is unwell and does not wear fitbit
MNAR
Low activity causes the missingness

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.1 What Are Outliers?
Definition: Outliers are observations with unusual combinations of characteristics that make them distinctly different from other observations. Typically, an observation with an unusually high or low value on a variable.






Warning



Do NOT automatically delete outliers! The Professor said: “Just don’t go ‘oh yeah, it’s an outlier, we’ll get rid of that’. You have to ask yourself, why is it there? Maybe it’s a valid outlier.”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.2 Classifying Outliers
3 types of outliers:








Type
What it means
What to do




Procedural error
A data entry mistake or equipment malfunction (e.g., blood pressure recorded as 2000 mmHg)
Investigate and correct; delete if unfixable


Extraordinary event
A real but exceptional circumstance (e.g., a Christmas sales spike)
Keep it, but note it in your report


Extraordinary observation
A valid but genuinely unusual data point — no clear explanation
Investigate; may keep; requires domain expertise

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.3 Handling Outliers
Before taking any action:

Verify the value — is it a real measurement or an error?
Understand the cause — why is this observation unusual?
Check implications — does removing it change your conclusions?
Consider domain knowledge — is this observation valid in your field?

Options:

Keep — if it represents a real, valid observation
Investigate and correct — if it is a measurement or data entry error
Remove — only if it is a confirmed error with no way to verify
Analyse with and without — run both analyses and report the difference

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.1 Why Assumptions Matter
Before fitting a statistical model, the data must satisfy the model’s assumptions. Some models are less affected by violations — this is called robustness.

6.1.1 Key Assumptions for Linear Models






Important⭐ EXAM TIP — Know these 4 assumptions






1. Linearity

The relationship between predictor (X) and outcome (Y) is linear (a straight line)
Check with: scatter plot
Violation fix: transformation or non-linear models

2. Independence

Observations are independent of each other
Common violations: time series, repeated measures, paired data
Check with: study design review

3. Homoscedasticity (constant variance)

The variance of errors is constant across all levels of the predictor
Check with: scatter plot of residuals — spread should be uniform
Violation fix: transformation

4. Normality

The errors (residuals) follow a normal distribution
Check with: Q-Q plot (quantile-quantile plot) or histogram of residuals
Note: large sample sizes make this less critical (Central Limit Theorem)
Violation fix: transformation or non-parametric tests


Professor’s note: “Some models are less affected by violating certain assumptions — this is called robustness. For example, if equal variance is not met in a t-test, you can still do the t-test — it is just modified to account for unequal variance.”

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.2 Data Transformation
If your data violates assumptions, transformation can help by stabilising variance, making distributions more normal, or making relationships more linear.

6.2.1 Common Transformations








Transformation
Use when
R code




Standardise (Z-score)
Normalising variables
(x - mean(x)) / sd(x)


Square root
Mild right skew
sqrt(x)


Log
Moderate–strong right skew, positive data
log(x) or log10(x)


Square
Non-linear (curved) relationship
x^2


Reciprocal
Severe right skew
1/x


Box-Cox
Optimal transformation unknown
MASS::boxcox()









Important



Back-transformation is essential. When you transform data, your results are in the transformed scale. You must convert them back to the original units for interpretation.
Example from class: Variance (σ²) is in squared units — hard to interpret. Taking the square root gives you standard deviation (σ), which is back in the original units. That is a back-transformation.




6.2.2 Important Considerations

Interpretation changes — after transformation, you interpret results on the transformed scale
Assumptions still apply — the transformation must result in assumptions being met
Try trial and error — if one transformation does not work, try another
Alternative approaches — non-parametric tests or robust methods may be better

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
9.1 Space for Your Own Notes
Add your personal annotations, questions, and additional examples below:
 
 
 
 

Notes compiled from: Data Analysis Protocol slides (Week 1), class transcript (Aula 1), and Exercise Sheet 1 — STAT9004 Regression Analysis.

title: Module Overview
text: How to use this guide: Read each section slowly. Every concept is explained simply. The R code blocks show exactly what to type in the lab exam. The <!-- ANNOTATION --> comments are spaces for your own notes!

title: Module Overview
text:
1.1 Key Topics

What is a Data Analysis Protocol?
What is missing data and why does it matter?
The 4 steps to handle missing data
R: how to detect and handle missing values

title: Module Overview
section: 1.2 What is a Data Analysis Protocol?
text:
1.2 What is a Data Analysis Protocol?
Imagine you are a detective. Before solving a crime, you need a plan: first look at the evidence, then form a theory, then test it. In statistics, this plan is called a Data Analysis Protocol.
There are three main steps:

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Look at your data using graphs and numbers. Find any weird values (outliers) or missing data. Check if the data looks normal. Sometimes transform the data.
Model Fitting: Build a mathematical equation (model) that describes the relationship.
Model Diagnostics: Check if your model is valid - did you break any rules?

title: Module Overview
text:
1.3 What is Missing Data?
Missing data means some values in your dataset are blank - like a survey where someone left a question empty. Problems include: software cannot analyse blank rows, it reduces your sample size, and it can bias your results.

1.3.1 The 4 Steps to Handle Missing Data
Step 1 - What TYPE is it?








Type
Simple Explanation
Action




Ignorable
Expected gaps in the research design
Do nothing


MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)
No pattern - totally random
Safe to delete


MAR (Missing At Random)
Depends on other measured variables
Impute


MNAR (Missing Not At Random)
Depends on the missing value itself
Complex



Step 2 - How MUCH is missing? If more than 5-10% is missing, you must act.
Step 3 - Is the missingness RANDOM? Use graphs and tests to check if there is a pattern.
Step 4 - IMPUTE (fill in) the missing values.

Mean/Median imputation: Replace blank with the average value
Multiple Imputation: Use a model to predict the value (more advanced)

title: Module Overview
text:
1.4 R Code - Week 1
# Install packages (run once only)
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("VIM")    # Visualising missing data
install.packages("mice")   # Multiple imputation

library(tidyverse)
library(VIM)
library(mice)

# Load example data
data("sleep", package = "VIM")

# How many NAs in each column?
colSums(is.na(sleep))

# What percentage is missing?
colMeans(is.na(sleep)) * 100

# Visualise missing data
barMiss(sleep)    # bar chart - missing shown in red
histMiss(sleep)   # histogram with missing highlighted

# Simple mean imputation (quick fix)
sleep_imputed <- sleep
sleep_imputed$Sleep[is.na(sleep_imputed$Sleep)] <-
  mean(sleep_imputed$Sleep, na.rm = TRUE)

# Multiple Imputation (better method)
imputed_data <- mice(sleep, m = 5, method = "pmm", seed = 123)
# m = 5: create 5 complete datasets
# method = "pmm": predictive mean matching

# Get one complete dataset
complete_data <- complete(imputed_data, 1)

title: Module Overview
text:
1.5 Summary Points - Week 1

Always explore your data FIRST before modelling
Missing data must be dealt with - never just ignore it
MCAR = delete is OK; MAR = impute; MNAR = very complex
Use colSums(is.na(data)) to find missing values
Use the mice package for multiple imputation

title: Module Overview
text:
2.1 Key Topics

What is statistical inference?
Null and alternative hypotheses
p-values explained simply
Type I and Type II errors
Confidence intervals
t-tests in R

title: Module Overview
text:
2.2 Statistical Inference - The Big Idea
You want to know the average height of ALL people in Ireland. You cannot measure everyone! Instead, you measure a sample of 100 people and use that to make a guess (inference) about the whole population. Statistical inference is using sample data to draw conclusions about a larger population.

title: Module Overview
text:
2.3 Hypothesis Testing - Step by Step
Think of hypothesis testing like a court of law:

Null hypothesis (H0) = “innocent until proven guilty” - we assume nothing is happening
Alternative hypothesis (Ha) = what you want to prove - “something IS happening”


2.3.1 The 5 Steps

State H0 and Ha using mu (the population mean)
Choose significance level - usually alpha = 0.05 (5%)
Calculate the test statistic from your data
Find the p-value
Decision: if p-value < alpha, REJECT H0. Otherwise, fail to reject H0.



2.3.2 What is a p-value?

“If the null hypothesis were true, how likely would I be to get data this extreme?”

A p-value of 0.03 means: if H0 were true, there is only a 3% chance of seeing data as extreme as mine. That is unlikely - so we reject H0.
Common mistake: the p-value is NOT the probability that H0 is true!

title: Module Overview
section: 2.4 Type I and Type II Errors
text:
2.4 Type I and Type II Errors









H0 is TRUE
H0 is FALSE




You REJECT H0
Type I Error (false alarm), prob = alpha
Correct! Power = 1-beta


You FAIL TO REJECT H0
Correct, prob = 1-alpha
Type II Error (missed), prob = beta




Type I Error = False positive (fire alarm goes off but no fire)
Type II Error = False negative (fire is real but alarm does not go off)
Power = 1 - beta = probability of detecting a real effect

title: Module Overview
text:
2.5 Confidence Intervals
A 95% confidence interval is a range of values. If we repeated the experiment 100 times, about 95 of the intervals would contain the true population mean.
Formula: x-bar plus-or-minus t * (s / sqrt(n))

title: Module Overview
text:
2.6 R Code - Week 2
# One-sample t-test: Is the mean waiting time different from 6 hours?
waiting_times <- c(5.1, 5.8, 6.2, 4.9, 5.5, 6.1, 5.0, 4.8, 6.3, 5.2)

t.test(waiting_times,
       mu = 6,                    # null hypothesis value
       alternative = "two.sided", # two-tailed test
       conf.level = 0.95)

# Two-sample t-test: Is the mean of group A different from group B?
groupA <- c(6.5, 7.1, 5.9, 6.8, 7.0)
groupB <- c(5.0, 5.5, 4.8, 5.2, 5.7)

t.test(groupA, groupB,
       var.equal = TRUE,   # pooled t-test when variances are equal
       alternative = "two.sided")

# Paired t-test: same subjects measured twice (before/after)
before <- c(120, 130, 125, 140, 135)
after  <- c(115, 122, 120, 132, 128)

t.test(before, after, paired = TRUE)

# Reading the output:
# t = test statistic
# df = degrees of freedom
# p-value: if < 0.05 then reject H0
# 95 percent CI: confidence interval

title: Module Overview
text:
2.7 Summary Points - Week 2

H0 is assumed true; Ha is what you want to show
p-value < 0.05 means reject H0 (evidence of a difference)
Type I = false alarm (probability = alpha)
Type II = missed detection (probability = beta)
Use t.test() in R for one-sample, two-sample, or paired tests

title: Module Overview
text:
3.1 Key Topics

What is statistical power?
What affects power?
How to calculate sample size using the pwr package

title: Module Overview
text:
3.2 What is Power?
Statistical power = the ability of a test to detect a real effect when it exists. Like a microscope: a more powerful microscope sees smaller things.
Power = 1 - beta = P(Reject H0 | H0 is false)

80% power means: if there IS an effect, there is an 80% chance the test will find it.
Most studies aim for 80% or 90% power.

title: Module Overview
text:
3.3 What Affects Power?



Factor
Effect on Power




Larger sample size n
Power increases


Larger effect size (bigger difference)
Power increases


Smaller standard deviation (less noise)
Power increases


Larger alpha (e.g. 0.10 instead of 0.05)
Power increases



Effect size (Cohen’s d) = |mu1 - mu2| / sigma

title: Module Overview
text:
3.4 R Code - Week 3
install.packages("pwr")
library(pwr)

# Calculate POWER for a given sample size
pwr.t.test(n = 30,           # sample size per group
           d = 0.5,           # effect size (Cohen's d)
           sig.level = 0.05,  # alpha
           type = "two.sample",
           alternative = "two.sided")

# Calculate SAMPLE SIZE for desired power
pwr.t.test(d = 0.5,
           sig.level = 0.05,
           power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample",
           alternative = "two.sided")

# Effect size example: mu0=4.0, muA=4.35, sigma=1.3
effect_size <- (4.35 - 4.0) / 1.3
pwr.t.test(n = 30,
           d = effect_size,
           sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample",
           alternative = "two.sided")

# Simulation: effect of sample size on p-values
set.seed(42)
A_10   <- rnorm(10,   0,   1)
B_10   <- rnorm(10,   0.1, 1)
A_1000 <- rnorm(1000, 0,   1)
B_1000 <- rnorm(1000, 0.1, 1)

t.test(A_10,   B_10)$p.value    # likely NOT significant
t.test(A_1000, B_1000)$p.value  # very likely significant
# Lesson: with large enough n, tiny differences become "significant"!

title: Module Overview
text:
3.5 Summary Points - Week 3

Power = probability of detecting a real effect (1 - beta)
You need effect size to calculate power, and power to calculate sample size
pwr.t.test() calculates both in R
Bigger sample = more power
Small effects need much bigger samples to detect

title: Module Overview
text:
4.1 Key Topics

Observational vs experimental data
Why randomisation matters
Causation vs correlation

title: Module Overview
text:
4.2 Observational vs Experimental Data
Observational study: You just watch and record. You do NOT control anything.

Example: “Coffee drinkers have higher mortality rates.”
Problem: Maybe coffee drinkers also smoke more! You cannot separate the effects.

Experimental study: You randomly assign treatments to subjects.

Example: “We randomly gave half the group coffee, half no coffee, for 10 years.”
Advantage: Random assignment allows you to claim CAUSATION (A causes B).


4.2.1 Causation vs Correlation

“Storks are correlated with baby births in Denmark!”

Both storks and babies are more common in rural areas - storks do not bring babies! This is called a confounding variable. Correlation does NOT equal causation, unless you have a randomised experiment.

title: Module Overview
text:
4.3 Key Principles of Experimental Design



Principle
Meaning




Randomisation
Assign treatments randomly - removes bias


Replication
Repeat measurements - reduces random error


Blocking
Group similar subjects - reduces variability

title: Module Overview
text:
4.4 Summary Points - Week 4

Experimental data allows causal claims; observational data does not
Always randomise treatment assignment
Correlation does not imply causation
The design of your experiment determines what conclusions you can draw

title: Module Overview
text:
5.1 Key Topics

What is ANOVA and when to use it
The ANOVA model and F-statistic
Checking assumptions
Post-hoc tests (Tukey HSD)
Effect size eta-squared

title: Module Overview
text:
5.2 What is ANOVA?
ANOVA = Analysis of Variance
Testing 3 fertilisers (A, B, C) on plant growth? Running 3 separate t-tests inflates your false positive rate! ANOVA tests ALL groups at once.

H0: all group means are equal (mu1 = mu2 = mu3)
Ha: at least one group mean is different

Rule: Use one-way ANOVA when you have 1 categorical factor with 3+ levels and 1 continuous response variable.

title: Module Overview
text:
5.3 The ANOVA Model
y_ij = mu + tau_i + e_ij
Where:

y_ij = the j-th observation in group i (the measurement)
mu = the overall grand mean
tau_i = the effect of treatment i (how different is group i from average?)
e_ij = random error (assumed Normal with mean 0)

title: Module Overview
text:
5.4 The ANOVA Table



Source
SS
df
Mean Square
F-ratio




Treatment
SS_Treat
a-1
SS_Treat/(a-1)
MS_Treat/MS_Error


Error
SS_Error
N-a
SS_Error/(N-a)



Total
SS_Total
N-1





F = variation BETWEEN groups / variation WITHIN groups. If p-value < 0.05, reject H0 (at least one group differs).

title: Module Overview
text:
5.5 ANOVA Assumptions

Independence - observations are independent (by design)
Normality - residuals follow a Normal distribution (check QQ plot)
Homogeneity of Variance - equal variances across groups (Fligner-Killeen test)
No outliers - extreme values distort results

title: Module Overview
text:
5.6 Effect Size eta-squared
eta-squared = SS_Treatment / SS_Total

0.01 = small; 0.06 = medium; 0.14 = large

title: Module Overview
text:
5.7 Post-Hoc Tests
ANOVA only tells you “at least one group differs” - not WHICH ones. Tukey’s HSD compares all pairs while controlling the error rate. Groups sharing a letter in their superscript are NOT significantly different.

title: Module Overview
text:
5.8 R Code - Week 5
library(tidyverse)
library(car)

# Load data
soil <- read.table("soil_data.txt", header = TRUE)

# Exploratory Data Analysis
soil %>%
  group_by(soil_type) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(yield), sd = sd(yield), n = n())

boxplot(yield ~ soil_type, data = soil,
        main = "Yield by Soil Type",
        xlab = "Soil Type", ylab = "Yield")

# Check equality of variances
fligner.test(yield ~ soil_type, data = soil)
# H0: variances are equal. If p > 0.05, assumption is satisfied.

# Fit the ANOVA model
model <- aov(yield ~ soil_type, data = soil)

# View ANOVA table
summary(model)
# Look at: F value and Pr(>F)
# If Pr(>F) < 0.05 --> at least one group mean is different

# Effect size eta-squared
anova_table <- summary(model)[[1]]
SS_treat <- anova_table["soil_type", "Sum Sq"]
SS_total <- sum(anova_table[, "Sum Sq"])
eta_sq   <- SS_treat / SS_total
cat("eta-squared =", round(eta_sq, 3))

# Post-hoc: Tukey's HSD
TukeyHSD(model)
plot(TukeyHSD(model))

# Model Diagnostics
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(model)
# Plot 1: Residuals vs Fitted - should be random scatter
# Plot 2: Normal Q-Q - points should follow the diagonal line
# Plot 3: Scale-Location - flat red line = equal variance
# Plot 4: Cooks Distance - no points should be > 0.5

# Non-parametric alternative (when normality is violated)
kruskal.test(yield ~ soil_type, data = soil)

title: Module Overview
text:
5.9 Summary Points - Week 5

ANOVA tests if 3+ group means are equal (one factor)
F = between-group variance / within-group variance
p-value < 0.05 means reject H0 (groups differ)
Check: normality (QQ plot), equal variance (Fligner test), no outliers
Use Tukey HSD to find WHICH pairs differ
eta-squared measures the effect size

title: Module Overview
text:
6.1 Key Topics

Two factors tested simultaneously
Main effects and interactions
Interaction plots
The two-way ANOVA table

title: Module Overview
text:
6.2 What is Two-Way ANOVA?
Two-way ANOVA tests TWO factors at the same time.
Example: Battery life (response) tested across:

Factor A: Material type (3 levels: 1, 2, 3)
Factor B: Temperature (3 levels: 15C, 70C, 125C)

Benefits: study both factors together, AND detect interactions between them.

title: Module Overview
text:
6.3 What is an Interaction?
An interaction means: the effect of Factor A DEPENDS on the level of Factor B.
Without interaction: Material 1 is always best at all temperatures.
With interaction: Material 1 is best at low temperature, but Material 3 is best at high temperature. The best material depends on temperature!
If there is a significant interaction, you CANNOT interpret main effects alone.

title: Module Overview
text:
6.4 Two-Way ANOVA Table



Source
df
F-ratio




Factor A
a-1
MS_A / MS_Error


Factor B
b-1
MS_B / MS_Error


Interaction AxB
(a-1)(b-1)
MS_AB / MS_Error


Error
ab(n-1)



Total
abn-1

title: Module Overview
text:
6.5 R Code - Week 6
library(tidyverse)

battery <- read.csv("battery.csv", header = TRUE)

# IMPORTANT: make factors categorical, not numeric
battery$Material <- factor(battery$Material)
battery$Temp     <- factor(battery$Temp)

# Summary statistics by group combination
battery %>%
  group_by(Material, Temp) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(Life), sd = sd(Life), n = n())

# Interaction plot - VERY IMPORTANT
# Parallel lines --> no interaction
# Crossing lines --> interaction exists!
interaction.plot(x.factor    = battery$Temp,
                 trace.factor = battery$Material,
                 response     = battery$Life,
                 main         = "Interaction Plot: Battery Life",
                 xlab         = "Temperature",
                 ylab         = "Mean Battery Life",
                 col          = c("blue", "red", "green"),
                 trace.label  = "Material")

# Fit two-way ANOVA WITH interaction (the * includes interaction)
model2way <- aov(Life ~ Material * Temp, data = battery)
summary(model2way)

# If interaction is NOT significant, fit without it (use +)
model_additive <- aov(Life ~ Material + Temp, data = battery)
summary(model_additive)

# Post-hoc tests
TukeyHSD(model2way)

# Model diagnostics
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(model2way)

title: Module Overview
text:
6.6 Summary Points - Week 6

Two-way ANOVA tests two factors AND their interaction
Interaction = the effect of one factor depends on the other
Always make the interaction plot - crossing lines = interaction
If interaction is significant, do not interpret main effects alone
Use aov(Y ~ A * B, data) for the interaction model

title: Module Overview
text:
7.1 Key Topics

Why multiple comparisons inflate error
Bonferroni correction
Non-parametric alternatives to ANOVA

title: Module Overview
text:
7.2 The Multiple Comparisons Problem
If you do ONE test at alpha = 0.05, you have a 5% chance of a false positive. If you do 20 tests, you expect about 1 false positive just by chance. The family-wise error rate (FWER) inflates rapidly.

title: Module Overview
text:
7.3 Solutions








Method
How
When




Bonferroni
Use alpha* = alpha/k for each test
Simple, conservative


Tukey HSD
Adjusts for all pairwise comparisons
Default after ANOVA


Holm
Less conservative than Bonferroni
General use

title: Module Overview
text:
7.4 Non-Parametric Alternatives
When ANOVA assumptions are violated (especially normality), use rank-based tests:



Parametric
Non-Parametric equivalent




One-sample t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank test


Two-sample t-test
Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney)


One-way ANOVA
Kruskal-Wallis test

title: Module Overview
text:
7.5 R Code - Week 7
# Bonferroni p-value adjustment
p_values <- c(0.01, 0.04, 0.03, 0.001, 0.20)
p.adjust(p_values, method = "bonferroni")
p.adjust(p_values, method = "holm")   # less conservative

# Kruskal-Wallis test (non-parametric one-way ANOVA)
kruskal.test(yield ~ soil_type, data = soil)

# Pairwise Wilcoxon post-hoc after Kruskal-Wallis
pairwise.wilcox.test(soil$yield, soil$soil_type,
                     p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")

# Wilcoxon rank-sum test for two groups
wilcox.test(groupA, groupB, alternative = "two.sided")

title: Module Overview
text:
7.6 Summary Points - Week 7

Multiple comparisons inflate Type I error - always correct for this!
Tukey HSD is the standard for ANOVA pairwise comparisons
Bonferroni: alpha* = alpha/k (number of tests)
If ANOVA assumptions fail, use Kruskal-Wallis (non-parametric)
Non-parametric tests use RANKS instead of raw values

title: Module Overview
text:
8.1 Key Topics

What is regression and when to use it
The regression equation
Estimating slope and intercept (least squares)
Testing the slope
R-squared
Confidence vs prediction intervals
Model diagnostics

title: Module Overview
text:
8.2 What is Regression?
Regression answers: “Can I predict Y from X?”
Unlike ANOVA where X is categorical, in regression X is continuous. Example: predict plant height (Y) from plant age (X).

title: Module Overview
text:
8.3 The Simple Linear Regression Model
y_i = beta0 + beta1 * x_i + e_i

y_i = response variable (what you measure)
x_i = explanatory variable (what you use to predict)
beta0 = intercept: value of y when x = 0
beta1 = slope: for every 1 unit increase in x, y changes by beta1
e_i = error term (Normal with mean 0)

This is the same as y = mx + c from school! beta1 = slope (m) and beta0 = intercept (c).

title: Module Overview
text:
8.4 Estimating the Line - Least Squares
We find the line that minimises the sum of squared errors (distances from line to points).
beta1-hat = SS_xy / SS_xx
beta0-hat = y-bar - beta1-hat * x-bar
Where:

SS_xy = sum(xiyi) - n  x-bar * y-bar
SS_xx = sum(xi^2) - n * (x-bar)^2


8.4.1 Plant Height Example
Fitted model: y-hat = -1.03 + 3.047 * x
Slope interpretation: For every 1 week increase in age, plant height increases by 3.047 cm on average.

title: Module Overview
text:
8.5 R-squared (Coefficient of Determination)
R-squared = SS_Reg / SS_Total

R-squared = 0.9875 means 98.75% of the variability in height is explained by age.
Range: 0 to 1. Higher = better fit.

title: Module Overview
text:
8.6 Testing the Slope

H0: beta1 = 0 (no linear relationship between X and Y)
Ha: beta1 ≠ 0

If p-value < 0.05, X is a useful predictor of Y.

title: Module Overview
text:
8.7 Confidence vs Prediction Intervals

Confidence Interval for mean response: Where is the average Y for a given X? (narrower)
Prediction Interval for individual: Where might a new single Y value fall? (wider - includes individual variation)

Prediction intervals are ALWAYS wider than confidence intervals.

title: Module Overview
text:
8.8 R Code - Week 8
library(tidyverse)

# Plant data
Age    <- c(2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8)
Height <- c(4, 5, 9, 11, 12, 14, 17, 21, 22, 24)
plant  <- tibble(Age, Height)

# Scatter plot
plot(Height ~ Age, data = plant,
     main = "Plant Height vs Age",
     xlab = "Age (weeks)", ylab = "Height (cm)",
     pch = 16, col = "blue")

# Pearson correlation (-1 to +1; close to +1 = strong positive)
cor(plant$Height, plant$Age)

# Fit linear model
plant_model <- lm(Height ~ Age, data = plant)

# Summary - READ THIS CAREFULLY IN THE EXAM
summary(plant_model)
# Coefficients: beta0 (Intercept) and beta1 (Age)
# Pr(>|t|): p-value - if < 0.05, coefficient is significant
# Multiple R-squared: proportion of variance explained
# Residual standard error: estimate of sigma (standard deviation of errors)

# ANOVA table for the regression
summary.aov(plant_model)

# Add regression line to scatter plot
plot(Height ~ Age, data = plant)
abline(plant_model, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Confidence Interval for MEAN height at Age = 3.5
new_data <- data.frame(Age = 3.5)
predict(plant_model, newdata = new_data,
        interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)

# Prediction Interval for an INDIVIDUAL plant at Age = 3.5
predict(plant_model, newdata = new_data,
        interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)
# The prediction interval will always be WIDER

# Model Diagnostics - the 4 important plots
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(plant_model)
# Plot 1: Residuals vs Fitted: random scatter = good; funnel = bad
# Plot 2: Normal Q-Q: points on diagonal = good; curves away = bad
# Plot 3: Scale-Location: flat red line = good; slope = bad
# Plot 4: Cooks Distance: all below 0.5 = good; labelled points = check

title: Module Overview
text:
8.9 Summary Points - Week 8

Regression predicts continuous Y from continuous X
Fit with lm(Y ~ X, data) in R
Slope = change in Y for 1 unit increase in X
R-squared = proportion of variance explained (higher = better)
Test H0: beta1 = 0 - if rejected, X is a useful predictor
Prediction intervals are WIDER than confidence intervals
Check the 4 diagnostic plots - residuals should look random

title: Module Overview
text:
9.1 Key Topics

Multiple regression with many predictors
Interpreting coefficients “holding other variables constant”
Adjusted R-squared
Multicollinearity and VIF
Model selection with AIC

title: Module Overview
text:
9.2 What is Multiple Linear Regression?
Simple regression (1 predictor): y = beta0 + beta1*x + e
Multiple regression (many predictors): y = beta0 + beta1x1 + beta2x2 + … + betak*xk + e
Example: predict savings rate (y) from population structure and income growth.

title: Module Overview
text:
9.3 Interpreting Coefficients
KEY IDEA: Each coefficient is interpreted holding all other variables constant.
“For every 1 unit increase in pop15, the savings rate changes by beta1, ASSUMING all other variables remain unchanged.”
This is very different from simple regression!

title: Module Overview
text:
9.4 Adjusted R-squared
Adding more variables ALWAYS increases R-squared - even useless variables! Adjusted R-squared penalises for adding variables that do not improve the model. Always use this when comparing models with different numbers of predictors.

title: Module Overview
text:
9.5 Multicollinearity
When two or more predictors are highly correlated, it becomes hard to separate their individual effects. Use Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) to detect this.

VIF > 5 = moderate concern
VIF > 10 = serious multicollinearity

title: Module Overview
text:
9.6 Model Selection



Method
Idea




AIC
Lower AIC = better model


Backward stepwise
Start with all variables, remove one by one

title: Module Overview
text:
9.7 R Code - Week 9
library(tidyverse)
library(car)

data(LifeCycleSavings)
head(LifeCycleSavings)

# Scatter plot matrix
pairs(LifeCycleSavings)

# Simple models first (one predictor at a time)
model_pop15 <- lm(sr ~ pop15, data = LifeCycleSavings)
summary(model_pop15)

# Two predictors
model2 <- lm(sr ~ ddpi + pop15, data = LifeCycleSavings)
summary(model2)
# Use Adjusted R-squared, not Multiple R-squared

# Full model with all predictors
model_full <- lm(sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi,
                 data = LifeCycleSavings)
summary(model_full)

# Check multicollinearity
vif(model_full)
# VIF > 5: moderate; VIF > 10: serious

# Model selection - backward AIC
model_step <- step(model_full, direction = "backward")
summary(model_step)

# Compare models by AIC (lower = better)
AIC(model2, model_full, model_step)

# Confidence intervals for coefficients
confint(model_full, level = 0.95)

# Model diagnostics
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(model_full)

# Predictions
new_obs <- data.frame(pop15 = 35, pop75 = 2.5, dpi = 1000, ddpi = 3.0)
predict(model_full, newdata = new_obs,
        interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)

title: Module Overview
text:
9.8 Summary Points - Week 9

Multiple regression: each coefficient = effect holding others fixed
Use Adjusted R-squared when comparing models
Check multicollinearity with VIF (> 10 is bad)
Use AIC for model selection (lower = better)
Same 4 diagnostic plots apply as in simple regression

title: Module Overview
text:
10.1 Key Topics

Why transform data?
Common transformations
The Box-Cox transformation
Checking if transformation worked

title: Module Overview
text:
10.2 Why Transform?
Sometimes data violates assumptions (non-normality, unequal variance, non-linear relationship). A transformation changes the scale to fix these problems.
Like converting Fahrenheit to Celsius - the information is the same, different scale.

title: Module Overview
text:
10.3 Common Transformations



Problem
Transformation




Right-skewed data
Log: log(y)


Count data
Square root: sqrt(y)


Proportions (0 to 1)
Arcsine: asin(sqrt(y))


Left-skewed
Square: y^2

title: Module Overview
text:
10.4 The Box-Cox Transformation
Finds the BEST power transformation automatically by trying different lambda values:

lambda = 1: no transformation needed
lambda = 0: log transformation
lambda = 0.5: square root
lambda = -1: reciprocal (1/y)

title: Module Overview
text:
10.5 R Code - Week 10
library(MASS)   # for boxcox()

# Log transformation
model_log <- lm(log(y) ~ x, data = mydata)
summary(model_log)

# Square root transformation
model_sqrt <- lm(sqrt(y) ~ x, data = mydata)

# Box-Cox: find the best lambda
model_orig <- lm(y ~ x, data = mydata)
boxcox(model_orig, lambda = seq(-2, 2, by = 0.1))
# The PEAK of the curve = best lambda
# If 95% CI includes 0: use log
# If 95% CI includes 0.5: use square root

# After transformation, re-check diagnostics
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(model_log)

# Test normality of residuals
shapiro.test(residuals(model_log))
# H0: residuals are normal. p > 0.05 means normality is satisfied.

title: Module Overview
text:
10.6 Summary Points - Week 10

Transform when ANOVA/regression assumptions are violated
Log transformation is most common - fixes right skew
Box-Cox finds the optimal transformation automatically
After transforming, always re-check diagnostic plots
Always interpret results back in the original scale

title: Module Overview
text:
11.1 Common Exam Templates

11.1.1 Type I / Type II Error

A Type I error would occur if we conclude that [treatment has an effect] when in fact [it does not have one]. A Type II error would occur if we fail to detect [the effect] when it does in fact exist.



11.1.2 Reading R Output from summary(lm(…))



Output
What it means




Estimate
The coefficient value (slope or intercept)


Pr(>
t


***
Very significant (p < 0.001)


Multiple R-squared
R-squared: proportion of variance explained


Adjusted R-squared
Use this for multiple regression


F-statistic and p-value
Overall model significance test





11.1.3 ANOVA Write-Up Template

A one-way ANOVA indicated that [factor] had a significant effect on [response], F([df1], [df2]) = [F value], p < 0.05, eta-squared = [value]. Tukey HSD indicated that [group A] and [group B] differed significantly (p < 0.05).



11.1.4 Regression Write-Up Template

A simple linear regression model was fitted to predict [Y] from [X]. The slope was beta1 = [value] (p < 0.001), indicating that for each 1 unit increase in [X], [Y] increases by [beta1] on average. The model explained R-squared = [value]% of the variance in [Y].

title: Module Overview
text:
