title: Module Overview
section: 11.3 Diagnostic Plots - What to Look For
text:
11.3 Diagnostic Plots - What to Look For








Plot
Good
Bad




Residuals vs Fitted
Random scatter around 0
Funnel shape or curve


Normal Q-Q
Points follow the diagonal line
Points curve off the line


Scale-Location
Flat red line
Red line slopes up or down


Cook’s Distance
All points below 0.5
Labelled points above 0.5

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
Important⚠️ Assessment Reminders




Lab Exam — Week 13, approximately 6th of May. Professor said: “When you’re prepping for the lab exam, you’re also prepping for the final exam.”
Final Exam — timetable to be confirmed by the exams office. Check Canvas and your email.
Coursework (25%) — Group critique assignment, due Week 7.
End of Module Exam (50%) — covers all topics including ANCOVA.


💡 Review the past exam paper (STAT9004_Exam_19.pdf) on Canvas — the Professor confirmed exam questions follow the same format as the R output exercises in these notes.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
2.1 The Big Picture
ANCOVA stands for Analysis of Covariance. The Professor put it simply in class:






Note💬 Professor’s Note



“It’s ANOVA with a C — and the C stands for Covariance. That’s a perfect way to describe it.”


Think of it as a combination of the two methods you already know:



Method
Explanatory Variable Type




ANOVA
Categorical only (e.g. treatment groups)


Linear Regression
Continuous only (e.g. size, temperature)


ANCOVA
Both at the same time



ANCOVA is the right tool when you have one main categorical variable you care most about, plus a continuous variable that also influences the outcome but isn’t your main focus.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
2.2 What is a Covariate?
A covariate is a continuous (quantitative) variable that:

You are not primarily interested in, but
You include in the model to control for its effect on the response variable







Note💡 Analogy — Why Include Something You Don’t Care About?



Think of a race where you want to compare two training programmes. But some runners are naturally faster. If you ignore natural speed when comparing programmes, your results will be unfair. The covariate (natural speed) is included to level the playing field and make the comparison between the two programmes fair.








Note💬 Professor’s Note



“The categorical explanatory variables are of major interest. The continuous variable is of lesser interest — but it’s included to control for its effect on the response variable.”

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
3.1 The Experiment
This is the central example for Week 10. The Professor confirmed the exam format mirrors this kind of analysis — you will be given R output and asked to interpret it.
Setup:

40 plants split into two groups:

Grazed — exposed to rabbits for the first two weeks of stem growth
Ungrazed — left alone, then fenced

At the end of the growing season, fruit production (dry weight in mg) was recorded

The three variables:








Variable
Type
Role in Model




Fruit
Continuous (mg)
Response variable — what we are measuring


Grazing
Categorical (2 levels)
Main explanatory variable — what we care about


Root
Continuous
Covariate — initial plant size (diameter of rootstock)

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
section: 3.2 Why Not Just Do a t-test?
text:
3.2 Why Not Just Do a t-test?






Warning⚠️ Common Mistake — Using a t-test When You Shouldn’t



Your first instinct might be: “Just split the data into grazed and ungrazed and run a t-test.”
This is wrong. The Professor emphasised this in class.
The problem: the amount of fruit produced also depends on the initial size of the plant. A bigger plant will produce more fruit regardless of grazing. Ignoring root size makes the group comparison unfair.








Note💬 Professor’s Note



“This is not the correct way to proceed, since the amount of fruit produced may also depend on the initial size of the plant.”

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
3.3 The Counter-Intuitive Result
Without ANCOVA, a t-test shows that grazed plants produce significantly MORE fruit. This seems biologically wrong — rabbits eating plants shouldn’t increase fruit production!
The reason this happens: the grazed plants happened to be larger to begin with. Once ANCOVA adjusts for initial size (Root), the misleading signal disappears. This is exactly why the covariate matters.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
4.1 Design Components — What to Identify
For any experiment, ask yourself these questions:







Question
Answer for This Example




What are the experimental units?
The 40 plants


How many factors?
One — Grazing


What are the levels of each factor?
Grazed and Ungrazed (2 levels)


What are the treatments?
Grazed and Ungrazed


Is there a covariate?
Yes — Root (initial plant size)


What is the response variable?
Fruit production (mg)

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
4.2 Design Classification
This experiment is:

A Single-Factor Design — only one independent variable (Grazing)
A Completely Randomized Design (CRD) — each plant has an equal probability of being assigned to either treatment







Note💡 What Does “Completely Randomized” Mean?



It means that the treatment (grazed or ungrazed) is assigned to each plant at random — like flipping a fair coin for each one. No plant is more likely to end up in one group than the other. This is the simplest and most common experimental design.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
4.3 Factorial Design — What Would Change?







Design Type
What It Requires




Single-Factor
1 factor, 2+ levels


Factorial
2+ factors, each with 2+ levels


Full Factorial
All possible combinations of factor levels tested (all main effects + all interactions)




Example: To make this a full factorial design, you could add a second factor like Soil Type (e.g. Sandy / Clay). Then all combinations — Grazed+Sandy, Grazed+Clay, Ungrazed+Sandy, Ungrazed+Clay — would be tested.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
5.1 1. Independence of Errors

Each observation must be independent — one result must not influence another
How to check: Plot residuals in time order of data collection — a positive pattern = violation
How to ensure: Proper randomisation before the experiment begins

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
5.2 2. Normality of Errors

The errors (residuals) must follow a normal distribution
How to check:








Tool
What to Look For




QQ plot
Points should fall on a straight diagonal line


Histogram of residuals
Should look like a bell curve


Shapiro-Wilk test
p-value > 0.05 → no evidence against normality


Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
Alternative normality test









Note💡 Which Tool to Use?



The histogram is better for large datasets (you can see the shape clearly). The QQ plot is useful at any sample size. A useful guide for interpreting QQ plots is linked in the worksheet: http://seankross.com/2016/02/29/A-Q-Q-Plot-Dissection-Kit.html

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
5.3 3. Homogeneity of Variance (Equal Variance)

The spread (variance) of residuals should be the same across all groups and fitted values
How to check:








Tool
What to Look For




Levene’s Test
p-value > 0.05 → equal variances


Residuals vs Fitted plot
No pattern; points scattered randomly around zero

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
6.1 Full Model (With Interaction)
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 Z + \beta_3 XZ + e\]



Symbol
Meaning in This Example




\(Y\)
Fruit production (response)


\(X\)
Root size — the continuous covariate


\(Z\)
Dummy variable: 0 = Grazed, 1 = Ungrazed


\(XZ\)
Interaction between Root and Grazing


\(e\)
Error (residual) term









Note💬 Professor’s Note



“Zero is grazed — that’s your reference group. The same as the previous models you’ve run with ANOVA, you have one reference group.”

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
6.2 What Each Coefficient Means







Coefficient
Plain-English Meaning




\(\beta_0\)
Expected Fruit when Root = 0, for Grazed plants (the reference group)


\(\beta_1\)
For every 1-unit increase in Root, Fruit increases by \(\beta_1\) — for Grazed plants


\(\beta_2\)
Difference in intercept when switching from Grazed → Ungrazed


\(\beta_3\)
Difference in slope when switching from Grazed → Ungrazed (the interaction)

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
6.3 Model Equations for Each Group
The power of ANCOVA is that it gives you a separate regression line for each group, derived from one model.
For Grazed plants (Z = 0 — the reference group):
\[\text{Fruit} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Root} + e\]
For Ungrazed plants (Z = 1):
\[\text{Fruit} = (\beta_0 + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_3) \cdot \text{Root} + e\]






Note💡 How to Remember This



When Z = 0 (Grazed), the \(\beta_2\) and \(\beta_3\) terms both drop out (multiplied by 0).
When Z = 1 (Ungrazed), they stay in and adjust both the intercept and the slope.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
section: 7.1 Full Model (With Interaction): lm(Fruit ~ Root * Grazing)
text:
7.1 Full Model (With Interaction): lm(Fruit ~ Root * Grazing)
lm(formula = Fruit ~ Root * Grazing, data = fruit)

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)          -125.173    12.811   -9.771  1.15e-11 ***
Root                   23.240     1.531   15.182  < 2e-16 ***
GrazingUngrazed        30.806    16.842    1.829   0.0757 .
Root:GrazingUngrazed    0.756     2.354    0.321   0.7500

Residual standard error: 6.831 on 36 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9293,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9234
F-statistic: 157.6 on 3 and 36 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

7.1.1 Step-by-Step Interpretation
Step 1 — Overall model (F-statistic, bottom line):
p-value < 2.2e-16 → much less than 0.05 → the overall model is statistically significant. At least one predictor has an effect on fruit production.
Step 2 — Interaction term (Root:GrazingUngrazed):
p-value = 0.75 → much greater than 0.05 → not significant. The slopes for grazed and ungrazed plants are not different. The two regression lines are parallel.
Step 3 — Main effects:










Term
Estimate
p-value
Significant?
Interpretation




(Intercept)
−125.173
1.15e-11
✅ Yes
Expected Fruit when Root = 0, for Grazed plants. Not scientifically meaningful (Root = 0 doesn’t exist).


Root
23.240
< 2e-16
✅ Yes
For every 1-unit increase in Root, Fruit increases by 23.24 mg — for Grazed plants


GrazingUngrazed
30.806
0.0757
❌ No (at 5%)
Ungrazed plants produce ~31 mg more fruit than Grazed plants — but this is not significant in this model


Root:GrazingUngrazed
0.756
0.7500
❌ No
The effect of Root on Fruit does NOT differ between Grazed and Ungrazed

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
7.2 Fitted Equations From This Model
Overall: \[\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -125.173 + 23.24 \cdot \text{Root} + 30.806 \cdot Z + 0.756 \cdot Z \cdot \text{Root}\]
Grazed (Z = 0): \[\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -125.173 + 23.24 \cdot \text{Root}\]
Ungrazed (Z = 1): \[\hat{\text{Fruit}} = (-125.173 + 30.806) + (23.24 + 0.756) \cdot \text{Root} = -94.367 + 23.996 \cdot \text{Root}\]

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
section: 8.1 All Hypotheses You Need to Know
text:
8.1 All Hypotheses You Need to Know

8.1.1 Test 1 — Overall Model (Global F-test)
\[H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(no predictor has any effect)}\] \[H_A: \text{at least one } \beta_i \neq 0\]
How to find it in R output: The F-statistic and p-value at the very bottom of summary().


8.1.2 Test 2 — Interaction Term (Are the slopes the same?)
\[H_0: \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(lines are parallel — same slope)}\] \[H_A: \beta_3 \neq 0 \quad \text{(lines have different slopes)}\]






Note💡 Plain English



This test asks: does the effect of Root on Fruit change depending on whether a plant is grazed or not?
If \(H_0\) is not rejected → the effect is the same → the lines are parallel.




8.1.3 Test 3 — Root (Covariate)
\[H_0: \beta_{\text{Root}} = 0 \quad \text{(Root has no effect on Fruit)}\] \[H_A: \beta_{\text{Root}} \neq 0\]


8.1.4 Test 4 — Grazing (Main Factor)
\[H_0: \beta_{\text{Grazing}} = 0 \quad \text{(Grazing has no effect on the intercept)}\] \[H_A: \beta_{\text{Grazing}} \neq 0\]


8.1.5 Test 5 — Do the Lines Coincide? (Same slope AND same intercept)
\[H_0: \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0\] \[H_A: \beta_2 \neq 0 \text{ or } \beta_3 \neq 0\]

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
9.1 What It Means






Note💬 Professor’s Note



“If we have two or more possible models, each of which fit the data adequately, the one with the fewest explanatory variables is preferred.”


Simpler is better — as long as the simpler model fits the data just as well.






Warning⚠️ Important Nuance



The Professor highlighted: “This isn’t always the case. You may actually want to keep non-significant terms in your model, depending on whether they’re of scientific interest.” This is a principle, not a rule.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
9.2 How to Drop Terms

Identify all non-significant terms (p-value > 0.05)
Start by dropping the term with the highest p-value (most insignificant)
Rerun the model and check again
Continue until all remaining terms are significant (or you have a reason to stop)

In our example: Root:GrazingUngrazed has p = 0.75 — the highest p-value. Drop it first.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
9.3 Comparing R² and Adjusted R²
After removing the interaction term:



Metric
With Interaction
Without Interaction
Change




R²
0.9293
0.9291
Decreased ↓


Adjusted R²
0.9234
0.9252
Increased ↑ ✅









Important⭐ Key Exam Concept — R² vs Adjusted R²




R² always decreases when you remove a term — even if that term was completely useless. Do not use R² alone to compare models of different sizes.
Adjusted R² penalises for adding extra terms. It goes up when you drop a term that wasn’t helping.
A small increase in Adjusted R² after removing a term confirms the term was not contributing to the model.

The Professor: “The adjusted R-squared is more appropriate here as it takes into account the number of terms in the model.”

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
section: 9.4 The Simpler (Final) Model: lm(Fruit ~ Root + Grazing)
text:
9.4 The Simpler (Final) Model: lm(Fruit ~ Root + Grazing)
lm(formula = Fruit ~ Root + Grazing, data = fruit)

Coefficients:
                Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)     -127.829      9.664  -13.23  1.35e-15 ***
Root              23.560      1.149   20.51  < 2e-16 ***
GrazingUngrazed   36.103      3.357   10.75  6.11e-13 ***

Residual standard error: 6.747 on 37 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9291,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9252
F-statistic: 242.3 on 2 and 37 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
All three terms are now significant. The final fitted equations are:
Grazed (Z = 0): \[\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -127.829 + 23.56 \cdot \text{Root}\]
Ungrazed (Z = 1): \[\hat{\text{Fruit}} = (-127.829 + 36.103) + 23.56 \cdot \text{Root} = -91.726 + 23.56 \cdot \text{Root}\]






Note💡 Key Observation



In the final model, both lines have the same slope (23.56) — they are parallel. The only difference is the intercept. Ungrazed plants produce approximately 36.1 mg more fruit than Grazed plants, at any given root size.

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
11.1 Background
The Birds.txt dataset records bird population densities in 56 forest patches in south-eastern Victoria, Australia. The goal is to identify which habitat variables affect bird abundance (ABUND).

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
11.2 Variables








Variable
Type
Description




ABUND
Continuous
Bird population density — response variable


AREA
Continuous
Size of the forest patch


DIST
Continuous
Distance to the nearest patch


LDIST
Continuous
Distance to the nearest larger patch


ALT
Continuous
Mean altitude of the patch


YR.ISOL
Continuous
Year of isolation by land clearing


GRAZE
Categorical (1–5)
Grazing intensity: 1 = light, 5 = intensive

title: Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)
text:
11.3 Step-by-Step Worksheet Questions

11.3.1 Step 1 — Exploratory Data Analysis

Make boxplots and histograms for all variables
Use a pairs plot to examine relationships between variables and check for correlations
Comment on the shape of distributions — are any variables skewed?



Code
Birds <- read.table("Birds.txt", header = TRUE)
Birds$GRAZE <- as.factor(Birds$GRAZE)

# Summary statistics
summary(Birds)

# Boxplots
par(mfrow = c(2, 3))
boxplot(Birds$ABUND, main = "Abundance")
boxplot(Birds$AREA,  main = "Area")
boxplot(Birds$DIST,  main = "Distance")
boxplot(Birds$LDIST, main = "L Distance")
boxplot(Birds$YR.ISOL, main = "Year Isolated")
boxplot(Birds$ALT,  main = "Altitude")

# Histograms
par(mfrow = c(2, 3))
hist(Birds$ABUND, main = "Abundance")
hist(Birds$AREA,  main = "Area")       # Note: likely right-skewed
hist(Birds$DIST,  main = "Distance")   # Note: likely right-skewed
hist(Birds$LDIST, main = "L Distance") # Note: likely right-skewed
hist(Birds$YR.ISOL, main = "Year")
hist(Birds$ALT,  main = "Altitude")




11.3.2 Step 2 — Transformations






Note💬 Professor’s Note



“With this dataset, with this exercise, you’ll have to do some data transformations — the log of area, the log of distance.”


AREA, DIST, and LDIST are right-skewed (many small values, a few very large ones). Log transformation makes them more symmetric and improves model fit.


Code
# Apply log transformations
Birds$LOGAREA  <- log10(Birds$AREA)
Birds$LOGDIST  <- log10(Birds$DIST)
Birds$LOGLDIST <- log(Birds$LDIST)

# Check histograms after transformation
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
hist(Birds$LOGAREA,  main = "log10(Area)")
hist(Birds$LOGDIST,  main = "log10(Distance)")
hist(Birds$LOGLDIST, main = "log(LDistance)")




11.3.3 Step 3 — Fit the Full ANCOVA Model
The model to fit is:
\[y = \beta_0 + \beta_1\log(\text{AREA}) + \beta_2\log(\text{DIST}) + \beta_3\log(\text{LDIST}) + \beta_4\text{ALT} + \beta_5\text{YR.ISOL} + \beta_6 G_2 + \beta_7 G_3 + \beta_8 G_4 + \beta_9 G_5 + e\]
where \(G_2, G_3, G_4, G_5\) are dummy variables for GRAZE levels 2–5 (GRAZE = 1 is the reference group).


Code
model1 <- lm(ABUND ~ LOGAREA + LOGDIST + LOGLDIST + YR.ISOL + ALT + GRAZE,
             data = Birds)
summary(model1)




11.3.4 Step 4 — Worksheet Questions to Answer






Important⭐ Practice Questions (Worksheet 8)



Work through these questions — they are the same style as exam questions.
Q1. Write down the equation of the fitted regression model using the estimated coefficients from R.
Q2. Interpret the coefficient for log(AREA). Is it positive or negative? What does that mean for bird abundance?
Q3. Test the overall model: \[H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_9 = 0\] \[H_A: \text{at least one } \beta_i \neq 0\] What do the results imply?
Q4. Test the hypothesis for log(LDIST): \[H_0: \beta_3 = 0 \qquad H_A: \beta_3 \neq 0\] What do the results imply for the model?
Q5. Calculate Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) for the model. What do they tell you about collinearity?
library(car)
vif(model1)
Q6. Run diagnostic plots (plot(model1)). Check all three assumptions: - Residuals vs Fitted — any pattern? - QQ plot — do residuals follow a straight line? - Scale-Location — is variance constant?
Q7. Fit the simpler model with only LOGAREA and GRAZE. Plot the five regression lines (one per grazing level). Are the lines parallel? What does that tell you?
model2 <- lm(ABUND ~ LOGAREA + GRAZE, data = Birds)
summary(model2)

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
Correlation analysis vs. regression analysis
Pearson’s correlation coefficient (linear) vs. Spearman’s rank (non-linear)
Interpreting the correlation coefficient: direction and strength
Simple linear regression model and its equation
Independent (predictor/explanatory) vs. dependent (response) variables
Scatter plots and the importance of visualisation
The line of best fit and the method of least squares
Residuals and model assumptions
Sum of squares: total, regression, and error
Coefficient of determination (\(R^2\))
ANOVA table for regression
Significance testing of the slope (\(\beta_1\))
Diagnostic plots (residuals vs. fitted, QQ plot, Scale–Location, Cook’s Distance)
Log transformation for non-linear data (Example 2 – Decay dataset)

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.1 What Is Regression Analysis?
Regression analysis is a statistical method used to determine and describe the relationship between two or more numeric variables. The key requirement is that the variables must be numeric (quantitative). For example, investigating whether the yield of a chemical process is related to the operating temperature — both are numbers, so regression is appropriate.
There are two closely related concepts introduced this week:

Correlation analysis asks: does a relationship exist between the two variables, and how strong is it?
Regression analysis goes further: what is the nature/equation of that relationship, and can we use it to make predictions?

In practice, correlation is usually examined first as a preliminary step to regression.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.2 Correlation: Pearson’s vs. Spearman’s
Correlation measures the extent to which two variables are related. Relationships can take many mathematical forms (linear, curved, etc.), but in this course we focus exclusively on linear relationships.








Coefficient
Used for
Symbol




Pearson’s r
Linear correlation
\(r\) (sample), \(\rho\) (population)


Spearman’s rank
Non-linear (monotonic) correlation
\(r_s\)




Important: You cannot determine whether a relationship is linear just by looking at the correlation coefficient number alone. You must also produce a scatter plot to visually confirm linearity. Three datasets can have the same \(r\) value but look completely different when plotted (e.g., one linear, one curved, one with an outlier).

The Pearson correlation coefficient \(r\):

Ranges from −1 to +1
+1 = perfect positive linear relationship
−1 = perfect negative linear relationship
0 = no linear relationship

From the value of \(r\), you can determine two things:

Direction — positive (both variables increase together) or negative (one increases as the other decreases)
Strength — the closer the value is to ±1, the stronger the relationship

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.3 The Straight Line Equation
A straight line in mathematics is described by:
\[y = mx + c\]
where \(m\) is the slope (gradient) and \(c\) is the y-intercept.
In statistics, the same concept uses different notation:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(Y\)
Response (dependent) variable


\(X\)
Explanatory (independent) variable


\(\beta_0\)
Intercept (equivalent to \(c\)) — where the line crosses the y-axis when \(X = 0\)


\(\beta_1\)
Slope (equivalent to \(m\)) — the change in \(Y\) for every one-unit increase in \(X\)


\(\varepsilon\)
Error term (random noise)




Note from class: The intercept is not usually meaningful on its own. The slope is the key parameter to interpret.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.4 Independent vs. Dependent Variables

The independent variable (also called the predictor or explanatory variable) goes on the x-axis. It is the variable we use to predict or explain the other.
The dependent variable (also called the response variable) goes on the y-axis. It responds to changes in the independent variable.

Tip from class: To figure out which is which, put it into a sentence: “Does Y depend on X, or does X depend on Y?” The variable that depends on the other is the dependent variable.
Example (plant data):
Does height depend on age? → Yes → Height = Y (y-axis), Age = X (x-axis)
When there is one independent variable and one dependent variable, this is called simple linear regression.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.5 Scatter Plots
Before doing any analysis, always plot your data. A scatter plot is a graph of all the \((X, Y)\) pairs. It helps you to:

Check whether the relationship looks linear (if not, linear regression is not appropriate)
Estimate the direction (positive or negative slope)
Get a rough idea of strength (how tightly clustered the points are around an imaginary line)

When interpreting a scatter plot, state three things in this order: 1. Linear (or not) 2. Positive / Negative 3. Strength: weak / moderate / strong

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
section: 2.6 The Line of Best Fit & Least Squares
text:
2.6 The Line of Best Fit & Least Squares
Because real data points do not all fall perfectly on a straight line, there are many possible lines we could draw. We need a principled way to choose the best one.
The method used is called the method of least squares. It selects the line that minimises the sum of the squared vertical distances (called residuals or errors) between each observed data point and the line.
Why square the distances? Because some points lie above the line (positive residual) and some below (negative residual). If we just added them up, positives and negatives would cancel out and mislead us. Squaring makes everything positive.
\[\text{Minimise: } \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\]
where \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\) is the residual for observation \(i\).
The estimated regression equation is then written:
\[\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x\]
where hats (^) denote estimated values.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.7 Interpreting the Slope
The slope \(\hat{\beta}_1\) tells you:

For every one-unit increase in X, Y increases (or decreases) by \(\hat{\beta}_1\) units.

Plant data example:
Fitted model: \(\hat{y} = -1.03 + 3.047x\)
→ For every one additional week of age, plant height increases by 3.047 cm.
The sign of the slope matches the direction of the correlation: positive slope ↔︎ positive correlation.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.8 Residuals and Model Assumptions
A residual is the difference between what was observed and what was predicted by the model:
\[e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\]
For the regression model to be valid, the residuals (errors) must satisfy these assumptions:

Linearity — the relationship between X and Y is linear
Independence — the errors are independent of each other
Normality — the errors follow a normal distribution (mean = 0)
Constant variance (homoscedasticity) — the spread of residuals is roughly the same across all fitted values

These are checked using diagnostic plots in R.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.9 Diagnostic Plots in R
Running plot(your_model) produces four plots:







Plot
What it checks




Residuals vs. Fitted
Constant variance — should show no pattern


Normal Q-Q Plot
Normality of residuals — points should fall on the diagonal line


Scale–Location
Another view of constant variance


Cook’s Distance
Influential observations — values > 1 are concerning



You can also use qqPlot(your_model) from the car package for a Q-Q plot with confidence bands.

Exam tip (from lecturer): These diagnostic plots are likely to appear in the terminal exam. Make sure you can interpret each one.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.10 Variation and \(R^2\) (Coefficient of Determination)
The total variation in Y can be split into two parts:
\[SS_T = SS_R + SS_E\]








Component
Name
Meaning




\(SS_T\)
Total Sum of Squares
Total variation in Y


\(SS_R\)
Regression Sum of Squares
Variation explained by the model


\(SS_E\)
Error Sum of Squares
Variation unexplained (due to random error)



The coefficient of determination \(R^2\) is:
\[R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = r^2\]
It represents the percentage of variation in Y explained by X.
Plant data example: \(r = 0.99\) → \(R^2 = 0.99^2 \approx 0.98\) → 98% of the variation in Height is explained by Age.
If \(R^2\) is very small (e.g. 17%), it means the model explains very little variation — there are likely other important variables missing from the model.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.11 ANOVA Table for Regression
Just like in ANOVA, we build a table to test the significance of the regression:










Source
df
SS
MS
F




Regression
1
\(SS_R\)
\(MS_R = SS_R / 1\)
\(F = MS_R / MS_E\)


Residuals
\(n-2\)
\(SS_E\)
\(MS_E = SS_E/(n-2)\)



Total
\(n-1\)
\(SS_T\)





The null hypothesis being tested is:
\[H_0: \beta_1 = 0 \quad \text{(no linear relationship)}\] \[H_1: \beta_1 \neq 0 \quad \text{(a linear relationship exists)}\]
If the F-statistic exceeds the critical value (or if p-value < 0.05), we reject \(H_0\) and conclude the slope is significantly different from zero.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
4.1 Example 1 — Plant Growth Data
Variables: Age (weeks) = X, Height (cm) = Y
Data: 10 observations
Age    <- c(2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8)
Height <- c(4, 5, 9, 11, 12, 14, 17, 21, 22, 24)
plant  <- tibble(Age, Height)

# Scatter plot and correlation
plot(Height ~ Age, data = plant)
cor(plant$Height, plant$Age)

# Fit model
plant_model <- lm(Height ~ Age, data = plant)
summary(plant_model)
summary.aov(plant_model)

# Plot regression line
plot(Height ~ Age)
abline(plant_model)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(plant_model)
qqPlot(plant_model)  # from library(car)
Fitted model result: \[\hat{y} = -1.0299 + 3.0469 \, x\]
Interpretation of slope: For every additional week of age, height increases by 3.047 cm.
R² = 0.9875 → 98.75% of variation in Height is explained by Age.
p-value < 0.001 → Age is a statistically significant predictor of Height.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
4.2 Example 2 — Decay Data (Transformation Required)
Variables: Time = X, Amount of organic material = Y
Dataset: Decay.txt
This dataset does not satisfy the linearity assumption — the relationship between amount and time is non-linear (exponential decay). A log transformation is applied to linearise it.
Decay <- read_tsv("Decay.txt")

# Check distribution and relationship
hist(Decay$amount)
plot(Decay$amount ~ Decay$time)

# Fit linear model to original data (model 1 — likely a poor fit)
model1 <- lm(amount ~ time, data = Decay)
summary(model1)
plot(model1)

# Try log transformation
plot(log(amount) ~ time, data = Decay)  # should look more linear

# Fit linear model to log-transformed data (model 2)
model2 <- lm(log(amount) ~ time, data = Decay)
summary(model2)

# Back-transform the equation:
# log(amount) = 4.547386 - 0.068528 * time
# => amount = exp(4.547386) * exp(-0.068528 * time)
# => amount = 94.385 * exp(-0.068528 * time)

# Prediction at time = 15:
# amount = 94.385 * exp(-0.068528 * 15) ≈ 33.77

# Optional: fit non-linear model directly (model 3)
model3 <- nls(amount ~ a * exp(b * time), data = Decay,
              start = list(a = 1, b = -0.1))
summary(model3)
Key concept: When the raw data violates linearity, try a log (or other) transformation. After transforming and fitting, back-transform the equation to interpret it in terms of the original variable.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
5.1 Personal Notes & Annotations

Use this space to add your own notes after reviewing the material.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
5.2 Questions to Follow Up

Note any concepts that need clarification here.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
5.3 Exam Reminders

Key things to remember for exams, from lecturer hints.


Interpret the slope clearly: “For every one-unit increase in X, Y increases/decreases by …”
Interpret \(R^2\) as a percentage: “X% of the variation in Y is explained by X.”
Diagnostic plots: know what each of the four plots is checking.
The residual diagnostic plots (Scale–Location, Cook’s Distance, Q-Q plot) will likely appear in the terminal exam.
Lab exam is expected in Week 10–11 and will be conducted remotely.







Important🎯 Likely Exam Questions



Based on Exam 19 (Q1 and Q2) and what Paula emphasised in Week 8, the following are highly likely:

Interpret the slope in context (Exam 19, Q1b.i style): “Write down and interpret the estimate for β₁.” State the numerical value, direction, and units. For MLR, say “holding all other variables constant.” This appeared verbatim on the past paper.
Test H₀: β₁ = 0 and state implications (Exam 19, Q1b.ii style): State the hypothesis, use the t-statistic and p-value from R output, make a decision, and state the practical implication (e.g., “training is a significant predictor of sales and should be retained in the model”).
Predict the response for a given X value (Exam 19, Q1b.iii): Substitute into the fitted equation. Straightforward calculation — don’t forget units.
Interpret diagnostic plots: Identify assumption violations from residuals vs. fitted (non-constant variance) or QQ plot (non-normality). Cook’s Distance for influential points. Paula specifically flagged these for the terminal exam.
Interpret R² and adjusted R²: State what proportion of variation in the response is explained by the model.
Parallel regression lines (ANCOVA setup, Exam 19 Q2): When a categorical variable is added to a regression model with an interaction term, write out the separate fitted equations for each group (livestock present vs. absent). This directly mirrors Q2 of the past paper.
Test whether regression lines are parallel or coincide (Exam 19 Q2e): Use the ANOVA comparison of models (with and without the interaction term) to test if the interaction is needed.

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
I want you to do well on Tuesday. So I am going to teach you differently from a normal study guide.
This is not a textbook. This is a speaking coach.
My job is to make sure that when your professor asks a question, the right English sentences come out of your mouth easily — without you having to translate in your head. That’s the secret.
Here is how we will do it together:

You learn 3 simple survival phrases for English first (these save you if you panic)
You learn a 3-sentence pattern for every answer (so your brain knows the shape)
For each topic, I give you a ready-to-say script (memorise this, not the theory)
You practise out loud, every day, for 15 minutes

That’s it. No textbook memorisation. Just speaking practice.

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
section: Topic 1 ⭐ — Data Leakage (example question the professor gave)
text:
Topic 1 ⭐ — Data Leakage (example question the professor gave)

🎯 Your script (memorise this)

“Data leakage is when the training data contains information that would not be available at the time of prediction in the real world. This makes the model look very accurate during testing, but it performs badly in production. A classic example is the Carpathia field we added to the Titanic dataset — the rescue ship — which could not be known before the Titanic sank.”



💡 Simple meaning
The model “cheats” by seeing information from the future. It looks great on paper, but it fails in real life.


🔍 If they ask for more

“Another example is the ‘customer cancelled card’ field in credit card fraud detection. A customer usually cancels the card after the fraud happens, so this field would not exist at the time we need to predict.”



🔍 If they ask: “How do you fix it?”

“You should always split the data first, and then calculate things like the mean age only on the training set, not on the whole dataset.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
section: Topic 2 ⭐⭐⭐ — Random Forest vs Decision Trees (the other example question!)
text:
Topic 2 ⭐⭐⭐ — Random Forest vs Decision Trees (the other example question!)

🎯 Your script (memorise this)

“A decision tree is a single tree that splits the data step by step using the best feature at each point. A random forest is an ensemble — it builds hundreds of decision trees and takes a majority vote from them. Random forest is much more accurate because each tree sees slightly different data, so their combined vote is more reliable than any single tree.”



💡 Simple meaning
One tree = one opinion. It can be wrong. Many trees = many opinions. The average is usually right.
Like asking one friend for advice versus asking 100 friends and taking the most common answer.


🔍 If they ask: “How are the trees made different?”

“Random forest uses two tricks. First, bagging — each tree gets a random sample of rows, with replacement. Second, at each split, only a random subset of features is considered. This forces variety between the trees.”



🔍 If they ask: “What is bagging?”

“Bagging means Bootstrap Aggregating. It means sampling rows with replacement, so the same row can be picked more than once. About 63% of rows end up unique in each sample.”



🔍 Memory aid — the marble jar
Imagine a jar of marbles. One person guessing how many marbles is usually wrong. But if 500 people guess independently, the average is almost perfect. That is random forest.

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 3 ⭐⭐ — Confusion Matrix, Precision, Recall

🎯 Your script

“A confusion matrix is a table with four boxes that shows predicted versus actual classes. Precision looks at the predicted column — of the ones we predicted positive, how many are really positive? Recall looks at the actual column — of the ones that are really positive, how many did we catch?”



💡 Simple way to remember (tutor’s tip!)
Professor’s own tip: - Recall = looks at real values (both start with R!) - Precision = looks at predicted values (both start with P!)
Memorise: R with R, P with P.


🔍 Medical example (easy to remember)

“For example, in medical diagnosis, recall means: of all the sick people, how many did we find? Precision means: of all the people we said are sick, how many actually are sick?”



🔍 If they ask: “When is accuracy bad?”

“Accuracy is bad when data is imbalanced. For example, if only 1% of people have a disease, a model that predicts ‘healthy’ for everyone gets 99% accuracy but catches nobody. In that case, we use F1 score or recall instead.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 4 ⭐⭐ — Overfitting and Underfitting

🎯 Your script

“Overfitting is when the model memorises the training data but fails on new data. Underfitting is when the model is too simple and fails on both training and test data. The goal is a good fit in the middle — a model that learns the real pattern without chasing random noise.”



💡 Simple meaning — the exam analogy

Underfitting: you did not study. You fail practice tests AND the real exam.
Overfitting: you memorised the practice test answers. You pass practice but fail the real exam.
Good fit: you understood the ideas. You pass both.



🔍 If they ask: “How do you detect overfitting?”

“If training accuracy is 99% but test accuracy is only 65%, the model is overfitting. The big gap between training and test is the warning sign.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 5 ⭐⭐ — Bias-Variance Trade-off

🎯 Your script

“Bias and variance are two types of error. High bias means the model is too simple and makes the same mistakes consistently — this is underfitting. High variance means the model is too sensitive to small changes and does not generalise — this is overfitting. The trade-off is finding a balance between the two.”



💡 The dartboard picture

High bias = all darts hit the same wrong spot (consistently wrong)
High variance = darts scattered around the bullseye (inconsistent)
Good model = darts clustered on the bullseye

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 6 ⭐⭐ — Imbalanced Data

🎯 Your script

“Imbalanced data means one class is much rarer than the other, like fraud detection where 99% of transactions are normal and 1% are fraud. The problem is that most algorithms will just predict the majority class, so accuracy looks high but the model is useless. We can fix this by using F1 or recall instead of accuracy, by changing the probability threshold, or by upsampling the minority class.”



🔍 If they ask: “What is SMOTE?”

“SMOTE stands for Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique. It creates new, synthetic examples of the minority class by interpolating between real minority examples. This is better than simply copying rows because it adds variety.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 7 ⭐⭐ — Cross-Validation

🎯 Your script

“Cross-validation is a method to test a model more reliably. We split the training data into five folds. We train the model five times, each time using four folds for training and one for testing. Then we average the five results. This gives a more stable estimate of accuracy, especially on small datasets.”



💡 Simple meaning
Instead of trusting one train-test split that might be lucky or unlucky, we try five different splits and average them. More fair, more trustworthy.

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 8 ⭐ — Logistic Regression

🎯 Your script

“Logistic regression is a classification algorithm, even though it has ‘regression’ in the name. It predicts the probability of a binary outcome, like survived or not survived, using a sigmoid function. Its advantage is interpretability — the coefficients tell us which features push the prediction up or down.”



🔍 If they ask: “How do you interpret the coefficients?”

“A positive coefficient means the feature increases the probability of the positive class. A negative coefficient means it decreases the probability. For example, in the Titanic model, the coefficient for ‘sex = male’ was very negative, meaning being male reduced the chance of survival.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 9 ⭐ — Support Vector Machines (SVM)

🎯 Your script

“SVM finds the line — or in higher dimensions, the boundary — that best separates two classes with the largest margin. It only uses the data points closest to the boundary, which are called support vectors. For non-linear data, it uses the kernel trick to project the data into a higher dimensional space where separation becomes possible.”



💡 Simple meaning
Imagine two groups of people standing in a field. SVM draws a line between them that is as far as possible from both groups. If the groups are mixed together, SVM rearranges them in space until they can be separated.

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 10 ⭐ — Naive Bayes

🎯 Your script

“Naive Bayes is a probabilistic algorithm based on Bayes’ theorem. It calculates the probability of a class given the features, assuming the features are independent from each other. This assumption is called ‘naive’ because in reality features are often related, but the algorithm still works surprisingly well, especially for text classification.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 11 ⭐ — Gradient Boosted Machines (GBM)

🎯 Your script

“GBM is another ensemble method like Random Forest, but with a key difference. Random Forest builds trees independently, in parallel. GBM builds trees sequentially — each new tree focuses on the errors of the previous tree. This usually gives higher accuracy, but GBM is slower to train and harder to interpret.”



🔍 The guessing game analogy

“It is like guessing a number. In Random Forest, 500 people guess independently and we take the average. In GBM, the first person guesses, then the second tries to correct their mistake, then the third corrects what is left, and so on.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 12 ⭐ — CRISP-DM

🎯 Your script

“CRISP-DM stands for Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining. It has six phases: Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modeling, Evaluation, and Deployment. It is an iterative process — you move back and forth between phases, not only forward.”



🧠 Memory trick — the 6 words in order
Business → Data → Preparation → Model → Evaluation → Deployment
Say them out loud in a rhythm: “Bu-si-ness… Da-ta… Pre-pa-ra-tion… Mo-del… E-val-u-a-tion… De-ploy-ment.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 13 ⭐ — One-Hot Encoding

🎯 Your script

“One-hot encoding converts a categorical variable into several binary columns — one for each category. For example, the ‘Embarked’ variable with values C, Q, and S becomes three new columns. If the person embarked at C, that column has 1, and the others have 0. This is needed because most machine learning algorithms require numeric input.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Topic 14 ⭐ — Missing Data

🎯 Your script

“There are three ways to handle missing data. First, we can drop the rows with missing values if they are few. Second, we can drop the whole column if most of its values are missing, like the Cabin field in Titanic. Third, we can impute the missing values using the mean or the median — but we must calculate this on the training data only, to avoid data leakage.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
section: Topic 15 ⭐ — ML Metric vs Business Metric
text:
Topic 15 ⭐ — ML Metric vs Business Metric

🎯 Your script

“A machine learning metric measures the model itself, like accuracy or F1 score. A business metric measures the real-world value, like money saved or customers kept. A model can have great ML metrics but no business value. For example, a fraud detector that predicts ‘not fraud’ for everyone has 99.9% accuracy but zero business value because it catches no fraud.”

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:

title: Your Q&A Speaking Coach
text:
Practice goals

Said each script out loud at least 5 times
Recorded myself on phone and listened back
Can answer without looking at the guide
Practised with a friend asking random questions

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
Important⭐ Assessment Reminder — Tuesday Q&A




Format: 1-to-1 breakout room with the lecturer (Mark Hodnett)
Length: 3–5 questions per student, verbal answers only
Coverage: All material up to and including Week 6
Rules: Video must be ON. No Google, no AI assistance allowed
Examples given by lecturer:

“Explain data leakage”
“Explain the random forest algorithm, and how it is different from decision trees”

Lecturer’s note: “The questions won’t be difficult” — he wants to see you understand the core ideas and can explain them in your own words

Preparation strategy: practise saying each topic out loud in 30–60 seconds, as if teaching it to a friend. No notes in front of you — that’s the skill being tested.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
3.1 Full explanation
Data leakage happens when your model accidentally learns from information it would never have at prediction time.
Imagine you are trying to predict if someone will get sick next year. If your training data accidentally includes a column called “prescribed cancer medication,” your model will look incredibly accurate — but only because that column is basically telling it the answer. In the real world, at the moment you actually need to predict, that information doesn’t exist yet.

3.1.1 The two main types








Type
What happens
Example




Target leakage
A predictor is recorded after the event you’re trying to predict, or is caused by it
“Customer cancelled credit card” used to predict fraud (you only cancel the card after the fraud)


Preparation leakage
You use the whole dataset (including test set) to compute something like a mean, then fill missing values with it
Computing mean age on all 891 rows, then imputing, then splitting into train/test





3.1.2 How to spot it

Ask yourself: “Would this column actually exist at the moment I need to make the prediction?”
Be suspicious if one feature gives near-perfect accuracy — that is almost always a leak.
Always split the data first, then compute imputation statistics on the training set only.







Note🎯 Analogy — the exam cheat



Imagine training for an exam, but someone slips you the answer key mixed into your study notes. You ace every practice test. But in the real exam, the answer key is gone — and you fail. That’s data leakage. The model “studied” with information it won’t have on the day.








WarningProfessor’s note — the Carpathia example




“If you build a model, you should always look at the best predictors and ask if data leakage could have occurred.”

In class we added a fake Carpathia field to the Titanic dataset (Carpathia was the rescue ship that picked up survivors). The decision tree immediately used Carpathia as its top split and got crazy-high accuracy. But Carpathia could not possibly have been known before the ship sank — so it’s a leak. If one variable dominates the model, be suspicious.




3.1.3 Anticipated follow-ups

“Why is it a problem?” → Because the model looks great in evaluation but collapses in production, which damages trust and causes real business losses.
“How do you fix the missing-value type of leakage?” → Split the data first (e.g. 90/10), compute the mean only on the training set, and apply that value to impute both training and test sets.
“Give another example” → In credit card fraud detection, a “card cancelled” flag, because cards are usually cancelled after fraud is detected.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
4.1 Full explanation

4.1.1 Decision tree (single tree)
A decision tree picks the best splitting feature at each step using a measure of “purity” — typically entropy or the Gini index. At each node it asks a yes/no question (e.g. “Is Sex = male?”) and splits the data into two branches. It keeps splitting until the leaves are pure or a stopping rule is hit.

Strengths: easy to interpret, handles non-linear data, works with categorical and continuous variables, can act as a data-exploration tool.
Weaknesses: not very accurate on its own, tends to overfit, can grow too deep and need pruning, struggles with missing data.



4.1.2 Random Forest (ensemble of trees)
Random Forest fixes decision trees’ weaknesses by building many trees together and combining their votes. But it can’t just build the same tree 500 times — that would give the same answer 500 times. So it introduces variation:








Change
What it does
Why




Bagging (Bootstrap aggregating)
Each tree is trained on a random sample of rows, sampled with replacement
Each tree sees a different slice of the data (~63% unique rows)


Random feature subset
At each split, only a random subset of features is considered (e.g. √columns)
Prevents one dominant feature (e.g. Sex in Titanic) from always being the top split


Shallow trees
Trees are often kept small (depth 1–2 in some cases)
Base trees deliberately “underperform” so the ensemble averages them out


Majority vote
Final prediction is the most common class across all trees
Combines diverse opinions into a stable answer









Note🎯 Analogy — the marble jar



Imagine a jar of marbles and you ask people to guess how many it contains. Any single person is probably off by 20–30%. But if you collect 500 independent guesses and take the average, you end up within 1–2% of the real number. That is Random Forest: hundreds of “guessers” (trees), each looking at slightly different evidence, voting together.




4.1.3 The critical difference summary








Feature
Decision Tree
Random Forest




Number of trees
1
Hundreds (default 500)


Rows used
All training data
Random sample with replacement


Features at each split
All
Random subset


Accuracy
Moderate
Usually high


Overfitting
Very prone
Much more resistant


Interpretability
High (you can draw it)
Lower (but feature importance is available)


Speed
Fast
Slower (but trees can run in parallel)









WarningProfessor’s note




“Random forest typically outperforms most of the other models.”


“All of the decision trees that are created are created independently. So when I create the seventh decision tree, it has nothing to do with the six that happened beforehand.”

This independence is the key contrast with GBM — in GBM, each tree depends on the errors of the previous tree.




4.1.4 Anticipated follow-ups

“What is bagging?” → Bootstrap Aggregating. Sampling rows from the dataset with replacement, so each tree gets a different training set and about 63% of rows are unique.
“Why random subset of features?” → To force variety. Otherwise every tree would start with the same dominant feature (e.g. Sex in Titanic) and all trees would look similar.
“What is the default number of trees?” → 500.
“How does it prevent overfitting?” → Individual trees might overfit their sample, but averaging hundreds of trees smooths out the noise.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
5.1 Key formulas
\[\text{Entropy}(t) = -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i|t) \log_2 p(i|t)\]
\[\text{Gini}(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} [p(i|t)]^2\]
Where \(c\) = number of categories and \(p\) = probability of being in that class. The information gain is the drop in impurity from parent to child nodes.

5.1.1 Pros and cons







Advantages
Disadvantages




Easy to interpret (you can draw the tree)
Not the most accurate


Handles categorical and numeric data
Prone to overfitting


Can be used as a data-exploration tool
Can grow too large (needs pruning)


Handles non-linear patterns
Doesn’t handle missing data well

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
6.1 The four cells




Actual Positive
Actual Negative




Predicted Positive
True Positive (TP)
False Positive (FP)


Predicted Negative
False Negative (FN)
True Negative (TN)

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
6.2 The formulas
\[\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}\]
\[\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}\]
\[\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\]
\[F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\]






Note🎯 Analogy — the professor’s tip for remembering




“Recall just looks at the actual values, and precision looks at the predicted values.”


Recall = “Of all the people who are actually sick, how many did we catch?”
Precision = “Of all the people we said are sick, how many actually are?”

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
6.3 The precision/recall trade-off
If you lower the cut-off (e.g. from 0.5 to 0.1), you predict “positive” more often → recall goes up, but precision goes down. You catch more of the truly sick people, but you also misdiagnose more healthy ones as sick.






WarningProfessor’s note — why accuracy can lie




“If only 1% of people who get tested actually have the disease, then a machine learning algorithm can get 99% accuracy by just declaring that nobody has the disease.”

This is why accuracy is useless on imbalanced data. Use F1, precision, or recall instead.



6.3.1 Anticipated follow-ups

“When should you not use accuracy?” → When the classes are imbalanced (e.g. fraud, rare disease).
“What’s more important in medical diagnosis, precision or recall?” → Usually recall, because missing a sick patient (false negative) is worse than falsely alarming a healthy one.
“How do you change the trade-off?” → Move the probability cut-off away from 0.5.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
7.1 Full explanation








Concept
What happens
Sign




Underfitting (high bias)
Model too simple (e.g. a straight line through curved data)
Bad on training AND test


Good fit
Model captures the trend without chasing noise
Training and test scores close


Overfitting (high variance)
Model memorises every wiggle in the training data
Great on training, bad on test









Note🎯 Analogy — the dartboard




High bias (underfit): darts spread all over the board, far from the bullseye — consistently wrong in the same wrong direction.
High variance (overfit): darts scattered around the bullseye but very spread out — inconsistent.
Good fit: darts tightly clustered on the bullseye.









Note🎯 Analogy — studying for an exam




Underfit: You barely studied. You do poorly on practice tests and poorly on the real exam.
Overfit: You memorised the exact practice questions. You ace practice but fail the real exam because it asks different questions.
Good fit: You understood the concepts. You do well on both.




7.1.1 How to detect it



Symptom
Diagnosis




Training accuracy 60%, test accuracy 60%
Underfitting


Training accuracy 99%, test accuracy 65%
Overfitting


Training accuracy 85%, test accuracy 82%
Probably good fit





7.1.2 Anticipated follow-ups

“How do you fix underfitting?” → Use a more complex model, add features, train longer.
“How do you fix overfitting?” → Simplify the model, regularise, use more training data, prune the tree, use ensemble methods like Random Forest, or use cross-validation.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
8.1 Why it matters
If 99% of transactions are legitimate and 1% are fraud, a model that always predicts “legitimate” gets 99% accuracy — but catches zero fraud. That makes accuracy a worthless business metric here.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
8.2 Ways to handle it







Technique
What it does




Use F1 / precision / recall
Better metrics than accuracy


Change the probability threshold
Lower it from 0.5 to e.g. 0.3 to predict positive more often


Upsample the minority class
Duplicate minority rows until balanced


Downsample the majority class
Remove majority rows until balanced


Use SMOTE
Generate synthetic minority examples by interpolating between real ones


Weight the loss function
Penalise false negatives more than false positives









WarningProfessor’s note




“When data is imbalanced, the person is focused on recall, not accuracy. They want a model with high recall, even if it has low precision.”

And on SMOTE: > “SMOTE stands for Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique… it creates synthetic data, not just copies the existing records.”



8.2.1 Anticipated follow-ups

“What does SMOTE stand for?” → Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique.
“Why not just duplicate minority rows?” → Duplicating can cause overfitting on those exact rows. SMOTE creates new synthetic examples by interpolating between real minority-class neighbours, giving more variety.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
9.1 How 5-fold cross-validation works

Split training data into 5 equal folds.
Train on folds 1–4, test on fold 5.
Train on folds 1–3 and 5, test on fold 4.
Continue until each fold has been the test fold once.
Average the 5 scores for the final estimate.







Note🎯 Analogy — rotating judges



Imagine judging a baking contest with 5 bakers. Instead of 1 judge scoring all 5 (biased), you have 5 rounds where each baker’s cake is scored by 4 judges while the 5th baker judges. Over 5 rounds, every baker’s cake is evaluated and every judge gets a turn. Averaging the scores gives a fairer, less volatile estimate.



9.1.1 Why it matters
A single 90/10 split can give accuracy anywhere from 73% to 81% depending on which rows land in the test set — especially on small datasets like Titanic. Cross-validation gives a stable estimate by using every row for both training and evaluation (across different folds).






WarningProfessor’s note




“Cross-validation allows you, with a small data set, to use all your rows within your model training and all your rows to evaluate the model, so it should give you a better, unbiased estimate of the model accuracy.”





9.1.2 Anticipated follow-ups

“What is k?” → The number of folds, usually 5 or 10.
“Should you still have a test set?” → Yes. Cross-validation uses only the training data. A separate test set gives the final unbiased estimate.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
10.1 The idea
Linear regression: \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + ...\) Logistic regression wraps this in a sigmoid so the output is a probability between 0 and 1.

10.1.1 Interpreting coefficients (Titanic example)

Sex = male: large negative coefficient → being male decreases survival probability.
Pclass: negative coefficient → higher class number (3rd class) decreases survival probability.



10.1.2 Limitations

Assumes a linear relationship between predictors and the log-odds.
Cannot handle categorical data directly — needs one-hot encoding (though R’s glm does it automatically).

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
11.1 Two key ideas

Maximum margin: find the widest gap between the two classes.
Kernel trick: implicitly project data into higher dimensions (e.g. via polynomial, radial, or sigmoid kernels) to handle non-linear patterns without actually computing the higher-dimensional features.


11.1.1 Pros and cons







Pros
Cons




Good accuracy with little tuning
Doesn’t scale to very large datasets


Robust to outliers
Harder to interpret than logistic regression


Strong statistical foundation
Must choose a kernel

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
12.1 Bayes’ theorem
\[P(\text{class} \mid \text{features}) = \frac{P(\text{features} \mid \text{class}) \times P(\text{class})}{P(\text{features})}\]






Note🎯 Analogy — the doctor’s diagnosis



A doctor estimates: “Given that a patient has a fever AND a cough, how likely is it they have flu?” Naive Bayes multiplies the probability of each symptom occurring with flu, assuming the symptoms are independent — which isn’t quite true, but it usually still works.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
section: 14.1 GBM vs Random Forest — the key distinction
text:
14.1 GBM vs Random Forest — the key distinction








Feature
Random Forest
GBM




Tree building
Parallel (independent)
Sequential (each depends on previous)


What each tree sees
Random sample of rows/features
Errors of the previous trees


Computation
Can parallelise
Cannot parallelise


Interpretability
Feature importance easy
Much harder to interpret


Input data
Handles categorical
Needs numeric (one-hot encoded)









Note🎯 Analogy — marble jar guessers, round 2




Random Forest: 500 people guess independently; you take the average.
GBM: The first person guesses. You tell the second “the previous one was 200 too high” and they focus on correcting it. The third corrects the remaining error. Each guesser depends on the previous one’s mistakes.

title: Q&A Assessment Preparation: Weeks 1–6
text:
15.1 The six phases



Phase
Question it answers




Business Understanding
What does the business need?


Data Understanding
What data do we have? Is it clean?


Data Preparation
How do we organise the data for modelling?


Modeling
What techniques should we apply?


Evaluation
Which model best meets the business objectives?


Deployment
How do stakeholders access the results?









WarningProfessor’s note




“The sequence of the phases is not rigid. Moving back and forth between different phases is always required.”

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
NoteAbout These Notes



These notes are a beginner-friendly summary of Week 6 content from STAT9013. They cover Gradient Boosted Machines, Data Leakage, Sampling & Cross-Validation, Imbalanced Datasets, and an introduction to R Shiny. Leave space below each section for your own annotations and questions.

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
2.1 Gradient Boosted Machines (GBMs)

2.1.1 What are they?
Gradient Boosted Machines (GBMs) are a family of machine learning algorithms that are among the most powerful tools available today. Together with Random Forests, GBMs tend to outperform most other algorithms on tabular data.
Both Random Forest and GBMs use many decision trees internally, but they build them in very different ways:









Random Forest
GBM




How trees are built
Independently, all at the same time
Sequentially — one after the other


How data is selected
Bagging (random sampling with replacement)
Focuses on errors from the previous tree


Easy to interpret?
Somewhat (feature importance available)
No — very difficult to interpret


Speed
Faster (trees can be built in parallel)
Slower (trees must be built one by one)





2.1.2 Bagging (for Random Forest)
Bagging is short for Bootstrap Aggregating. The idea is to randomly select rows from your dataset with replacement (meaning the same row can be picked more than once). If you have 100 rows and you sample 100 rows with replacement, approximately 63% of rows will be unique — the rest are duplicates. This gives each decision tree a slightly different view of the data.


2.1.3 How GBMs work (step by step)

Train the first decision tree on the full training data
Look at which records the model got wrong (the errors, called residuals)
Build a second decision tree that focuses on those errors
Repeat: each new tree tries to fix the mistakes of the previous tree
Combine all trees to make the final prediction


💡 Analogy: Imagine a student taking a practice test, then reviewing only the questions they got wrong, then taking another test focused on those weak areas. GBMs do exactly this, but with decision trees.



2.1.4 Disadvantages of GBMs

Hard to interpret — you cannot easily see which features matter most
Computationally expensive — trees must be built one by one (cannot be parallelised)
Require numeric input — all data must be converted to numbers first (using one-hot encoding)
Benefit a lot from hyperparameter tuning (unlike Random Forests which often work well “out of the box”)



2.1.5 R Code: Fitting a GBM with LightGBM
library(lightgbm)

# Step 1: One-hot encode the training data (GBMs need all-numeric input)
dmy <- dummyVars(" ~ .", data = train_data)
trsf <- data.frame(predict(dmy, newdata = train_data))
tesf <- data.frame(predict(dmy, newdata = test_data))
tesf$Survived <- NULL  # Remove the target from test data

# Step 2: Separate the target (label) from the features
labels <- trsf$Survived
trsf$Survived <- NULL

# Step 3: Convert to matrix (required by LightGBM)
m_trsf <- as.matrix(trsf)

# Step 4: Train the GBM model
gbm_model <- lightgbm(
  data  = m_trsf,
  label = labels
)

# Step 5: Make predictions on the test set
pr <- predict(gbm_model, newdata = as.matrix(tesf))
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)   # Convert probabilities to class labels
pr <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))

# Step 6: Evaluate accuracy
tab      <- table(pr, actuals)
accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
print(accuracy)  # ~76.7% on the Titanic dataset






TipYour Notes



Add your own observations about GBMs here…

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
2.2 Data Leakage

2.2.1 What is it?
Data leakage happens when the data used to train a model contains information about the target variable that would not actually be available at the time you make a real prediction in production.
The result: the model looks very accurate during testing, but performs poorly in the real world.


2.2.2 Two types of data leakage
1. Target leakage — a predictor variable was created or updated after the target event occurred.

Example (medical): You are predicting whether a patient has a disease. One of your features is “prescribed antiviral drug X”. But the patient was only prescribed that drug because they were already diagnosed. So this variable was set after the event you are trying to predict.


Example (Titanic): We created a fake field called Carpathia (the ship that rescued survivors). If someone is in the Carpathia list, they survived. This field could only be known after the Titanic sank — it cannot be used to predict survival.

2. Data preparation leakage — you use information from the test set to prepare features in the training set.

Example: If you calculate the mean age from the entire dataset (train + test) and use that to fill in missing ages, the test set has “leaked” into your training preparation. You should always calculate statistics like the mean only on the training data, then apply that value to both sets.



2.2.3 How to spot data leakage

A single feature produces a suspiciously high accuracy by itself
The most important feature in your model is something that logically could not be known at prediction time
Your model is very simple (e.g. only 2–3 splits) but shows very high accuracy



2.2.4 R Code: Correct way to impute missing values (no leakage)
# CORRECT: calculate the mean from training data only
meanAge <- mean(train_data$Age, na.rm = TRUE)

# Apply that value to BOTH training and test sets
train_data[is.na(train_data$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge
test_data[is.na(test_data$Age), ]$Age   <- meanAge






TipYour Notes



Write examples of data leakage you can think of in real life…

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
2.3 Sampling and Cross-Validation

2.3.1 The problem with a single train/test split
When we split our data 90% train / 10% test and use set.seed(42) for reproducibility, the results we get depend on which 10% of records end up in the test set. For small datasets, this can cause the reported accuracy to vary a lot depending on the random split.
# Running the same model with different seeds shows the variability:
# [1] 80.00  73.33  74.44  77.78  76.67  83.33  81.11  75.56  80.00  71.11  76.67
The accuracy above ranges from 71% to 83% — a big swing caused only by which records happened to be in the test set!


2.3.2 Why set.seed()?
Using set.seed(42) (or any fixed number) at the start of your code ensures that your results are reproducible — anyone who runs your code with the same data will get the same results. The number 42 has no special meaning; it is just a popular choice in the data science community (a reference to The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy).


2.3.3 Cross-Validation
K-fold cross-validation is a better way to estimate model accuracy, especially for small datasets. Here is how it works:

Split your data into k equal groups (called folds)
Train k separate models. Each model uses k−1 folds for training and 1 fold for testing
Average the accuracy across all k models

The advantage is that every record gets used for testing exactly once, giving a more stable and unbiased estimate of accuracy.

Example with k=5 (5-fold cross-validation): - Model 1 trains on folds 2,3,4,5 — tests on fold 1 - Model 2 trains on folds 1,3,4,5 — tests on fold 2 - … and so on



2.3.4 R Code: Cross-Validation with caret
library(caret)

# Run 5-fold cross-validation using a decision tree (rpart)
model <- train(
  as.factor(Survived) ~ .,
  data      = train_data,
  method    = "rpart",
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5)
)

# Evaluate on the test set
pr       <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "raw")
pr       <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))
tab      <- table(pr, actuals)
accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
print(accuracy)  # ~75.6%






TipYour Notes



Add notes about when you would and would not use cross-validation…

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
2.4 Imbalanced Datasets

2.4.1 What is the problem?
In many real-world classification problems, one class is much rarer than the other. Examples:

Medical diagnosis: 99% of people screened do not have the disease
Fraud detection: less than 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent
Churn analysis: most customers stay each month
Online advertising: click-through rates can be less than 0.1%

The problem: most machine learning algorithms assume roughly equal numbers of each class. When the data is imbalanced, the algorithm often just predicts the majority class every time — and still gets a high accuracy!

Example: If 92% of records are “did not survive”, a model that always predicts “did not survive” achieves 92% accuracy — but it is completely useless at finding survivors.



2.4.2 Three strategies to handle imbalanced data

2.4.2.1 Strategy 1: Change the probability cut-off threshold
By default, a model predicts class 1 if the probability is ≥ 0.5. You can lower this threshold so that more records are classified as the minority class.
# Default threshold: 0.5
# Increase recall by lowering the threshold to 0.1
pr <- predict(rpart, newdata = test_data)        # Get raw probabilities
pr <- ifelse(pr[, 1] > 0.9, 0, 1)               # Class 0 if P(class=0) > 0.9
pr <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))


2.4.2.2 Strategy 2: Assign a higher penalty to minority class errors
Some algorithms allow you to say: “Penalise a False Negative more heavily than a False Positive.” This forces the model to try harder to detect the rare class.
# In rpart, use the 'loss' matrix:
# loss[i,j] = penalty for predicting class j when true class is i
# Here: False Negatives (predicting 0 when truth is 1) cost 5x more than False Positives
rpart_model <- rpart(
  Survived ~ .,
  data   = train_data,
  method = "class",
  parms  = list(loss = matrix(c(0, 1, 5, 0), byrow = TRUE, nrow = 2))
)


2.4.2.3 Strategy 3: Resampling the training data
The most widely used approach is to change the training data itself so the classes are more balanced:

Upsampling (oversampling): duplicate rows from the minority class until it matches the majority class size
Downsampling (undersampling): remove rows from the majority class

# Split training data by class
train_data0 <- train_data[train_data$Survived == 0, ]  # Majority class (549 rows)
train_data1 <- train_data[train_data$Survived == 1, ]  # Minority class (40 rows)

nrows <- 300  # Target size for each class

# Downsample the majority class (take 300 from 494)
sample0     <- sample(nrow(train_data0), nrows)
train_data0 <- train_data0[sample0, ]

# Upsample the minority class (take 300 from 40, with replacement = duplicates)
sample1     <- sample(nrow(train_data1), nrows, replace = TRUE)
train_data1 <- train_data1[sample1, ]

# Combine and shuffle
train_data <- rbind(train_data0, train_data1)
train_data <- train_data[sample(nrow(train_data)), ]  # Shuffle rows

table(train_data$Survived)
# 0   1
# 300 300  <-- Now balanced!



2.4.3 The Precision–Recall Trade-Off
When you try to increase recall (finding more of the minority class), you will usually decrease precision (some of your positive predictions will be wrong). This is a fundamental trade-off.







Term
Plain English




Recall (Sensitivity)
Of all the actual positives, how many did the model find?


Precision
Of all the records the model flagged as positive, how many were actually positive?


High recall, low precision
Find most of the sick/fraud cases, but also flag many healthy/legitimate ones


Low recall, high precision
Only flag cases you are very sure about, but miss many real positives




💡 A business user may say: “I need the model to find as many fraud cases as possible. We will then review them manually.” This means they want high recall, and they accept that some clean transactions will also be flagged for manual review.







ImportantKey Insight: Accuracy Is Not Always the Right Goal



When data is imbalanced and you care about the rare class, accuracy is a misleading metric. Focus on recall (and precision) instead.








TipYour Notes



Can you think of a business problem where you would want high precision instead of high recall?

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
2.5 R Shiny (Introduction)
R Shiny is an R framework for building interactive web applications. It allows you to:

Create web dashboards and visualisations without knowing HTML/CSS/JavaScript
Let users interact with the data in real time (sliders, dropdowns, inputs)
Share your analysis as a live web link


2.5.1 How to create your first Shiny app

In RStudio: File → New File → Shiny Web App
Give your app a name (e.g. my_shiny_app)
Select Multiple File
Click Create
Click Run App and then Open in Browser

You will see a working histogram app where a slider controls the number of bins — and the graph updates in real time.


2.5.2 Useful resources



Resource
Link




Shiny tutorial
https://shiny.posit.co/r/getstarted/shiny-basics/lesson1/index.html


Shiny gallery (examples + code)
https://shiny.posit.co/r/gallery/


Mastering Shiny (free book)
https://mastering-shiny.org/index.html


Dashboard examples
https://rstudio.github.io/shinydashboard/examples.html









TipYour Notes



What kind of data would you like to visualise with a Shiny app?

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
3.1 Accuracy
\[
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct predictions}}{\text{Total predictions}} \times 100
\]

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
3.2 Precision
\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
3.3 Recall (Sensitivity)
\[
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\]
Where:

TP = True Positives (correctly predicted positives)
FP = False Positives (predicted positive, actually negative)
FN = False Negatives (predicted negative, actually positive)

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
section: 4.1 Example 1: GBM on the Titanic Dataset
text:
4.1 Example 1: GBM on the Titanic Dataset
The lightgbm package was used to train a Gradient Boosted Machine on the Titanic survival dataset. After one-hot encoding all categorical variables and converting to a matrix, the model achieved approximately 76.7% accuracy on the test set.

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
section: 4.2 Example 2: Data Leakage — the Carpathia Field
text:
4.2 Example 2: Data Leakage — the Carpathia Field
A fake field Carpathia was added to the Titanic dataset. This field was strongly correlated with survival (survivors boarded the rescue ship Carpathia). Including this field:

Boosted accuracy from ~76% to 91%
Produced a very simple decision tree using only 3 variables
The top split was Carpathia = yes/no — a clear warning sign

Lesson: Always ask: Could this variable have been recorded before the event I am predicting?

title: STAT9013 – Week 6 Study Guide
text:
4.3 Example 3: Cross-Validation Stability
Running the same decision tree model with 10 different random seeds produced accuracies ranging from 71% to 83%. Using 5-fold cross-validation with caret gave a more stable estimate of ~76%.
