title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
TipHow to use this guide



This guide is organised the same way the class was taught. Read in order. Every green ⭐ callout is an exam-relevant idea the professor emphasised, often more than once. Code blocks are set to eval: false so the document renders quickly — you can switch them on locally to experiment.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
📋 Assessment Reminders






ImportantExam-relevant signals from class



The professor did not announce a new formal assessment in the Week 5 recording. Carry forward the general principles stated in earlier weeks:

Concepts to be exam-ready on this week: precision vs recall, linear vs logistic regression, log transformation, one-hot encoding, factor vs numeric variables, sigmoid function, interpreting GLM output (coefficients + significance), stepwise feature removal, and feature engineering on the Titanic dataset.
Homework set in class: Read Chapter 3 of An Introduction to Statistical Learning (ISLR). The professor said “try to do the labs if possible” from that book.
Next class note: The class two weeks after Week 5 falls on St Patrick’s Day (a bank holiday in Ireland). The professor said he would pre-record that session rather than run it live.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
🎯 Key Topics Covered

Precision & recall recap — why translating metrics into business language matters
Linear regression — formula, coefficients, assumptions and diagnostic plots
Data transformation — using log() to fix non-linear relationships
One-hot encoding — converting categorical text to numeric columns
Factor vs numeric — why Pclass must be a factor in R
Logistic regression — linear regression + sigmoid = classification probability
Reference categories — what is absorbed into the intercept and why
Statistical significance — reading p-values and the *** significance codes
Stepwise feature removal — removing one insignificant variable at a time
Feature engineering — extracting Title from names, smarter age imputation, binary flags
Manual prediction — reproducing predict() output using the coefficients + sigmoid

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
1 Recap: Precision and Recall
The class opened with a recap of last week’s classification metrics because the professor felt they were central enough to revisit.

1.1 The four outcomes of a binary classifier
When a model predicts a yes/no outcome (survived/not survived, fraud/not fraud, sick/healthy), there are four possible results. Two are correct and two are wrong:







Outcome
Meaning




True Positive (TP)
Model said yes, reality was yes — ✅ correct


True Negative (TN)
Model said no, reality was no — ✅ correct


False Positive (FP)
Model said yes, reality was no — ❌ false alarm


False Negative (FN)
Model said no, reality was yes — ❌ missed case





1.2 Recall and precision — formulas and intuition
\[
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \qquad \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]








Metric
Question it answers
Memory hook




Recall (a.k.a. sensitivity)
Of the actual positive cases, how many did we find?
“Did we re-call (remember) all of them?”


Precision
Of the cases we predicted as positive, how many were correct?
“How precise were our yes answers?”









NoteAnalogy — the fishing net



Imagine you’re fishing in a lake that contains 100 trout.

Recall = how many of the 100 trout you actually caught. If you caught 60, recall is 60%.
Precision = of everything in your net (trout, boots, bottles), how much was actually trout. If your net contains 60 trout and 40 pieces of rubbish, precision is 60%.

A wide net catches more trout (high recall) but also more rubbish (low precision). A small, targeted net catches only trout (high precision) but misses many (low recall).




1.3 The precision–recall trade-off
You can’t usually max both at once. Lowering the classification threshold (from 0.5 → 0.2) flags more cases as positive, which raises recall but drops precision.








Scenario
When it’s useful
Watch out for




High recall, low precision
Cancer screening, fraud detection — missing a positive is very costly
Many false alarms → need a secondary filter (e.g., a human reviewer)


High precision, low recall
When false positives carry a big cost and missing some positives is acceptable
You leave many real cases undetected









ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Error asymmetry drives the metric



The professor returned to this theme throughout class:

“The cost of a false negative in this case is much higher than the cost of a false positive.” — Professor Hodnett

When the two types of error have different costs (medical diagnosis, fraud detection), accuracy is not a good enough metric. You must consider precision and recall — and often prioritise recall when missing a positive is catastrophic.




1.4 Translating metrics into business language
One of the professor’s repeated points: most stakeholders don’t understand precision and recall. Your job is to translate.
Professor’s note (on the medical diagnosis example):

“Recall of 50% says that of those that are sick, the model found 50% of those cases. So if a person is sick, it’s a 50/50 chance whether this test will confirm the person is sick or not. On the other hand, 82% of those the model predicted to be sick were actually sick.”

Professor’s note (on the role of a data scientist):

“It is your job to help [stakeholders] to make the decision whether they decide it’s a good model or not. It’s not your job to decide yes, this is the best model out there.”







ImportantExam tip ⭐ — The breast check scandal



The professor gave this real-world Irish example to illustrate why recall matters in medical screening. A screening programme that misses positive cases (low recall) can have terminal consequences because people classified as healthy are not followed up. Expect a question where you must justify the choice of recall over precision (or vice versa) for a specific domain.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
2 Linear Regression

2.1 What linear regression does
Linear regression is one of the oldest machine learning algorithms and still the most widely used. It predicts a continuous numeric value (y) as a weighted sum of one or more input variables (x).
Professor’s note:

“Regression (linear and logistic) is a family of machine learning algorithms. It is probably the oldest and still the most used machine learning algorithm used today.”

Simplest form — one predictor: \[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
\]
Multiple predictors: \[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n
\]








Symbol
Name
Meaning in plain language




\(y\)
Predicted value
What we want to forecast (body weight, price, etc.)


\(\beta_0\)
Intercept
The value of \(y\) when every \(x\) is zero — it’s the “starting point”


\(\beta_1, \beta_2, ...\)
Coefficients (a.k.a. betas, slopes, weights)
How much \(y\) changes for a one-unit change in that \(x\)


\(x_1, x_2, ...\)
Predictors (a.k.a. features, inputs)
The information we feed into the model





2.2 How the coefficients are found — minimising residuals
The best line is the one that minimises the residuals — the vertical distances between the actual data points and the regression line. Residuals = errors = actual − predicted.






NoteAnalogy — the tightrope walker



Imagine trying to stretch a tightrope across a field of people throwing darts at the ceiling. Every dart’s string hangs down a certain distance from the rope. Regression finds the position of the rope that makes the total stretched-string length as short as possible. Each string is a residual.


The formulas (for one predictor) are:
\[
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \qquad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
\]
In R, you never compute this by hand in real work — lm() does it:
library(openintro)
data(mammals)
model1 <- lm(body_wt ~ brain_wt, data = mammals)
print(model1$coefficients)
#> (Intercept)    brain_wt 
#>  -56.855545   0.902913

# Manual verification — same answer
mean_x <- mean(mammals$brain_wt)
mean_y <- mean(mammals$body_wt)
numerator   <- sum((mammals$brain_wt - mean_x) * (mammals$body_wt - mean_y))
denominator <- sum((mammals$brain_wt - mean_x) ^ 2)
b1 <- numerator / denominator
b0 <- mean_y - (b1 * mean_x)
sprintf("b0=%.2f, b1=%.2f", b0, b1)
#> "b0=-56.86, b1=0.90"
Professor’s note:

“This shows that regression is actually a mathematical function that can actually be calculated, usually through matrix multiplications.”



2.3 The four assumptions of linear regression






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Memorise these four assumptions












#
Assumption
What it means (plain language)
How to fix if violated




1
Linearity
The relationship between each x and y should be a straight line
Apply a log transformation, square/cube the variable, or use polynomial regression


2
Homoscedasticity (constant variance)
Errors should be about the same size for small and large predicted values
Transform y (often log); check residuals-vs-fitted plot


3
Independence
Each row must be independent of the others (no time dependency)
Use a time-series model instead for seasonal/trended data


4
Normality
Residuals should follow a bell curve
Transform y; check the Q-Q plot



The professor admitted “I never pronounce this word, so not even going to try” about homoscedasticity — you won’t need to say it aloud, just recognise it.




2.4 Diagnostic plots — did the model behave?
After fitting a linear model you should always look at two plots.
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(model1, which = 1, col = "blue")  # Residuals vs Fitted
plot(model1, which = 2, col = "blue")  # Normal Q-Q
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))








Plot
What you want to see (good model)
What a bad model looks like




Residuals vs Fitted
Random scatter around 0, flat red trend line, no visible pattern
A clear funnel, curve, or tilt → pattern in errors, assumptions violated


Normal Q-Q
Points fall on the diagonal line from bottom-left to top-right
Points bend away from the line at the ends → residuals not normal





2.5 Data transformation — log() as a lifesaver
Recall from Week 2: the raw mammals dataset has a few huge animals (elephants) that dominate everything. The raw-data model is a “poor” model: diagnostic plots show a clear pattern and the Q-Q is badly curved.
Transforming with log() fixes this almost magically:
mammals$log_body_wt  <- log(mammals$body_wt)
mammals$log_brain_wt <- log(mammals$brain_wt)
model2 <- lm(log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt, data = mammals)
summary(model2)








Model
R²
Diagnostic plots




Raw (body_wt ~ brain_wt)
0.87
Poor — clear pattern, curved Q-Q


Log (log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt)
0.92
Good — random residuals, diagonal Q-Q









ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Why this matters



Going from R² = 0.87 to 0.92 is a 35% reduction in error term. The professor’s direct quote:

“By just doing that mathematical transformation on the actual data, we decrease the error term by 35%.”

Always do exploratory analysis (histograms, scatterplots) before building models. If a variable is heavily skewed, a log transform often dramatically improves the fit.








WarningCommon mistake



Don’t assume a high R² means a good model. The raw mammals model had R² = 0.87 but failed the diagnostic plots. Accuracy metrics alone are not enough — you must check assumptions.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
3 Categorical Variables and One-Hot Encoding

3.1 The problem
Most ML algorithms (including regression) can only work with numbers. Datasets often contain categorical text like gender, mood, Embarked. We need a way to convert these to numbers — without accidentally implying that “female” and “male” have some numeric relationship.


3.2 What is one-hot encoding?
One-hot encoding creates a new binary column for each unique value in a categorical variable. Each row gets a 1 in the column that matches its value and 0 everywhere else.
Before:



id
gender
mood
outcome




1
male
happy
1


2
female
sad
1


3
female
happy
0



After one-hot encoding:



id
gender.female
gender.male
mood.happy
mood.sad
outcome




1
0
1
1
0
1


2
1
0
0
1
1


3
1
0
1
0
0



In R with caret:
library(caret)
customers <- data.frame(
  id      = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
  gender  = c('male', 'female', 'female', 'male', 'female'),
  mood    = c('happy', 'sad',    'happy',  'sad',  'happy'),
  outcome = c(1, 1, 0, 0, 0)
)
dmy  <- dummyVars(" ~ .", data = customers)
trsf <- data.frame(predict(dmy, newdata = customers))
head(trsf)


3.3 What glm() does automatically






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — glm() one-hot encodes behind the scenes



When you pass a character or factor column to glm(), R automatically one-hot encodes it. Importantly, R creates N − 1 columns (not N). The “missing” column becomes the reference category and is absorbed into the intercept.
Example with Pclass (values 1, 2, 3): R creates Pclass2 and Pclass3. The 1st class (Pclass1) is the reference — its effect is baked into the intercept.




3.4 Factor vs numeric — the ticket class trap
Pclass is stored as 1, 2, 3 in the Titanic dataset. It looks numeric, but it’s actually a category. This is one of the most important lessons of the class.






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — The arithmetic test



Professor Hodnett’s exact test for whether a variable should be a factor:

“Can you do multiplication or addition on the variable to transform it into another format? I can’t subtract a third class passenger from a second class passenger to get a first class passenger. It makes no sense. So we should have changed it into a factor variable.”

The rule: if the arithmetic is meaningless, make it a factor.
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)


If you leave Pclass as numeric, R builds a single Pclass coefficient — implying the difference between 1st and 2nd class is identical to the difference between 2nd and 3rd. That is a wrong assumption.
A factor variable in R is like an enum in programming: it can only hold values from a fixed set of levels. Assigning “unknown” to a factor whose levels are (female, male) will be rejected.


3.5 The reference category — what sits inside the intercept
The first level of a factor (alphabetically or numerically) becomes the reference. In the final Titanic model, the intercept represents a 1st-class female passenger who embarked at Cherbourg — because Pclass1, Sexfemale, and EmbarkedC are all absorbed.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
4 Logistic Regression

4.1 From linear regression to classification
Linear regression predicts a number. For classification (survived / not survived) we need a probability between 0 and 1. Logistic regression is built in two steps.


4.2 Step 1 — Calculate the log odds (linear part)
First, do the same linear combination as linear regression:
\[
\text{log\_odds} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n
\]
This output can range from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\).


4.3 Step 2 — Apply the sigmoid function
\[
p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\text{log\_odds}}}
\]
This squashes any real number to a value between 0 and 1:



Input to sigmoid
Output (probability)




Very negative (−5)
Close to 0


0
Exactly 0.5


Very positive (+5)
Close to 1



x <- seq(-5, 5, by = 0.01)
y <- 1 / (1 + exp(-x))
plot(x, y, main = "Sigmoid function", col = "blue")






NoteAnalogy — the squash ball



Imagine a huge rubber band stretching from minus infinity to plus infinity. The sigmoid is a machine that squashes the whole band into the narrow space between 0 and 1. The middle of the band (0) gets placed at 0.5. Anything much above 0 is pushed toward 1; anything much below 0 is pushed toward 0.




4.4 Step 3 — Apply a threshold (default 0.5)



Probability
Predicted class




> 0.5
1 (e.g., survived)


≤ 0.5
0 (e.g., not survived)



You can change this threshold to trade off precision and recall (from the opening recap).


4.5 Building a logistic regression model on Titanic
library(titanic)
data(titanic_train)
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)  # critical!

set.seed(42)
nobs  <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

target       <- "Survived"
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Cabin", "Ticket",
                  "Age", "Parch", "Embarked")
input        <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

train_data <- titanic_train[train, c(input, target)]
test_data  <- titanic_train[test,  c(input, target)]

logistic_model <- glm(Survived ~ ., family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      data = train_data)

pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)


4.6 Reading the summary() output — coefficients and significance
The summary has two critical columns:







Column
What it tells you




Estimate (coefficient, β)
Sign = direction of effect on the log-odds. Magnitude = strength (but depends on the scale of the variable — cannot directly compare)


Pr(>|z|) (p-value)
Statistical significance. Lower is better.



Significance codes (the little stars):



Code
Meaning




***
p < 0.001 — very highly significant


**
p < 0.01 — highly significant


*
p < 0.05 — significant


.
p < 0.1 — borderline


(blank)
not significant









ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Interpreting coefficients




Positive coefficient → feature increases the probability of class 1 (survival).
Negative coefficient → feature decreases it.
You cannot directly compare magnitudes across features unless the features are scaled. The professor’s example: Sexmale is only 0 or 1, but Fare ranges 0–500+. A small Fare coefficient multiplied by 500 can still have a large effect.


“You can’t just make an assumption here that the absolute values here are related to each other, because it depends on the unit of measurement.” — Professor Hodnett





4.7 Collinearity — why a variable can appear “unimportant”
If Fare has a low significance, that does not mean it’s unrelated to survival. It likely means its information is already captured by Pclass (high-class tickets cost more).
Professor’s note:

“This does not mean that Fare is not related to Survived. It is, but its effect is captured more accurately by other fields, e.g. Pclass. If we removed these fields from the regression model, Fare would become more statistically significant.”

This is called multicollinearity — correlated predictors steal each other’s apparent significance.


4.8 Manual prediction — the superpower of regression
One of the most powerful features of logistic regression: you don’t need predict(). You can reproduce predictions with just the coefficients + sigmoid, which means you can deploy the model in SQL, JavaScript, Python, Excel, or any language.
coeff <- logistic_model$coefficients

# 1) Encode male as 1/0 manually
test_data$genderMale <- ifelse(test_data$Sex == "male", 1, 0)

# 2) Compute log odds (linear part)
test_data$log_odds <- coeff[1] +
  coeff[2] * test_data$genderMale +
  coeff[3] * test_data$SibSp +
  coeff[4] * test_data$Fare +
  coeff[5] * test_data$TicketClass2 +
  coeff[6] * test_data$TicketClass3

# 3) Apply the sigmoid to get a probability
test_data$pr_manual <- 1 / (1 + exp(-test_data$log_odds))

# Verify: same as predict()
pr_auto <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
all.equal(pr_auto, test_data$pr_manual)   # TRUE






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Why manual prediction matters



Professor Hodnett emphasised this twice:

“You can have a nice system where you use R to actually do the analysis and create your models, but then when it comes time to implement your model, you can implement it using any programming language or any web framework or any other environment you want, because you can calculate the prediction values manually.”

Be ready to calculate a prediction by hand from a given set of coefficients + feature values.




4.9 Visualising the model — log odds plot
library(ggplot2)
test_data$Survived <- as.factor(test_data$Survived)
ggplot(test_data, aes(x = log_odds, y = pr_manual, colour = Survived)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, col = "blue") +
  geom_point() + ylim(0, 1) +
  geom_text(x = -3, y = 0.75, label = "predict=1", col = "green") +
  geom_text(x = -3, y = 0.25, label = "predict=0", col = "red")
How to read this plot:



What you see
What it means




Green dot above 0.5 line
Correctly predicted survivor ✅


Red dot below 0.5 line
Correctly predicted non-survivor ✅


Green dot below 0.5 line
Missed survivor (false negative) ❌


Red dot above 0.5 line
False alarm (false positive) ❌

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
section: 5 Worked Example: Titanic Stepwise Feature Engineering (Lab 5)
text:
5 Worked Example: Titanic Stepwise Feature Engineering (Lab 5)
This is the hands-on part of the class. The professor demonstrated an iterative process that is almost certainly exam-relevant.

5.1 The BuildAndEvaluateModel() helper function
The professor wrote a reusable function to avoid copy-pasting code during experiments:
BuildAndEvaluateModel <- function(train_data, test_data, actuals) {
  logistic_model <- glm(Survived ~ ., family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                        data = train_data)
  pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
  pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)
  pr <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))
  tab <- table(pr, actuals)
  accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))
  print(paste("accuracy=", round(accuracy, 2), "%"))
  return(logistic_model)
}


5.2 The stepwise process — remove one variable at a time






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — The stepwise regression workflow




Build a baseline model with all available features.
Look at summary() — find the feature with the highest p-value (least significant).
Remove only that one feature.
Rebuild and check accuracy.
Repeat until every remaining feature is significant.
Then try feature engineering (extract new features).

Professor’s note:

“Only remove one feature at a time, because there might be collinearity that you need to account for. You don’t remove them all together.”

This technique is called stepwise regression (the professor also called it “regression” and half-jokingly “aggression” when trying to remember the name).




5.3 The actual experiments performed in Lab 5









Step
What was tried
Accuracy
Outcome




1
Baseline (all variables, mean age imputation)
74.44%
Parch, Fare, Embarked look insignificant


2
Remove Parch
74.44%
Same accuracy, model cleaner


3
Also remove Fare
74.44%
Still fine


4
Also remove Embarked
~76%
✅ Improved — simpler and more accurate


5
Replace Age with AgeOver15 flag
~74%
❌ No improvement, reverted


6
Replace SibSp/Parch with SibSpYes/ParchYes flags
~73%
❌ Worse, reverted


7
Extract Deck (first letter of Cabin)
~75.5%
❌ Deck levels not significant, reverted


8
Extract Title from Name, rationalise to 4 groups
~75.5%
❌ Not significant (gender/age capture it), reverted


9
Use Title × Pclass to smart-impute missing ages
75.5%
✅ Improved — better Age values


10
Re-try AgeOver15 on smart-imputed data
Similar
AgeOver15 becomes highly significant


11
Cap Fare at 100, re-add
Similar
Fare slightly significant but no accuracy gain → dropped



Final model features: Pclass, Sex, SibSp, AgeOver15 — ~75.5% accuracy, every feature statistically significant.


5.4 Key feature-engineering snippets
Mean age imputation (starter):
meanAge <- mean(train_data$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
titanic_train$OrigAge <- titanic_train$Age
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge
Extract title from name (regex):
titanic_train$Title <- gsub('(.*, )|(\\..*)', '', titanic_train$Name)

# Rationalise rare titles
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Mlle", "Ms")] <- "Miss"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Mme", "Lady",
                                               "the Countess")] <- "Mrs"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Capt", "Rev", "Col", "Don",
                                               "Major", "Sir", "Dr",
                                               "Jonkheer")] <- "Mr"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title == "Master"] <- "XMaster"  # see note






NoteWhy rename Master to XMaster?



Alphabetically, Master would come first and get absorbed into the intercept. The professor wanted Miss to be the reference and Master visible as a coefficient (because it usefully identifies young boys). Prefixing with X pushes it to the end of the alphabet so Miss becomes the reference instead.


Smart age imputation using Title × Pclass groups:
library(tidyverse)
ageGroups <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ] %>%
  group_by(Pclass, Title) %>%
  summarize(GroupAge = mean(Age))

titanic_train <- merge(titanic_train, ageGroups, all.x = TRUE)
titanic_train <- titanic_train[order(titanic_train$PassengerId), ]  # restore order!
titanic_train$Age <- ifelse(is.na(titanic_train$Age),
                            titanic_train$GroupAge,
                            titanic_train$Age)
Sample group ages produced:



Pclass
Title
Mean Age




1
Miss
29.7


1
Mr
42.4


1
Mrs
40.4


1
XMaster
5.31



This is much smarter than filling every missing age with a single overall mean of ~30 — a 5-year-old missing-age passenger titled “Master” in 3rd class gets ~5, not 30.






ImportantExam tip ⭐ — Feature engineering matters more than algorithm choice




“I started out with looking at all the variables. I removed the variables that were not statistically significant, one by one… Finally I looked at the Name column, extracted out the title, and then tried to calculate the age of somebody based on the ticket class and their title. My original logistic regression algorithm had an accuracy of 73%, and now it has an accuracy of 75%. Also my algorithm is much simpler as well.” — Professor Hodnett

Know the takeaway: Simpler models with well-engineered features often beat complex models on raw data.








WarningCommon mistakes during feature engineering




Removing multiple variables at once instead of one at a time (hides collinearity effects).
Forgetting to apply the same transformation to both train and test data.
Leaving Survived in the input list (R tries to predict Survived from itself → fails).
Losing row order after merge() — always re-sort by the ID before splitting back into train/test.
Forgetting as.factor() on numeric-looking categoricals like Pclass.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
Summary Points — Pre-Exam Checklist

✅ Recall = captured positives; Precision = correct positive predictions — translate both into domain language.
✅ In medical/fraud contexts, recall usually outweighs precision; adjust the threshold to prioritise it.
✅ Linear regression formula: \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n\); best line minimises residuals.
✅ Four assumptions: Linearity, Homoscedasticity, Independence, Normality (LHIN).
✅ Check residuals-vs-fitted (no pattern) and Q-Q (diagonal line).
✅ A log transform can fix non-linear relationships and improve R² dramatically.
✅ One-hot encoding turns categorical text into N binary columns — needed for regression.
✅ R’s glm() auto one-hot encodes character/factor variables, creating N − 1 dummies.
✅ Numeric-looking categoricals (e.g. Pclass) must be converted via as.factor().
✅ The first level of a factor is the reference category, absorbed into the intercept.
✅ Sigmoid: \(p = 1 / (1 + e^{-x})\) — squashes any number to [0, 1].
✅ Logistic regression = linear part → sigmoid → threshold at 0.5.
✅ Coefficient sign = direction of effect; magnitude cannot be compared across unscaled features.
✅ Low p-value = statistically significant. Significance codes: ***, **, *, ., (blank).
✅ Stepwise removal: drop the least significant variable one at a time.
✅ A feature with a low p-value may still be important — its signal can be captured by a correlated feature.
✅ Feature engineering (titles from names, smarter imputation) can beat algorithm tuning.
✅ You can deploy a logistic model without predict() — just coefficients + sigmoid.

title: Week 5: Linear & Logistic Regression, Categorical Encoding and Feature Engineering
text:
7 Pre-Topic Checklist — Before Next Class
Before moving on, you should be able to:

Explain recall and precision in plain business language for a medical and a fraud scenario.
Write the multi-variable linear regression equation from memory.
Name the four assumptions of linear regression.
Describe what a good vs bad residuals-vs-fitted plot looks like.
Recognise when to apply a log transformation.
One-hot encode a toy categorical variable by hand on paper.
Decide whether a variable should be a factor using the “can I do arithmetic on it” test.
State which categories are absorbed into the intercept for a given model summary.
Sketch the sigmoid function and state values at x = −∞, 0, +∞.
Given coefficients and feature values, calculate a prediction by hand.
Read a summary() output and identify the least significant feature.
Apply the stepwise removal workflow to a new dataset in your head.
Extract a title from a name field using regex.
Explain why group-based imputation is better than global mean imputation.
Work through the Lab 5 script end-to-end and reproduce all accuracies.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
0.1 Overview
This week is a big one. It covers how we judge whether a machine learning model is actually any good (evaluation metrics), introduces logistic regression as a classification method, explains the bias–variance trade-off (the single most important concept for understanding why models fail), and gives a high-level tour of other popular algorithms — Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Networks, and Random Forest.






Note



The professor emphasised that this week covers a lot of material and that he deliberately gave a “helicopter view” of some algorithms (Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Networks) because they are covered in other modules. He said: “If you feel that we should go into any of those algorithms in more detail, then it’s certainly worth it. What I would say is the ones that are really interesting are Support Vector Machines and Random Forests.”

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
0.2 Key Topics

Why accuracy alone is misleading (imbalanced data & unequal error costs)
Confusion matrix: TP, FP, FN, TN
Precision, Recall, and the F1 score
ROC curve and AUC (Area Under the Curve)
The precision / recall trade-off via threshold tuning
Logistic regression — how it works, its strengths, and its weakness (no missing data)
Regression evaluation metrics: MAE, MSE, RMSE, R²
Bias vs Variance — underfitting vs overfitting
Polynomial regression as a demonstration of the trade-off
Survey of other algorithms: Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Networks, Random Forest
Bagging and sampling with/without replacement
Communicating model performance in plain English to non-technical people

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
1.1 The Problem With Accuracy
Accuracy is the most obvious measure: the percentage of predictions that were correct.
\[\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}\]
It is easy to understand — 100% means every prediction was right. But there are two situations where accuracy can fool you badly.

1.1.1 Situation 1 — Unequal Cost of Errors
In many real-world problems, the two types of errors are not equally bad.






NoteExample — Medical Diagnosis




A false positive (predicting “sick” when the person is healthy) is upsetting but not life-threatening — the patient might get further tests and eventually be cleared.
A false negative (predicting “healthy” when the person is actually sick) can be fatal, because treatment is delayed.

These two errors are not the same, but accuracy treats them as identical.








Important⭐ Professor’s real-world example — The Irish BreastCheck scandal



Professor’s note: “We had a scandal in the medical industry for our BreastCheck, which was an early screening programme to detect cancers… it probably managed to diagnose correctly whether a person was sick or healthy 95, 98, 99%. But it missed a number of people who had cancerous tumours and subsequently missed out on treatment, and by the time their cases progressed and they got the treatment, their cancers had spread, and the number of people unfortunately died from it.”
The professor’s point: “It doesn’t really matter… whether you’re 99% accurate or not. It’s the number of people that are diagnosed as healthy but in fact they’re sick. That’s the only metric that you’re worried about.”
This kind of story is exactly the type of thing the professor uses to build an exam question around — be ready to explain why recall matters more than accuracy in medical screening.




1.1.2 Situation 2 — Imbalanced Datasets
When one class is much more common than the other, a lazy model can score very high on accuracy while being useless.






WarningCommon Mistake



If 1% of transactions are fraudulent, a model that predicts “not fraud” for every transaction is 99% accurate — and catches zero fraud cases. Accuracy looks great; the model is worthless.
Professor’s note: “A machine learning model could just turn on and basically say, 100% everyone’s healthy, and I wouldn’t catch those records. So in a lot of cases, accuracy is actually a misleading evaluation metric to use.”


Real examples of imbalanced data the professor mentioned:



Domain
Positive class (what we want to catch)
Typical prevalence




Credit card fraud
Fraudulent transactions
< 0.1%


Medical screening (cancer)
Person has disease
~5%


Spam filtering
Spam emails
Varies, often a minority


Manufacturing defects
Defective items
< 1%

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
2.1 The Four Outcomes
We always pick one class to call Positive (the thing we are trying to detect). The other is Negative.









Actual Positive (1)
Actual Negative (0)




Predicted Positive (1)
True Positive (TP) ✅
False Positive (FP) ❌


Predicted Negative (0)
False Negative (FN) ❌
True Negative (TN) ✅









NoteHow to Remember “True” and “False”




Words that start with “True” → the model was correct.
Words that start with “False” → the model was wrong.
The second word (Positive/Negative) tells you what the model predicted.

So “False Negative” = the model predicted Negative, but was wrong (i.e. the actual answer was Positive).

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
2.2 The Titanic Example (Used Throughout Class)
The decision tree model from Week 3 produced this confusion matrix (with Survived = 1 as the Positive class, after re-ordering):
         actuals
pr         1    0
   1      17    4      ← Model predicted SURVIVED
   0      19   50      ← Model predicted DID NOT SURVIVE
Reading this:



Cell
Count
Meaning




TP
17
Did survive AND the model said they survived ✅


FP
4
Did not survive, but the model said they did ❌


FN
19
Did survive, but the model said they didn’t ❌


TN
50
Did not survive AND the model agreed ✅



Total accuracy = (17 + 50) / 90 = 74.4%






Important⭐ Exam-Critical Detail — The R Default vs. The “Standard” Layout



R by default puts the class labelled 0 in the first row/column. Most textbooks and Python libraries put the Positive class (1) first, because that is the class we care most about.
Professor’s note: “Unfortunately R kind of reverses your confusion matrix… so we’re going to reverse that, because we want to treat our first class, or class where it’s equal to 1, as a primary class for calculating other metrics.”
The R code to flip the matrix is:
actuals <- factor(test_data$Survived, levels=c(1,0))
pr      <- factor(pr, levels=c(1,0))
tab     <- table(pr, actuals)
If you are ever asked to calculate precision and recall from an R confusion matrix, check which class is the positive one first.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
section: 3.1 Precision — “When the model says YES, how often is it right?”
text:
3.1 Precision — “When the model says YES, how often is it right?”
\[\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}\]
Precision looks at the predicted column. Of all the cases the model flagged as Positive, what fraction actually were Positive?
Titanic example: Precision = 17 / (17 + 4) = 0.81






NotePlain-English Analogy — Precision is like a fishing net’s “keep rate”



If you cast a net and pull in 100 things, and 81 of them are fish (the rest are seaweed), your precision is 81%. The net is quite clean — when it catches something, it’s usually a fish.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
section: 3.2 Recall (Sensitivity) — “Of all the real YES cases, how many did the model find?”
text:
3.2 Recall (Sensitivity) — “Of all the real YES cases, how many did the model find?”
\[\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\]
Recall looks at the actual column. Of all the cases that truly were Positive, what fraction did the model catch?
Titanic example: Recall = 17 / (17 + 19) = 0.47






NotePlain-English Analogy — Recall is like a search party’s “find rate”



If 100 people are lost in a forest and the search party brings back 47 of them, the recall is 47%. It doesn’t matter how many false alarms there were — only how many of the real lost people they found.








Important⭐ Mnemonic the Professor Gave for the Exam



Professor’s note: “Precision and recall are two things that a lot of people have a bit of confusion about, and it can be hard to remember the definitions. The way I try and remember recall is: recall just looks at the actual values, and precision looks at the predicted values.”

Recall → look at the row where the Actual = 1 (focus on actuals)
Precision → look at the row where the Predicted = 1 (focus on predictions)

Expect to calculate both by hand from a confusion matrix on the exam.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
3.3 F1 Score — The Balanced Summary
\[F_1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\]
F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It gives a single number that only gets high when both precision and recall are high.
Titanic example: F1 = 2 × (0.81 × 0.47) / (0.81 + 0.47) ≈ 0.60






WarningWhy Harmonic Mean and Not Simple Average?



Because the harmonic mean punishes extreme imbalance. Consider a model with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.0:

Simple average: 0.5 (looks okay)
F1 score: 0.0 (correctly shows the model is broken)

Professor’s note: “If precision is 0.99 but the recall is 0.01, it just doesn’t take an average, call it 50% and call it done. It basically will allocate a very low F1 score on that.”

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
3.4 Quick Reference Table









Metric
Formula
Answers the question
When it matters most




Accuracy
(TP+TN)/Total
“Overall, how often right?”
Balanced data, equal costs


Precision
TP/(TP+FP)
“When I say YES, am I right?”
When false alarms are expensive


Recall
TP/(TP+FN)
“Did I catch all the real YES cases?”
When missing a case is expensive


F1
Harmonic mean of P & R
“Are both P and R good?”
Imbalanced data, both errors matter

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
section: 4.1 How to Translate Each Metric for a Business Audience
text:
4.1 How to Translate Each Metric for a Business Audience
Imagine the Titanic model is actually a disease-screening model (1 = sick, 0 = healthy). Its precision is 0.81 and recall is 0.47.







Technical statement
Plain-English translation




“Recall is 0.47”
“Of the people who are actually sick, the model finds 47% of them.”


“Precision is 0.81”
“Of the people the model says are sick, 81% really are sick.”


“F1 is 0.60”
(Don’t use F1 with business — use precision and recall separately.)

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
4.2 Is This a “Good” Model?
Depends entirely on context:

Serious disease (like cancer): missing sick patients is unacceptable → recall is king → this model is bad (only 47%).
Quick screening tool (e.g., COVID thermometer gun at the door): high precision with some missed cases may be acceptable → “better than nothing” says the professor.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
5.1 What Is the ROC Curve?
The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve is a plot that shows how well a classifier can distinguish between the two classes across all possible thresholds.






NoteFun Historical Analogy



The ROC curve was invented for radar operators in World War II who had to decide whether a blip on the screen was a real enemy plane or just noise. That’s why it’s called “Receiver Operating Characteristic.”



Y-axis: Sensitivity = Recall = TP / (TP + FN)
X-axis: 1 − Specificity = FP / (FP + TN) = False Positive Rate

A perfect model would go straight up the left side and across the top — 100% recall with 0% false positives.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
5.2 AUC — Area Under the Curve
AUC is a single number summarising the ROC curve.



AUC value
Interpretation




1.0
Perfect classifier


0.9 – 1.0
Excellent


0.8 – 0.9
Good


0.7 – 0.8
Fair


0.5
No better than random guessing


< 0.5
Worse than random (predictions inverted)



Titanic decision tree AUC: 0.764 (decent but not great)
library(tidymodels)
test_data$predicted <- pr[,2]
test_data$Survived <- factor(test_data$Survived, levels=c(1,0))
autoplot(roc_curve(test_data, Survived, predicted))
roc_auc(test_data, Survived, predicted)

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
6.1 The 0.5 Threshold Is Not Sacred
Most classifiers output a probability between 0 and 1. By default we convert this to a class using:

If prob >= 0.5 → predict Positive
Else → predict Negative

But 0.5 is just a convention. By moving the threshold, we can prioritise precision or recall.






NoteThreshold as a Volume Knob



Think of the threshold as a volume knob for how eager the model is to say “Positive”:

Low threshold (e.g., 0.1) — the model shouts “Positive!” at almost everything. Catches most real positives (high recall) but also raises lots of false alarms (low precision).
High threshold (e.g., 0.9) — the model only says “Positive” when 90%+ sure. Rarely wrong when it does (high precision) but misses many real cases (low recall).

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
section: 6.2 Actual Results from Lab 4a (Logistic Regression on Titanic)
text:
6.2 Actual Results from Lab 4a (Logistic Regression on Titanic)










Threshold
Accuracy
Precision
Recall
Character




0.1
~65%
0.54
0.92
“Paranoid” — catches almost everyone, lots of false alarms


0.5
~73%

0.53
Balanced default


0.9

1.00

“Cautious” — only when very sure



You cannot push up both precision AND recall — improving one almost always costs the other.






Important⭐ Exam Application



If the exam describes a scenario, you should be able to recommend a threshold:



Scenario
Prioritise
Move threshold…




Cancer screening — don’t miss a case
Recall
Lower


Spam filter — don’t send real email to junk
Precision
Higher


Fraud detection — catch as many as possible
Recall
Lower

Precision
Higher

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
7.1 What Is It?
Logistic regression is a classification algorithm (despite “regression” in the name). It takes the framework of linear regression but maps the output through a sigmoid (logistic) function so the result is a probability between 0 and 1.
Linear regression formula: \[z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n\]
Sigmoid transformation: \[P(y = 1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\]
The output is the probability that the observation belongs to the positive class.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
7.2 Why It Is Popular

Interpretable coefficients — each \(\beta\) tells you how the variable affects the probability.
Fast to train, even on large data.
Statistical significance is reported via p-values.
A good baseline before trying fancy algorithms.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
7.3 Key Points From Class

7.3.1 Interpreting the Coefficients

Negative coefficient → higher values of that feature decrease the probability of survival.
Positive coefficient → higher values increase the probability of survival.

Professor’s note: “If you look at the way the formula for logistic regression is … if the coefficient is negative, that means that a higher value is an indication that the person did not survive. So in this case, Pclass is negative, which means the higher value for Pclass, the less likely somebody survived.”


7.3.2 Handling Categorical Variables
When R sees a categorical variable like Sex:

It creates indicator variables (Sexmale = 0 or 1).
One category is absorbed into the intercept — typically the alphabetically first value.
So Sexfemale has no explicit coefficient — it’s wrapped into the intercept.







NoteWhy the Intercept Is So Large



The intercept effectively represents the “baseline person” — in the Titanic case, a female passenger who embarked at Cherbourg. Both conditions are associated with higher survival, so the intercept comes out large and positive.




7.3.3 The Missing-Data Problem






Warning⚠️ Common Mistake — Don’t Forget to Handle NA’s!



Logistic regression cannot handle missing values. If you train a model using a column with NAs (like Age in Titanic), the predict() function will return NA for those rows — and you’ll get a broken confusion matrix.
Two options:

Drop the column entirely (quick fix)
Impute the missing values (better: use the mean of the training set only, never the full dataset)

Professor’s note: “We only calculate the average of the age across the [training] split of the records… We don’t take the average over all of them, because that would mean that we’re leaking data into the [model].”




7.3.4 Occam’s Razor — Simpler Is Often Better
In the lab, removing Embarked, Parch, and Fare (all statistically insignificant) improved accuracy slightly — from 73% to 74%.
Why? These variables added noise without adding signal. Correlated features (like Fare and Pclass) don’t both need to be in the model.






Important⭐ The Concept of Multicollinearity



A variable may appear “insignificant” in the summary not because it has no relationship with the outcome, but because another variable in the model already captures the same information.
Example: Embarked looks insignificant because Pclass already explains most of its predictive power (people from Cherbourg were more likely to be first class).

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
7.4 Lab 4a — Core Code
# Fit the model
logistic_model <- glm(formula = Survived ~ .,
                      family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      data = train_data)
summary(logistic_model)   # p-values, coefficients, significance stars

# Get probabilities (NOT classes)
pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")

# Convert to classes at threshold 0.5
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Evaluate
actuals <- factor(test_data$Survived, levels = c(1,0))
pr      <- factor(pr, levels = c(1,0))
tab     <- table(pr, actuals)
accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab) / sum(tab))

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
8.1 The Four Regression Metrics









Metric
Formula
Units
Key property




MAE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert\)
Same as y
Easy to explain, treats all errors equally


MSE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\)
y²
Penalises big errors heavily


RMSE
\(\sqrt{\text{MSE}}\)
Same as y
Penalises big errors, yet still interpretable


R²
Correlation(y, ŷ)²
Unitless 0–1
Proportion of variance explained

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
8.2 MAE — Mean Absolute Error
\[\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|\]






NoteProfessor’s Example — Customer Spending Model



Professor’s note: “If we said that we had a model that was trying to predict how much a person was going to spend in our store in the next month, and if it basically said that the mean absolute error was 15, then the model is accurate plus or minus 15 euros.”
MAE’s big advantage: it is in the same units as the target. Easy to communicate to business.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
8.3 MSE — Mean Squared Error
\[\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\]
Squaring has two effects:

Removes negatives (a -5 error and a +5 error both contribute 25).
Big errors are punished more than small ones (2² = 4 vs 5² = 25).

Good for models where you really don’t want huge misses.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
8.4 RMSE — Root Mean Squared Error
\[\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}\]
Squaring puts the error back in the same units as y, but RMSE still penalises large errors more than MAE does. In practice, RMSE ≥ MAE always.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
8.5 R² — Coefficient of Determination
Tells you what fraction of the variance in the target is explained by the model.

R² = 1 → perfect fit
R² = 0 → model is no better than just predicting the mean
R² < 0 → model is worse than just predicting the mean (yes, this can happen on test data!)

For most regression tasks, R² and RMSE are the preferred metrics.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
9.1 The Two Evils










Concept
What it means
Training error
Test error
Also called




Bias
Model too simple; misses the pattern
High
High
Underfitting


Variance
Model too complex; memorises noise
Low
High
Overfitting









NoteAnalogy — The Student Studying for an Exam




High bias (underfit) — the student only skimmed the textbook. They don’t know the material. They do badly on the practice exam and the real exam.
Good fit — the student studied properly and understands the concepts. They do well on both practice and real exams.
High variance (overfit) — the student memorised every word of the practice exam answers. On the practice exam they score 100%. On the real exam (with slightly different questions) they score badly because they never learned the underlying concepts.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
9.2 Visualising It (from Lab 4b)
Using synthetic data, three polynomial regression models were fitted:










Model
Formula
R² (training)
MAE (training)
Real-world behaviour




1
\(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x\)
0.17
3.45
Underfits — a straight line cannot capture the curve.


2
\(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3\)
0.79
1.54
Good fit — captures the general shape.


3
Degree 6 polynomial
0.97
0.62
Overfits — hits every training point but oscillates wildly outside the training range.









Warning⚠️ The Deceptive “Best” Model



Model 3 has the best training metrics (R² = 0.97, lowest MAE). It looks like the winner. But it would fail catastrophically on new data, because it has memorised the random noise in the training set, not the underlying relationship.
This is the most common failure mode in applied machine learning. Never trust training metrics alone.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
9.3 How to Detect Overfitting
Three practical tests:

Compare training vs test performance. A big gap (great on training, mediocre on test) means the model overfit.
Change the random seed and refit. If results vary wildly, the model is unstable.
Use cross-validation (covered in a later class) for a systematic check.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
10.1 Summary Comparison Table










Algorithm
Core idea
Titanic accuracy
Strengths
Weaknesses




Naive Bayes
Update beliefs with evidence using Bayes’ theorem
~72%
Very fast, scalable, great baseline
Assumes features are independent (rarely true)


SVM
Draw the widest possible gap between classes
~76%
Robust to outliers, good default
Slow on large datasets


Neural Network
Network of nodes learning non-linear patterns
~75–78%
Approximates any function
Needs huge data, hard to interpret


Random Forest
Average the votes of 100+ decision trees
~78%
Best out-of-the-box on tabular data
Less interpretable than a single tree

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
10.2 Naive Bayes

10.2.1 The Big Idea
Start with a prior belief, then update it each time new evidence arrives.
\[\text{Posterior} = \frac{\text{Prior} \times \text{Likelihood}}{\text{Prior} \times \text{Likelihood} + (1 - \text{Prior}) \times (1 - \text{Likelihood})}\]


10.2.2 The Professor’s Soccer Analogy






NoteBayesian Updating in Sport



Professor’s note: “Two player teams are playing a knockout match… the home team in the league usually wins 60% of the matches. So in Bayesian statistics, you call this a prior belief…”

Starting belief (prior): Home team wins 60% of matches.
New evidence: Team A has a star striker who scores a lot — when he plays, the team wins 70%.
Updated belief (posterior): 0.78 (78% chance of winning).
More evidence: Key defender is injured — alone this suggests only 45% chance of winning.
Final updated belief: 0.74 (74% chance of winning).

Bayesian thinking = combining independent pieces of evidence sequentially.




10.2.3 Why “Naive”?
It assumes every feature is independent of every other feature — which is almost never true in practice. In the Titanic data, Fare and Pclass are obviously correlated, but Naive Bayes pretends they aren’t.
library(e1071)
nb_model <- naiveBayes(Survived ~ ., data = train_data)
pr <- predict(nb_model, newdata = test_data, type = "raw")

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
10.3 Support Vector Machines (SVM)

10.3.1 The Big Idea
Find the best-separating boundary between two classes — specifically, the one that leaves the widest possible gap (margin) between the closest points of each class.


10.3.2 Two Key Concepts

Maximum margin — among all possible separating lines, pick the one furthest from any point. The points closest to the boundary are called support vectors.
The kernel trick — map the data into a higher-dimensional space so that patterns that aren’t linearly separable in 2D become separable in higher dimensions. Conceptually similar to polynomial regression.







NoteWhy SVM Is Robust to Outliers



Professor’s note: “It really only is going to focus on [the support vectors at the boundary]. They really are the rows that are going to determine how it’s going to separate the data.”
Points far from the boundary don’t influence the model at all. So one or two wild outliers have no effect — unlike logistic regression, where every point contributes.


library(kernlab)
svm_model <- ksvm(Survived ~ ., data = train_data,
                  kernel = "rbfdot", prob.model = TRUE)

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
10.4 Neural Networks

10.4.1 The Big Idea
A network of nodes arranged in layers. Each node does a small mathematical calculation (weighted sum + nonlinear activation function) and passes its result to the next layer. By stacking many layers, the network can approximate almost any function.


10.4.2 How Training Works







Step
What happens




1. Forward pass
Inputs flow through the layers, producing a prediction.


2. Calculate error
Compare prediction to actual value using a cost function.


3. Backpropagation
Adjust every weight slightly, working backwards, to reduce the error.


4. Repeat
Loop forward + backward hundreds or thousands of times.





10.4.3 Practical Constraints

Requires numeric input — categorical variables must be one-hot encoded.
Requires scaling — all numeric features should be mapped to a 0–1 range.
Slow and unstable on small datasets.







Warning⚠️ The Professor’s Warning



Professor’s note: “In practice, what I basically say about neural networks is: don’t use neural networks typically… I hardly ever use them outside of this class. I don’t think I’ve ever touched neural networks on tabular data unless you have a large amount of data, or it is something that is designed like a computer vision problem or [NLP] problem.”
For tabular data, Random Forest and SVM almost always outperform neural networks unless you have millions of rows. Save neural networks for image/text data.

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
10.5 Random Forest (Ensemble Method)

10.5.1 The Big Idea
Rather than building one decision tree, build hundreds of trees — each trained on slightly different data and features — and take the majority vote.


10.5.2 The Statistical Foundation — Central Limit Theorem






NoteAnalogy — Guessing Marbles in a Jar



Professor’s note: “If I ask everyone here in this class how many marbles are in the jar, you could be off by 20 or 30%. If I asked 100 people their guess on how many marbles are in the jar, and I took the average of that, it would be within 1 or 2% of the actual value.”
The average of many imperfect guesses is almost always better than any individual guess — the Central Limit Theorem. Random Forest exploits this by averaging many mediocre decision trees.




10.5.3 How Random Forest Creates Diverse Trees
If every tree saw the same data and features, they’d all be identical and the vote would be pointless. Random Forest uses three tricks to force diversity:








Trick
What it does
Why




Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)
Each tree gets a random sample of rows, with replacement (~63% unique)
Different rows = different trees


Random feature selection
At each split, only a random subset of features is considered
Stops one dominant feature (like gender) being chosen every time


Shallow trees
Each tree is kept small (depth 1–2)
Prevents any single tree from overfitting









NoteWhy Intentionally Weaken the Base Models?



Professor’s note: “If a few base models are very poor, then they will just be outvoted by the other base models.”
Each individual tree is kind of useless — but together, they make a very strong ensemble. Think wisdom of crowds.




10.5.4 Random Forest Feature Importance (Titanic)
The Random Forest also tells you which features matter most:
Sex > Fare > Age > Pclass > SibSp > Parch > Embarked






Warning⚠️ Different Algorithms, Different Opinions



Professor’s note: “For Random Forest, it’s gender, fair, age are the three most important variables, just going to show that different algorithms have different ways of deciding which are the most important features within your data set. When you have highly correlated variables like fair and ticket class, one algorithm might prioritise one of those columns over the others.”
Logistic regression put Pclass ahead of Fare; Random Forest does the opposite. Feature importance is algorithm-specific — don’t treat it as absolute truth.


library(randomForest)
rf_model <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ ., data = train_data)
plot(rf_model, main = "Error Rates Random Forest")
varImpPlot(rf_model, type = 2)

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
11.1 Without Replacement (The Default)
Like drawing bingo balls: once a number is called, it’s set aside and can’t come up again. All selected items are unique.
# Select 90 values from 1..100 WITHOUT replacement
tmp <- sample(100, 90)
length(unique(tmp))   # 90 — all unique

title: Week 4: Evaluation Metrics, Logistic Regression & ML Algorithm Survey
text:
11.2 With Replacement
Like drawing a marble, noting its number, and putting it back before the next draw. Items can repeat.
# Select 90 values from 1..90 WITH replacement
tmp <- sample(90, 90, replace = TRUE)
length(unique(tmp))   # approximately 57 unique (~63%)






Important⭐ Key Number to Remember — The 63% Rule



When sampling n items from n with replacement, approximately 63% will be unique and ~37% will be duplicates. This is the mathematical basis of bagging in Random Forest.
The ~37% that get left out are called out-of-bag (OOB) samples and can be used to estimate error without needing a separate validation set.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
3.1 The Four Core Terms
Understanding the relationship between these four terms is the single most important concept from Week 1. They are not the same thing — they are nested inside each other like Russian dolls.






Note🪆 Analogy: Russian Dolls



Think of it like nesting dolls:

The biggest doll is Artificial Intelligence — the broadest idea.
Inside it sits Machine Learning — a specific approach to building AI.
Inside Machine Learning sits Deep Learning — a specific family of ML algorithms.
Data Mining is an older term that sits roughly where Machine Learning sits — consider them interchangeable in modern usage.











Term
Plain-Language Definition
Example




Artificial Intelligence (AI)
When a computer does something that normally requires human intelligence
Google Maps route planning; a chess program; a self-driving car


Machine Learning (ML)
Using statistical algorithms so machines improve automatically with more data and experience
A spam filter that gets better the more emails it sees


Data Mining
Extracting patterns and rules from large amounts of data. An older term — treat as interchangeable with ML today
Finding which supermarket products are bought together


Deep Learning
A family of ML algorithms that works especially well for images and text
Face recognition; ChatGPT; image generation



Professor’s note: “Data mining — extracting rules from large quantities of data — is an older term, rarely used actually anymore in the industry. I consider it interchangeable with machine learning.”

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
section: 3.2 How They Fit Together — The Nested Diagram
text:
3.2 How They Fit Together — The Nested Diagram
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          Artificial Intelligence        │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │        Machine Learning           │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │       Deep Learning         │  │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
Concrete example — Self-driving car:

The whole car = AI (doing something human-like: driving)
Its vision system = Deep Learning (recognising objects, lanes, pedestrians)
That vision system is also ML because Deep Learning is a subset of ML







Important⭐ Exam Tip: Don’t Confuse Gen AI with the Whole of ML



Professor’s note: “Don’t be fooled with the Gen AI boom. Gen AI is a whole other massive area of artificial intelligence. There are wholesale businesses out there that are using machine learning every day of the week, and Gen AI is not involved in it.”
Gen AI (like ChatGPT) is just one small part of a very large field. Most ML in industry is about tabular data, predictions, and classification — not text generation. This course focuses on tabular ML.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
3.3 Deterministic vs. Stochastic Systems
This is a key distinction that separates traditional software from machine learning.








Feature
Traditional Software (Deterministic)
Machine Learning (Stochastic)




Rules
Programmer writes all the rules
Algorithm learns rules from data


Same input → same output?
Always yes
Can change as model updates


Handles new patterns?
Only if programmer adds new rules
Yes — learns automatically


Can improve without code change?
No
Yes (retrain on fresh data)


Example
A spreadsheet formula =A1*B1
A fraud detection model


Failure mode
Crashes / wrong output
Quietly produces biased or confident-but-wrong predictions









Note💡 Analogy: Recipe vs. Cooking Lessons




Traditional software is like handing someone a precise recipe: “add exactly 200g of flour.” Same ingredients → same cake, every time.
Machine Learning is like giving someone cooking lessons. They practise, taste, adjust. Over time they develop judgement. Two different trained cooks given the same ingredients may produce slightly different cakes — because they have learned different intuitions from different training.









Warning⚠️ Common Misconception: “ML is magic” / “ML is objective”



Many non-technical stakeholders believe ML is either sorcery or flawless objective truth. It is neither.

It is maths — specifically, statistics and linear algebra applied to patterns in data.
It is only as good as the data you feed it.
It is pattern-matching, not reasoning. A model that has never seen a situation will make poor predictions about it.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
4.1 Summary Across All Industries







Industry
Most Common ML Problems




Marketing
Churn prediction, customer segmentation, recommendation engines, cross-sell/upsell, survey analysis


Finance & Banking
Fraud detection, credit risk, algorithmic trading, document extraction


Manufacturing
Predictive maintenance, anomaly detection on production lines, supply & demand forecasting


Telecoms
Churn, speech recognition for customer service, cell-tower siting


Pharmaceuticals
Drug discovery, clinical trial analysis, radiology imaging, outbreak prediction



Notice how churn and anomaly detection appear in multiple industries. These are general-purpose ML tasks — the same technique solves different business problems. Most of Weeks 3–10 builds up the tools to tackle them.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
4.2 Marketing — Deep Dive








Application
What It Does
Why It Matters




Customer survey analysis
Uses NLP to categorise written feedback into topics
Automates work that would take humans weeks


Churn prediction
Identifies customers likely to leave 1–2 months before they decide
By the time someone cancels, it’s too late — you need to predict it early


Cross-sell / upsell
Recommends related products, not the same product again
A customer who just bought a TV won’t buy another TV — offer them a soundbar


Customer segmentation
Groups customers by behaviour, lifestyle, or spending
Enables targeted marketing instead of mass campaigns









Important⭐ Exam-Worthy Concept: Churn Prediction Is Predictive, Not Reactive



Professor’s note: “By the time a customer has decided they’re going to opt out or unsubscribe, it’s probably too late to change their mind. You really need to look at their behaviour maybe a month or two beforehand. So it gets clever — you’re not asking ‘who will leave in January’, you’re asking in November, ‘who are the likely customers that will leave in the next 6 to 8 weeks?’”
The key idea: you must build a model that predicts churn before it happens, so the business has time to intervene (e.g. discount, special offer).








Note📖 The Tesco Clubcard Story — A Classic Data Mining Case Study



Professor’s note: “They went in to do a presentation to the board of Tesco’s. The chairman waited maybe 30 seconds after the presentation — they thought they’d blown it. And he said: ‘I’ve worked in this industry for over 30 years. I’ve worked in Tesco’s for over 20 years, and it scares me that you know more about my customers than I do.’”
In the early 1990s, Tesco launched the Clubcard. A data analytics company (Dunnhumby) used customer segmentation on the transaction data to group customers — e.g. “double income, no kids”, “young families”, “early retirees”. Instead of sending every customer the same vouchers, Tesco could target each segment, and could tell suppliers: “We can reach your exact target demographic.”
The result was so powerful that Tesco bought a controlling share in Dunnhumby to stop competitors accessing the same capability.
The technique used: Clustering / Segmentation (covered in Week 6).








Note☕ The Petrol Station Coffee Story



Professor’s note: “If somebody comes in for €50 worth of fuel, but the person buys a coffee — the company makes as much money from the cup of coffee as they do from the entire €50 worth of fuel. The fuel itself has a very low margin, maybe 5%. If you sell €50 of fuel and make 5% profit, you have €2.50. But they can make €2.50 just selling a coffee.”
A petrol station the lecturer worked with used loyalty data to spot customers who came in for fuel but never bought food or coffee. By targeting them with specific promotions, the business significantly increased the sale of high-margin items. The key insight: all customers are not equally profitable, and the same customer at different points in time isn’t equally profitable either.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
4.3 Finance & Banking — Deep Dive







Application
Notes




Fraud detection
One of the earliest and most successful ML applications. Contactless payments only work because fraud is kept below ~0.001%


Credit risk
Automates loan decisions — but legal restrictions apply: race, gender, etc. cannot be used as features


Automated trading
Well over 99% of stock exchange transactions are executed by algorithmic bots, not humans


Document processing
AI/GenAI extracts data from mortgage applications, bank statements, payslips


Digital banks
Revolut, N26, Monzo operate without physical branches — entirely ML-driven backend









Important⭐ Fraud Detection: The “Accuracy Trap” (Preview of Week 3)



Professor’s note: “If you have a machine learning algorithm that classifies every transaction as non-fraudulent, that algorithm is going to have 99.999% accuracy. Which shows that a machine learning metric of just judging something on accuracy is a worthless business metric — because it hasn’t found any fraudulent transactions at all.”
This is a critical exam concept. Raw accuracy is often misleading. With fraud data:



What the model does
Accuracy
Fraud caught




Predicts “not fraud” for every transaction
99.999%
0


A properly tuned ML model
~99.9%
thousands



The first model looks better on paper but is completely useless. This is the imbalanced dataset problem (Week 5). Better metrics: precision, recall, F1 score (Week 3).

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
4.4 Manufacturing, Telecoms, Pharmaceuticals

Manufacturing: optimal production schedules, supply/demand forecasting, anomaly detection (spotting defective products on a production line), predictive maintenance (predicting machine failure before it happens, avoiding expensive downtime).
Telecoms: speech recognition for customer service, churn prediction, ad-spend analysis across customer segments, spatial analysis for cell-tower placement.
Pharmaceuticals: drug discovery, clinical trial analysis, radiology (image analysis), electronic health records, epidemic outbreak prediction.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
section: 5.1 Why Do We Need a Workflow?
text:
5.1 Why Do We Need a Workflow?
ML projects are research problems, not standard software projects. You don’t know in advance what will work. You must explore, experiment, and iterate.
Professor’s note: “Essentially, it is a research problem, and needs to be treated as such.”
Two common workflows are mentioned: CRISP-DM and SEMMA. CRISP-DM is the industry standard and is the one you need to know.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
5.2 The CRISP-DM Framework
CRISP-DM = CRoss Industry Standard Process for Data Mining.
It is an agile, iterative process — you frequently move backwards and forwards between phases. This is normal and expected.
Business Understanding ←→ Data Understanding
           ↓                    ↑
    Data Preparation ←──────────┘
           ↓
        Modelling ←──┐
           ↓         │
       Evaluation ───┘  (may loop back to any earlier phase)
           ↓
       Deployment








Phase
Key Question
What Happens




1. Business Understanding
What does the business actually need?
Define the problem. What does success look like? What is the business cost of a wrong prediction?


2. Data Understanding
What data do we have? Is it clean?
Explore, visualise, and assess data quality (Week 2 covers this!)


3. Data Preparation
How do we organise the data for modelling?
Clean, transform, encode, engineer features. This takes the most time in a real project — often 60–80% of total effort.


4. Modelling
What techniques should we apply?
Train ML algorithms on the prepared data


5. Evaluation
Which model meets the business objective?
Not just accuracy — does it actually solve the problem?


6. Deployment
How do stakeholders access the results?
Put the model into production; monitor it afterwards









Important⭐ Key Exam Point: CRISP-DM Is Not Linear



Professor’s note: “The sequence of the phases is not rigid. Moving back and forth between different phases is always required. The outcome of each phase determines which phase, or particular task of a phase, has to be performed next.”
You might: build a model → realise the data needs more cleaning → go back to Data Preparation → rebuild the model → evaluate again. This cycling is the correct way to work, not a mistake.








Warning⚠️ Common Mistake: Rushing to Modelling



Junior data scientists often want to jump straight into building models because that feels like “real” data science. The professor has emphasised (and will emphasise again in Week 7) that feature engineering and data understanding matter far more than the choice of algorithm. A great model on bad data is worthless. A simple model on well-prepared data often wins.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
section: 5.3 Ethics and Fairness in Machine Learning
text:
5.3 Ethics and Fairness in Machine Learning
This is not optional — it is a professional and legal responsibility.






Warning⚠️ Common Mistake: Assuming ML is “Objective”



ML models learn from historical data. If historical data contains bias (which it almost always does), the model will learn and reproduce that bias. The algorithm itself is not the problem — the data is. But the data scientist is responsible for recognising and addressing this.
Professor’s note: “ML is only a complex pattern matcher. If your data is biased or unfair to a group, it is almost certainly caused by the data — it’s just reflecting existing biases.”


Real-world examples of ML bias:








Case
What Happened
Lesson




Amazon’s hiring tool
Trained on historical CVs (mostly male), it penalised CVs that included the word “women’s” (e.g. “women’s chess club”). Amazon scrapped it.
Historical bias → model bias


Facial recognition
Shown to be far less accurate on darker skin tones, leading to wrongful arrests.
Unbalanced training data → poor generalisation


Criminal justice AI
Risk scores used in sentencing decisions showed racial disparities.
High-stakes decisions need extra scrutiny









Important⭐ Professional Responsibility



Professor’s note: “You, as the data scientist, have the responsibility to stand up inside the business and say: if I build a model with this dataset, I think it might not work in all cases. And that’s a hard thing to say. But the reputational damage companies can suffer from having machine learning models that perform poorly or discriminate — it’s worth standing up and saying I don’t think this is going to work, if you have doubts about something beforehand.”
The reputational and legal damage from a biased ML model can be severe. It is better to raise concerns early than to deploy a model that discriminates.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
6.1 Supervised Learning in Detail
This is the main focus of this course (Weeks 2–5, 8–10).
How it works:

You have historical data with known outcomes (the label or target variable, Y).
You also have features (predictor variables, X) — the information used to make predictions.
You train an algorithm on this data → it learns the relationship between X and Y.
You apply the trained model to new data to make predictions.

Professor’s note: “The process of creating a machine learning model is known as model building or model training. Once we have the model, we can use it on new data to make predictions.”

6.1.1 Supervised Learning: Classification vs. Regression









Sub-type
What Is Predicted
Output
Algorithms




Classification
A category (class label)
Discrete: “spam” / “not spam”; fraud / not fraud; class 0/1/2/3
Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Networks


Regression
A continuous number
e.g. house price = €345,000; temperature = 18.4 °C
Linear Regression, Generalised Linear Models, Decision Trees, Random Forest, Deep Learning









Important⭐ Classification vs. Regression — Know the Difference



Classification predicts a category. Think: “Which box does this belong in?”
Regression predicts a number. Think: “How much / how many?”

Binary classification = only 2 possible classes (e.g. fraud / not fraud).
Multi-class classification = 3 or more possible classes (e.g. handwritten digits 0–9).

Notice that Decision Trees, Random Forest, and Deep Learning appear in both lists — many algorithms can do either, depending on how you configure them.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
text:
6.2 Reinforcement Learning — The AlphaGo Story
While this course will not cover reinforcement learning in depth, the professor shared a memorable example.






Note🎮 AlphaGo and the Power of Self-Play



Chess engines beat the best humans in the late 1990s. But Go is vastly harder — the number of possible board positions is astronomical compared to chess.
Researchers programmed the rules of Go and set up two copies of the machine to play against each other — millions, possibly billions of times. Over time, the machines discovered strategies no human had ever invented. When AlphaGo eventually played human grandmasters, it won. Some of its moves were initially described as “bad” by commentators — and then, several moves later, revealed themselves as brilliant.
Why this matters: reinforcement learning is behind self-driving cars, robotics, and advanced game-playing AI. It is fundamentally different from supervised learning because the “teacher” is the environment — the agent receives rewards for good actions and penalties for bad ones, and has to discover the right strategy on its own.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Data Mining & Machine Learning
section: 7.1 The Setup: What Are We Doing?
text:
7.1 The Setup: What Are We Doing?
We are going to:

Create a “true” mathematical relationship: y = 1.4x − 1.2
